transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Respiratory failure.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Respiratory failure.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE: , Tracheotomy.,ANESTHESIA: , General inhalation.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION: , The patient was taken to the operating room, placed in the supine position on the operating table.  General inhalation anesthesia was administered via the patient's existing 4.0 endotracheal tube.  The neck was extended and secured with tape and an incision in the midline of the neck, approximately 2 finger widths above the sternal notch, was made.  The incision measured approximately 1 cm and was located just below the palpable cricoid cartilage and first tracheal ring.  The incision area was infiltrated with 1% Xylocaine with epinephrine 1:100,000. A #67 blade was used to make the incision.  Electrocautery was used to remove excess fatty tissue to expose the strap muscles.  The strap muscles were grasped and divided at the midline with a sharp electrocautery.  Sharp dissection was used to expose the anterior trachea and cricoid cartilage.  The thyroid isthmus was identified intersecting just below the cricoid cartilage.  This was divided in the midline by electrocautery.  Blunt dissection was used to expose adequate cartilaginous rings.  A 4.0 silk was used for the holding sutures on the midline of the cricoid.  Additional retaining sutures were placed on either side of the third tracheal ring.  Thin DuoDerm was placed around the stoma.  The tracheal incision was made with a No. 11 blade through the second, third, and fourth tracheal rings.  The cartilaginous edges were fixed to the skin edges with interrupted Monocryl #4-0.  A 4.5 PED stem-tight cuffed Bivona tube was placed and secured with Velcro fasteners.  A flexible endoscope was passed through the tracheostomy tube.  The carina was visualized approximately 1.5 cm distal to the distal end of the tracheostomy tube.  Ventilation was confirmed.  There was good chest rise and no appreciable leak.  The procedure was interrupted.  The patient was in stable condition.  The bleeding was negligible and she was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit in stable condition.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Thrombosed left forearm loop fistula graft.,2.  Chronic renal failure.,3.  Hyperkalemia.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Thrombosed left forearm loop fistula graft.,2.  Chronic renal failure.,3.  Hyperkalemia.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, Left forearm loop graft thrombectomy.,ANESTHESIA:, Local with sedation.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, Less than 5 cc.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,OPERATIVE FINDINGS:, Venous outflow was good.  There was stenosis in the middle venous limb of the graft. INDICATIONS: The patient is an 81-year-old African-American woman with an occluded left forearm loop graft.  She was unable to undergo her dialysis as usual.  Her potassium levels were significantly elevated at 7 p.m. on the first evening of her scheduled surgery.  Surgery and anesthesia thought it would be too risky.  So, she was given medication to lower her potassium and a temporary hemodialysis catheter was placed in the scheduled femoral vein so she would be dialyzed that night as well as this morning.  This morning her pre-dialysis potassium level was 6 so she was scheduled for surgery after her dialysis. PROCEDURE: The patient was taken to the operating room and prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A transverse incision was made at the level of the venous anastomosis of the graft.  Subsequent dissection was performed down to the catheter.  The vein appeared soft and without thrombus.  This output does not appear to be significantly altered.  A transverse incision was made with a #11 blade on the venous branch of the graft near the anastomosis.  Then, a thrombectomy was performed using a Fogarty No. 4 catheter.  A portion of the clot and thrombus was removed from the venous limb.  The balloon got stuck in several places along the venous limb, signifying some degree of stenosis.  After most of the clots were removed from the venous limb before removing the plug, dilators were passed into the venous limb, also indicating the area of ​​stenosis.  At this point, we thought the patient would benefit from curettage of the vein branch of the graft.  This was done and subsequent passes with the dilator and balloon were then very easy and smooth after the curettage.  Fogarty's ball was then passed past the clot and stopper.  The sheet has been viewed and inspected.  This also gave good rapid bleeding from the graft.  The patient was heparinized and hepatitis saline was injected into the venous limb and the angular vascular clamp was applied to the venous limb.  Attention was focused on its anastomosis and the vein.  The Fogarty balloon and thrombectomy were also done quite well this way.  There was quite a bit of venous bleeding from the back after that.  The area was checked for any stenosis with the dilators and none was present.  Next, a No. 6-0 Prolene suture was used in running mode to close the graft.  Just before tying the suture, the graft was allowed to drain to remove debris or air.  The suture was also checked at this time for augmentation, which was good.  The suture was tied and the wound was irrigated with an antibiotic solution.  Next, a No. 3-0 Vicryl was used to approximate the subcutaneous tissues and a No. 4-0 undyed Vicryl was used subcutaneously to approximate the skin edges.  Steri-Strips were applied and the patient was brought to recovery in stable condition.  She tolerated the procedure well.  She will come out of recovery once stable.  She is scheduled to return to her regular dialysis schedule and report for dialysis tomorrow.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Thrombosed arteriovenous shunt left forearm.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Thrombosed arteriovenous shunt, left forearm with venous anastomotic stenosis.,PROCEDURE: , AV shunt thrombectomy, left forearm and patch angioplasty of the anastomosis venous.,ANESTHESIA: , Local.,SKIN PREPARATION: , Betadine.,DRAINAGES: , None.,PROCEDURE TECHNIQUE: ,The left arm was prepared and draped.  Xylocaine 1% was administered and a transverse antecubital incision was made on the venous limb of the graft, which was dissected and surrounded by a vascular loop.  The runoff vein was dissected and also surrounded by the vessel loop.  A longitudinal incision was made above the venous anastomosis.  There was a narrowing in the area and the incision was extended slightly more proximally.  There was good bleeding in the vein as well as the most distal vein.  These were occluded with non-crushing DeBakey forceps and the patient received 5,000 units of heparin intravenously.  A No. 4 Fogarty was used to systematically extract the thrombus from the graft until the arterial plug was removed and excellent inflow was established.  There was a narrowing in the middle portion of the venous limb of the graft, which was dilated using a No. 5 coronary dilator. The Fogarty catheter was then introduced into the vein, but no clot was obtained.  A PTFE patch was fabricated and sutured to the graft with 6-0 Gore-Tex suture.  The clamps were removed and flow was established.  A thrill was easily palpable.  Hemostasis was achieved and the wound was irrigated and closed with 3-0 Vicryl subcutaneous suture followed by 4-0 nylon to the skin.  A sterile dressing was applied.  The patient was transported to the recovery room in satisfactory condition having tolerated the procedure well.  The number of sponges, instruments and needles was reported as correct.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Non-small cell carcinoma of the left upper lobe.,2.  History of lymphoma in remission.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Non-small cell carcinoma of the left upper lobe.,2.  History of lymphoma in remission., PROCEDURE: Left muscle-sparing mini thoracotomy with left upper lobectomy and mediastinal lymph node dissection.  Intercostal Nerve Block for Five Level Postoperative Pain Relief.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS: This is an 84 year old lady who was referred by Dr. A for treatment of her left upper lobe carcinoma.  The patient has a history of lymphoma and is in remission.  An enlarged right axillary lymph node was recently biopsied and found to be negative for lymphoma.  A mass in the left upper lobe was biopsied by fine-needle aspiration and found to be a primary non-small cell carcinoma of the lung.  Furthermore, PET scan was negative for spread and resection was advised.  All risks and benefits were fully explained to the patient and she chose to proceed as planned.  She was transferred to rehab for a few weeks to regain strength before surgery.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: In the operating room under anesthesia, she was prepared and draped appropriately.  Dr. B was the staff anesthesiologist.  A mini thoracotomy sparing the left muscle was performed.  The serratus major and latissimus dorsi muscles were not cut but moved outwards.  Access to the chest was obtained through the fifth intercostal space.  Two right-angled Tuffier retractors provided adequate exposure. The lower pulmonary ligament was not dissected freely and the station 9 lymph nodes have now been sent to pathology.  The parietal pleural reflection around the hilum was now circumcised and the lymph nodes were harvested at stations 8 and 5. The branches of the pulmonary artery up to the upper lobe were now individually stapled with a 30/2.5 stapler or/and the smaller one was ligated with 2-0 silk.  The left upper pulmonary vein was divided using a TA30/2.5 stapler and the fissure was completed using shots from a 60/4.8 endo-GIA stapler.  Finally, the left upper lobe bronchus was divided using a TA30/4.8 stapler.  Please note that this patient had a somewhat unusual variation of a small bronchus that exited posteriorly from the main trunk of the pulmonary artery and supplied a small section of the posterior portion of the left upper lobe. The sample was delivered and sent to pathology.  The mass was clearly palpable in the upper lingular portion of this left upper lobe.  The frozen section showed that the margin was negative. The chest was irrigated with sterile warm water and when the left lower lobe inflated, there was no air leak.  A single 32 French chest tube was inserted and an intercostal block was performed with Marcaine infiltrated two spaces above and two spaces below, thus obtaining a five-level block. 30 ml of Marcaine was used in total.  #2 Vicryl pericostal sutures have now been applied.  The serratus major and latissimus dorsi muscles have retracted.  A French Blake #19 drain placed in the subcutaneous tissues and Vicryl 2-0 used for the fat followed by Monocryl 4-0 for the skin.  The patient was transferred to intensive care in stable condition.

transcription: POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Type 4 thoraco-abdominal aneurysm.,OPERATION/PROCEDURE:, Replacement with 26 mm Dacron graft of a T10 type 4 thoraco-abdominal aneurysm at the bifurcation of the aorta, reimplantation of the celiac, superior mesenteric and right renal artery in island and the left renal in interposition of 8 mm. Dacron transplant,using left heart bypass and cerebrospinal fluid drainage.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION IN DETAIL:The patient was brought to the operating room and placed in a supine position, and general endotracheal anesthesia was induced through a double-lumen endotracheal tube.  The patient was placed in a thoracoabdominal position with the left chest elevated and hips back at a 30-degree angle.  The left groin, abdomen, and chest were prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  A thoraco-abdominal incision was made.  The 8th intermediate space has been entered.  The costal margin was divided.  The retroperitoneal space was entered and dissected bluntly down to the psoas, bringing all peritoneal contents toward the midline, exposing the aorta.  The lower pulmonary ligament was then removed so that the aorta could be dissected freely at the T10 level, just above the diaphragm.  It was freely dissected circumferentially.  The aortic bifurcation was freely dissected, freely dissecting both iliac arteries.  The left lower pulmonary vein was then dissected and a 4-0 Prolene bursa was placed over it.  The patient was heparinized.  Using a stab wound in the center of this, a right-angled venous cannula was then placed at the left atrium and attached to a Rumel tourniquet.  This was connected to a venous inflow from a left heart bypass machine.  A 4-0 Prolene bursa was placed over the aneurysm and, through a stab wound in the center of it, an arterial cannula was placed and hooked to exit.  The bypass was instituted.  The aneurysm was cross-clamped just above T10 and also just below the diaphragm.  The area was divided at this point.  A 26 mm graft was then sutured in place with 3-0 Prolene suture.  The graft was inserted into the diaphragm.  Clamps were then placed on the iliacs and the pump was stopped.  The aorta was opened longitudinally, posteriorly between the left and right renal arteries, and was completely divided at its bifurcation.  The SMA, celiac, and right renal arteries were then freely dissected as a complete island, and the left kidney was freely dissected as a complete Carrell patch.  The island was placed in the graft for visceral lining, and it was sutured in place with 4-0 Prolene suture with 4-0 Prolene sutures pledged around the circumference.  The clamp was then moved under the visceral vessels and the clamp on the chest was removed, restoring flow to the visceral vessels.  The graft was cut to fit the bifurcation and sutured in place with 3-0 Prolene suture.  All clamps were removed and flow was restored.  An 8 mm graft was sutured end to end to the Carrell patch and the left kidney.  A partial occlusion clamp was placed.  A graft area was removed.  The end of the graft was cut to accommodate this and sutured in place with a running Prolene suture.  The partial occlusion clamp was removed.  Protamine was administered.  Good hemostasis was noted.  The arterial cannula, of course, had been removed when that part of the aneurysm was removed.  The venous cannula was removed and covered with 4-0 Prolene suture.  Good hemostasis was noted.  A 36 French posterior and a 32 French anterior chest tube were placed.  The ribs were closed with Vicryl No. 2 in the shape of an eight.  The fascial layer was closed with Prolene No. 1, subcutaneously with Dexon 2-0, and the skin with a subcutaneous stitch of Dexon 4-0.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left mesothelioma, focal.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left pleural nodule.,PROCEDURES PERFORMED:,1.  Left thoracoscopy.,2.  Left mini thoracotomy with resection of the left pleural mass.,FINDINGS:, left anterior pleural nodule, which was on a thin pleural pedicle without invasion into the chest wall.,FLUIDS:, 800 ml of crystalloid.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, minimal.,DRAINS, TUBES, CATHETERS:, 24 French chest tubes in the left thorax plus catheter Foley.,SAMPLES:,Left pleural nodule.,INDICATION FOR OPERATION:,The patient is a 59-year-old woman with a history of follicular thyroid cancer, approximately 40 years ago, after resection with recurrence in the 1980s, who had a left pleural mass identified on a chest x-ray.  Preoperative evaluation included a CT scan that showed a focal mass.  CT and PET confirm a previous lesion.  Therefore, the patient was seen in our thoracic tumor committee where resection with chest wall reconstruction was recommended.  On an outpatient basis, the patient was willing to continue. PROCEDURE CARRIED OUT IN DETAIL: After obtaining informed consent, the patient identified himself correctly.  She was taken to the operating room where an epidural catheter was placed without difficulty by Anesthesia.  She was sedated and intubated without difficulty with a double-lumen endotracheal tube.  She was positioned with the left side up.  Appropriate pressure points have been padded.  The left chest was prepared and draped in the standard surgical manner.  The skin incision was made in the posterior axillary line, approximately in the 7th intercostal space with a No. 10 blade, descended through the tissues and Bovie electrocautery. The pleura has been penetrated.  There was good deflation of the left lung.  The __________ port was placed, followed by the 0 degree 10 mm endoscope with appropriate patient positioning.  Posteriorly, a 2.5 x 3 cm pedunculated pleural mass was identified on the anterior chest wall.  There were fine adhesions to the pleura, but no chest wall invasion could be identified.  The tumor was very mobile and was on a pedunculated stem of approximately 1.5 cm.  It was felt that this could be resected without the need to reconstruct the chest wall due to the narrow stem. Therefore, a second port was placed in the anterior axillary line, approximately at the 8th intercostal space, in the usual manner.  The camera was placed via this port.  Laparoscopic scissors were placed through the posterior port, but it was necessary to have another instrument to provide more tension than just gravity.  Therefore, due to the need to pass the sample through the chest wall, a small 3 cm thoracotomy was performed, incorporating the posterior port site.  This was brought back to the subcutaneous tissue with a Bovie electrocautery.  A periosteal elevator was used to lift the intercostal muscle.  The ribs were not spread.  Thanks to this 3 cm incision, the laparoscopic scissors as well as the Prestige forceps could be placed.  Prestige forceps were used to extract the specimen from the chest wall.  Care was taken not to injure the capsule.  Laparoscopic scissors on cautery were used to resect the parietal pleura from the chest wall.  We took care not to cut the stem.  The specimen detached from the chest wall very easily.  Hemostasis was good. At this point, the EndoCatch bag was placed through the incision.  The specimen was placed in the bag and then removed from the field.  Hemostasis was good.  The camera has been removed.  A 24 French chest tube was placed through the anterior port and secured with a 2-0 silk suture.  The posterior port site was closed first with 2-0 Vicryl in a routine manner for the intercostal muscle layer, followed by 2-0 closure of the latissimus dorsi fascia along with subcutaneous suturing, 4-0 Monocryl was used for the skin, followed by Steri-Strips and sterile drapes.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was extubated in the operating room.

transcription: TITLE OF OPERATION: 1. Repair of total abnormal pulmonary venous connection.,2.  Ligation of the patent ductus arteriosus.,3.  Repair the secundum type atrial septal defect (autologous pericardial patch).,4.  Subtotal thymectomy.,5.  Insertion of a peritoneal dialysis catheter.,INDICATION FOR SURGERY: This neonate was diagnosed after birth with a total anomalous pulmonary venous connection.  After initial stabilization, she was transferred to the hospital for complete correction.,PREOPERATION DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Total abnormal pulmonary venous connection.,2.  Atrial communication.,3.  Patent ductus arteriosus.,4.  Operating weight less than 4 kilograms (3.2 kilograms).,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,CROSS CLAMP TIME: , 63 minutes.,CARDIOPULMONARY BYPASS TIME MONITOR: 35 minutes, deep hypothermic circulatory arrest time (4 plus 19) is equal to 23 minutes.  Low flow perfusion 32 minutes., RESULTS: Horizontal pulmonary venous confluence with the right upper and middle veins with two veins entering the confluence on the right and multiple entry sites for the left sided veins.  Large patulous anastomosis between the posterior surface of the left atrium and the anterior surface of the pulmonary venous confluence.  Unobstructed ascending vein ligated.  Persistence of the diminutive ductus arteriosus of the left atrium with posterior atrial septal defect with deficient lower margin.  At the end of the procedure, right ventricular pressure close to half of normal systemic sinus rhythm, good biventricular function by visual inspection.,PROCEDURE: After informed consent, the patient was brought to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  During induction of general endotracheal anesthesia and establishment of indwelling arterial and venous monitoring lines.  The patient was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner, from chin to groin.  A median sternotomy incision was made.  Dissection was carried out through the deepest planes until the sternum was incised and divided with an oscillating saw.  A subtotal thymectomy was performed.  Systemic heparinization was performed and the pericardium was penetrated and shaped to the cradle.  A small portion of the anterior pericardium was removed and fixed in glutaraldehyde for patch closure of the atrial septal defect segment during the procedure.  Purse strings were deployed over the ascending aorta on the right.  Auricular appendage.  The aorta was then cannulated with an 8-French aortic cannula and the right atrium with an 18-French Polystan right-angle cannula.  With an ACT greater than 400, a larger lung bypass was started with excellent cardiac decompression and the patent ductus arteriosus was ligated with a 2-0 silk tie.  Systemic cooling was started and the head was packed and iced and systemic steroids were administered.  During cooling, a traction suture was placed at the apex of the left ventricle.  After 25 minutes of cooling, the aorta was cross-clamped and the heart stopped by administration of 30 cubic centimeters/kilogram of cold blood cardioplegia delivered directly into the aortic root after cross-clamping of the aorta.  After successful cardioplegic arrest, a low flow perfusion period was started and a 10 French catheter was inserted into the right atrial appendage replacing the 18 French Polystan venous cannula.  The heart was then rotated to the right and the venous confluence was exposed.  It was incised and enlarged and a corresponding incision in the dorsal and posterior aspect of the left atrium was made.  The two openings were then anastomosed end-to-side with several interlocking sutures to avoid the purse-string effect with a 7-0 PDS suture running.  Once the anastomosis was complete, the heart was returned to the chest and the patient's blood volume was drained into the reservoir.  A right auriotomy was then performed during the period of circulatory arrest.  The atrial septal defect was very difficult to expose, but it was sealed

transcription: HISTORY AND PHYSICS: The patient is a 13-year-old boy who has a history of Shone complex and presents with complete heart block.  He is on a pacemaker.  He had a coarctation of the aorta and it was repaired when he was a baby.  He has been followed in our cardiology clinic here and is doing well.  However, last night he was sleeping and he states that he felt like he was having a dream, and there was thunder in the dream that woke him up.  He then felt his defibrillator go off and it continued and he felt like his heart rate was not normal.  So his father put him in the car and transported him here.  It has been reviewed here.  He had scar tissue at one point when the internal stimulator wasn't working properly and he had to have it replaced.  It was 2 a.m. when he woke up and, again, he was brought here by private vehicle.  He was fine before going to bed.  No cough, cold, runny nose, fever.  No trauma was noted.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Shone complex, pacemaker dependent.,MEDICATIONS:, He is not currently taking any medications.,ALLERGIES:, He has no allergies.,IMMUNIZATIONS:, Up to date.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, He lives with his parents.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Negative.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEM:, Twelve questions, all negative unless otherwise noted. above.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: This is an awake and alert male, who appears to be in mild distress.,HEENT: Pupils are equal, round and responsive to light.  Extraocular movements are intact.  His TMs are clear.  His nostrils are clear.  The mucous membranes are pink and moist.  The throat is clear. NECK: Supple without lymphadenopathy or masses.  Trachea is midline.,LUNGS: clear.,HEART: shows bradycardia at 53. Has good distal pulses.,ABDOMEN: soft, not tender.  Positive bowel sounds.  No guard, no bounce.  No rash is observed.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,Initial blood pressure is 164/90.  He was transferred to room 1. He was placed on a nasal cannula.  The pulse ox was 100%, which is normal.  We put it on a monitor.  We did an ECG; it doesn't seem to be picking up his pacemaker at the moment.  Shortly after the patient arrived, the Medtronic technician came to test his pacemaker.  The Medtronic representative informed me that the lead they had in place had been recalled because it was prone to microfractures, oversensing, and automatic defibrillation.  As instructed, he was moved to room 1, placed on a monitor, pulse ox.  An IV was placed.  A standard blood test was sent.  A chest x-ray was taken showing a normal heart size, the lead appeared to be in place.  There were no signs of pulmonary edema.  His pacemaker didn't seem to be picking up.  We placed him on a transthoracic tube.  However, it is difficult to get good placement with these due to the area where his pacemaker was placed.  The Medtronic technician first turned off his defibrillation mode and lowered his sensor.  However, we were unable to get our transthoracic pacemaker to capture his heart.  When the Medtronic representative turned off the pacemaker, the heart rate seemed to drop into the 40s. The patient appeared to be in pain.  We put it back at the rate of 60 at that point.  He still has sinus bradycardia, but no signs of extrasystoles.  No widening of his QRS complex.  I spoke with Cardiology.  The cardiology department arrived and assessed him at my bedside.  Again, we increased the transthoracic pacemaker, but it doesn't seem to be speeding up yet, and his heart rate continues with the Medtronic's internal pacemaker.  So, he and the ICU doctor on call, Dr. X, agreed to take this young man to the ICU. An hour after the presentation here, the ICU was ready for bed.  I accompanied the patient to the ICU.  He remained awake and alert.  At first, he complained of severe chest pain.  Once the defibrillator was turned off, he no longer had any pain.  He was transported to the pediatric PICU and delivered in stable condition. LABORATORY DATA: CBC was normal.  Chem-20 was also normal.

transcription: DIAGNOSIS AND PREOPERATIVE INDICATIONS:, Acute MI without ST-segment elevation., POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS AND SUMMARY:, The patient presented with an acute MI without ST-segment elevation.  Despite medical treatment, she still suffered from intermittent angina.  Angiography demonstrated that severe LAD was the culprit lesion.  This was treated as noted above with angioplasty alone as the stent could not be advanced safely.  She has 75% residual lesions in the proximal right coronary and 60% in the proximal circumflex, along with the other residual LAD lesions as noted above.  She will continue her medical treatment.  At 90 years old, she is not a good candidate for aortic valve replacement and coronary artery bypass grafting.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,Selective coronary angiography, coronary angioplasty.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,After obtaining informed consent, the patient was taken to the catheterization laboratory, placed on the table in the supine position.  The right femoral artery area was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  By percutaneous technique, a 6-French sheath was placed in the right femoral artery under fluoroscopic guidance.  With the guidewire in place, a 5-French JL-4 catheter was used to selectively angiogram the left coronary system.  The catheter was removed.  The sheath reddens.  The 5-French 3DRC catheter was then used to selectively angiography the right coronary artery.  The catheter was removed and the sheath rinsed. It was decided that intervention was needed on the severe LAD lesions, which appeared to be the causative lesions for non-ST-segment elevation MI.  The patient received a heparin bolus and an ACT of approximately 50 seconds was obtained, we rebolized and the ACT was slightly lower.  We repeated the level and it was slightly higher.  We administered an additional 500 units of heparin and then did an ACT for approximately 270 seconds before the 500 units of IV heparin.  Additionally, the patient received Plavix 300 mg orally during the procedure and an Integrilin IV bolus, and then a maintenance infusion was started. A 6-French CLS 3.5 left coronary guide catheter was used to cannulate the left main portion and one HEW guidewire was positioned in the distal LAD and another HEW guidewire in the relatively large third diagonal.  A 2.5 x 15 mm Apex balloon was positioned in the distal portion of the mid LAD stenosis and inflated to 6 atmospheres for 15 seconds, then deflated.  An angiogram was then performed, demonstrating marked improvement in the stenosis and this image was used to size the final stent needed.  The balloon was pulled more proximally and then inflated again to 6 atmospheres for approximately 20 seconds, with the proximal end of the balloon positioned distal to the origin of the third diagonal so as not to compromise the ostium.  The balloon was inflated and removed, a repeat angiogram was performed.  We attempted to advance a Driver 2.5 x 24 mm bare metal stent, but I was unable to advance it beyond the proximal LAD, where there was significant calcification.  The stent was removed.  Attempts to advance the same 2.5 x 15mm Apex balloon previously used were unsuccessful.  It was removed, a new Apex 2.5 x 15 mm balloon was then positioned in the proximal LAD and inflated to 6 atmospheres for 15 seconds, then deflated and slightly advanced with the distal end of the balloon close to the third diagonal ostium and it was inflated to 6 atmospheres for 15 seconds then deflated and removed.  Repeat angiography showed no signs of dissection.  Another attempt was made to advance the Driver 2.5 x 24 mm bare metal stent, but again, I was unable to advance it beyond the calcified plaque in the proximal LAD despite the presence of the buddy wire in the diagonal.  I reasoned that further attempts into this calcified vessel in a 90-year-old patient with severe aortic stenosis and severe aortic regurgitation would likely result in dissection complications, so the stent was removed.  The guidewires and guiding catheter were removed.

transcription: CONSULTATION INDICATION:, Increasing need for oxygen.,HISTORY:, The little boy, XYZ, is an infant of 29 3/7 weeks gestation.  His mother had a premature rupture of membranes on 12/20/08.  She then presented to the labor and delivery unit with flu-like symptoms.  The baby was then induced and delivered.  The mother had previously had premature babies.  This baby was doing well, and then we had a significant increase in ambient air oxygen requirements up to 85%.  He is now on 60% FiO2.,PHYSICAL FINDINGS,GENERAL: He appeared to be pink, well perfused and slightly jaundiced.,VITAL SIGNS: pulse 156, respiratory rate 56, sat 92% and blood pressure 59/28 mmHg.,SKIN: He was pink.,He was on a high frequency ventilator with good agitation.,His echocardiogram showed normal structure. anatomy.  He presents with signs of significant pulmonary hypertension.  A large ductus arteriosus was observed with a bidirectional shunt.  Foramen ovale shunting was also noted with bidirectional shunting.  The shunt for the canal and foramen ovale was equal left to right and right to left.,IMPRESSION: My impression is that little boy, XYZ, has significant pulmonary hypertension.  The best therapy for this is to continue administering oxygen.  If the clinical condition worsens, he may need nitric oxide.  Certainly, Indocin should not be used at this time.  He must have lower pulmonary artery pressures for this to be taken into account. Thank you so much for allowing me to be involved in baby XYZ's care.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Ruling out obstructive sleep apnea syndrome. The patient sample is a pleasant 61-year-old obese African-American male with a significant past medical history of hypertension who presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of loud snoring and episodes of apnea observed by his wife of at least five years.  He denies any episodes of panting, choking, or coughing while sleeping at night.  He goes to bed between 10 p.m. and 11 p.m., has no difficulty falling asleep, and usually gets up around 7 a.m. feeling refreshed.  He has two to three episodes of nocturia per night.  He denies any morning symptoms.  He has mild excessive daytime sleepiness that manifests as sleepiness during boring activities.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Hypertension, gastritis, and low back pain.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, TURP.,DRUGS:, Hytrin, Motrin, Lotensin, and Zantac.,ALLERGIES:, None.,HISTORY FAMILY:, Hypertension.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Important for about a year. Tobacco consumption of 20 packs per year, stopped in 1991. No consumption of ethanol or illicit drugs.  He is married.  He has a dog at home.  He was employed at Budd Automotors as a die fitter for approximately 37 to 40 years.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: His weight has remained stable over the years.  Collar size is 17½". He denies any chest pain, cough or shortness of breath. Last chest x-ray last year, according to his report, was normal.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, A pleasant, obese African-American male, with no apparent respiratory distress. Weight: 198 lbs. HEENT: Short, thick neck, low palate, enlarged scalloped tongue, narrow, shortened pharynx, clear nostrils, and no JVD. HEART: Regular rate and rhythm without any incidental sounds. CHEST: Clear lungs bilaterally. ABDOMEN: Obese abdomen with active bowel sounds.,No cyanosis, clubbing or edema.,IMPRESSION:,1.Hypertension.,3. tobacco consumption.,PLAN:,1.We will schedule an overnight sleep study to assess for obstructive sleep apnea syndrome.,2.Weight loss encouraged.,3.Requested not to drive or engage in any activity that may endanger self or others while asleep.,5.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 91-year-old man with a history of coal mine work and significant silica exposure resulting in pneumoconiosis and pulmonary fibrosis.  The patient also has a positive history of smoking in the past.  Currently, he is admitted for ongoing management of respiratory depression with other medical complications.  The patient was treated for multiple problems at Jefferson Hospital before coming here, including abdominal discomfort due to a ureteral stone leading to hydronephrosis and hydroureter.  Additionally, he also developed heart complications, including atrial fibrillation.  The patient was evaluated by the cardiologist as well as the pulmonary and urology department.  He underwent cystoscopy and a left ureteral stone was removed along with placement of a left ureteral stent on 07/23/2008.  He then suffered a cardiac arrest and was resuscitated at that time.  He was intubated and placed on mechanical respiratory support.  Subsequent weaning was unsuccessful.  He then underwent a tracheotomy.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:,1.  Albuterol.,2.  Pacerone.,3.  Theophylline,4.  Lovenox.,5.  Atrovent.,6.  Insulin.,7.  Lantus.,8.  Zestril.,9.  Magnesium oxide.,10.  Lopressor.,11.  Zegerid.,12.  Tylenol as needed.,ALLERGIES:,PENICILLIN.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Disease history of coal miners.,2.  History of COPD.,3.  History of atrial fibrillation.,4.  History of coronary heart disease.,5.  History of coronary artery stenting.,6.  History of gastric obstruction.,7.  History of prostate cancer.,8.  History of chronic diarrhea.,9.  History of pernicious anemia.,10.  History of radiological proctitis.,11.  Antecedents of anxiety.,12.  History of ureteral stones.,13.  History of hydronephrosis.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,Patient was previously a smoker.  No further could be obtained due to tracheotomy at this time.,FAMILY HISTORY:,Not contributory to current condition and review of his previous records.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Patient is currently agitated.  Move your upper limbs quickly.  No further history regarding his systems could be obtained from the patient.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General: The patient is currently agitated with some level of distress.  He has a rapid respiratory rate.  He responds to verbal commands by looking at the eyes.,Vital Signs: According to monitors, they are stable.,Extremities: Inspection of the upper extremities reveals extreme xerosis of the skin with multiple areas of bruising and skin tears, some of them up to stage II, particularly on the backs of the hands and forearms.  There is also forearm swelling extending to the middle of the arm.  Palpation of the upper limbs reveals greater fibrosis in the right forearm with maximum edema at the elbow on the ulnar side.  There are also scabs from some of the earlier skin tears in the upper forearm area.,PRINT:,1.  Ulceration of bilateral upper limbs.,2.  Cellulitis of the upper limbs.,3.  Upper limb lymphedema.,4.  Other non-infectious disorders of the lymphatic channels.,5.  Ventilator-dependent respiratory failure.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , Management of blood pressure., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a 38-year-old woman admitted following childbirth.  The patient had a cesarean section.  Following this, the patient was treated for her blood pressure.  She was sent home and returned apparently with uncontrolled blood pressure.  She takes several medications and is unable to control her blood pressure.  Cardiacly, the patient denies any symptoms of chest pain or shortness of breath.  She complains of fatigue and exhaustion.  The child had a birth defect and was taken to hospital, where he underwent surgery.  The patient is in the intensive care unit.,CORONARY RISK FACTORS: history of hypertension, history of gestational diabetes mellitus, non-smoker and normal cholesterol.  No history of established coronary heart disease or family history non-contributory to coronary heart disease.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Not significant.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,No major surgery except cesarean section.,DRUGS:,Currently, Cardizem and metoprolol have been discontinued.  I started taking 50 mg of hydralazine 3 times a day and 200 mg of labetalol 2 times a day, hydrochlorothiazide and insulin supplementation.,ALLERGIES: , None.,PERSONAL HISTORY: , Non-smoker.  Do not consume alcohol.  No history of recreational drug use.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Hypertension, gestational diabetes mellitus, pre-eclampsia, this is her third child with a miscarriage.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,CONSTITUTIONAL: No history of fever, chills or rigors.,HEENT: No history of cataracts, blurred vision or glaucoma.,CARDIOVASCULAR: No congestive heart.  No arrhythmia.,RESPIRATORY: No history of pneumonia or valley fever.,GASTROINTESTINAL: No epigastric discomfort, hematemesis or melena.,UROLOGICAL: No frequency or urgency.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: No arthritis or muscle weakness.,SKIN: Not significant.,NEUROLOGICAL: No TIA.  No CVA.  No seizure disorder.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: pulse 86, blood pressure 175/86, afebrile and respiratory rate 16 per minute.,HEENT: atraumatic and normocephalic.,NECK: neck veins are flat.,LUNGS: clear.,HEART: S1 and S2 regular.,ABDOMEN: soft and not tender.,EXTREMITIES: not edema.  Palpable pulses., LABORATORY DATA: Electrocardiogram shows sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST-T changes.  Labs were noted.  BUN and creatinine within normal limits.,PRINT:,1.  Preeclampsia, condition after cesarean delivery with uncontrolled blood pressure.,2.  No history of heart disease except borderline gestational diabetes mellitus.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,1.  We will obtain an echocardiogram to assess left ventricular function.,2.  The patient will start taking labetalol and hydralazine to see how he does.,3.  Depending on the response to the medications, we will make further adjustments.  Discussed the care plan with the patient, he understands it perfectly and agrees to it.  All questions answered in detail.

transcription: INDICATION: ,Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,The patient is a pleasant 55-year-old white female with multiple myeloma.  She is in post-chemotherapy and autologous stem cell transplant status.  The latter occurred on 05/02/2007.  At that time, she was on a telemetry monitor and noticed that she had normal sinus rhythm. As part of the study protocol for an investigational drug for mucositis prophylaxis, she underwent an electrocardiogram on 02/06/2007.  This demonstrated the underlying rhythm of atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response at 125 beats per minute.  It was then transferred to telemetry for observation.  A cardiology consultation was requested.  Prior to formal consultation, the patient underwent an echocardiogram performed on 06/02/2007, which showed a structurally normal heart with normal left ventricular (LV) systolic function, 60% ejection fraction, aortic sclerosis without stenosis, trivial pericardial effusion without signs of immunosuppression, and mild tricuspid regurgitation with normal pulmonary atrial pressures.  Overall, essentially normal heart.,At the time of my evaluation, the patient was feeling somewhat jittery and jittery, but otherwise asymptomatic.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Multiple myeloma, diagnosed June 2006, condition after treatment with thalidomide and Coumadin.  Subsequently, with high-dose chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, Cosmetic surgery of the nose and forehead.,ALLERGIES:, NO KNOWN DRUG ALLERGIES.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS,1.  Acyclovir 400 mg p.o. offers,2.  Filgrastim 300 mcg subcutaneous daily.,3.  Fluconazole 200 mg daily.,4.  Levofloxacin 250 mg p.o. daily.,5.  Pantoprazole 40 mg daily.,6.  Ursodiol 300 mg p.o. offers,7.  The investigational drug is directed to ondansetron 24 mg p.r.n.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Unremarkable.  Father and mother both living in their mid-sixties.  The father has an unspecified heart problem and diabetes.  The mother has no significant medical problems.  She has a brother, a sister aged 53, who was implanted with a pacemaker for unknown reasons.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,The patient is married.  Has four adult children.  Good health.  She is a lifelong non-smoker and a social alcohol drinker.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: Before treatment for her multiple myeloma, she was able to walk four miles nonstop.  Currently, she suffers from exertional dyspnea of ​​the order of a block.  She denies any orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.  She denies any edema of the lower limbs.  She presents neither symptomatic palpitations nor tachycardia.  She never had presyncope or syncope.  She denies any chest pain.  She denies any history of coagulopathy or bleeding diathesis.  His oncological disorder is multiple myeloma.  Pulmonary systems examination is negative for recurrent pneumonia, bronchitis, reactive airway disease, asbestos exposure, or tuberculosis.  Gastrointestinal (GI) systems examination is negative for known gastroesophageal reflux, GI bleeding, and hepatobiliary disease.  Genitourinary systems examination is negative for nephrolithiasis or hematuria.  Musculoskeletal systems examination is negative for significant arthralgia or myalgia.  Examination of central nervous system (CNS) systems is negative for tics, tremor, transient ischemic attacks (TIAs), seizures, or stroke.  Psychiatric examination of systems is negative for known affective or cognitive disorders.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: This is a well-nourished, well-developed white female who appears her age listed and somewhat anxious.,VITAL SIGNS: She is afebrile at 97.4 degrees Fahrenheit with a heart rate ranging from 115 to 150 beats per minute, irregularly irregular.  Respirations are 20 breaths per minute and blood pressure ranges from 90/59 to 107/68 mmHg.  The oxygen saturation of the ambient air is 94%.,HEENT: Benign being normocephalic and atraumatic. transcription: HISTORY: The patient is a 5 1/2 year old child who recently presented with a heart murmur diagnosed due to patent ductus arteriosus.  An echocardiogram on 09/13/2007 demonstrated a 3.8 mm patent ductus arteriosus with a restrictive left-to-right shunt.  There is slight hypertrophy of the left atrial chamber with an LA/AO ratio of 1.821.  An electrocardiogram demonstrated normal sinus rhythm with possible left atrial enlargement and left ventricular hypertrophy.  The patient underwent cardiac catheterization for closure of a ductus arteriosus.,PROCEDURE: After sedation and local anesthesia with xylocaine, the patient was prepared and draped.  Cardiac catheterization was performed as indicated in the attached supplementary data sheets.  Vascular entry was done by percutaneous technique and the patient was heparinized.  Monitoring during the procedure included continuous surface ECG, continuous pulse oximetry, and cyclical cuff blood pressures, in addition to intravascular pressures. Using a 5-French sheath, a 5-French wedge catheter was inserted into the right femoral vein and advanced through the right cardiac structures to the secondary pulmonary arteries.  The atrial septum was not patented by the probe. Using a 4-French sheath, a 4-French marker pigtail catheter was inserted into the right femoral artery advanced retrograde toward the descending aorta, ascending aorta, and left ventricle.  A descending aortogram showed a small patent ductus arteriosus type A with a small left-to-right angiographic shunt.  The minimum diameter was approximately 1.6 mm with a bulb diameter of 5.8 mm and a length of 6.2 mm.  The wedge catheter could be directed from the main pulmonary artery through the ductus arteriosus to the descending aorta.  This catheter was wire exchanged for a 5 French nit occlusion delivery catheter through which a flexible 6/5 nit occlusion coil was advanced and allowed reconfiguration of the descending aorta.  The entire system was then introduced into the ductal ampulla or a spiral loop was introduced into the main pulmonary artery.  Once stable device configuration was confirmed by fluoroscopy, the device was released from the delivery catheter.  Hemodynamic measurements and angiography in the descending aorta were then repeated approximately 10 minutes after device implantation.,Flows were calculated by the Fick technique using measured assumed oxygen consumption and contents derived from Radiometer hemoximeter saturations and hemoglobin capacity.,Cineangiograms were obtained with injection into the descending aorta.,After angiography, two systems Normal appearing renal collectors were visualized.  Catheters and sheaths were removed and topical pressure applied for hemostasis.  The patient was returned to the recovery room in satisfactory condition.  There were no complications.,DISCUSSION:Oxygen consumption was assumed to be normal.  Mixed venous saturation was normal with slight increased saturation of the pulmonary arterial branches due to left-to-right shunting through the ductus arteriosus.  The left heart was completely saturated.  Right and left phasic pressures were normal.  The calculated systemic flow was normal and the pulmonary flow was slightly increased with a QP:QS ratio of 1:1.  Vascular resistance was normal.  A cineangiogram with contrast injection into the descending aorta showed a small cone-shaped ductus arteriosus with a small left-to-right angiographic shunt.  The pulmonary arterial branches appeared normal.  There is also a normal left aortic arch. After spiral embolization of the ductus arteriosus, there is no change in mixed venous saturation.  No evidence of residual left-to-right shunting.  There is no change in pressures on the right side.  There is a slight increase in phasic pressures on the left side.  The calculated systemic flow was unchanged compared to the resting state and the pulmonary flow was similar with a QP:QS ratio of 1:1. A slight fever has been observed in recent weeks.  The patient also presents with chest pain described as a dull ache in the precordial region.  Nothing to do with effort or activity.  No aggravating or relieving factor.  A chest CT scan was performed, which showed pericardial effusion.  This consultation is for the same.  The patient denies any dizziness or vertigo.  No presyncope or syncope.  Activity is fairly stable. CORONARY RISK FACTORS: History of borderline hypertension.  No history of diabetes mellitus.  Non-smoking.  Cholesterol level is within normal limits.  No established history of coronary heart disease. transcription: PRE-PROCEDURE DIAGNOSIS:, Complete heart block.,POST-PROCEDURE DIAGNOSIS:, Complete heart block.,PROCEDURES PLANNED AND PERFORMED,1.  Implantation of a dual-chamber pacemaker.,2.  Fluoroscopic guidance for implantation of a dual-chamber pacemaker.,FLUOROSCOPY DURATION: 2.6 minutes.,MEDICATIONS AT TIME OF STUDY,1.  Poured 2.5 mg.,2.  Fentanyl 150 mcg.,3.  Benadryl 50 mg.,CLINICAL HISTORY: The patient is a pleasant 80 year old woman who presented to the hospital with complete heart block.  She was referred for pacemaker implantation.,RISKS AND BENEFITS:The risks, benefits, and alternatives to dual-chamber pacemaker implantation were discussed with the patient.  The patient agreed verbally and by written consent.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:,The patient was transported to the cardiac catheterization laboratory in a fasting state.  The left deltopectoral groove region was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  Lidocaine 1% (20 ml) was administered to the area.  After achieving appropriate anesthesia, percutaneous access to the left axillary vein was then performed under fluoroscopy.  A guidewire was advanced into the vein.  Following this, a 4-inch-long transverse incision was made through the skin and subcutaneous tissue, exposing the pectoral fascia and muscle beneath.  Hemostasis was achieved by electrocautery.  Lidocaine 1% (10 ml) was then administered to the medial aspect of the incision.  A pocket was then fashioned in the medial direction.  Using the previously placed wire, a 7-French lateral arm sheath was advanced over the wire into the left axillary vein.  The dilator was then removed over the wire.  A second wire was then advanced through the sheath to the left axillary vein.  The sheath was then removed over the two wires.  A wire was then pinned to the field.  Using the remaining wire, a 7 French lateral arm sheath was advanced to the left axillary vein.  The dilator and wire were removed.  A passive stimulation lead was then advanced to the right atrium.  The peelable sheath has been removed.  The probe was then passed through the tricuspid valve and positioned at the apical location.  Adequate stimulation and sensing functions have been established.  The suture sleeve was advanced to the tissue entry point and securely connected to the tissue.  With the remaining wire, a 7-French lateral arm sheath was advanced over the wire into the axillary vein.  The wire and dilating sheaths were removed.  An active pacing lead was then advanced to the right atrium.  The peelable sheath has been removed.  The preformed J stylet was then advanced into the lead.  The probe was positioned at the location of the appendix.  The probe body was then rotated and the active fixation screw was secured to the tissue.  Adequate stimulation and sensing functions have been established.  The suture sleeve was advanced to the tissue entry point and securely connected to the tissue.  The pouch was then washed with saline solution infused with antibiotics.  The pulse generator was obtained and securely connected to the cables.  The wires were then carefully wound behind the pulse generator and the entire system was placed in the pocket.  The pocket was then closed with 2-0, 3-0 and 4-0 Vicryl using a common mattress stitch.  The number of sponges and needles was correct at the end of the procedure.  No acute complications were noted.,DEVICE DATA,1. Pulse generator, manufacturer Boston Scientific, model no.12345, serial number 1234.,2.  Right ear probe, manufacturer Guidant, model no. 12345, serial number 1234.,3.  Right ventricular lead, manufacturer Guidant, model no. 12345, serial no. 1234., INTRAOPERATIVE DATA MEASURED, 1. Impedance of the right atrial lead 534 ohms.  P waves measured at 1.2 millivolts.  Pacing threshold 1.0 volt at 0.5 milliseconds.,2.  Right ventricular lead impedance 900 ohms.  The R waves measured 6.0 millivolts.  Pacing threshold 1.0 volt at 0.5 milliseconds.,DEVICE PARAMETERS:, DDD 60 to 130.,CONCLUSIONS,1.  Successful implantation of a dual chamber pacemaker

transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, DDDR permanent pacemaker.,INDICATION:, Tachybrady syndrome.,PROCEDURE:,Once all risks, benefits and alternatives of the procedure were explained in detail to the patient, informed consent was obtained verbally and in writing.  The patient was taken to the cardiac catheterization department where the right subclavian region was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A 1% lidocaine solution was used to infiltrate the skin overlying the left subclavian vein.  Once adequate anesthesia was achieved, a thin-walled No. 18 gauze argon needle was used to cannulate the left subclavian vein.  A steel guidewire was inserted through the needle into the vascular lumen without resistance.  The needle was then withdrawn over the guidewire and the guidewire was attached to the field.  A second #18 argon gauze needle was used to cannulate the left subclavian vein and, once again, a steel guidewire was inserted through the needle into the vascular lumen.  Similarly, the needle was withdrawn over the guidewire and the guidewire was then attached to the field.  Next, a #15 knife blade was used to make a 1- to 1.5-inch linear incision over the area.  A #11 knife blade was used to make a deeper incision.  Hemostasis was made complete.  The edges of the incision were grasped and retracted.  Using Metzenbaum scissors, the dissection was carried out up to the fascial plane of the pectoral muscle.  Blunt-tip digital dissection was used to create a pacemaker pocket large enough to accommodate the pacemaker generator.  Metzenbaum scissors were then used to dissect the head to expose the guidewires.  The guidewires were then pulled through the pacemaker pocket.  A guide wire was attached to the field. A bloodless introducer sheath was then advanced over a guidewire into the vascular lumen under fluoroscopic guidance.  The guidewire and dilator were then removed.  Next, a ventricular pacemaker lead was advanced through the sheath and into the vascular lumen and under fluoroscopic guidance guided downward into the right atrium.  The pacemaker lead was then placed in the appropriate position in the right ventricle.  Stimulation and detection thresholds were obtained.  The probe was sewn at the pectoral muscle plane using #2-0 silk suture in interrupted stitches around the ________.  The stimulation and detection thresholds were then reconfirmed.  Next, a second bloodless introducer sheath was advanced over the second guidewire into the vascular lumen.  The guidewire and dilator were then removed.  Under fluoroscopic guidance, the atrial probe was passed into the right atrium.  The sheath was then returned in a standard manner.  Using fluoroscopic guidance, the ear probe was then placed in the appropriate position.  Stimulation and detection thresholds were obtained.  The probe was sewn to the facial plane of the pectoral muscle using a No. 2-0 silk suture around the ________.  The detection and stimulation thresholds were then reconfirmed.  The leads were cleared of blood and placed into the pacemaker generator.  The pacemaker generator leads were then placed in the pocket, with the leads posterior.  The deep tissues were closed using #2-0 chromic suture in an interrupted stitch fashion.  An undyed Vicryl #4-0 was then used to close the subcutaneous tissue into a continuous subcutaneous stitch.  Layered Steri-Strips.  A sterile dressing was placed over the site.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the cardiac catheterization room in stable and satisfactory condition.,PACEMAKER DATA (GENERATOR DATA):,Manufacturer: Medtronics.,Model: Sigma.,Model No.: 1234.,Serial No.: 123456789.,SERIES INFORMATION:,Right Atrial Probe :,Manufacturer: Medtronics.,Model #: 1234.,Serial #: 123456789., VENTRICULAR LEAD:, Manufacturer: Medtronics., Model #: 1234., Serial #: 123456789., PACE AND SENSING THRESHOLDS:, Right Atrial Bipolar Lead: Pulse Width 0.50 milliseconds, impedance 518 ohms, P wave detection 2.2 millivolts, polarity is bipolar.,Bipolar ventricular lead

transcription: PROCEDURE NOTE: ,Interrogation of the ICD about the pacemaker. ,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,The patient is a 67-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital.  He underwent implantation of an ICD pacemaker.  This is a DRRS pacemaker, current model 12345 St. Jude Medical.,DIAGNOSIS:, Severe non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with anterior ventricular tachycardia.,FINDINGS:, Patient is in DDD mode, base rate of 60, maximum tracking rate of 110 beats per minute, atrial lead is set at 2.5 volts with pulse width of 0.5 ms, ventricular bypass is set to 2.5. volts with a pulse width of 0.5 ms.  Pacemaker interrogation shows atrial capture is 0.75 volts at 0.5 ms, ventricular capture is 0.5 volts at 0.5 ms, in-atrium sensing is 5.34 to 5.8 millivolts, R sensing is 12 to 12.0 millivolts, atrial lead impedance 590 ohms, the impedance of the ventricular lead 750 ohms.  The defibrillator part is set to VT1 at 139 beats per minute, with SVT discrimination on treatment only monitored.  VT2 detection criteria is 169 beats per minute with SVT discrimination when treating ATP times 3 followed by 25 joules, followed by 36 joules, followed by 36 joules times 2. VF detection criteria set at 187 beats per minute with 25 joules treatment, followed by 36 joules times 5. The patient has normal sinus rhythm.

transcription: MEDICATIONS: Plavix, atenolol, Lipitor and folic acid.,CLINICAL HISTORY:,This is a 41 year old male patient who presents with chest pain, had a previous MI in 07/2003 and stenting in 2003, who presents for a stress myocardial perfusion scan.,With the patient at rest, 10.3 mCi of Cardiolitis. technetium-99 m sestamibi was injected and myocardial perfusion imaging was obtained.,PROCEDURE AND INTERPRETATION:,The patient exercised for a total of 12 minutes according to the standard Bruce protocol.  The maximum workload was 12.8 METS.  The resting heart rate was 57 beats per minute and the maximum heart rate was 123 beats per minute, or 69% of the age-predicted maximum heart rate response.  The blood pressure response was normal with a resting blood pressure of 130/100 and a maximum blood pressure of 158/90.  The test was stopped due to fatigue and leg pain.  Resting ECG showed normal sinus rhythm.  Peak stress ECG revealed no ischemic ST-T wave abnormalities.  Ventricular bigeminy was observed during exercise, but no sustained tachycardia was observed.  At most, no chest pain was noted.  The test was stopped due to fatigue and pain left.  At peak stress, the patient was injected with 30.3 mCi of Cardiolite technetium-99 m sestamibi and myocardial perfusion imaging was obtained and compared to resting myocardial perfusion imaging.,MYOCARDIAL PERFUSION IMAGING:,1.  The overall quality of the analysis was good.,2.  There were no diagnostic abnormalities on rest and stress myocardial perfusion imaging.,3.  The left ventricular cavity appeared to be normal size.,4.  Gated SPECT images revealed mild septal hypokinesia and mild apical hypokinesia.  Overall left ventricular systolic function was low and normal with a calculated ejection fraction of 46% at rest.,CONCLUSIONS:,1.  Good exercise tolerance.,2.  Less than adequate cardiac stress.  The patient was on beta-blocker treatment.,3.  No ECG evidence of stress-induced ischemia.,4.  No chest pain due to stress.,5.  Mild ventricular bigeminy with exercise.,6.  No diagnostic abnormalities on rest and stress myocardial perfusion imaging.,7.  Monitored SPECT images revealed septal and apical hypokinesia with overall low and normal left ventricular systolic function with a calculated ejection fraction of 46% at rest.

transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  Left heart catheterization, left ventriculogram, aortogram, coronary angiography.,2.  PCI of LAD and left main coronary artery with Impella assist device.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS:,Unstable angina and congestive heart failure with impaired LV function.,PROCEDURE TECHNIQUE:,After obtaining informed consent, the patient was brought to the cardiac catheterization room in a post-absorptive state and without sedation.  The right groin was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  Lidocaine 2% was used for infiltration anesthesia.  Using the modified Seldinger technique, a 7-French sheath was introduced into the right common femoral artery and a 6-French sheath was introduced into the right common femoral vein.  Through the arterial sheath, an angiogram of the right common femoral artery was obtained.  Subsequently, a 6-French pigtail catheter was advanced to the level of the distal aorta where an angiogram of the distal aorta and the bifurcation of the right and left common iliac arteries was obtained.  Subsequently, a 4-French sheath was introduced into the left common femoral artery using the modified Seldinger technique.  Thereafter, the pigtail catheter was advanced over a 0.035-inch J-wire into the left ventricle and LV grammetry was performed in RAO view and after removal, aortogram was performed in LAO view.  Therefore, 6-French JL4 and JR4 guiding catheters were used to engage the left and right coronary arteries, respectively, and multiple orthogonal views of the coronary arteries were obtained., ANGIOGRAPHIC RESULTS: ,1.  LV gram: LVEDP was 15 mmHg.  LV ejection fraction 10% to 15% with global hypokinesia.  Only the anterior wall contracts.  There was no mitral regurgitation.  There was no gradient across the aortic valve during removal, and on aortography there was no evidence of aortic dissection or aortic regurgitation.,2.  The right coronary artery is a dominant vessel with an average stenosis of 50 to 70% that has not been treated.  The left main coronary artery is a calcified vessel with disease.,2.  The left anterior descending artery had an average stenosis of 80 to 90%.  The first diagonal branch had more than 90% stenosis.,3.  The circumflex coronary artery had a patent stent. INTERVENTION: After reviewing the angiographic images, we elected to proceed with left anterior descending artery intervention.  The 4-French sheath in the left common femoral artery was enlarged into a 12-French Impella sheath through which an Amplatz wire and 6-French multipurpose catheter were advanced into the left ventricle.  The Amplatz wire was swapped out for 0.018 inch Impella stiff wire.  The multipurpose catheter was removed and the Impella was advanced into the left ventricle and a performance level of 8 was achieved with a cardiac output of 2 to 2.5 l/min.  Subsequently, a 7-French JL4 guiding catheter was used to engage the left coronary artery and a 0.014 inch Asahi soft wire was advanced into the left anterior descending artery and a second 0.014 inch Asahi soft wire was advanced into the diagonal branch.  The diagonal branch was predilated with a 2.5 x 30 mm Sprinter balloon to nominal atmospheres, then a 2.5 x 24 Endeavor stent was successfully deployed in the mid-LAD ​​and a 3.0 x 15 mm Endeavor stent was deployed in the proximal LAD.  The stent delivery balloon was used to post-dilate the overlapping segment.  The LAD, diagonal was rewired with 0.014 inch Asahi soft wire and a 3.0 x 20 mm Maverick balloon was advanced into the LAD for post-dilation and a 2.0 x 30 mm Sprinter balloon was advanced diagonally for kiss inflations which were performed at nominal atmospheres.  At this point, the left main section was noted to have retrograde dissection.  A 3.5 x 18 mm Endeavor stent was successfully deployed in the left main coronary artery.  The diagonal Asahi soft wire was removed and placed in the circumflex coronary artery.  Kiss inflation of the LAD and circumflex corona

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Airway obstruction secondary to laryngeal subglottic stenosis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,Airway obstruction secondary to laryngeal subglottic stenosis and tracheal stenosis.,OPERATION PERFORMED:, Suspension microlaryngoscopy, rigid bronchoscopy, stenosis dilation tracheal.,INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY:,The patient is a 56-year-old white female with a history of recurrent polychondritis, which resulted in saddle nose deformity in glottic and subglottic stenosis for which she has undergone a number of upper airway procedures in the past.  She is currently dependent on the trachea for her airway due to glottic and subglottic stenosis, but she has no significant problems breathing and speaking around her tracheal tube and has come in for further evaluation.  Endoscopic reassessment of his tube and nature of the proposed intervention performed.  Risk and complications of bleeding, infection, impaired speech or swallowing, inability to improve airway, and loss of voice.  The results of cardiorespiratory anesthesia have been discussed at length.  The patient states that she understands and wishes to continue.,DESCRIPTION OF OPERATION:The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position.  Under adequate general endotracheal anesthesia, the patient's No. 5 metal tracheostomy tube was removed and a laser-secured No. 5 endotracheal tube was inserted.  The patient was then prepared for an endoscopy.  The Kantor laryngoscope was then inserted.  The oral cavity, hypopharynx, larynx and nasal cavity showed good dentition with good tongue, oral cavity and mucosa without lesions.  The larynx was then a short ***** epiglottis.  The larynx was suspended with significant scarring beginning in the supraglottic area with loss of laryngeal contour beginning in the supraglottis with extensive scar tissue at the level of the false cord, obliteration of the ventricles and true cords.  This appeared stable and the airway was patent at the supraglottic and glottic levels with some narrowing at the subglottic level with mild to moderate subglottic stenosis otherwise it appeared stable.  However, distally, the level of the tracheal site is examined under the microscope and with telescopes at 0 and 30 degrees.  The patient noted a marked narrowing with a dense posterolateral scar on the left ensuring good visualization of the tracheal tube.  The laryngoscope was removed and a 5 x 30 pediatric rigid bronchoscope was then passed.  The LP contact tip laser was used to vaporize the scar tissue and release the scar band, after which the endoscope was passed and further dilation was performed.  The middle and distal tracheas were largely patent.  The trachea and main bronchi were patent without obvious disease.  The patient did not appear to have recurrent polychondritis with progressive scar tissue in the trachea and the posterior wall of the trachea was significant.  This was further dilated, then removed and a new #5 metal tracheostomy tube inserted.  The patient tolerated the procedure well without complications and was transported to the recovery room in satisfactory condition.

The estimated and calculated EF was 56%.,The cardiac perfusion examined showed no indicative reversible defects of myocardial risk and no fixed defects indicative of myocardial risk. scars.,PRINT:,1.  Normal stress/rest cardiac perfusion without indication of ischemia.,2.  Normal LV function and low probability of significant epicardial coronary narrowing.

transcription: CHART NOTE: She's here to discuss a possible open lung biopsy that she actually has scheduled for tomorrow.  Dr. XYZ had seen her due to her complaints of shortness of breath.  Then she had a lung function test and CT scan and he felt that she probably had usual interstitial lung disease, but he wanted her to have an open lung biopsy, so he had her see Dr. XYZ Estep.  He agreed with Dr. XYZ that an open lung biopsy was appropriate and she was in fact scheduled for it, but Dr. XYZ and I were not available before the originally scheduled procedure, so he delayed her so she could talk to us before having the biopsy.  She was ready to move forward and thought it was important that she find out why she is out of breath.  She is very concerned about the results of her CT scan and lung function test.  She seemed alarmed to announce that Dr. XYZ had found that her lung capacity was reduced to 60% of what should be normal.  However, I told him that two years ago, Dr. XYZ had performed lung function studies that showed the same functional change.  And that his pulmonary function tests, at least compared to two years ago, hadn't really changed over that time.  After discussing the serious nature of an open lung biopsy, the fact that her lung function studies haven't changed in two years, the fact that she likely has a number of other factors contributing to her shortness of breath, including deconditioning and obesity, she seemed comfortable with the idea of ​​just monitoring this for a little longer before undergoing something as risky as an open lung biopsy.  In fact, when I called Dr. XYZ to talk to him about canceling the procedure, he stated that he would be very uncomfortable doing an open lung biopsy on someone whose lung function studies had not changed.  I also explained to the patient that I did not think Dr. XYZ knew that she had lung function studies two years prior and that she certainly did not know the results of those.  And I also spoke with Dr. XYZ who agreed that while the two different tests may have some minor differences explaining some similarity in results that may or may not be completely accurate, this is generally a case of someone with progressive interstitial lung disease without a fairly substantial change on the lung function tests, even though they were done at different facilities. go ahead and decided to cancel it.  I also told him that we could continue to monitor his breathing issues as well as his CT, X-ray, and lung function tests.  And if there were signs that it was a progressive problem, she could always do a lung biopsy.  But she had to understand that the treatment and likely diagnosis from an open lung biopsy were not likely to be of much help.  She understands that diagnoses made from an open lung biopsy are not that specific and that treatment for the few specific things that can be detected is often not well tolerated or extremely helpful. We will see her again in a month to see how her breathing is going.  We will cancel her open lung biopsy for tomorrow and decide if she should see a pulmonologist at that time.  I told him I would try to talk to his sister in a day or two.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS,1.  Acute coronary artery syndrome with ST-segment elevation in the anterior wall distribution.,2.  Documented coronary artery disease with prior angioplasty and stenting in left anterior descending artery and circumflex artery, last procedure in 2005.,3.  Primary malignant ventricular arrhythmia and the need for ventricular fibrillation.  He is intubated and ventilated., POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Acute coronary artery syndrome with ST segment elevation in the anterior wall distribution.  Primary ventricular arrhythmia.  Obstructed left anterior descending artery, successfully re-canalized by angioplasty and implantation of the drug-eluting stent.  Previously stented circumflex with mild stenosis and previously documented occlusion of the right coronary artery, well secured., PROCEDURES:, Left heart catheterization, selective bilateral coronary angiography and left ventriculography.  Revascularization of the left anterior descending with angioplasty and implantation of a drug-eluting stent.  Right heart catheterization and placement of a Swan-Ganz catheter for monitoring.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:,The patient arrived from the emergency room intubated and ventilated.  He is hemodynamically stable on heparin and bolus Integrilin and the infusion has been initiated.  The right femoral area was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  2 mL of lidocaine was then filled locally.  The right femoral artery was cannulated with an 18-gauge needle followed by a 6-French vascular sheath.  An XB 3.5 guiding catheter was advanced and manipulated to cannulate the left coronary artery and an angiogram was obtained.  A confirmed occlusion of the left anterior descending artery with minimal collaterals and also an occlusion of the right coronary artery, well collateralized.  An angioplasty wire with the current wire was advanced into the left anterior descending artery and was able to pass through the occlusion area inside the stent.  An angioplasty balloon measuring 2.0 x 15 was advanced and three inflations were achieved.  He successfully recanalized the artery.  There is evidence of residual stenosis in the distal aspect of previous stents.  A Xience 2.75 x 15 drug-eluting stent was advanced and positioned in the stenosis area with its distal marker adjacent to the bifurcation with a diagonal branch and was deployed at 12 and 18 atmospheres.  The intermittent result was improved.  Additional inflation was obtained more proximally.  Her blood pressure fluctuated and dropped into the 70s, correlating with additional sedation.  There is patency of the left anterior descending artery and good antegrade flow.  The guiding catheter was replaced with a straight Judkins 5-French catheter manipulated to cannulate the right coronary artery and selective angiography was obtained.  The catheter was then advanced into the left ventricle and a pressure measurement was obtained, including withdrawal across the aortic valve.  The right femoral vein was cannulated with an 18-gauge needle followed by an 8-French vascular sheath.  A Swan-Ganz 8-French catheter was then advanced under fluoroscopic and hemodynamic control and pressure stenting was obtained from the right ventricle, pulmonary artery and pulmonary capillary wedge position.  The cardiac catheter was determined by thermal dilution.  The procedure was then completed, well tolerated and without complications.  The vascular sheath was secure and the patient returned to the coronary care unit for further monitoring.  The duration of fluoroscopy was 8.2 minutes.  The total amount of contrast was 113 ml. HEMODYNAMIC: The patient remained in sinus rhythm with intermittent ventricular bigeminy after revascularization.  His initial blood pressure was 96/70 with a mean of 83 and left ventricular pressure was 17 mmHg.  There was no gradient across the aortic valve.  Closing pressure was 97/68 with an average of 82. Right heart catheterization with right atrial pressure at 13, right ventricle 31/9, lung

transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  Right heart catheterization.,2.  Left heart catheterization.,3.  Left ventriculogram.,4.  Aortogram.,5.  Bilateral selective coronary angiography., ANESTHESIA:, 1% lidocaine and IV sedation including Versed 1 mg., INDICATION:, The patient is a 48 year old female with echocardiographically diagnosed severe mitral stenosis, moderate aortic regurgitation, and moderate to severe pulmonary hypertension who is being evaluated as part of a preoperative workup for valve repair or replacement mitral and possible aortic.  She suffered from atrial fibrillation and previous episodes of congestive heart failure.  She suffers from exertional dyspnea and sometimes orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.,PROCEDURE: After the risks, benefits and alternatives of the above-mentioned procedure were explained in detail to the patient, informed consent was obtained, both verbally and in writing.  The patient was taken to the cardiac catheterization laboratory where the procedure was performed.  The right inguinal area was carefully cleaned with Betadine solution and the patient was draped in the usual manner.  A 1% lidocaine solution was used to anesthetize the right inguinal area.  Once adequate anesthesia was achieved, an argon-walled needle was used to cannulate the right femoral vein.  A guidewire was advanced into the lumen of the vein without resistance.  The needle was removed and the guidewire was attached to the sterile field.  The needle was rinsed and then used to cannulate the right femoral artery.  A guidewire was advanced through the needle lumen without resistance.  A small cut was made in the skin and the needle was removed.  This pressure was maintained.  A No. 6 French arterial sheath was advanced over the guidewire without resistance.  The dilator and guidewire were removed.  A FiO2 sample was obtained and the sheath was rinsed.  A #8 French sheath was advanced over the guidewire into the femoral vein, after which the dilator and guidewire were removed and the sheath was flushed.  A Swan-Ganz catheter was advanced through the venous sheath to a pulmonary capillary and the balloon was temporarily deflated.  An angulated pigtail catheter was advanced into the left ventricle under direct fluoroscopic visualization using a guidewire.  The guide wire has been removed.  The catheter was connected to a manifold and flushed.  Left ventricular pressures were measured continuously and the balloon was reinflated and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure was remeasured.  The joint use of two transducers and the mitral valve radius were estimated.  The balloon was deflated and a mixed venous sample was obtained.  Hemodynamics were measured.  The catheter was returned to the right ventricle from the pulmonary artery and the right atrium.  The right atrium sample was obtained and found to be negative for shunting.  The Swan-Ganz catheter was then removed and a left ventriculogram was performed in the RAO projection with a single power injection of non-ionic contrast material.  A pullback was then performed, revealing a minimal LV-AO gradient.  The patient showed aortic insufficiency on her echocardiogram, an aortogram was performed in LAO projection with a single powerful injection of non-ionic contrast material.  The pigtail catheter was then removed and a left #4 Judkins catheter was advanced to the level of the ascending aorta under direct fluoroscopic visualization using a guidewire.  The guide wire has been removed.  The catheter was connected to the manifold and flushed.  The ostium of the left main coronary artery was carefully engaged.  Using multiple manual injections of nonionic contrast material, the left coronary system was evaluated in different views.  This catheter was then removed and a #4 straight Judkins catheter was advanced to the level of the ascending aorta under direct fluoroscopic visualization using a guidewire.
