transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,Right heart catheterization.,INDICATION:,CHF refractory to maximal medical treatment.,PROCEDURE:,After the risks, benefits and alternatives of the above-mentioned procedure were explained in detail to the patient and his family, informed consent was obtained verbally and in writing.  The patient was taken to the cardiac catheterization department where the right internal jugular region was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A 1% lidocaine solution was used to infiltrate the skin overlying the right internal jugular vein.  Once adequate anesthesia was achieved, a thin-walled 18-gauge argon needle was used to cannulate the right internal jugular vein.  A steel guidewire was then inserted through the needle into the vessel without resistance.  A small cut was then made in the skin and the needle was removed.  A No. 8.5 French venous sheath was then advanced over the guidewire into the vascular lumen without resistance.  The guidewire and dilator were then removed.  The sheath was then rinsed.  A Swan-Ganz catheter was inserted 20 cm and the balloon was inflated.  Under fluoroscopic guidance, the catheter was advanced into the right atrium through the right ventricle and into the pulmonary artery wedge position.  Hemodynamics were measured en route.  Pulmonary artery saturation was achieved.  The Swan was then held in place for the patient to be transferred to the intensive care unit for further medical titration.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.  The patient returned to the cardiac catheterization waiting area in a stable and satisfactory condition.,FINDINGS: Body surface area is equal to 2.04, hemoglobin is equal to 9.3, O2 is in a 2 liter nasal cannula.  Pulmonary artery saturation is 37.8.  Pulse oximetry on a 2 liter nasal cannula is equal to 93%.  Right atrial pressure is 8, right ventricular pressure is 59/9, pulmonary arterial pressure is 61/31 with a mean of 43, pulmonary arterial pressure is 21, cardiac output is 3.3 by Fick's method, cardiac index is 1.6 by Fick's method, systemic vascular resistance is 1821, and transpulmonic gradient is equal to 22.,IMPRESSION: The results of the exam and Swan are consistent with low perfusion given that the mixed venous O2 is only 38% under current medical therapy along with high right-sided filling pressures and high systemic vascular resistance.,PLAN:, Given that the patient is unable to tolerate vasodilator therapy secondary to significant orthostasis and the fact that the patient will not respond to oral titration at this point due to lack of cardiac reserve, the patient will have to go home on Primacor.  The patient is unable to continue treatment with dobutamine due to nonsustained ventricular tachycardia.  At that time, we will transfer the patient to the intensive care unit for titration of Primacor therapy.  We will also increase his Lasix to 80 mg IV q.d.  We will increase his amiodarone to 400 mg daily.  We will also continue his Coumadin therapy.  As previously stated, we will stop the vasodilator treatment starting with Isordil.

Selective coronary angiography.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was informed of all risks, benefits,and alternatives to the procedure.  The patient agreed to continue and informed consent was signed. Both groins were prepped and draped in the usual sterile manner.  After local anesthesia with 2% lidocaine, a 6-French sheath was inserted into the right femoral artery and a 7-French sheath was inserted into the right femoral vein.  Then, a right heart catheter was performed using a 7-French Swan-Ganz catheter.  The catheter was placed in the pulmonary capillary wedge position.  Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure, PA pressure were obtained, cardiac output was obtained, then RV, RA pressures were obtained.  The right heart catheter _______ has come out.  Then, selective coronary angiography was performed using a 6-French JL4 and 6-French 3DRC catheter.  Next, an attempt was made to cross the aortic valve with a 6-French pigtail catheter, but without success.  After the procedure, the catheters were removed, the sheath was removed, and hemostasis was achieved by manual pressure.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.  There were no complications.,HEMODYNAMICS:,1.  Cardiac output was 4.9 per liter per minute.  Wedge pulmonary capillary wedge pressure was 7, PA pressure was 20/14, RV pressure was 26/5, mean RA pressure was 5.2.  Coronary angiography, the left main part is a dense _______ calcified complex.,3.  LAD has 70% proximal calcified stenosis and patent stent in the middle of LAD and diagonal 1 is a moderate sized vessel, has 70% stenosis.  The left circumflex presents diffuse luminal irregularities.  OM1 has 70% stenosis and is a medium sized vessel.  The right coronary artery is dominant and has minimal luminal irregularities.,SUMMARY:,Three-vessel coronary artery disease with aortic stenosis by echo with normal pulmonary artery systolic pressure.,RECOMMENDATION:,Aortic valve replacement by coronary artery bypass grafting.

The patient was sedated with sevoflurane and propofol. One ml of 1% lidocaine was used for airway anesthesia. The 2.8 mm flexible pediatric bronchoscope was passed through the left nostril. The upper respiratory tract was visualized. The epiglottis, arytenoids, and vocal cords were all normal. The bezel had passed under the cords. The subglottic space was normal. The patient had normal tracheal rings and a normal membranous portion of the trachea. There is a slight deviation of the trachea to the right. At the hull level, the right and left main stems were evaluated. The right upper lobe, right middle lobe, and right lower lobe were all anatomically normal. The endoscope was stuck in the right middle lobe, 10 ml of saline was infused, 10 ml was returned. This was sent for cell counts, cytology, lipid index and quantitative bacterial cultures. The left side was then evaluated and normal heartbeats were noted on the left. Some dynamic collapse of the left trunk during the respiratory cycle was also noted. The left upper lobe and left lower lobe were normal. The scope has been removed. The patient tolerated the procedure well., ENDOSCOPIC DIAGNOSIS: Bronchomalacia of the left main trunk.

Bilateral retrograde coronary angiography.,4.  Abdominal aortogram with pelvic drainage.,5.  Left external iliac angiogram with runoff to the patient's left foot.,6.  Left external iliac angiogram with runoff to the patient's right leg.,7.  Angiogram of the right common femoral artery flowing to the patient's right leg.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,The patient was taken to the cardiac catheterization laboratory after obtaining valid consent.  It was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner. After local infiltration with 2% Xylocaine, the right common femoral artery was introduced percutaneously and a 4-French sheath was placed over the artery.  The arterial sheath was flushed throughout the procedure.,Conscious sedation was achieved using Versed IV and IV fentanyl.,Using a Wholey wire, a 4-French, 4-curve Judkins right coronary artery catheter was advanced into the ascending aorta.  The wire was removed and the catheter was flushed.  The catheter was engaged in the left main canal.  The injections were performed on the left under different views.  The catheter was then replaced with an RCA catheter, 4 French curves, which was advanced into the ascending aorta using a J-wire. The wire was removed and the catheter was flushed.  The catheter was engaged in the RCA.  The injections were performed at the RCA under different views. The catheter was then replaced with a 5 French Omniflush catheter, which was advanced into the abdominal aorta using a regular J-wire.  The thread has been removed.  The catheter has been flushed. A right common iliac angiogram with runoff to the patient's right leg was then performed.  The catheter was then removed using a J-wire. The J-wire was left in the abdominal aorta.  The manual injection was performed in the right common femoral artery in 2 locations with flow to the patient's right leg. The wire was then removed.  The arterial sheath was then removed after rinsing.  Hemostasis was achieved by hand compression. The patient tolerated the procedure well and had no complications. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Hemoptysis.,2.  History of lung cancer.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Right middle lobe occlusion tumor with friability.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, Fiberoptic bronchoscopy, diagnosis.,LOCATION: , Endoscopy suite #4.,ANAESTHESIA:, General by anesthesia department.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, Minimal.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS:,The patient presented to ABCD Hospital with a known history of lung cancer and acute hemoptysis with associated chest pain.  Due to her history, it was felt that she would benefit from diagnostic fiberoptic bronchoscopy to help determine the etiology of the hemoptysis.  She was brought to endoscopy room #4 and informed consent was obtained.  ,PROCEDURE DETAILS: ,The patient was placed in the supine position and intubated by the anesthesia department.  Intravenous sedation was administered according to anesthesia.  The fiberscope was passed through the No. 8 endotracheal tube to the main trachea.  The right main bronchus was examined.  The right upper lobe and its subsegments appeared overall within normal limits with no endobronchial lesions noted.  During examination of the right middle lobe, a tumor was noted obstructing the lateral segment of the right middle lobe and a clot noted on the medial segment of the right middle lobe. The clot was washed with normal saline and a tumor was noted behind this clot.  The tumor completely obstructed both segments of the right middle lobe.  The study then moved to the subsegments of the right lower lobe, which were examined individually and found to be completely free of endobronchial lesions.  The scope was brought back to the level of the middle trachea and passed into the left main bronchus.  The left upper lobe and its subsegments were examined and found to be completely free of endobronchial lesions.  The left lower lingula and subsegments were all examined individually and noted to be virtually free of endobronchial lesions.  Secretions were noted throughout the left lung.  The endoscope was retracted and passed back to the right main bronchus.  The right middle lobe area was re-examined.  The tumor was found to be extremely friable with oozing from the tumor with minimal manipulation.  It did not appear that an endoscope or cannula could be passed downstream of the tumor.  Due to continued oozing, 1 cc of epinephrine was applied topically with adequate hemostasis achieved.  The area was examined for approximately one minute to ensure adequate hemostasis.  The endoscope was then retracted and the patient was sent to the recovery room in stable condition.  She will be extubated according to the anesthesia department.  Cytology and cultures were not sent due to the patient's known diagnosis.  Other recommendations are pending at this time.

transcription: OPERATION,1.  Ivor-Lewis esophagogastrectomy.,2.  Feeding the jejunostomy.,3.  Placement of two chest tubes #28-French on the right side.,4.  Right thoracotomy.,ANESTHESIA: ,General endotracheal anesthesia with a double-lumen tube.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: ,After obtaining informed consent from the patient, including a thorough explanation of the risks and benefits of the above-mentioned procedure, the patient was taken to the operating room and general endotracheal anesthesia was administered.  Before general anesthesia was administered, the patient underwent epidural anesthesia.  In addition, he was placed with a double-lumen endotracheal tube.  The patient was placed in a supine position to begin the procedure.  His abdomen and chest were prepared and draped using the standard surgical method.  After applying sterile dressings, a #10 blade scalpel was used to make an upper midline incision from the level of the xiphoid to just below the umbilicus.  Dissection was performed along the line using a Bovie electrocautery.  The abdomen was open.  Next, a Balfour retractor was positioned as well as a mechanical retractor.  Next, our attention turned to releasing the stomach.  To attempt to do this, we identified the right gastroepiploic artery and arcade.  We incised the omentum and removed it from the stomach and gastroepiploic arch.  The omentum was divided using suture ligation with 2-0 silk.  We did this along the greater curvature and then moved to the lesser curvature where the short gastric arteries were removed by ligation using 2-0 silk.  Next, we turned our attention to performing a Kocher maneuver.  This was done and the stomach was released.  We dismantled the falciform ligament as well as the caudate attachment to the diaphragm.  We widened the diaphragmatic hiatus so that we could place approximately 3 fingers in the chest.  We also performed part of the dissection of the esophagus from the abdomen to the chest.  The esophagus and esophageal hiatus were identified in the abdomen.  We then turned our attention to the left gastric artery.  The left gastric artery was identified at the base of the stomach.  We first harvested the left gastric vein by ligating and dividing it using 0 silk ties. The left gastric artery was then harvested using suture ligation with silk ties, followed by 2-0 stick tie reinforcement.  At this point, the stomach was freely mobile.  We then turned our attention to creating our jejunostomy feeding tube.  A 2-0 Vicryl bursa was placed in the jejunum approximately 20 cm distal to the ligament of Treitz.  We then used the Bovie electrocautery to open the jejunum at this site.  We placed a 16 French red rubber catheter through this site.  We tied ourselves in place.  We then used 3-0 silk sutures to create a Witzel.  Next, the jejunum loop was secured to the abdominal wall using 2-0 silk ties.  After doing this and removing the feeding jejunostomy through the skin and securing it appropriately, we turned our attention to closing the abdomen.  This was done with Prolene No. 1.  We put a 2nd layer of Vicryl 2-0.  The skin was closed with Monocryl 4-0. Next, we turned our attention to performing the thoracic portion of the procedure.  The patient was placed in left lateral decubitus.  The right chest has been prepared and draped appropriately.  We then used a scalpel with a No. 10 blade to make a posterolateral incision, without sparing the muscles.  The dissection was carried out up to the level of the ribs with the Bovie electrocautery.  Then the ribs were counted and the 5th space in between was entered.  The lung was deflated.  We placed standard chest retractors.  Next, we incised the peritoneum above the esophagus.  We dissected the esophagus just above the azygos vein.  The azygos vein, in fact, was harvested with 0 silk ligatures and reinforced with 2-0 stick ties.  As mentioned, we dissected the esophagus proximally and distally to the level of the hiatus.  After doing this, we

transcription: PROCEDURE: , Endotracheal intubation.,INDICATION: ,Respiratory failure.,BRIEF HISTORY: ,The patient is a 52-year-old man with metastatic osteogenic sarcoma.  He was admitted two days ago with a small bowel obstruction.  He has been on Coumadin for a previous PE and currently on a heparin drip.  He became altered and then deteriorated quite quickly to the point where he was no longer breathing on his own and had minimal responsiveness.  A code blue was called.  When I arrived, the patient's vital signs were stable.  His systolic blood pressure is 140 s and his heart rate is 80 s.  However, he has no respiratory effort and does not even respond to painful stimuli.  The patient received 20 mg of etomidate.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION: ,The patient placed the appropriate equipment at the bedside and administered 20 mg of etomidate and 100 mg of succinylcholine.  A Mac-4 blade was used.  A 7.5 ET tube placed on the 24th tooth.  There is a good color change on the capnograph with bilateral breath sounds.  After intubation, the patient's blood pressure began to drop.  He received 2 L bolus.  I put him on a 10mcg dopamine drip.  Dr. X was at the bedside, who is the primary caregiver, he assumed the care of the patient, who will be transferred to the ICU.  The chest x-ray will be examined and the lungs will be consulted.

transcription: PROCEDURE: 1. Implantation,dual chamber ICD.,2.  Fluoroscopy.,3.  Defibrillation threshold test.,4.  Venography., PROCEDURE NOTE: After obtaining informed consent, the patient was taken to the operating room.  The patient was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  Using the modified Seldinger technique, the left subclavian vein was attempted to be punctured, but without success.  Approximately 10 cc of intravenous contrast material was injected into the peripheral vein of the left upper limb.  A venogram was then performed.  Under fluoroscopy via the modified Seldinger technique, the left subclavian vein was pierced and a guidewire was passed through the vein into the superior vena cava, then into the right atrium, and finally into the inferior vena cava.  A second guidewire was placed in the same manner.  An incision of approximately 5 cm was made in the upper left anterior chest.  Skin and subcutaneous tissue were dissected from the prepectoral fascia.  Both guidewires were brought into the pocket area.  A sheath was placed over the lateral guidewire and guided fluoroscopically to the vena cava.  The dilator and guidewire were removed.  A ventricular fixation lead, under fluoroscopic guidance, was placed through the sheath into the superior vena cava, the right atrium and then the right ventricle.  Using straight and curved stylets, it was positioned and screwed into the right ventricular apex.  Once the stimulation and sensing parameters were established in the lead, the lead collar was sutured to the pectoral muscle with Ethibond suture.  A guiding sheath was placed over the guidewire and placed fluoroscopically into the superior vena cava.  The dilator and guidewire were removed.  An active fixation atrial lead was passed fluoroscopically through the sheath, into the superior vena cava, and then into the right atrium.  Using straight and J-shaped stylets, it was placed in the appropriate position and screwed into the right atrial appendage area.  After significant stimulation parameters were established in the lead, the lead collar was sutured to the pectoral muscles with Ethibond suture.  The tract was flushed with saline.  A Medtronic pulse generator was attached to both leads and secured to the pectoral muscle with Ethibond suture.  The deep and superficial layers were closed with 3-0 Vicryl in a routine manner.  Steri strips were placed over the incision.  Tegaderm was placed on the Steri-strips.  A pressure dressing was applied to the pocket area.

transcription: DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Respiratory failure improved.,2.  Hypotension resolved.,3.  Anemia of stable chronic diseases.,4.  Anasarca improves.,5.  Protein malnutrition improves.,6.  End stage liver disease. HISTORY AND HOSPITAL COURSE: The patient was admitted after undergoing a prolonged process with small bowel obstruction.  His bowel function began to improve.  He was on TPN before arriving at the hospital.  He remained on TPN throughout his stay here, but his appetite and p.o. intake improved.  The patient had an episode during his stay in which his blood pressure reached an all-time low, causing him to spend several days in the intensive care unit on dopamine.  At one point we were unsuccessful in weaning him off dopamine, but after about 11 days he finally began to tolerate the withdrawal parameters, was successfully cleared of dopamine, and maintained his blood pressure without difficulty.  The patient also required BiPAP to help him oxygenate and it appeared he had developed left-sided pneumonia.  This has been treated successfully with Zyvox, Levaquin and Diflucan.  He seems to be doing much better now.  He only uses BiPAP in the evening.  As noted above, he is eating better.  He showed signs of exquisite redness and swelling around his genital area and lower abdomen.  It may mainly be anasarca-dependent edema.  The patient has received aggressive diurination over the past 4-5 days, which appears to have resulted in some improvement in his swelling.  This morning, the patient denies any acute distress.  He states that he feels well and understands that he is being discharged to another facility for continued care and rehabilitation.  He will be discharged to the Garden Court Skilled Nursing Facility.,MEDICATIONS/DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:, He will take Protonix 40 mg daily, Metoclopramide 10 mg every 6 hours, Zyvox 600 mg daily for 5 days, Diflucan 150 mg p.o. daily for 3 days, Bumex 2 mg p.o. daily, Megace 400 mg p.o. b.i.d., make sure 1 can t.i.d. with meals, and MiraLax 17 g p.o. every day.  The patient will need physical therapy to help with strength training.  He will also need respiratory care to work with his BiPAP.  Its initial settings are rate of 20, pressure support of 12, PEEP of 6, FIO2 of 40%.  The patient will need a sleep study, which the nursing home can arrange.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: On the day of discharge, heart rate 99, respiratory rate 20, blood pressure 102/59, temperature 98.2, O2 sat 97%.,GENERAL: A well-developed white male who does not appear in apparent distress.,HEENT: Nothing abnormal., CARDIOVASCULAR: Positive. S1, S2 without murmur, rub or gallop.,LUNGS: clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheezing or crackles.,ABDOMEN: positive for bowel sounds.  Soft, not distended.  He has widespread redness around the abdominal area and groin.  This seems improved from last week's presentation.  Swelling in this area also appears improved.,EXTREMITIES: Show no clubbing or cyanosis.  He presents with lower extremity edema, more than 2 distal pedal pulses are present.,NEUROLOGICAL: The patient is alert and oriented to person and place.  He is alert and aware of his surroundings.  We haven't had any confusion difficulties here lately.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Patient moves all extremities without difficulty.  It's just low in general.,LABORATORY DATA: Lab work done today shows the following: white count 4.2, hemoglobin 10.2, hematocrit 30.6, and platelet count 184,000. Electrolytes show sodium 139, potassium 4.1, chloride 98, CO2 26, glucose 79, BUN 56 and creatinine 1.4.  Calcium 8.8, Phosphorus a little high at 5.5, Magnesium 2.2, Albumin 3.9.,PLAN: ,Remove this gentleman from the hospital and admit him to Garden Court SNF where they can continue his rehabilitation and conditioning.  Hopefully long term planning will make it possible to get home.  It has a history

The patient was started on IV steroids, bronchodilators as well as antibiotics. He also complained of chest pain which appeared to be more pleuritic with a history of coronary artery disease and orthopnea.  He was evaluated by Dr. X in cardiology, who performed a stress test.  The stress test came back positive for reversible ischemia, but cardiology decided to follow the patient to the clinic.  The patient's last cardiac catheterization was in 2008. The patient has improved significantly clinically and wishes to return home.  His hemoglobin level on admission was 8.8 and remained stable.  He is afebrile, hemodynamically stable.,ALLERGIES: LISINOPRIL AND PENICILLIN.,DRUGS AT DISCHARGE:,1. Follow up with the primary care physician's office tomorrow.,SPECIAL INSTRUCTIONS:,1.  If you experience increasing shortness of breath, chest pain, fever, or acute symptoms, return to the emergency department.,2.  Discussion of discharge plan and instructions with patient at bedside.  He understands and accepts.  We also discussed discharge plan instructions with the patient's nurse.

transcription: COMPARISON STUDIES: None.,DRUG:, Lopressor 5 mg IV.,POST-DRUG HEART RATE: 64 bpm,EXAMINATION:,TECHNIQUE: Tomographic images were obtained of the heart and chest with a 64-detector row scanner using slice thicknesses less than 1 mm. 80 cc of Isovue 370 was injected into the right arm.,TECHNICAL QUALITY:,The examination is limited secondarily to extensive artifact from the defibrillator wires.,There is good demonstration of the coronary arteries and good bolus timing.,FINDINGS:,LEFT MAIN CORONARY ARTERY:,The left main coronary artery is a medium sized vessel with a normal ostium. There is no calcified or non-calcified plaque. The vessel divides into a left anterior descending artery and a left circumflex artery.,LEFT ANTERIOR DESCENDING ARTERY:,The left anterior descending artery is a moderately sized vessel, with a small first diagonal branch and a large second diagonal branch. The vessel continues as a small vessel, narrowing at the apex of the left ventricle. There is calcific plaque in the midvessel, with dense calcific plaque at the bifurcation of the second diagonal branch. This limits assessment of the vessel lumen and, although a flow-limiting lesion cannot be excluded, there is no evidence of high-grade stenosis. There is also ostial calcification within the second diagonal branch. The LAD distal to the second diagonal branch is small compared to the most proximal vessel, which is concerning for a proximal flow-limiting lesion. In addition, there is a marked tapering of the distal D2 branch in relation to the proximal and ostial calcific plaque. This is worrying either in cases of occlusion or high-grade stenosis. There is only minimal contrast identified in the distal vessel.,LEFT CIRCUMFLEX ARTERY:,The left circumflex artery is a medium-sized vessel with a patent ostium. There is calcified plaque in the proximal vessel. There is a dense calcific plaque at the bifurcation of OM1 and at the branch of the AV sulcus. The branch of the AV groove narrows like a small vessel at the base of the heart. Dense calcified plaque within the OM1 bifurcation and AV sulcus branch limits assessment of the vessel lumen. There is no high-grade stenosis demonstrated, but a flow-limiting lesion cannot be excluded here.,RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY:,The right coronary artery is a medium-sized vessel with a patent ostium. There is a mixed calcified and noncalcified proximal plaque, but there is no flow-limiting lesion. The vessel continues as a moderate-sized vessel to the core of the heart, supplying a small posterior descending artery and moderate to large posterolateral ventricular branches. There is scattered calcific plaque in the medial vessel and there is also calcific plaque in the distal vessel at the origin of the posterior descending artery. No flow-limited lesions have been demonstrated.,The right coronary artery is dominant.,NON-CORONARY CARDIAC STRUCTURE:,CARDIAC CHAMBERS:,There is diffuse myocardial thinning in the left ventricle, particularly at the apex where there is subendocardial calcification, consistent with chronic infarction. There is ventricular hypertrophy. There are no aneurysms or pseudoaneurysms demonstrated.,HEART VALVES:,There is calcification in the cusp of the left aortic valve. The aortic valve has three leaflets. Mitral valve normal.,PERICARDIUM:, Normal.,LARGE VESSELS:,There are atherosclerotic changes in the aorta.,Lung parenchyma, mediastinum and chest wall visualized:,Normal.,PRINT:,Limited examination secondary to extensive artifact from the pacemaker wires.,There is extensive calcific plaque in the left anterior descending artery as well as the second proximal diagonal. branch. There is marked tapering of the D1 branch distal to the D1 bifurcation, which is concerning for a flow-limiting lesion, but there is no evidence of occlusion.

Symptoms include shortness of breath, fatigue and exhaustion.  The main complaints are right-sided and abdominal pain. Given his history, multiple risk factors, and recent workup, as discussed below, the patient was admitted for further evaluation. No history of recreational drug use.,MEDICAL HISTORY: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking history, coronary artery disease, cardiomyopathy, COPD and presentation as above. No cyanosis.,CNS: Grossly intact.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Arthritic changes.,PSYCHOLOGICAL: Normal effect.,LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSIS DATA:,ECG shows sinus bradycardia, intraventricular conduction defect. transcription: REASON FOR CATHETERIZATION:,ST segment elevation myocardial infarction.,PROCEDURES UNDERTAKEN,1. Cineangiography of the left coronary system.,2.  Cineangiography of the right coronary system.,3.  Left ventriculogram.,4.  PCI to the left circumflex with a 3.5x12 and 3.5x8mm bare metal Vision stent post-dilated with a 3.75mmx2 non-compliant balloon.,PROCEDURE: After all risks and benefits were explained to the patient, informed consent was obtained.  The patient was taken to the cardiac catheterization department.  The right groin was prepared in the usual sterile manner.  The right common femoral artery was cannulated with the modified Seldinger technique.  A 6-French sheath has been introduced.  Then, the right Judkins catheter was used to engage the right coronary artery and cineangiography was recorded in multiple views.  Next, an EBU 3.5 guidewire was used to engage the left coronary system.  Cineangiography was recorded on multiple views and 99% left proximal circumflex stenosis was noted.  The Angiomax bolus and drip were started after checking an ACT, which was 180, and a universal wire was advanced through the left circumflex beyond the lesion.  Then, a 3.0 x 12 mm balloon was used to pre-dilate the lesion.  Next, a 3.5 x 12 mm bare metal Vision stent was advanced to the stenosis area and deployed at 12 atmospheres.  A displacement of the proximal plate at the edge of the stent was noted.  Therefore, a 3.5 x 8 mm bare metal Vision stent was advanced to cover the proximal margin of the first stent and deployed at 12 atmospheres.  Then, a non-compliant 3.75 x 13 mm balloon was advanced into the stent margin and two inflations to 20 atmospheres were performed for 20 seconds.  The final images showed excellent results with an initial stenosis of 99% reduced to 0%.  The patient continues to have residual stenosis in the middle and distal OM branch.  At this point the wire has been removed.  Final images confirmed the initial stent results, with no evidence of dissection, perforation or complications. Then, an angled pigtail catheter was advanced into the left ventricular cavity.  LV pressure was measured.  The LV gram was performed in the LAO and RAO projections and a withdrawal gradient across the aortic valve was performed and recorded.  Eventually all guides were removed.  The right femoral artery access site was imaged and Angio-Seal deployed to achieve excellent hemostasis.  The patient tolerated the procedure very well without complications.,DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS,1.  Left Main: The left main is a large caliber vessel bifurcating into LAD and left circumflex without significant disease.,2.  The LAD: LAD is a large caliber vessel, wraps around the apex, gives off multiple septal perforators, three small to medium caliber diagonal branches without any significant disease.,3.  Left circumflex: The left circumflex is a large caliber vessel, gives off a large distal branch of PDA, has 99% proximal lesion, 50% middle vessel lesion, and 50% lesion in OM, which is a distal branch.,4.  Right Coronary Artery: The right coronary artery is a moderate caliber, dominant vessel, bifurcated into PDA and PLV branches, and has only mild disease.  Otherwise, no significant stenosis was seen.,5.  LV: LVEF 50%.  Hypokinesia of the inferolateral wall.  No significant mitral regurgitation.  No gradient across the aortic valve upon removal.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction with 99% stenosis of the proximal left circumflex treated with a 3.5 x 12 mm Vision bare metal stent and a 3.5 x 8 mm Vision bare metal stent.  Excellent results, 0% residual stenosis.  The patient continues to have 50% residual stenosis in the left circumflex system and mild disease in other vessels.  Therefore, we will medically treat this patient aggressively with close outpatient monitoring.

Percutaneous intervention of the left circumflex branch and obtuse marginal branch with planoballoon angioplasty unable to pass the stent.,NARRATIVE: After all risks and benefits were explained to the patient, informed consent was obtained.  The patient was taken to the cardiac catheterization department.  The right groin was prepared in the usual sterile manner.  The right common femoral artery was cannulated using a modified Seldinger technique and a long 6-French AO sheath was introduced secondary to the tortuous aorta.  Next, the left Judkins catheter was used to engage the left coronary system.  Cineangiography was recorded in multiple views.  Next, the right Judkins catheter was used to engage the right coronary system.  Cineangiography was recorded in multiple views.  Next, the right Judkins catheter was used to engage the SVG into the OM.  A cineangiography was recorded.  Next, the Judkins line was advanced into the left subclavian and exchanged over a long exchange length J-wire for a 4-French left internal mammary artery which was used to engage the LIMA graft into the LAD and cineangiography was recorded in multiple views.  Then, an angled pigtail catheter was advanced into the left ventricular cavity.  LV pressures were measured.  An LV gram was performed and a shrinkage gradient across the aortic valve was performed and recorded.  Then, an aortography was performed and recorded.  It was then that I decided to carry out a percutaneous intervention on the left circumflex.  Therefore, the AVA 3.5 guide was used to engage the left coronary artery.  The Angiomax bolus and drip were started.  A universal wire was advanced beyond the lesion and a 2.5 balloon was advanced first to the proximal lesions and predilations were performed to 14 atmospheres, then to the distal lesion and predilation was performed to 12 atmospheres.  Next, we attempted to advance a 3.0 x 12 stent to the distal lesion; however, we were unable to pass the stent.  Then, second expansions were repeated with the balloon 2.5 to 18 atmospheres; however, we are unable to break the lesion.  We then attempted a cut-out balloon.  Again, we were unable to cross the lesion, so a buddy wire technique was used with a PT choice support wire.  Once again, we were unable to cross the lesion with the stent.  We then tried to cross with a non-compliant balloon, without success.  We also try to cut the ball again, without success.  Despite multiple dilations, we were unable to pass anything beyond the non-compliant balloon through the lesion; therefore, the proceedings were ultimately terminated.  Final images showed no signs of dissection, perforation, or additional complications.  The right groin was filled after taking an image to confirm placement of the sheath over the bifurcation with excellent results.  The patient tolerated the procedure very well without complications, was removed from the operating table and transferred to the cardiac telemetry floor.,DIAGNOSIS RESULTS,1.  The LV.  LVEDP was 4. LVES is approximately 50% to 55% with inferobasal hypokinesia.  No significant MR. No gradient across the aortic valve.,2.  Aortogram.  The ascending aorta shows no significant dilation or signs of dissection.  The valve shows no significant aortic insufficiency.  The abdominal aorta and the distal aorta show significant tortuosity.,3.  The main left.  The left main coronary artery is a large caliber vessel, bifurcating the LAD and left circumflex with mild distal disease of approximately 10-20%.,4.  Left circumflex.  The left circumflex vessel is a large vessel which gives off an obtuse marginal branch with distal branching.  The upper pole of the OM shows retrograde filling of the distal graft and also at this stage approximately 70-80% stenosis.  The left middle circumflex is an 80% high-grade diffuse tortuous stenosis.,5.

transcription: PROCEDURE: Delayed primary thoracic closure.,INDICATIONS:,The patient is a neonate with a diagnosis of hypoplastic left heart syndrome who, 48 hours before the current procedure, underwent a modified stage 1 Norwood operation.  Given the scale of the procedure and the size of the patient (2.5 kg), we chose to leave the chest open to facilitate postoperative care.  He is now returned to the operating room for delayed primary chest closure.,PREOP DX:, Open chest status after a modified Norwood stage 1 operation.,POSTOP DX:, Open chest status after a modified Norwood stage 1 operation.,ANESTHESIA:, General endotracheal.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,FINDINGS:, No evidence of intramediastinal purulence or hematoma.  He tolerated the procedure well.,PROCEDURE DETAILS:,The patient was brought to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  After general endotracheal anesthesia, the chest was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  The previously placed AlloDerm membrane was removed.  Mediastinal cultures were obtained, and the mediastinum was extensively irrigated and suctioned.  Both cavities were also irrigated and vacuumed.  The drains were flushed and repositioned.  About 30 cubic centimeters of blood was drawn slowly from the right ear line.  The sternum was then coated with a vancomycin paste.  The proximal aspect of the 5 mm RV-PA conduit was marked with a small titanium clip on its inferior aspect and with another on its lower right side.  The sternum was then closed with stainless steel wires, followed by closure of the subcutaneous tissues with interrupted monofilament sutures.  The skin was closed with interrupted nylon sutures and a sterile dressing was placed.  The peritoneal dialysis catheter as well as the atrial and ventricular pacing wires were removed.  The patient was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit shortly thereafter in very stable condition. I was the surgeon present in the operating room and responsible for the surgical procedure throughout the duration of the case.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Right hemothorax.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Right hemothorax.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, Insertion of a French chest tube no. 32 on the right hemithorax.,ANESTHESIA:, 1% lidocaine and sedation.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS:, This is a female 54-year-old with cervical carcinoma.  The patient is scheduled to have an Infuse-A-Port insertion today.  Postoperatively, she started having pink foamy sputum tinged with blood.  A chest x-ray was obtained and showed evidence of a right-sided hemothorax, on the opposite side of the Infuse-A-Port, and a wider mediastinum.  The decision was made to place a chest tube in the right hemithorax to allow the patient to be stable during transfer out of the operating room.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:The area was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  The area was anesthetized with 1% lidocaine solution.  The patient received sedation.  A No. 10 blade scalpel was used to make an incision approximately 1.5 cm long.  Then, curved scissors were used to dissect down to the rib level.  A blunt peon was then used to re-enter the right hemithorax.  Immediately, a blood-tinged effusion was released.  The chest tube was placed and directed in a posterior and superior direction.  The chest tube was connected to the Pleur-evac device which was a ________ tip suction.  The chest tube was attached with a U-shaped No. 0 silk suture. It was sutured with a sterile dressing and silk tape.  The patient tolerated this procedure well.  We will obtain a postoperative chest x-ray to ensure proper placement and continue to monitor the patient very closely.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Stenosis of the right common, internal and external carotid artery.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Stenosis of the right common, internal and external carotid artery.,OPERATIONS,1.  Right common carotid endarterectomy.,2.  Right internal carotid endarterectomy.,3.  Right external carotid endarterectomy.,4.  Hemashield patch angioplasty of the right common, internal and external carotid arteries., ANESTHESIA:, General endotracheal anesthesia., URINE FLOW:, Not recorded, OPERATION IN DETAIL:, After obtaining informed consent from the patient, including a thorough explanation of the risks and benefits of the above procedure, the patient was taken to the operating room and general endotracheal anesthesia was administered.  Next, the right neck was prepared and draped in the standard surgical manner.  A #10 blade scalpel was used to make an incision at the anterior end of the sternocleidomastoid muscle.  Dissection was carried out to the level of the carotid artery using a Bovie electrocautery and sharp dissection with Metzenbaum scissors.  The common, internal and external carotid arteries were identified.  The facial vein was ligated with #3-0 silk.  The hypoglossal nerve was identified and preserved as it passed through the carotid artery.  After dissecting an adequate length of common, internal and external carotid artery, heparin was administered.  Next, an umbilical tape was passed around the common carotid artery.  A No. 0 silk suture was passed around the internal and external carotid arteries.  The hypoglossal nerve was identified and preserved.  An appropriately sized Argyle shunt was chosen.  A Hemashield patch has been cut to the appropriate size.  Then, vascular clamps were placed on the external carotid artery.  DeBakey microphones were used to monitor the internal carotid artery and the common carotid artery.  A No. 11 blade scalpel was used to make an incision on the common carotid artery.  The arteriotomy was extended over the internal carotid artery.  Next, the Argyle shunt was placed.  It was fixed in place.  Then, endarterectomy was performed; and this was done on the common internal carotid and external carotid arteries.  A reversal technique was used on the external carotid artery.  The artery was irrigated and loose debris was removed.  Next, we sewed the Hemashield patch to the artery using #6-0 Prolene in a routine manner.  Before our anastomosis was complete, we removed our shunt.  We have completed the anastomosis.  Next, we removed our clamp from the external carotid artery, followed by the common carotid artery and finally the internal carotid artery.  There was no trace of bleeding.  A full dose of protamine was administered.  The incision was closed with Vicryl #0, followed by Vicryl #2-0, followed by PDS #4-0 subcutaneously.  A sterile dressing was applied.

transcription: PROCEDURE: , Direct current cardioversion.,REASON FOR PROCEDURE: , Atrial fibrillation.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: ,The procedure was explained to the patient with the risks and benefits, including the risk of stroke.  The patient understands as well as her husband.  The patient had previously had a transesophageal echocardiogram showing the absence of thrombus in the left atrial appendage or thrombus in the left atrium.  Spontaneous contrast on the echocardiogram was observed.  Patient was anticoagulated with Lovenox, previously received 3 mg of Versed and 25 mcg of fentanyl for TEE followed by the next 2 mg of Versed for a total of 5 mg of Versed.  Pads applied anteriorly and posteriorly.  With a biphasic waveform synchronized at 150 J, a shock successfully restored sinus rhythm.  The patient had occasional PACs with occasional sinus tachycardia.  The patient had no immediate postoperative complications.  Rhythm was maintained and a 12-lead ECG was ordered.,PRINT: ,Successful DC cardioversion with restoration of sinus rhythm following atrial fibrillation without immediate complications.

She has symptoms suggestive of intracranial pathology, but so far the results have been negative. In the meantime, she is doing quite well and has been advised to continue taking her current medications and return to the office in three months for follow-up.

transcription: HISTORY:, The patient is an 86-year-old woman with a history of aortic valve replacement in the past with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation who was admitted yesterday with recurrence of the same in the context of hypokalemia, incomplete compliance with treatment of obstructive sleep apnea with CPAP, chocolate/caffeine ingestion and significant mental stress.  Despite electrolyte replenishment and treatment with IV Diltiazem, she maintained atrial fibrillation.  I discussed the risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure in detail with the patient.  After a thorough discussion of the procedure (please see my initial consultation for details), I asked the patient this morning if she would like me to repeat this, as this discussion had taken place yesterday.  The patient refused.  I asked her questions and she stated that she did not have one and wanted to proceed with the cardioversion that seemed appropriate., PROCEDURE NOTE: The appropriate temporization procedure was performed according to medical center protocol, including proper identification of patient, physician, procedure, documentation, and no safety concerns were identified by myself or staff.  The patient actively participated.  She then received a total of 4 mg of Versed and 50 micrograms of fentanyl with conscious sedation titrated to good effect.  She was placed in the supine position and hands-free patches had previously been placed in the AP position and she received an attempt at synchronized cardioversion after stopping the Diltiazem drip with successful resumption of normal sinus rhythm.  This was confirmed on a 12-lead ECG.,PRINT/PLAN:,Successful resumption of normal sinus rhythm following recurrent atrial fibrillation.  The patient's electrolytes are now normal and will need to be monitored closely to avoid hypokalemia in the future. Additionally, she has already been advised to strictly follow CPAP sleep apnea treatment and perhaps a new sleep assessment would be appropriate to titrate her parameters, as well as avoid caffeine ingestion, including chocolate, and minimize mental stress.  She will be discharged on her usual robust AV nodal antiarrhythmic therapy with 80 mg sotalol po. b.i.d., metoprolol 50 mg p.o. twice daily, Diltiazem CD 240 mg p.o. daily and digoxin 0.125 mg p.o. daily and to be clear, she is having a permanent pacemaker implanted.  She will follow up with her regular cardiologist, Dr. All of this was discussed in detail with the patient, as well as with her granddaughter with the patient's verbal consent at the bedside.

transcription: ADMISSION DIAGNOSIS:,1. Respiratory arrest.,2. End-stage chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.,3. Coronary heart disease.,4. History of hypertension.,DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:,1. Post-respiratory arrest status.,2. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.,3. Congestive heart failure.,4. History of coronary heart disease.,5. History of hypertension.,SUMMARY:,The patient is a 49-year-old man who was admitted to the,hospital in respiratory distress and required intubation shortly after,admission to the emergency department. The patient's history is notable for a history of coronary artery disease with prior myocardial infarctions in 1995 and 1999. The patient was recently admitted to the hospital for pneumonia and respiratory failure. The patient has been smoking for three to four months. On the day of admission, the patient experienced severe dyspnea and called an ambulance. The patient denied any progressive increase in wheezing, any increase in coughing, any increase in chest pain, any increase in spitting up before the onset of his sudden dyspnea.,ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: showed a well-developed, slightly obese male who was in extremis.,NECK: supple, without jugular venous distension.,HEART: showed tachycardia without murmurs or galloping.,PULMONARY: Condition showed decreased breathing sounds, but no clearly defined rales or wheezes.,EXTREMITIES: Free of edema.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,The patient was admitted to the special care unit and intubated. He received intravenous antibiotic therapy with Levaquin. He received intravenous diuretic treatment. He received portable bronchodilator treatment. The patient also received intravenous corticosteroid therapy with Solu-Medrol. The patient's progress gradually improved and after approximately three days the patient was extubated. He remained quite dyspneic, with wheezing and basilar rales. After pulmonary consultation, the pulmonary consultant felt that the patient's overall clinical picture suggested that he had a significant element of congestive heart failure. Thanks to this, the patient was subjected to increased doses of Lisinopril and Digoxin, with an improvement in his respiratory condition. On the day of discharge, the patient had minimal basilar rales; his chest also had minimal expiratory wheezing; he had no edema; his heartbeat was regular; his abdomen was soft; and the veins in his neck were not distended. It was therefore considered that the patient was stable for further outpatient treatment. DIAGNOSIS DATA: The patient's admission laboratory data were notable for his initial blood gases, which showed a pH of 7.02 with a pCO2 of 118 and a pO2 of 103. The patient's electrocardiogram showed nonspecific ST-T wave changes. The patent CBC showed a white count of 24,000, with 56% neutrophils and 3% bands.,DISPOSITION: The patient was discharged home.,DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS: His diet was to be an ADA diet of 2 grams sodium and 1,800 calories. His medications were to be Prednisone 20 mg twice daily, Theo-24 400 mg daily, Furosemide 40 mg 1-1/2 tablets p.o. daily; Acetazolamide 250 mg once p.o. per day, Lisinopril 20 mg. one p.o. twice a day, Digoxin 0.125 mg one p.o. qd, nitroglycerin paste 1 inch h.s., K-Dur 60 mEq p.o. offer. He also had to use a Ventolin inhaler every four hours as needed, and Azmacort four puffs twice a day. She was asked to return for a follow-up with Dr. X in one to two weeks. Arrangements were made for the patient to undergo an echocardiogram to further evaluate his congestive heart failure later on the day of discharge.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, This 66 year old white male was seen in my office on DD month YYYY.  The patient was recently discharged from Doctors Hospital at Parkway after being treated for pneumonia.  The patient continues to present with severe orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, cough with greenish expectoration.  His exercise tolerance is about two to three meters for shortness of breath.  The patient stopped taking Coumadin for reasons that are not entirely clear to him.  He was recently documented to be suffering from atrial fibrillation.  The patient has a long-standing history of ischemic heart disease, end-stage LV systolic dysfunction, and his status is post-ICD implantation.  Fasting blood sugar this morning is 130.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure is 120/60.  Breaths 18 per minute.  Heart rate 75-85 beats per minute, irregular.  Weight 207 pounds.,HEENT: Normocephalic head.  Eyes, no signs of anemia or jaundice.  Oral hygiene is good. ,NECK: Flexible.  JVP is flat.  The upward movement of the carotid artery is good. ,LUNGS: Severe inspiratory and expiratory wheezing heard in all lung fields.  Fine crackles heard at the base of the lungs on both sides. ,CARDIOVASCULAR: PMI felt in the left fifth intercostal space, 0.5 inch lateral to the midclavicular line. The first and second heart sounds are normal.  There is a II/VI systolic murmur best heard at the top.,ABDOMEN: Soft.  There is no hepatosplenomegaly.,EXTREMITIES: Patient has 1+ pedal edema.,DRUGS: , ,1.  Ambien 10 mg at bedtime p.r.n.,2.  Coumadin 7.5 mg daily.,3.  Diovan 320 mg per day.,4.  Lantus insulin 50 units in the morning.,5.  Lasix 80 mg per day.,6.  Novolin R p.r.n.,7.  Toprol XL 100 mg per day.,8.  Flovent 100 mcg twice daily.,DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Atherosclerotic coronary vascular disease with old myocardial infarction.,2.  Moderate to severe LV systolic dysfunction.,3.  Diabetes mellitus.,4.  Diabetic nephropathy and renal failure.,5.  Status after DCI implementation.,6.  New onset of atrial fibrillation.,7.  Chronic Coumadin therapy.,PLAN:,1.  Continue current therapy.,2.  The patient will be seen again in my office in four weeks.

transcription: INDICATIONS:,Preoperative cardiac evaluation in patient with chest pain in left hip fracture.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,The patient is a 78-year-old white female with no prior cardiac history.  She suffered a mechanical fall followed by a fracture of the neck of the left femur.  She was transferred to XYZ Hospital for definitive care.  At the XYZ emergency room, the patient described six to seven seconds of acute chest pain without radiation, with no associated symptoms.  An electrocardiogram was obtained, which showed nonspecific ST segment flattening in the high lateral leads I, aVL.  She also had left axis deviation.  Serial troponins were obtained.  She has had four negative troponins since admission.  Due to his age and history of chest pain, a cardiology consultation was requested preoperatively.,At the time of my evaluation, the patient was complaining of left hip pain, but not chest pain, dyspnea, or symptomatic dysrhythmia.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Mesothelioma.,2.  Recurrent urinary tract infections.,3.  Gastroesophageal reflux/gastritis.,4.  Osteopenia.,5.  Right sciatica.,6.  Hypothyroidism.,7.  Peripheral neuropathy.,8.  Fibromyalgia.,9. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Post-infarction angina.,PROCEDURE TYPE: ,Left heart catheterization with right and left selective coronary angiography.,PROCEDURE: ,After obtaining informed consent, the patient was brought to the cardiac catheterization laboratory and the groin was prepared in the usual manner.  Using 1% lidocaine, the right groin was infiltrated and, using the Seldinger technique, the right femoral artery was cannulated.  Using this, a movable guidewire was then advanced to the level of the diaphragm, and through it, a 6 French pigtail catheter was advanced under hemodynamic monitoring to the ascending aorta and inserted into the left ventricle.  Pressure measurements were obtained and cineangiograms in the RAO and LAO positions were then obtained.  The catheter was then removed and a non-bleeding French #6 handgun sheath was then introduced, and through this, a Judkins French 6 left coronary catheter was then advanced under hemodynamic monitoring to the engaged left coronary ostium.  Cineangiograms were obtained from the left coronary system.  This catheter was then exchanged for a Judkins 4 right coronary catheter of similar size and, under hemodynamic monitoring, it was again advanced toward the engaged right coronary ostium.  Cineangiograms were obtained, and then the catheter and sheath were removed.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and left the cardiac catheterization laboratory in stable condition.  No signs of hematoma formation or active bleeding.  ,COMPLICATIONS: , None.  ,TOTAL CONTRAST: , 110 cc of Hexabrix.  ,TOTAL FLUOROSCOPY DURATION: ,1.8 minutes.  ,MEDICATIONS: , Reglan 10 mg p.o., 5 mg p.o. Valium, Benadryl 50 mg p.o. and heparin 3000 units IV push.

transcription: REASON FOR VISIT:, Six-month follow-up visit for coronary artery disease.,He is a 67-year-old man who suffers from chronic anxiety, coronary artery disease and DJD.,He has a lot of back and left knee pain.  He also has difficulty controlling his nerves.  He has stomach pains and occasional nausea.  His teeth are bad and need to be pulled out. He's had some chest pains, but overall he doesn't seem too worrisome.  He notices more shortness of breath than usual.  He had no palpitations or dizziness.  No edema issues.,MEDICATIONS: Lipitor 40 mg q.d., Metoprolol 25 mg twice daily, Plavix 75 mg q.d - stopped, Enalapril 10 mg twice daily, Aspirin 325 mg - reduced to 81 mg, Lorcet 10/650 - with a prescription of 60 tablets and Xanax 0.5. mg b.i.d - given a prescription of 60 tablets., SYSTEMS REVIEW:, Otherwise nothing remarkable., PEX:, BP: 140/78. Moderate LVH.  Possible gallstones observed.,PRINT:,1.  CAD status after anterior wall MI 07/07 and found to have multivessel CAD.  He has a stent in his LAD and obtuse marginal.  Quite stable.,2.  Dyspnea-Appears to be due to his weight and knee disability.  His echocardiogram shows no systolic or diastolic function.,3.  Knee pain - We are referring to Scotland Orthopedics and will refill his prescription for Lorcet 60 tablets without refill.,4. Prevention - I don't think he needs to take more Plavix as he has been relatively stable for two years.,PLAN:,1.  Stop Plavix.,2.  Aspirin reduced to 81 mg per day.,3.  Lorcet and Xanax prescriptions given.,4.  Refer to Scotland Orthopedics.,5.  He was given Peridex mouthwash for his poor teeth and told he was cardiovascularly stable and would have his teeth extracted.

transcription: The patient and his wife were given the opportunity to ask questions, all of which were answered and the patient stated clearly, competently, and coherently that he wished to proceed with the cardiac catheterization, which I felt was appropriate.,PROCEDURE NOTE:,The patient was brought to the cardiac catheterization laboratory in a fasted state.  All appropriate laboratories were examined.  Bilateral groins were prepared and draped in the usual manner for sterile conditions.  The proper time-out procedure was performed with proper identification of the patient, procedure, physician, position and documentation, all under my direct supervision and no safety concerns were raised by staff.  He received a total of 2 mg of Versed and 50 mcg of Fentanyl using a titrated concentration with good effect.  The bilateral groins had been prepared and draped in the usual manner.  The right femoral inguinal fossa was anesthetized with topical 1% lidocaine and a 6-French vascular sheath was placed percutaneously via a guidewire exchanger with a search needle.  All catheters were passed using a J-tip guidewire. Left heart catheterization and left ventriculography performed using a 6 French braid catheter.  Coronary angiography of the left system performed using a 6-French JL4 catheter.  Coronary angiography of the right system performed using a CDRC 6-French catheter.  After the procedure, all catheters were removed.  Manual pressure was maintained with the Neptune pad and the patient was returned to his room.  I inspected the femoral arteriotomy site after the procedure was completed and it was benign with no signs of hematoma or bruit with intact distal pulses.  There were no apparent complications.  A total of 77 cc of Isovue dye and 1.4 minutes of fluoroscopy were used during the case.,FINDINGS:,HEMODYNAMIC:, LV pressure is 120, EDP is 20, aortic pressure 120/62, mean 82.,LV function is normal, EF 60%, no wall motion abnormalities.,ANATOMY CORONARY:,1.  The left main lesion has a 30-40% distal left main lesion that is tapered and is not felt significantly obstructive.,2.  The LAD demonstrates a moderate proximal 50% lesion and a severe lesion in the middle of the LAD immediately after takeoff from this large 99% diagonal which is quite severe with TIMI-3 flow throughout the LAD and left main portion.,3.  The left circumflex shows severe damage of approximately 90% with TIMI-3 flow.,4.  The right coronary artery was the dominant artery giving rise to the right posterior descending artery which presents a slight luminal irregularity.  There is a moderate distal PDA injury of 60%.,PRINT:,1.  Mild to moderate left main stenosis.,2.  Very severe mid-LAD ​​stenosis with severe mid-left circumflex stenosis and moderate prox-LAD coronary artery disease. We will continue the patient's aspirin and beta blocker therapy as long as the heart rate tolerates it because he tends to run on the bradycardic side and add statins.  We will check a fasting lipid profile and ALT and titrate statin therapy to maintain LDL at 70 mg/deciliter or less, but in the past the patient's LDL has been higher or higher.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, angina and coronary artery disease.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, angina and coronary artery disease.,OPERATION NAME:, coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) x2, left internal mammary artery to left anterior descending vein and reverse saphenous vein graft to circumflex, St. Jude proximal anastomosis used for vein graft.  Medtronic off-pump technique for the left internal mammary artery and BIVAD technique for the circumflex.,ANESTHESIA:, General.,PROCEDURE DETAILS:,The patient was brought to the operating room and placed supine on the table.  After adequate general anesthesia, the patient was prepared with soap and Betadine solution in the usual sterile manner.  Elbows were protected to avoid ulnar neuropathy, chest wall expansion avoided to avoid ulnar neuropathy, phrenic nerve protectors used to protect the phrenic nerve and removed at the end of the case. A mid-sternum skin incision was made and descended through the sternum which was divided with the saw.  The pericardial and thymus fat pads were divided.  The left internal mammary artery was harvested and spatulated for anastomosis.  Heparin was administered. Vein resected from the thigh, side branches fixed using 4-0 silk and hemoclips.  The thigh was closed using the Vicryl and Dexon multilayer technique.  A Pulsavac wash was performed, a drain was placed. The left internal mammary artery is stitched to the left anterior descending using the Prolene technique running 7-0 with Medtronic off-pump retractors.  After that, the patient was fully heparinized, cannulated with a 6.5 ear cannula and a 2-stage venous catheter and started cardiopulmonary bypass and maintained normothermia.  Medtronic retractors used to expose the circumflex.  Before moving on to the pump, we stapled the vein graft in place to the aorta. Then, at the pump, we performed the distal anastomosis with a Prolene 7-0 technique.  The right side graft was delivered to the posterior descending artery using the 7-0 Prolene technique.  A deaeration procedure was carried out.  The bulldogs have been kidnapped.  The patient maintained good normal sinus rhythm with good average perfusion.  The patient was weaned off cardiopulmonary bypass.  The arterial and venous lines were removed and double secured.  Protamine was delivered.  Meticulous hemostasis was present.  Platelets were administered for coagulopathy.  A chest tube was placed and meticulous hemostasis was present.  The anatomy and flow of the grafts were excellent.  The shutdown has started. The sternum was closed with wire, followed by closure of the linea alba and pectus fascia with 0 Vicryl sutures in double-layer technique.  The skin was closed with the Dexon 4-0 subcutaneous suture technique.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the intensive care unit in stable condition. We minimized the pumping time to 16 minutes only for the distal circumflex anastomosis to reduce renal injury because the patient already has renal insufficiency with a creatinine of 3.0.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Foreign body in the airway.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Plastic piece, foreign body in the right main bronchus.,PROCEDURE: ,Rigid bronchoscopy with removal of the foreign body.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS: ,This patient is a 7 month old boy who presented to the emergency room today with increasing stridor and shortness of breath according to mom.  The patient underwent a chest X-ray, and based on this, the radiology department is concerned that it may be a foreign body in the right main stem.  The patient was taken to the operating room for rigid bronchoscopy and removal of foreign bodies.,DESCRIPTION OF THE PROCEDURE: ,The patient was taken to the operating room, placed in the supine position, put under general mask anesthesia.  Using a 3.5 rigid bronchoscope, we visualized between the cords in the trachea.  There was some discharge but it looked good.  Went down to the level of the hull to notice a foreign body beating in the right main rod.  I then used pliers to grab and try to pull in the scope itself.  I was unable to do this, so I had to remove the scope along with the retained foreign body with pliers.  It appeared to be made of some sort of piece of plastic that had broken off from another object.  I took the telescope and put it back into the airway.  Again there was secretion in the trachea which we suctioned out.  We looked toward the right bronchus intermedius.  No other pathology was noted, just some irritation in the right area of ​​the main stem.  I also looked at the left main stem and that looked good as well.  I then removed the scope.  The trachea looked good as well as the cords.  I put the patient back on an oxygen mask to wake him up.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: INDICATIONS FOR PROCEDURE:Persistent cough producing sputum requiring repeated courses of oral antibiotics within the last six weeks in a bone marrow transplant patient with terminal chemotherapy and radiation-induced pulmonary fibrosis.,PREDICATION:,1.  Demerol 50 mg.,2.  Phenergan 25 mg.,3.  Atropine 0.6 mg IM.,4.  Nebulized lidocaine 4%, followed by nasal insufflation of lidocaine through the right nostril and topical lidocaine 4% gel through the right nostril, 4 mg of Versed was administered at the start of the procedure and an additional 1 mg shortly after passing through the cords. the right naris at the level of the strings.  The cords move normally with phonation and ventilation.  Twice 2 mL of 1% lidocaine was instilled onto the cords and the cords were crossed.  Additionally, 2 mL of 1% lidocaine was instilled into the trachea just distal to the cords, in the middle of the trachea above the carina, and over the right and left mainstem bronchi.  The scope was then introduced to the left where immediately some hyperemia of the mucosa was noted.  The upper lobe and lingula were unremarkable.  There appeared to be some narrowing or tensioning of the left lower lobe bronchus, such that upon inspection of the upper segment, the bronchoscope almost had to be rotated to descend into the left main stem.  This was a change from previous bronchoscopy whose significance was unclear.  Distal to this, there was no hyperemia, inspissated mucus, mucoid discharge, or signs of infection.  The scope was positioned in the posterior basal segment of the left lower lobe and a BAL was performed with good, slightly hemorrhagic feedback.  The scope was then removed, reintroduced to the right upper basal, middle lobe, upper segment, right lower, anterior lateral and posterior basal sub-segments which were all assessed and unremarkable.  The scope has been removed.  Patient saturation remained between 93% and 95% throughout the procedure.  Blood pressure was 103/62.  The heart rate at the end of the procedure was around 100. The patient tolerated the procedure well.  The samples were sent as follows.  Washes for AFB, Gram-stained Nocardia, Aspergillus and routine culture.  Washing for AFB, Nocardia by Gram stain, Aspergillus, cell count with differential, cytology, culture of viral mycoplasma and Chlamydia, GMS staining, RSV by antigen and culture of Legionella and Chlamydia.

transcription: PROCEDURE:, Bronchoscopy, right upper lobe biopsies and right upper lobe bronchial washing as well as precarinal transbronchial needle aspiration.,PROCEDURE DETAILS:,The risks, alternatives and benefits of the procedure were explained to the patient as well as conscious sedation and she agreed to proceed.  The patient received topical lidocaine via nebulization.  The flexible fiberoptic bronchoscope was introduced orally.  The patient had normal teeth, normal tongue, normal jaw, and her vocal cords moved symmetrically and were free of lesions.  I proceeded to the right upper lobe where a mucus plug was noted in the subsegmental bronchus of the posterior segment of the right upper lobe.  I proceeded under fluoroscopic guidance to guide the biopsy wire into this area and performed four biopsies.  Follow-up fluoroscopy was negative for pneumothorax.  I stuck the bronchoscope in the subsegmental bronchus and achieved good hemostasis after three minutes. I then proceeded to inspect the remainder of the tracheobronchial tree, which was free of lesions.  I performed bronchial washing after the right upper lobe biopsies.  I then performed two transbronchial needle aspirations with a Wang needle biopsy in the precarinal area.  All these samples were sent for histology and cytology respectively.  Estimated blood loss was approximately 5 cc.  Good hemostasis was achieved.  The patient received a total of 12.5 mg of Demerol and 3 mg of Versed and tolerated the procedure well.  His ASA score was 2.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: , Increased work of breathing., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a 2 month old girl with a 9 day history of illness.  According to mom's report, the illness started 9 days ago with a dry cough.  The patient was eating normally until about three days ago.  Mom used a spray at night, which she said helped.  The patient's cough gradually worsened and three days ago the patient had a cough that was getting significantly worse.  At this time, the patient also had significant and increasing congestion.  Two days ago, the patient was taken to the primary care physician's office and the patient was given 2 puffs of Xopenex every 4-6 hours for home treatment, but according to the mother's report, this did not improve the patient's symptoms.  On Wednesday evening, the patient's congestion and work of breathing increased and the patient was gagging after feedings.  The patient was brought to the Children's Hospital emergency room and was subsequently evaluated.  A chest x-ray was performed and found to be normal.  The patient's saturations were found to be normal and the patient was discharged home.  Last night the patient had several episodes of vomiting after feeding with cough and today there was a decrease in his activity.  The patient had a temperature of 101 Wednesday evening, but did not have a true fever.  The patient experienced a slight decrease in urine output today and secondary to the persistent increase in work of breathing, cough, and posttussive vomiting, the patient was brought to Children's Hospital for reevaluation. above., IMMUNIZATIONS:, None., MEDICAL HISTORY:, No hospitalizations.  No surgery.,BIRTH HISTORY: The patient was born to a G8, P2, A6 mother through normal spontaneous vaginal delivery.  Birth weight 6 pounds 12 ounces.  Mom said she had a uterine infection during her pregnancy and at the time of delivery, but the patient was only in the hospital for 24 hours with her mother after giving birth.  The patient was full term and the mother was found to have gestational diabetes which was controlled by diet during pregnancy.,FAMILY HISTORY: Brother, mother and father all suffer from asthma. transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Hospital follow-up is required for persistent problems with atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response, resistant to treatment with diltiazem and amiodarone, followed by Dr.  This afternoon, as I see the patient, the nurses inform me that the levels were finally controlled with esmolol, but the systolic blood pressures had dropped to the 70s with a MAP of 52. Dr. X was again consulted at the bedside.  We agreed to try fluid boluses and then consider neo-synephrine pressure support if that was not successful.  Additionally, over the past 24 hours, extensive discussions were held with the family and questions were answered by nursing staff regarding a possible move of the patient to Tahoe Pacific or a long-term acute care facility.  Other problems requiring monitoring today are elevated transaminases, persistent fever, pneumonia, resolution of adult respiratory distress syndrome, ventilatory-dependent respiratory failure, hypokalemia, non-ST elevation MI, hypernatremia, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, BPH, atrial flutter, inferior vena cava filter, and diabetes.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION, VITAL SIGNS: T-max 103.2, blood pressure at this point is in the 70s/mid 40s with a MAP of 52, heart rate is 100. GENERAL: The patient appears much more alert than when I last examined about 3 weeks ago.  He denies any pain, appears mentally intact and is in no apparent distress.,EYE: Round, light-reactive, anicteric pupils with intact external eye movements.,CARDIOVASCULAR: Reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm.,LUNGS: Have diminished breath sounds but are clear forward.,ABDOMEN: Somewhat distended but without guarding, rebound or obvious tenderness to the palpation.,EXTREMITIES: Show marks. edema without clubbing or cyanosis.,NEUROLOGICAL: Patient moves all extremities without focal neurological deficits.,LABORATORY DATA:, Sodium 149; that's down from 151 yesterday.  Potassium 3.9, chloride 114, bicarb 25, BUN 35, creatinine 1.5 compared to 1.2 yesterday, hemoglobin 12.4, hematocrit 36.3, WBC 16.5, platelets 231,000. 1.4 INR.  Transaminases continue to increase by SGOT 546, SGPT 256. Also noted is a small amount of highly concentrated urine in the bag.,IMPRESSION:,Overall impressions continue to be a critically ill 67 year old male with multiple medical problems probably still showing signs of volume depletion with hypotension and atrial flutter with a difficult to control rate.,PLAN,1.  Hypotension.  I was aggressively trying to fill the patient with fluid by giving him another liter of fluid.  If this does not work as discussed with Dr.  Increased transaminases.  Presumably this is due to increased congestion.  This is certainly concerning.  We will continue to monitor this.  Liver ultrasound was apparently negative.,3.  Fever and high white count.  The patient has a history of pneumonia and empyema.  We will continue to take current antibiotics per infectious disease and continue to monitor the patient's white count.  He's not exceptionally toxic right now.  Indeed, it looks improved since my last review.,4.  Ventilator-dependent respiratory failure.  The patient has received a tracheotomy since my last examination.  Vent management according to PMA.,5.  Hypokalemia.  This has been resolved.  Continue supplementation.,6.  Hypernatremia.  This is improving somewhat.  I hope that with increasing fluids it will continue to do so.,7.  Diabetes mellitus.  Fingerstick blood sugars are reviewed and are within target.  We will continue current management.  This is a critically ill patient with multiorgan dysfunction and sig

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: ,Management of atrial fibrillation.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,The patient is a very pleasant 62-year-old African American female with a history of hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and stroke, referred by Dr.  The patient states that on Monday, during Holt's routine examination, Dr. X detected that her heart was irregular on examination.  The ECG subsequently obtained revealed atrial fibrillation, and the patient was started on Coumadin as well as Toprol and referred for evaluation.  The patient states that over the past 3 years she has had episodes of her heart racing.  This can last from a few minutes to up to an hour, and will sometimes be linked to eating a large meal or drinking caffeine or chocolate.  Denies dyspnea, sweating, presyncope, or syncope with events, and she has had no episodes of chest pain.  They then resolve on their own and do not limit it in any way.  However, she states that in recent years it may take her up to 7 years to remember that she was tired and that in the last two years her symptoms have become more severe.  She said she couldn't walk more than 25 feet without getting tired.  She states that she needs to rest for her symptoms to go away, but she has been limited in this regard.  Denies peripheral edema, PND, orthopnea, abdominal pain, swelling, fever, or recent chills.  In fact, today she has no complaints and reports that she has been compliant with her medications and started taking her Coumadin as directed.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Hypertension.,2.  Myocardial infarction in 2003.,3.  Left heart catheterization at University Hospital.,4.  Hypercholesterolemia.,5.  Arthritis.,6.  Stroke in 2002 and 2003 with blindness in the right eye.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,1.  Total left knee arthroplasty in 2002.,2.  Drainage of left lower quadrant abscess in 12/07, FAMILY MEDICAL HISTORY:, Important for lung and brain cancer.  There is no history that she is aware of cardiovascular disease in her family and no member of her family has suffered sudden cardiac death.,SOCIAL HISTORY: She is retired as a cook in a school cafeteria, where she worked for 34 years.  She retired 7 years ago due to lower back pain.  She smoked 2 1/2 packs a day for 32 years, but quit in 1995. She denies alcohol and denies intravenous or illicit drug use.,ALLERGIES:,No known drug allergies.,DRUGS:,1.  Coumadin 5 mg daily.,2.  Toprol-XL 50 mg per day.,3.  Aspirin 81 mg per day.,4.  Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily.,5.  Plendil 10 mg per day.,6.  Lipitor 40 mg daily.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: ,As above, indicating that as a result of her stroke she has blindness in her right eye, but she has minimal vision in its periphery.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: blood pressure 138/66, pulse 96, and weight 229 pounds or 104 kg.  GENERAL: A well-developed, well-nourished, middle-aged African American female in no acute distress.  NECK: Soft.  No JVD.  No carotid sounds.  CARDIOVASCULAR: Irregular rhythm and frequency.  Normals S1 and S2.  No murmuring, galloping or rubbing.  LUNGS: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.  ABDOMEN: The bowel appears positive, soft, non-tender and non-distended.  No masses.  EXTREMITIES: No clubbing, cyanosis or edema.  Pulse 2+ bilaterally.,LABORATORY DATA:,EKG today revealed atrial fibrillation with non-specific lateral T-wave abnormalities and a rate of 94.,IMPRESSION:,The patient is a very pleasant 62-year-old African American female with atrial fibrillation of unknown duration with symptoms of paroxysmal episodes of palpitations, who is doing well today.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,1.  His level is not optimally controlled, we will increase his Toprol-XL to 75 mg per day.,2.  We will obtain a transthoracic echocardiogram to assess his LV function as well as his valve function.,3.  We will check a thyroid function panel.,4.  We will continue Coumadin as directed and follow up with Dr.
