transcription: PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After obtaining written consent from the patient, the patient was brought back to the operating room and identified.  The patient was placed on the operating table in a supine position and anesthetized. Once adequate anesthesia was obtained, a careful examination of the shoulder was performed.  It does not reveal any patholigamentous laxity.  We then placed the patient in a beach chair position, maintaining neutral alignment of the head, neck, and thorax.  The shoulder was then prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  We then injected the glenohumeral joint with 60 cc of sterile saline.  A small incision was made 2 cm below and 2 cm medial to the posterolateral angle of the acromion.  Through this incision, a rounded trocar was placed. We then placed the camera through this cannula and the shoulder was insufflated with sterile saline.  An anterior portal was made just below the subscapularis and then we began to inspect the shoulder joint. We found that the joint surface was in good condition.  The biceps was found to be intact.  There was a SLAP tear noted just behind the bicep.  Photos were taken.  No Bankart or Hill-Sachs lesions were noted.  The rotator cuff was examined and there were no tears below the surface.  Photos were taken again. We then created a lateral portal crossing the muscular belly of the rotator cuff.  A drill hole was made and then a knotless suture anchor was placed to repair the problem.  Photos were taken.  We then washed the joint with copious amounts of sterile saline solution.  He was empty.  Our 3 incisions were closed using 3-0 nylon suture.  A pain pump catheter was inserted into the shoulder joint.  Xeroform, 4 x 4, ABD, duct tape and sling were placed. The patient was successfully removed from the beach chair position, extubated, and brought to the recovery room in stable condition.  I then went out and spoke with the patient's family, reviewing the case, post-operative instructions, and follow-up care.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Sacroiliitis (720.2), lumbosacral segmental dysfunction (739.3), thoracolumbar segmental dysfunction (739.2), associated with myalgia/fibromyositis (729.1).,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Sacroiliitis (720.2), segmental dysfunction lumbosacral (739.3), thoracolumbar segmental dysfunction (739.2), associated with myalgia/fibromyositis (729.1).,ANESTHESIA: , Conscious sedation.,INFORMED CONSENT: , After adequate explanation of medical-surgical and procedural options, this patient decided to proceed with the recommended spinal manipulation under anesthesia (MUA).  The patient was informed that more than one procedure may be necessary to achieve satisfactory results.,INDICATION:,This patient has failed prolonged conservative care of his condition/dysfunction by means of aggressive physical, medical and pharmacological intervention.,COMMENTS:,This patient understands the essence of the diagnosis and reasons for MUA. Risks associated with the procedure, including anesthesia complications, fractures, stroke, disc herniation, and post-procedure discomfort, were carefully discussed with the patient.  Alternatives to the procedure, including disease progression without MUA, were discussed.  The patient understands the chances of success of an MUA and that no guarantees are made or implied regarding the outcome.  The patient provided verbal and written informed consent for the listed procedure.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,The patient was draped in the appropriate gown and escorted to the operative area.  After their sacral block injection, they were asked to lie on their backs on the operating table and they were placed on the appropriate monitors for this procedure.  When the patient and I were ready, the anesthesiologist administered the appropriate medications to help the patient enter twilight sedation using medications that allow for the stretching, mobilization, and adjustments necessary to achieve the desired outcome. Thoracic Spine: With the patient in the supine position on the operating table, the upper extremities were flexed at the elbow and crossed over the patient's chest to obtain maximum traction on the patient's thoracic spine.  The first assistant held the patient's arms in the correct position and helped roll the patient for the adjustment procedure.  With the help of the first assistant, the patient was rolled onto the right side, the point of contact was selected, and the patient was returned to the doctor's hand.  The resilient elastic barrier was found and low velocity thrust was achieved using a specific anterior to posterior/superior closed reduction manipulation procedure.  The procedure was carried out at the TI-TI2 level.  Cavitation was achieved.,LUMBAR/SACROILIAC JOINTS: With the patient supine on the procedure table, the attending physician addressed the patient's lower extremities which were alternately elevated by raising the right leg approximately 90 degrees from horizontal.  Linear force was used to gradually increase hip flexion during this maneuver.  Simultaneously, the first assistant physician applied a myofascial release technique to the calf and posterior thigh muscles.  Each lower limb was bent independently at the knee and subjected to a cephalic traction in a neutral sagittal plane, a lateral oblique cephalic traction, and a medial oblique cephalic traction maneuver.  The attending physician then moved the opposite knee closer to its neutral to medial position slightly beyond the elastic resistance barrier. (a pyriform myofascial release was performed at this time).  This was repeated with the opposite lower limb. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Severe degenerative disease of the right shoulder joint.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Severe degenerative disease of the right shoulder.,PROCEDURE: , Hemi-resurfacing of the right shoulder using Biomet Copeland humeral head component size 5, uncemented.,ANAESTHESIA: , General endotracheal.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , Less than 100 mL.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.  The patient was taken to the post-anesthesia care unit in stable condition.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.,INDICATIONS:The patient is a 55-year-old female who presents with increased right shoulder pain.  X-rays and an MRI showed a severe arthritic presentation of the humeral head with slight osteoarthritis of the glenoid.  She had an intact rotator cuff at a young age and with glenoid potential, so it was felt that a hemi-resurfacing was appropriate for her right shoulder focusing on the humeral head.  All the risks, benefits, expectations and complications of the surgery were explained to him in detail, including nerve and vascular damage, infections, risk of hardware failure, need for revision surgery with the potential for some problems even if surgically performed.  The patient still wanted to proceed with surgery.  The patient received 1 g of Ancef preoperatively.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was taken to the operating room, placed in the supine position on the operating table.  The anesthesia department administered general endotracheal anesthesia, which the patient tolerated well.  The patient was moved to a beach chair position.  All ends were well padded.  His head was cushioned against the table.  Her upper right extremity was draped in a sterile manner.  A saber incision was made from the coracoid to the axilla.  The skin was incised down to the subcutaneous tissue, the cephalic vein was retracted as well as all neurovascular structures were retracted in the case.  By dissecting through the deltopectoral groove, the subscapularis tendon was found as well as the bicipital tendon, an incision was made 1 finger medial to the bicipital tendon.  The subscapularis tendon was released.  The humeral head was brought back; there were large osteophytes which were removed with an osteotome.  The glenoid was then evaluated and noted mild osteoarthritis, but no surgery was needed in this area.  A calibrator has been placed.  It was felt that size 5 was appropriate for this patient, after which the guide was used to place the rod and pin.  This was placed, after which a reamer was placed along the humeral head and reamed to a size 5. Any additional osteophytes were excised.  The supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons were intact.  Then, the excess bone was removed and irrigated, after which reaming of the central part of the humeral head was performed. A test was carried out and showed that there was adequate fit and proper fixation.  The arm had excellent range of motion.  There are no signs of significant dislocation.  Drill holes were made in the humeral head, after which a size 5 Copeland hemi-resurfacing component was placed in the humeral head, held in an appropriate position, with excellent fixation in the humeral head.  The excess bone that had been reamed was placed into the Copeland metal component, after which this was seated in place.  After which, the wound site was abundantly irrigated with saline and antibiotics and the humeral head was reduced and subjected to range of motion; had adequate range of motion, full internal and external rotation as well as forward flexion and abduction.  There were no obvious signs of dislocation.  The wound site was again irrigated extensively with saline antibiotics.  The subscapularis tendon was repositioned with Ethibond #2, after which the bicipital tendon sustained a significant tear; it was therefore tenoded in the pectoralis major tendon.  After which, the wound site was again irrigated with saline.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: This 18 year old male presented today with right shoulder pain.  Location: It indicates that the location of the problem is the right shoulder diffusely.  Quality: The quality of pain is described by the patient as aching, throbbing, and tolerable.  The patient rates the pain on a scale of 0 to 10 to 5/10.  Severity: Severity has worsened over the past 3 months.  Timing (onset/frequency): Onset was gradual and after throwing a baseball game.  Modifying Factors: The patient's condition is aggravated by throwing.  He plays basketball with difficulty.  Previous conservative treatments include NSAIDs and muscle relaxants.,ALLERGIES:, No known medical allergies.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, None.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Childhood illnesses: (+) strep throat (+) mumps (+) chickenpox,SURGICAL HISTORY:, No surgery prior.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Patient admits to a family history of maternal-associated arthritis.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Patient denies smoking, alcohol abuse, illicit drug use, and STDs.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Musculoskeletal: (+) joint or musculoskeletal symptoms (+) stiffness in the morning.,Psychiatric: (-) psychiatric difficulties or Emotional.,Eyes: (-) visual disturbances or changes.,Neurological: (-) neurological symptoms or problems. Endocrine: (-) symptoms related to the endocrine system.,Allergic. / Immunological: (-) allergic or immunological symptoms.,Ears, nose, mouth, throat: (-) symptoms involving the ears, nose, mouth or throat.,Gastrointestinal: (-) gastrointestinal symptoms.,Genitourinary: (-) GU symptoms.,Constitutional symptoms: (-) constitutional symptoms such as fever, headache, nausea, dizziness.,Cardiovascular: (-) cardiovascular problems or chest symptoms.,Respiratory: (-)breathing difficulties, respiratory symptoms.,Physical exam: BP Standing: 116/68 Resp: 16 HR: 68 Temp: 98.1 Height: 5 ft 11 in. Weight: 165 lbs.  The patient is an 18-year-old man who appears pleasant, without apparent distress, considering his age, well developed, well nourished and with good attention to hygiene and body habits.  Oriented to person, place and time.  The right shoulder shows signs of swelling and tenderness.  Radial impulses are 2/4, bilateral.  Brachial pulses are 2/4, bilateral.,Appearance: normal.,Tenderness: anterior – moderate, biceps – none, posterior – moderate and subacromial – moderate right.,Range of motion: Right shoulder ROM shows decreased flexion, decreased extension, decreased adduction, decreased abduction, decreased internal rotation, decreased external rotation.  Shoulder normal.,Strength: External rotation - fair.  Internal rotation - poor on the right., AC joint: Pain with ABD and thoracic cross - mild on the right., Rotator cuff: Impingement - moderate.  Arch pain - moderate on the right.,Instability: None.,TEST RESULTS AND X-RAYS: Shoulder x-rays were taken.  X-ray of the right shoulder reveals the presence of rotator cuff arthropathy.,PRINT:,Rotator cuff syndrome, right.,PLAN:,The diagnosis of rotator cuff tendinitis and shoulder impingement were discussed.  I noticed that this is a very common pathology causing significant difficulty in using the arm.  Several treatment options and their potential benefits have been described.  Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs can be helpful, but they usually work slowly.  Cortisone injections can be very effective and completely safe.  Often, multiple injections may be needed.  Physical therapy may also be helpful, especially if there is loss of shoulder mobility or strength.  If these treatments fail to resolve symptoms, an MRI or arthrogram of the shoulder may be necessary to rule out a rotator cuff tear.  Shoulder joint injected and with Celestone Soluspan 1.0 cc.  Ordered X-rays of the right shoulder.,PRESCRIPTIONS:, Vioxx Dosage: 25 mg tablet Sig: Dispensing TID: 60 Refills: 0 Allow Generic: Yes, PATIENT INSTRUCTIONS:, Patient has been instructed to restrict activity.  The patient received instructions on RICE therapy.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: 1. Left pilon fracture.,2.  Fracture of the proximal phalanx of the left big toe.,POSTOP DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Fracture of the left pilon.,2.  Fracture of the proximal phalanx of the left big toe.,OPERATION PERFORMED:,1.  External fixation of the left pilon fracture.,2.  Closed reduction of left big toe, T1 fracture.,ANESTHESIA: ,General.,BLOOD LOSS: ,Less than 10 ml.,Number of needles, instruments and sponges were taken and corrected.,DRAINS AND TUBES: ,None.,SAMPLES: ,None.,INDICATION FOR OPERATION: ,The patient is a 58 year old woman who was involved in a car accident rather than a tree accident. 06/15/2009.  The patient suffered at that time from a fracture of a distal tibia and fibula as well as the big toe on the left side.  The patient was evaluated by the emergency department and underwent further evaluation due to loss of consciousness.  She underwent a temporary reduction and splinting in the emergency room, followed by further evaluation of her heart and brain by the medical department and she was fit for surgery.  Due to the comminuted nature of her tibia fracture as well as soft tissue swelling, the patient requires staged surgery with 1st stage external fixation followed by open treatment and definitive plate and screw fixation.  The patient had swollen lower extremities, but the compartments were soft and she had no signs of compartment syndrome.  The risks and benefits of the procedure were discussed in detail with the patient and her husband.  All questions were answered and consent was obtained.  Risks including blood vessel and nerve damage with painful neuroma or numbness, impaired limb function, loss of range of motion, need for additional surgery, infection, complex regional pain syndrome, and deep vein thrombosis have all been discussed as potential risks of surgery.,FINDINGS:,1.  There was a comminuted fracture of the distal tibia with a fracture of the fibular shaft.  After traction, the coronal and sagittal alignment of the fracture fragments was adequate and, based on the length of the fibula, the fracture fragments were too long.,2.  The base of his proximal phalanx fracture was evaluated and reduced with virtually no joint removal and approximately 1 mm of displacement.  As the reduction was stable with Buddy Taping, no pinning was performed.,3.  Her compartments were full, but not firm and she had no signs of compartment syndrome and no compartment release was performed.,OPERATIVE REPORT IN DETAIL: ,The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area.  The left leg was identified and marked at the patient's surgical site.  She was then taken to the operating room where she was transferred to the operating room in a supine position, placed under general anesthesia by the anesthesiology team.  She received Ancef for antibiotic prophylaxis.  A time-out was then conducted to verify the correct patient, correct limb, visibility of preoperative markings, availability of equipment, and administration of preoperative antibiotics.  When everything was checked by the surgeon, the anesthesia and circulation personnel of the left lower limb were prepared and draped in the usual manner.  At this point, intraoperative fluoroscopy was used to identify the fracture site as well as the appropriate starting point both in the calcaneus for a crossed transcalcaneal stent and in the proximal tibia taking care to allow sufficient room for subsequent plate fixation without contaminating the future surgical site.  A single centrally threaded transverse calcaneal tunnel was then placed through the calcaneus parallel to the articular surface, followed by placement of 2 Schantz pins in the tibia and a frame-type external fixator was then applied in traction with attempts to extend the fracture fragments to length, but without overly distracting the fracture and restoring coronal and sagittal alignment as much as possible.  When this was found to be adequate, the fixation appliance was locked in place and x-ray images were taken to verify proper placement.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Rupture of the right pectoralis major tendon.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Rupture of the right pectoralis major tendon.,OPERATION PERFORMED:, Open repair of the right pectoralis major tendon.,ANESTHESIA:, General with interscalene block.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,Needle and sponge counting was performed and correct.,INDICATION OF OPERATION:,The patient is a 26-year-old male, right hand dominant, working in sales, who was performing heavy bench press exercises when he felt a burning, tearing pain in his right shoulder.  The patient had minor bruising on the right side proximal arm, with radiograph showing no fracture.  Due to concerns about a torn pectoralis tendon, he was sent for an MRI evaluation where a complete rupture of part of the pectoralis major tendon was noted.  Due to the patient's young age and active lifestyle, surgical treatment was recommended in order to achieve the best result.  The risks and benefits of the procedure were discussed in detail with the patient, including scarring, infection, blood vessel and nerve damage, re-rupture, need for additional surgery, loss of range of motion, inability to return to strenuous activity such as weight lifting, usual complex pain syndrome and deep vein thrombosis as well as anesthetic risks.  Understanding all the risks and benefits, the patient wishes to proceed with the surgery as planned.,FINDINGS:,1.  After a deltopectoral approach to the right shoulder, the pectoralis major tendon was encountered.  The clavicular head was intact.  A complete rupture of the sternal head of the pectoralis major tendon was noted with an oblique type tear comprising a remaining cuff on the humerus and a tendon attached to the retracted part.,2.  After releasing the adhesions using tracks and sutures, the pectoralis major tendon was able to approach its insertion site on the humerus just lateral to the biceps.,3.  A soft tissue repair was performed with #5 FiberWire suture and a single 5 x 5 bioresorbable suture anchor was placed to decrease tension at the repair site.  After soft tissue repair and use of bone anchor, good apposition of the tendon with the edges and a strong repair was observed.,OPERATIVE REPORT IN DETAIL: ,The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area.  His right shoulder was identified, marked with its appropriate surgical site after verification with the patient.  He was then transported to the operating room where he was transferred to the operating table in a supine position and placed under general anesthesia by the anesthesiology team.  He then received prophylactic antibiotics.  A time-out was then conducted to verify the correct patient, the correct limb, the surgical procedure performed, the administration of antibiotics and the availability of equipment.  At this point the patient was placed in a modified position on a beech chair ensuring that all appropriate pressure points were padded and there was no pressure on the eyes.  The right upper limb was then prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  The preoperative markings were still visible at this stage.  A deltopectoral incision was made in the lower part.  The dissection was carried out.  The deltoid was retracted laterally.  The clavicular head of the pectoralis major was intact with the absence of sternal insertion.  A small margin of tissue remained on the proximal humerus associated with the clavicular head.  Gentle probing medially revealed the retracted sternal end, Ethibond #5 traction sutures were used to allow retraction and release of the slight adhesion.  This made it possible to bring the retracted tendon closer to the tendon stump.  At this point, a repair using FiberWire No. 5 was then performed from the pectoralis major tendon to the proximal humerus stump, noting good apposition of the tendon edges and no spacing of the repair site. transcription: DIAGNOSIS:,Low back pain and degenerative lumbar disc disease.,HISTORY:,The patient is a 59-year-old woman, who was referred for physical therapy, secondary to low back pain and degenerative disc disease.  The patient states that she underwent some sort of cauterization of the nerves in her lower back to help relieve the painful symptoms.  The patient states that this occurred in October 2008 as well as November 2008. The patient has a history of low back pain, secondary to a fall that initially occurred in 2006. The patient states that she slipped on a newly polished floor and fell on her coccyx and lumbar region.  The patient then had her second fall in March 2006. The patient states that she was qualifying at the shooting range with a handgun and lost her footing and states that she fell more due to weakness in her lower extremities rather than loss of balance.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Medical history is important for allergies and thyroid problems.,HISTORY SURGICAL:,The patient has a surgical history of appendectomy and hysterectomy.,MEDICATIONS:,1.  TriCor.,2.  Vytorin.,3.  Estradiol.,4.  Levothyroxine.,5.  The patient also occasionally takes 800 mg of ibuprofen as needed to manage pain.  The patient states that she rarely takes them and does not like to take painkillers whenever possible.  The patient states that she has had uncomplicated pregnancies in the past.,SOCIAL HISTORY: The patient states that she lives in a single-story house with her husband, who is in good health and is able to help her with any tasks or activities with which the patient has difficulty.  The patient considers her general health to be excellent, denies having smoked and reports very occasional alcohol consumption.  The patient specifies that she does daily exercises for an hour to an hour and a half per day.  However, he has been unable to complete these routine exercises since around June 2008, due to back pain.  The patient works full time as a project manager and is required to take long walks at different times of the work day.,MEDICAL IMAGING: The patient states that she recently had an MRI; however, results are not available at the time of evaluation.  The patient states that she is able to bring the report to the next visit., SUBJECTIVE: The patient rates her pain as 7/10 on an analog pain scale, 0 to 10, 10 being worst.  The patient describes her pain as deep, primarily in her right lower back and gluteal region.  Aggravating factors include stairs and prolonged driving, as well as general limitations in household tasks and projects.  The patient reports that she is a very active person and notices extreme limitations in her ability to complete household tasks and projects that she was capable of completing., NEUROLOGICAL SYMPTOMS: The patient reports having occasional shooting pains in her lower extremities.  However, these occur less frequently and now occur more frequently in the right lower extremity than in the left lower extremity when they occur.,FUNCTIONAL ACTIVITIES AND HOBBIES:,Include exercise and general activities.,PATIENT OBJECTIVE:,The patient wishes to improve her overall body movement and return to the daily exercise routine safely and maintaining her safety.,OBJECTIVE:,After observation, the patient moves independently without using assistive device.  However, he presents with mild lameness and favors the left lower extremity after prolonged standing and walking activity.  The patient has slight difficulty moving from a sitting to standing position.  However, once standing, the patient denies any increase in pain or symptoms.,Lumbar Spine ACTIVE MOVEMENT RANGE:,Forward flexion is 26 cm, fingertip to floor, lateral lateral bend, fingertip to floor is 52.5 cm bilaterally.,STRENGTH:, Strength is roughly 4/5.  The patient denies any significant tenderness to palpation.  However, there is a slight increase in sensitivity on the right.

transcription: HISTORY: We had the pleasure of seeing the patient today in our pediatric rheumatology clinic.  He was sent here with a chief complaint of joint pain in several joints for a few months.  This is a 7-year-old white male with no history of systemic illness, who until 2 months ago was doing well and 2 months ago he began complaining of pain in his fingers, elbows, and neck.  Right now it's better and it's almost gone, but for several months he was having pain to the point where he was crying at one point.  He is not a complainer according to his mother and he is a very active child.  There is no history of illness or gastrointestinal problems.  He has allergy problems, especially seasonal ones, and he takes Claritin for that.  Other than that, he had no other problems.  He denies any swelling, except for the swelling mentioned by the doctor in his elbow.  There is no history of rash, no stomach pain, no diarrhea, no fever, no weight loss, no ulcers in the mouth except canker sores.  No lymphadenopathy, no eye problems and no urinary problems.,MEDICATIONS: ,His medications consist only of Motrin only when needed and Claritin currently for seasonal allergies and rhinitis.,ALLERGIES: ,He has no allergies to any medications.,BIRTH HISTORY: ,Pregnancy and delivery without complications.  He has no history of hospitalization or surgery.,FAMILY HISTORY: Positive for arthritis in his grandmother.  No history of pediatric arthritis.  There is a history of psoriasis in his father.,SOCIAL HISTORY: He lives with mom, dad, brother, sister and everyone is healthy.  They live in Easton.  They have 4 dogs, 3 cats, 3 mules and no deer.  At school, he is in the second year and he does physical education without any limitations.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature is 98.7, pulse is 96, respiratory rate is 24, height is 118.1 cm, weight is 22.1 kg and blood pressure is 61/44.,GENERAL: He is alert, active, in no way distressed, very cooperative.,HEENT: He does not have a rash on his face.  No lymphadenopathy.  The oral mucosa is clear.  No tonsillitis.  His ear canals are clear and his pupils respond to light and accommodation.,CHEST: Clear to auscultation.,HEART: Regular rhythm and no murmurs.,ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, no visceromegaly.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Has no limitations in any of his joints or active swelling today.  He also has no feeling in any of his joints.  Muscle strength is 5/5 in proximal muscles.,LABORATORY DATA: Includes arthritis panel.  He has normal uric acid, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 2, rheumatoid factor of 6, and negative antinuclear antibodies and C-reactive protein of 7.1.  His mother stated that this was done while he was experiencing symptoms.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,This patient may have had reactive arthritis.  He is seen frequently and the patient has a family history of psoriatic arthritis or psoriasis.  I don't see any issues at this time on his labs or physical exam.  This may have been linked to a recent episode of viral infection or any infection.  Mother has been informed of the finding and my recommendation is to observe him and if there is a recurrence of symptoms or persistence of swelling or limitation in any of his joints, I will be happy to see him again. If you have any questions about further evaluation and plan, please do not hesitate to contact us.

transcription: DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Cervical dystonia.,2.  Post-cervical laminectomy pain syndrome. Ms. XYZ states that the pain has now shifted to the left side.  She noticed a marked improvement on the right side, which was injected with botulinum toxin about two weeks ago.  She has had no side effects from the Botox injection and feels her activities of daily living are increased, but she still takes oxycodone and methadone.  The patient's husband confirms that she is doing much better, is more active, but there are still issues with anxiety, depression, and frustration regarding neck pain.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,The patient is adequate.  She is well dressed and oriented x3.  She still smells of cigarette smoke.  Neck examination shows excellent reduction in muscle spasms of the right paraspinal, trapezius and splenius muscles of the head.  No trigger points are felt and the range of motion in his neck is still somewhat protected, but significantly improved.  On the left side, however, there are significant muscle spasms with tight bands involving the multifidus muscle with trigger point activity and lots of tenderness and guarding.  This extends to the trapezius muscle, but the splenius capitis does not appear to be involved.,TREATMENT PLAN: After a long discussion with the patient and her husband, we decided to go ahead and do a botulinum toxin injection into the left multifidus/trapezius muscles.  A total of 400 units of Botox are planned.  The procedure is being programmed.  The patient's medications are refilled.  She will continue to see Dr. Berry and continue her therapy with Mary Hotchkinson in Victoria.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Back pain and right leg pain.  The patient has a three-year history of small cell lung cancer with metastases.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is on my schedule today to explore treatment of the above complaints.  She has a two-year history of small cell lung cancer, which she believes has spread to metastasize to both femurs, lower lumbar spine and pelvis.  She says she has undergone numerous chemotherapy and radiation treatments and told me she has lost count.  She says she just completed a round of 10 radiation treatments to relieve pain.  She says she continues to have significant pain symptoms.  Most of his pain seems to be in his lower back on the right side, radiating from the back of his right leg to his knee.  She also has some numbness on the bottom of her left foot and sometimes sharp pain in her left foot.  She complains of diffuse pain in the middle of her back.  She describes the pain as sharp, dull and aching in nature.  She rates her back pain at 10, her right leg pain at 10, with 0 meaning no pain and 10 being the worst possible pain.  She states that it seems to be worse when sitting in the car, sitting, standing or walking for long periods of time.  She takes large doses of narcotics.  She had multiple CT scans looking for metastases.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:,Significant for cancer as above.  She also has depression.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, Important for placement of a chest port.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:, Consist of Duragesic patch 250 mcg total, Celebrex 200 mg once daily, Iron 240 mg twice daily, Paxil 20 mg daily, and Percocet.  She doesn't know how strong up to eight a day.  She also takes 1 mg of warfarin per day, which she says is simply to maintain her chest port certification.  She takes Neurontin 300 mg three times a day.,HABITS: She has smoked a pack a day for 30 years.  She drinks beer about twice a day.  She denies using recreational drugs. SOCIAL HISTORY: She is married.  She lives with her partner.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Important for two brothers and a father with cancer.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS:, Important mainly for her complaints of pain.  For further review of systems, patient appears stable.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General: Reveals a pleasant, somewhat emaciated Caucasian female.,Vital Signs: Height is 5 feet 2 inches.  Weight is 130 pounds.  She is afebrile.,HEENT: benign.,Neck: shows functional range of motion with negative Spurling.,Chest: clear on auscultation.,Heart: regular rate and rhythm.,Abdomen: soft and regular bowel sounds.,Musculoskeletal: examination shows functional range of joint motion.  No focal muscle weakness.  She is deconditioned. Neurological: She is alert and oriented with appropriate mood and affect.  The patient has normal tone and coordination.  Reflexes are 2+ in both knees and absent in both ankles.  Sensation is diminished distally in the left foot, otherwise intact up to the pinprick. Spine: Examination of his lumbar spine shows normal lumbar lordosis with a fairly functional range of motion.  The patient had significant tenderness in the lower lumbar facet and sacroiliac joints, which appears to replicate many of her lower back and right leg complaints.,FUNCTIONAL EXAMINATION: ,Gait has a normal stance and swing phase with no analgesic component.,INVESTIGATION: ,She again underwent multiple scans, including a full body bone scan, which showed abnormal fixation involving the femurs bilaterally.  She had bilateral increased uptake in the sacroiliac joint regions.  Chest CT showed no evidence of recurrent metastatic disease.  CT scan of the abdomen showed no evidence of metastatic disease.  MRI of the lower hip joints showed heterogeneous spinal cord signal in both proximal femurs.  The CT scan of the pelvis showed a trabecular appearance with healed metastases.  CT scan of the orbits showed a small amount of fluid in the mastoid cells on the right, otherwise normal CT scan.  Brain MRI showed no acute intracranial abnormalities

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Dislocation by fracture, C2.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Dislocation by fracture, C2.,OPERATION PERFORMED,1.  Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of a comminuted C2 fracture.,2.  Posterior spinal instrumentation C1-C3, using the Synthes system.,3.  Posterior cervical fusion C1-C3.,4.  Insertion of a morselized allograft at C1 to C3.,ANESTHESIA:, GETA.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, 100 ml.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,DRAINAGES:, Hemovac x1.,Spinal cord monitoring is stable throughout the case.,DISPOSITION:, Vital signs are stable, extubated and returned to the ICU in satisfactory condition and stable.,INDICATIONS FOR OPERATION:The patient is a middle-aged female, who had a significantly displaced comminuted C2 fracture.  This is secondary to a car accident and has been appropriately translated as 1 cm.  The risks and benefits have been conferred on the patient as well as the family, they wish to continue.  The patient was taken to the operating room for C1-C3 posterior cervical fusion, instrumentation, open reduction and internal fixation.,THE OPERATION IN DETAIL: After obtaining appropriate consent from the patient, the patient was taken back to operating room #5.  The patient was placed in the usual supine position and intubated and under general anesthesia without any difficulty.  Monitoring of the spinal cord was induced.  No changes were observed from the beginning to the end of the case. The Mayfield languages ​​have been placed appropriately.  This was placed in line with the pinna of the ear as well as one cm above the tip of the earlobes.  The patient was then rolled onto the OSI fluoroscopic table into the usual supine position with chest rollers.  The patient's Mayfield tongue was fixed in the usual standard manner.  The patient was then prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  The midline incision was extended from the base of the skull to the C4 spinous process.  Full thickness cutaneous fascia developed.  The fascia was incised at the midline and the posterior elements at C1, C2, C3 as well as the lower surface of the occiput were exposed.  Intraoperative radiography confirmed that the level was C2. Translaminar screws were placed bilaterally at C2.  The trajectory was completed with a hand drill and probed in all four quadrants to ensure there were no violations of the pedicles. Once this was done, two 3.5 mm translaminar screws were placed bilaterally at C2.  Good placement was observed in both the AP and lateral planes by fluoroscopy.  Facet screws were then placed at C3.  Using the standard Magerl technique, from the infero-medial quadrant, trajectories of 14 mm in the caudad-cephalic direction of 25 degrees as well as 25 degrees in the lateral-medial direction were made.  This sound was then played in all four quadrants to ensure that there was no elevation of the trajectory.  14 x 3.5mm screws were then placed appropriately.  The side controls at the level of the C1 plate have been placed appropriately.  The medial and lateral boundaries were demarcated by a Penfield.  The greater occipital nerve was retracted.  The starting point was made with a high-speed electric burr and a medial and lateral mass bilaterally.  Using a 20 degree caudad-cephalic trajectory as well as a 10 degree lateral to medial direction, the trajectory was completed in 8 mm increments and then probed in all four quadrants to ensure there was no violation of the pedicle wall of the trajectory.  Once this was done, 24 smooth 3.5mm Schanz screws were placed appropriately.  Pre-contoured titanium rods were then placed between the screws at levels C1, C2, C3 and the castings were placed appropriately.  Once this was done, all the bits were properly tightened.  This completed the open reduction and internal fixation of the C2 fracture, which showed perfect alignment.  It should be noted that the reduction was partially performed on the table by lateral fluoroscopy before instrumentation, almost reducing the dosage.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Open, displaced and infected left atrophic mandibular fracture; Failing dental implant.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Open, displaced and infected left atrophic mandibular fracture; failed dental implant.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of left atrophic mandibular fracture, removal of failed dental implant from left mandible.,ANESTHESIA:, general nasotracheal.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, 125 ml.,FLUIDS ADMINISTERED:, 1 L of crystalloids.,SAMPLE: , Soft tissues from the fracture site sent for histological diagnosis.,CULTURES: ,Also sent for Gram stain, aerobic and anaerobic, culture and sensitivity.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS: ,The patient is a 79-year-old man, who fell in his hometown, following an episode of syncope.  He suffered blunt force trauma to his ribs which resulted in multiple fractures and likely also struck his mandible, resulting in the fracture mentioned above.  He was admitted to the hospital in Harleton, Texas, where his initial evaluation showed that the rib fractures also included a nodule on his right upper lobe as well as a mediastinal mass.  His fracture of the mandible was not initially noted.  The patient also has a history of prostate cancer and renal cell carcinoma.  The patient then underwent bronchoscopy with a biopsy of the mediastinal mass and the results of this biopsy are still awaited.  The patient then consulted a local oral surgeon.  He diagnosed her fractured mandible and advised her to seek treatment in Houston.  He presented to my office for evaluation on January 18, 2010 and was found to have an extremely atrophic mandible with a fracture in the left parasymphysis region involving a failed dental implant, which had been placed approximately 15 years ago.  The patient felt significant discomfort and could hardly eat food and drink liquids.  Due to the nature of his fracture and complex medical history, he was sent to the hospital for admission and after cardiac clearance, he was scheduled to undergo surgery today.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position.  After nasal intubation and induction of general anesthesia, the surgeon then scrubbed, dressed and gloved in a normal sterile manner.  The patient was then prepared and draped in a manner consistent with sterile procedures.  A marker pen was first used to outline the incision in the submental region and it was extended from the left mandibular body to the right mandibular body region, approximately 1.5 cm medial to the lower border of the mandible.  One ml of 1% lidocaine with epinephrine 1:100,000 was then infiltrated along the incision, and then a 15-bladed blade was used to incise the skin and subcutaneous tissue.  A combination of sharp and blunt dissections was then used to perform the dissection upward to the lower border of the mandible.  Electrocautery along with 4.0 silk ties were used for hemostasis.  A 15-blade blade was then used to incise the periosteum along the lower border of the mandible and it was reflected, exposing the mandible as well as the fracture site.  The fracture site was slightly distracted, allowing access to the dental implant inside the bone and it was easily removed from the wound.  Cultures from this site were also obtained, and then wound granulation tissue was also removed from the wound by curettage and sent for histologic diagnosis.  Manipulation of the mandible was then used to achieve an anatomic reduction, and then a Synthes 11-hole reconstruction plate was then used to place over the fracture site.  Since there was an area of ​​weakness in the right parasymphyseal region, at the site of another dental implant, the bone plate was extended posterior to this site.  Once the plate was fitted to the mandible, it was then fixed to the bone with 9 screws of 2 mm diameter each and each screw was placed bicortically.  All screws were also locking screws.  Next pla

transcription: TITLE OF OPERATION: 1. Open reduction internal fixation (ORIF) with irrigation and debridement of open fractures including skin, muscle and bone using one Synthes 3.5mm locking plate on the lateral malleolus and two 4.5mm Synthes cannulated screws on the medial malleolus.,2.  Closed reduction and screw fixation of a right femoral neck fracture using one 8.0 mm Asnis cannulated screw and two 6.5 mm cannulated screws.,3.  Retrograde Femoral Nail using a Striker T2 10 x 340 Retrograde Nail with a 10mm INCAP and two 5mm distal locking screws and two 5mm proximal locking screws.,4.  Irrigation and debridement of the right knee.,5.  Irrigation and debridement of right elbow abrasions.,DIAGNOSIS PRE-OPERATION:,1.  Right open ankle fracture.,2. NARRATIVE: The patient is a 53-year-old woman who was a pedestrian struck in a motor vehicle accident and suffered numerous injuries.  She suffered a compound fracture of her right ankle, a fracture of her right femur, a fracture of her right femoral neck, a split right knee and abrasions to her right elbow.  Given the emergent nature of the fracture of the right femoral neck and its young age as well as the open fracture, it was decided to proceed with urgent operative intervention.  The risks of surgery were discussed in detail and consents were signed.  The surgical site was marked.  The patient was taken to the operating room where she received clindamycin preoperatively.  The patient then underwent general anesthesia performed by anesthesia. A well-padded lateral tourniquet was placed.  Attention first turned to the right ankle.  The large medical laceration was extended and tissue was debrided.  All injured bones, muscles and tissues were debrided.  The wound was then abundantly irrigated with 8 liters of normal saline.  At this point, the medial malleolus fracture was identified and reduced.  This was then fixed with two 4.5 mm cannulated Synthes screws. Next, attention was focused on the lateral malleolus.  An incision was made on the distal fibula.  It was strongly transported through the skin to subcutaneous areas.  Precautions were taken to preserve the superficial peroneal nerve.  The fracture was identified and a highly comminuted fracture of the distal fibula was noted.  The fracture was reduced and confirmed by fluoroscopy.  A Synthes 3.5mm 7-hole locking plate was placed.  This was placed as a bridge with three screws above and three screws below the fracture.  Appropriate reduction was confirmed under fluoroscopy.  A cotton ball test was performed and the ankle did not open.  Therefore, it was decided not to proceed with the syndesmotic screw. Then the patient was placed on the fracture table and all extremities were well padded.  All the protuberances were padded.  The right leg was then prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A 2 cm incision was made just distal to the greater trochanter.  This was transported abruptly through the skin to the fascia.  The femur has been identified.  The guide wire for a 6.5mm Striker Asnis screw has been placed in the appropriate position.  The triangular guide was then used to ensure proper triangular formation of the remainder of the screws.  A reduction of the fracture was performed before placement of all the guide wires.  A single 8mm Asnis screw was placed at the bottom, followed by two 6.5mm screws at the top. Then, the abrasions on the right elbow were abundantly irrigated.  Necrotic and dead tissue was removed.  The one

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Avulsion fracture of the left tibial tubercle.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Comminuted avulsion fracture of the left tibial tubercle with intra-articular extension.,PROCEDURE:, Open reduction and internal fixation of the left tibia.,ANESTHESIA:, General.  Patient received 10ml of 0.5% marcaine local anesthetic.,GUARANTEE DURATION: 80 minutes.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: minimal.,DRAINAGES: ,A JP drain was placed.,COMPLICATIONS: ,No complications or intraoperative samples.  The hardware consisted of two K 4-5 wires, a 6.5, 60 mm partially threaded cancellous screw and a 45, 60 mm partially threaded cortical screw and 2 washers.,HISTORY AND PHYSICS:,The patient is a 14 year old male who reported having knee pain for 1 month.  Apparently, while playing basketball on 12/22/2007, while jumping, he felt a pop in his knee.  The patient was seen at an outside facility where he was fitted with a splint and was then referred to Children's for definitive care.  Radiographs confirmed a comminuted tibial tubercle avulsion fracture with high patella.  Surgical intervention is recommended to the grandmother and then to the father by telephone.  Surgery would consist of open reduction and internal fixation with the subsequent need for further hardware removal.  Surgical risks include the risks of anesthesia, infection, bleeding, changes in sensation in most limbs, hardware failure, need for subsequent hardware removal, inability to restore tension to the extensor mechanism, and need for postoperative rehabilitation.  All questions were answered and the father and grandmother agreed to the above plan.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating table.  General anesthesia was then administered.  The patient received Ancef preoperatively.  A nonsterile tourniquet was placed on the patient's upper left thigh.  The patient's extremity was then prepared and draped using the standard surgical method.  A midline incision was marked on the skin extending from the tibial tubercle proximally and the extremities wrapped in Esmarch.  Ultimately, the patient had a tourniquet that was running at 75 mmHg.  Esmarch was then expelled.  The incision was then made.  The patient presented with a significant tear of the posterior retinaculum medially with proximal migration of the tibial tubercle located in the joint, there was significant comminution and intra-articular involvement.  We were able to see the underside of the anterior horn of the medial and lateral meniscus.  The intra-articular cartilage was restored using two 45 K pins. The final position was verified by fluoroscopy and the corners were buried in the cartilage.  There was a large free-floating metaphyseal piece that included portions of the proximal tibial physis.  This was replaced in an anatomical location and fixed using a 45 cortical screw with washer.  The avulsed fragment with the patellar tendon was then fixed distally to this area using a 6.5, 60 mm cancellous screw with washer.  The cortical screw did not provide good compression and fixation at this distal fragment.  The retinaculum was repaired best using 0 Vicryl suture.  The hematoma was evacuated at the beginning and end of the case.  The knee was abundantly irrigated with physiological serum.  The subcutaneous tissue was reapproximated with Vicryl 2-0 and the skin with Monocryl 4-0.  The wound was cleaned, dried and dressed with Steri-Strips, Xeroform and 4x4.  The tourniquet was released after 80 minutes.  The JP drain was placed on the middle gutter.  The extremity was then wrapped in an Ace wrap from the proximal thigh to the toes.  The patient was then placed in a knee mobilizer.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.  Subsequently, he was extubated and taken to recovery in stable condition.,POSTOP PLAN:The patient was hospitalized overnight to decrease swelling and manage his pain.  It can support weight as tolerated using a knee brace.  The postoperative results relayed to the

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Displaced intra-articular fracture, right distal radius.,2.  Right carpal tunnel syndrome.,PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Displaced intra-articular fracture, right distal radius.,2.  Right carpal tunnel syndrome.,OPERATIONS PERFORMED:,1.  Open reduction and internal fixation of a right distal radius fracture - four-piece intra-articular fracture.,2.  Right Carpal Tunnel Release.,ANESTHESIA:,General.,CLINICAL SUMMARY:,The patient is a 37-year-old right dominant Hispanic female who suffered a severe right wrist fracture approximately one week ago.  This was a four-part intra-articular fracture displaced dorsally.  Additionally, the patient had previously undergone carpal tunnel release but had carpal tunnel pre-operation symptoms.  She is admitted for reconstructive surgery.  Carpal tunnel symptoms were present before surgery and worsened after the injury.,OPERATION:The patient was brought from the outpatient unit and placed on the operating table in a supine position and administered general anesthesia by anesthesia.  Once adequate anesthesia was obtained, the right upper extremity was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A tourniquet was placed around the right upper limb.  The upper limb was then elevated and bled using an Esmarch dressing.  The tourniquet was raised to 250 mmHg.  The entire operation was carried out with a magnification of 4.5 loops.  At this time, an approximately 8 cm longitudinal incision was then made above the right flexor carpi radialis tendon, from the flexion crease to the wrist proximally.  This was transported to the flexor carpi radialis, which was then retracted ulnarly.  The floor of the flexor carpi radialis was then incised, exposing the pronator flexor muscles.  The flexor pollicis longus was retracted ulnarly and the pronator quadratus was incised longitudinally 1 cm from its origin.  It was then elevated from the fracture site, exposing the fracture site, which was displaced dorsally.  It was a four-part intra-articular fracture.  Under image control, both palmar and dorsal pieces were then carefully manipulated and reduced.  Next, two inch 2.06 inch K wires were drilled radially into the volar ulnar piece, and then a second K wire was then drilled from the dorsal radial to the dorsal ulnar piece.  A third K pin was then drilled from the palmar radial to the dorsal ulnar piece.  The fracture was then manipulated.  The ends of the fracture were abundantly irrigated with normal saline and curetted, and then the fracture was reduced in the usual manner by recreating the defect and distracting it.  Additional K wires were then placed through the radial styloid into the proximal fragment.  A full-sized Hand Innovations DVR plate for the right wrist was then shaped and placed over the distal radius and secured with two K-wires. At this time, the distal screws were then placed.  The distal screws were the small screws.  These were non-locking screws, all eight screws were placed.  They were placed in the usual way by drilling with a small drill, removing the small introducers and then using its depth.  Again, these were 18-20mm screws.  After placing three of the screws, the K wires had to be removed. There was excellent reduction of the fragments and fracture; excellent reduction of the intra-articular component and the fracture.  After the distal screws were placed, the fracture was reduced and held in place with K-wires, which were replaced and the proximal screws were drilled with the larger drill guide and drill bit.  The screws were then placed.  They were 12mm screws.  There were 4 of them placed. The K wires were then removed.  Finally, a 3 cm intrathenar incision was made from 1 cm distal to the wrist flexor crease. transcription: CC: ,Bilateral lower extremity numbness.,HX: ,21 years old RHM complained of progressive numbness and incoordination of both lower extremities starting around 5/11/96. Symptoms peaked over a 12-24 hour period and have not changed since. Symptoms consist of tingling sensations in the distal lower extremities, approximately mid-calf height, bilaterally. He noted a decrease in coordination of both lower extremities, which he thought might be due to uncertainty about where his feet were placed in space. He denied having any bowel/bladder problems, or weakness or numbness elsewhere. Hot showers may improve his symptoms. He has not suffered from any recent flu-like illness. Medical and family history is unremarkable. He was not taking any medications.,EXAMINATION:, Nothing abnormal except for slight loss of distal vibratory sensation in the toes (R>L).,LAB:, CBC, Gen Screen, TSH, FT4, SPE, ANA were all WNL.,L-SPINE MRI:, Normal.,COURSE:, Normal examination and decrease in symptoms at next visit 4/23/93.

transcription: CC:, BLE, weakness and numbness., HX:, This 59-year-old RHM was seen and released from an emergency room 1 week prior to this presentation for a 3-week history of progressive sensory and motor deficits in both lower extremities. He reported numbness beginning in the trunk and slowly progressing to the lower extremities over a 4-week period. On presentation he felt numb from the nipple line onwards. Additionally, he began experiencing progressive weakness in his lower extremities over the past week. He began using a cane 5 days before being seen and had difficulty walking and climbing stairs. He claimed he couldn't stand. He denied losing control of his bowels or bladder. However, he had not had a bowel movement in 3 days and he had not urinated in 24 hours. His lower limbs had been cold for a day. He denied any associated back or neck pain. He suffers from chronic shortness of breath, but he feels like his situation has gotten worse. He had also experienced dizziness when getting up more easily than usual for 2 days prior to presentation., PMH:, 1) CAD with chronic CP, 2) NQWMI 1994, S/P Coronary Angioplasty, 3) COPD (previous FEV 11.48 and FVC 2.13), 4) D/O Anxiety, 5) DJD, 6) Confusion developed with use of the metoprolol, 7) HTN., MEDS:, Benadryl, ECASA, Diltiazem, Isordil, Enalapril, Indomethacin, Terbutaline MDI, Ipratropium MDI, Folic Acid, Thiamine., SHX:, 120 pk-yr smoking, ETOH abuse in the past, Dock Hand retired, FHX:, unremarkable except for ETOH abuse, EXAM:, T98.2 96 bpm 140/74 mmHg R18,Thin, cachetic man in moderate distress.,MS: A&O on person, place and time. Speech was fluent and without dysarthria. Comprehension, naming, and reading were intact.,CN: unremarkable.,Motor: full strength in both upper extremities.,HF HE HDd HAbd KF KE AF AE,RLE 3 3 4 4 3 4 1 1,LLE 4 4 4+ 4+ 4+ 4 4 4,There was mild spastic muscle tone in the lower extremities. There was normal muscle mass throughout.,SENSORY: Decreased PP in the LLE from the foot to the nipple line and in the RLE from the knee to the nipple line. Decrease in the sensation of temperature of the feet up to the umbilicus, bilaterally. No loss of Vibration or Proprioception. Decreased light contact of the feet up to the nipple line, bilaterally.,Gait: Unable to walk. Supports with support only.,Station: no pronator drift or truncal ataxia.,Reflexes: 2+/2+ in BUE, 3+/3+ kneecaps, 0/1 ankles. Babinski signs were present bilaterally. Abdominal reflexes were absent. CV: RRR with a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left sternal border. Lungs: CTA with slightly labored breathing. Abdomen: NT, ND, NBS, but bladder distended. The extremities were cold to the touch. Peripheral pulses were intact and capillary refill was rapid. Rectal: decreased rectal tone and absence of anal reflex. Right prostatic nodule at the lower pole.,COURSE:,Intake labs: FEV1=1.17, FVC 2.19, ABG 7.39/42/79 on room air. WBC 10/5, Hgb 13, Hct 39, electrolytes were normal. PT and PTT were normal. Direct catheterization revealed a residual volume of 400 cc of urine. He underwent an emergent MRI of the T spine. This revealed a T3-4 vertebral body lesion that had invaded the spinal canal and was compressing the spinal cord. He was treated with Decadron and underwent emergent spinal cord decompression on 5/7/95. He regained some lower limb strength after the operation. Pathological analysis of the tumor was compatible with adenocarcinoma. Her primary tumor was not localized despite a thoracic-abdominal-pelvic CT scan and gastrointestinal and gingival workup including cystoscopy and endoscopy. He received 3,000 cGy of XRT and died 5 months after presentation.

transcription: CC:, BLE weakness.,HX:, This 82-year-old RHM was referred to the Neurology department by the Neurosurgery department for evaluation of acute paraplegia. He was in his usual healthy state until 5:30 p.m. on 4/6/95, when he developed sudden "pressure-like" epigastric discomfort associated with bilateral lower extremity weakness, SOB, dizziness, and sweating. He knelt on the ground and “fell asleep.” Emergency medical services were alert and arrived a few minutes later. He was then easily awakened, but unable to move or palpate his lower extremities. No associated upper limb or bulbar dysfunction was noted. He was taken to a local hospital where his INR was 9.1. He received 15 mg of vitamin K and was transferred to UIHC to rule out spinal epidural hemorrhage. A T-spine MRI was obtained and the preliminary reading was “normal.” The neurology department was then invited to evaluate the patient.,MEDS:, Coumadin 2 mg qd, Digoxin 0.25 mg qd, Prazosin 2 mg qd.,PMH:, 1)HTN. 2) A-Fib on coumadin. 3) Peripheral vascular disease: left s/p femoropopliteal bypass (8/94) and graft thrombosis-thrombosis (9/94). 4) Prostate adenocarcinoma: s/p TURP (1992).,FHX:, nothing remarkable.,SHX:, farmer, married, no tobacco/ETOH/illicit drug use.,EXAMINATION:, BP165/60 HR86 RR18 34.2C SAO2 98% on room air.,MS: A&O on person, place, time. In no acute distress. Lucid.,CN: banal.,ENGINE: strength 5/5 in BUE. Flaccid paraplegia in BLE, Sensory: sensory level T6 to LT/PP, bilaterally. Decreased vibrational sense in the BLE in a distribution of low, distally.,Coord: FNF and RAM intact in the BUE. Unable to do HKS.,Station: no pronator drift.,Gait: not performed.,Reflexes: 2/2 BUE, Absent in BLE, plantar responses were flexing, bilaterally.,Rectal: decreased rectal tone.,EXAMINATION GEN: No carotid sounds. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. CV: irregular rate and rhythm with slight diastolic murmur at left sternal border. Abdomen: flat, soft, non-tender, without noisy or pulsating masses. Distal pulses were strong in all extremities., COURSE:, Hgb 12.6, Hct 40%, WBC 11.7, Plt 154k, INR 7.6, PTT 50, CK 41, GS was normal. ECG showed A-Fib at 75 BPM with competing junctional pacemaker, virtually unchanged from 9/12/94. The patient was suspected to have suffered an anterior-cervico-thoracic spinal cord infarction with resulting paraplegia and T6 sensory level. An X-ray radiograph was performed in the emergency department before admission. This revealed cardiomegaly and an enlarged mediastinum. He returned from the x-ray room and suddenly became unconscious and suffered a cardiopulmonary arrest. Resuscitation measures failed. Pericardiocentesis was unremarkable. Autopsy revealed massive aortic dissection extending from the aortic root to the origin of the iliac arteries with extensive pericardial hematoma. The dissection was retrospectively observed on the T-spine on MRI.

transcription: CC: ,Paraplegia.,HX:, This 32-year-old RHF had been doing missionary work in Jos, Nigeria, for several years and gave birth to her 4th child vaginally on 04/10/97. Delivery was induced with Pitocin, but was otherwise uneventful. During the first 4 days postpartum, she noted clear watery diarrhea without blood and mild abdominal discomfort. This spontaneous situation resolved without medical treatment. The second week after delivery, she had 4-5 days of sinusitis, purulent nasal discharge and facial pain. She was otherwise well until 5/4/97, when she was stationed in a more rural area of ​​Nigeria, she noted a dull pain in both knees (lateral to the patella) and in the proximal tibia, bilaterally. The pain was not relieved by massage and seemed more bothersome when sitting or lying down. She had no sensory loss at that time. On 05/06/97, she woke up with pain radiating from her knees to her anterior tibia. Over the next few hours, the pain radiated circumferentially around both calves and affected the soles of his feet and posterior BLE to his buttocks. Getting out of bed became a laborious task and she needed help getting to the bathroom. Ibuprofen provided minimal analgesia. In the evening, the sole of one foot was numb. She woke up the next morning, 5/9/97, with a “tingling” sensation in the BLE all the way to her buttocks. She was given Darvocet for analgesia and took a plane to the larger city in which she was based. Over the course of the hour-long flight, her BLE weakness progressed to a non-weight-bearing state (i.e., she could no longer stand). Local assessment revealed BLE weakness proximal 3/3 and distal 4/4. She had a sensory level up to her waist on PP and LT tests. She also suffered from mild lower back pain. Local laboratory assessment: WBC 12.7, ESR 10. She was presumed to have Guillain-Barré syndrome and was placed on Solu-Cortef 1000 mg qd and Sandimmune IV IgG 12.0 g. On 05/10/97 she was flown to Geneva, Switzerland. Upon arrival, she underwent total anesthesia from the feet to the inguinal region, bilaterally. There was flaccid areflexic paralysis from BLE and she was unable to urinate or defecate. Direct catheterization of the bladder revealed a residual volume of 1000 cc. 05/12/CSF analysis revealed: Protein 1.5 g/l, Glucose 2.2 mmol/l, WBC 92 (O PMNS, 100% lymphocytes), RBC 70, Clear CSF, bacterial-fungal-AFB cultures were negative. Broad-spectrum antibiotics and Solu-Medrol 1 g IV qd were started. MRI of T-L spine, 05/12/97, revealed a T12-L1 intradural lesion that improved slightly with gadolinium and was associated with spinal cord edema in the affected area. Brain MRI on 5/12/97 was unremarkable and showed no signs of demyelinating disease. HIV, HTLV-1, HSV, Lyme, EBV, Malaria and CMV serological titers were negative. On 05/15/97, the IFAT serological titer for Schistosomiasis Mekongi came back positive at 1:320 (normal <1:80). 12/05/97 Schistosomiasis Mekongi of the CSF The IFAT and ELISA tests were negative. She then received a 1-day course of Praziquantel, 3.6 g total in 3 doses; and started taking Prednisone 60 mg po qd; broad-spectrum antibiotics and Solu-Medrol were discontinued. On 05/22/97, a rectal biopsy was performed to assess parasite eradication. The result came back positive for eggs and granulomas after he left for UIHC. The organization was not specified. 05/22/97 CSF schistosomiasis ELISA and IFAT titers were positive at 1.09 and 1:160, respectively. These titles were not known when he arrived at UIHC. After administration of Praziquantel, she regained some BLE sensations but the paraplegia and urinary retention remained. 1)G4P4.,FHX:, mundane.,SHX:,Missionary. transcription: EXAMINATION:,MRI RIGHT KNEE WITHOUT GADOLINIUM,CLINICAL:,This is a 21-year-old man suffering from right knee pain following a twisting injury on 07/31/05. The patient previously underwent a lateral meniscectomy in 2001.,FINDINGS:,Examination was performed on 3/8/05,Normal medial meniscus with no intra-substance degeneration, surface fraying, or discrete meniscal tear.,There is a subtle irregularity along the upper and lower articular surfaces of the lateral meniscus, likely reflecting prior partial meniscectomy and contouring, although a subtle superficial tear cannot be excluded, particularly along the lower surface of the lateral meniscectomy. lateral meniscus (series no. 3, image no. 17). There is no displaced tear or displaced meniscal fragment.,There is a mild interstitial sprain of the anterior cruciate ligament with no focal tear or discontinuity.,Normal posterior cruciate ligament.,Normal medial collateral ligament.,There is strain of the popliteus muscle and tendon without a complete tear.,There is a sprain of the posterolateral and posterocentral joint capsule (series #5 images #10-18). There is bone marrow edema in the posterolateral corner of the tibia and linear signal adjacent to the cortex suggesting that there may be a Segond fracture for which correlation with radiographs is recommended (Series #6, Images #4-7). The biceps femoris tendon and iliotibial band are intact and there is no discrete tear of the fibular collateral ligament. Normal quadriceps and patellar tendons. There is contusion at the posterior non-weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle, as well as at the posteromedial corner of the tibia. There is a linear signal oriented vertically within the distal tibial diaphyseal-metaphyseal junction (series no. 7, image no. 8; series no. 2, images no. 4-5). There is no discrete fracture line, which is of uncertain significance, but this must be correlated with radiographs. The patellofemoral joint is congruent without patellar tilt or subluxation. Normal medial and lateral patellar retinacula. There is a joint effusion.,IMPRESSION:,Changes within the lateral meniscus most likely reflect prior partial meniscectomy and remodeling, although a subtle subsurface tear of the anterior horn may be present.,Mild interstitial sprain of the anterior cruciate ligament.,There is strain of the popliteus muscle and tendon and there is a sprain of the posterolateral and posterocentral joint capsule with a possible second fracture which should be correlated with radiographs.

The peroneus longus tendon can be identified in its short axis extending to its distal plantar insertion at the base of the first metatarsal with minimal synovitis.,There is minimal synovial fluid in the tendon sheath of the flexor digitorum longus and flexor hallucis longus with fluid accumulation in the area of Henry's knot.,There is edema extending along the deep surface of the extensor digitorum brevis muscle. toes.,Normal anterior, subtalar and deltoid ligament complex.,Normal naviculocuneiform, intercuneiform and tarsometatarsal joints.,The Lisfranc ligament is intact.,The Achilles tendon insertion has been excluded from the field of view.,Normal plantar fascia and intrinsic plantar muscles of the foot.,There is slight venous distension of the foot veins in the tarsal tunnel.,There is a minimal edema. of the sinus tarsus. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Lumbar stenosis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Lumbar stenosis and cerebrospinal fluid fistula.,TITLE OF OPERATION,1.  Lumbar laminectomy for decompression with microtechniques foraminotomies L3-L4, L4-L5, L5-S1.,2.  CSF fistula repair, L5-S1 microtechniques, application of DuraSeal.,INDICATIONS:,The patient is an 82-year-old woman who has been suffering from approximately four months of urinary incontinence and numbness in her legs and hands, and difficulty moving around.  She was evaluated with an MRI, which showed very high grade stenosis in her lumbar spine, and subsequent evaluation included a myelogram, which demonstrated cervical stenosis at C4-C5, C5-C6, and C6-C7 as well as complete contrast block at L4-L5 and no contrast either at L5-S1 and stenosis at L3-L4 and all the way up, but worse at L3-L4, L4-L5 and L5-S1.  Yesterday she had an anterior cervical discectomy and C4-C5, C5-C6, C6-C7 fusions and saw some improvement in her symptoms and an increase in her strength, even in the recovery room.  She was kept in the ICU due to her age and the need to take her back to the operating room today for a decompressive lumbar laminectomy.  The reason she had surgery is close to the fact that she is normally on Coumadin for atrial fibrillation, although she was cardioverted.  She and her son understand the nature, indications, and risks of the operation and have agreed to move forward.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was brought from the Neuro ICU to the operating room, where general endotracheal anesthesia was obtained.  She was rolled into a prone position on the Wilson frame.  The back was prepared in the usual manner with a Betadine soak, followed by Betadine paint.  Markings have been applied.  Sterile fields were applied.  Using the usual anatomical landmarks, a linear midline incision was made on L4-L5 and L5-S1.  A sharp dissection was performed into the subcutaneous tissue, and then a Bovie electrocautery was used to isolate the spinous processes.  A Kocher forceps was placed in the anterior spinous ligament and was found to be L5-S1.  The incision was extended rostrally and deep Gelpis were inserted to expose the spinous processes and lamina of L3, L4, L5, and S1.  Using the Leksell rongeur, the spinous processes of L4 and L5 were completely removed, as well as the caudal portion of L3.  A high-speed drill was then used to thin the caudal lamina of L3, the entire lamina of L4, and L5.  Then, using various Kerrison punches, I performed a laminectomy.  By removing the L5 blade, a dural band was attached to the ligamentum flavum, which caused an approximately 3 mm tear in the dura mater.  There was an LCS leak.  Blade ablation was continued, the ligamentum flavum was removed to expose the entire dura mater.  Then, using 4-0 Nurolon suture, a continuous locking suture was used to close the approximately 3 mm long dural fistula.  There was no CSF ​​leak with Valsalva. I then continued the laminectomy by removing the entire lamina of L5 and L4, removing the ligamentum flavum between L3-L4, L4-L5 and L5-S1.  The foraminotomies were performed bilaterally.  The caudal aspect of the L3 blade was also removed.  The dura mater went pretty well.  I explored along the L4, L5 and S1 nerve roots after completing the foraminotomies, the roots were quite free.  Additionally, the thecal sac came out quite well.  To ensure no CSF ​​leak, we would follow the patient out of the operating room.  The dural closure was covered with a small piece of fat.  Everything was then covered with DuraSeal glue.  Gelfoam was placed on top, then the muscle was closed with interrupted 0 Ethibond.  The lumbodorsal fascia was closed tightly with multiple interrupted Ethibond 0 sutures.  Scarpa's fascia was closed with a running Vicryl 0, and finally the skin was closed with a running locking nylon 3-0.  The wound was blocked with 0.5% plain marcaine.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: The estimated blood loss for this case was approximately 100 ml.

transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,Lumbar puncture.,The procedure, benefits, risks, including possible risks of infection, were explained to the patient and his father, who signed the consent form.  Alternatives were explained.  They agreed to perform the lumbar puncture.  The permit has been signed and appears on the map.  The indication was to exclude toxoplasmosis or any other CNS infection. ,DESCRIPTION:, The area was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  Lidocaine 1% 5 mL was applied to the L3-L4 spinal space after the area was prepared three times with Betadine.  A 20-gauge spinal needle was then inserted into the L3-L4 space.  The attempt was successful on the first try and several ml of clear, colorless CSF were obtained.  The spinal needle was then removed and the area cleaned and dried and a dressing applied to the clean and dry area.,COMPLICATIONS: None.  The patient was resting comfortably and tolerating the procedure well.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:,None.,LAYOUT:,The patient was resting comfortably with effortless breathing and the incision was clean, dry, and intact.  Labs and cultures were sent for the usual tests in addition to a few additional tests that had been ordered. Opening pressure was 292, closing pressure was 190.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Low back pain.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Low back pain.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  L2-3 lumbar discogram.,2.  L3-4 lumbar discogram.,3.  L4-5 lumbar discogram.,4.  L5-S1 Lumbar Discogram.,ANESTHESIA: ,IV Sedation.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: ,The patient was brought to the radiology room and placed face down on a radiolucent table.  The C-arm was brought into the surgical field and AP, left right oblique, and lateral fluoroscopic images from the L1-2 to L5-S1 levels were obtained.  We then proceeded to prepare the lower back with a Betadine solution and drape it sterilely.  Using an oblique approach to the spine, the L5-S1 level was approached using an inclined C-arm with oblique projection to allow perpendicular penetration of the disc space.  A metal marker was then placed laterally and a needle entry point was determined.  A skin papule was lifted with 1% Xylocaine and a #18 gauge needle was advanced to the level of the disc space using AP, oblique, and lateral fluoroscopic projections.  A second needle, a 6-inch 22-gauge needle, was then introduced into the disc space and, with AP and lateral fluoroscopic projections, was placed centrally in the nucleus.  We then performed similar needle placement at the L4-5, L3-4, and L2-3 levels. A solution of Isovue 300 with 1 g of Ancef was then drawn into a 10 cc syringe and without informing the patient of our injection, we then proceeded to sequentially inject the disc spaces.

transcription: OPERATION PERFORMED:, Ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition arthroplasty of the right wrist.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:, With the patient under adequate anesthesia, the right upper limb was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.,Attention was turned to the base of the thumb where a longitudinal incision was made over the anatomical snuff box and extended over the carpometacarpal joint.  Using blunt dissection, the radial sensory nerve was dissected and retracted out of the surgical field.  Further dissection exposed the radial artery, which was dissected and retracted from the trapezius.  An incision was then made through the scaphotrapezial joint distally over the trapezius and toward the carpometacarpal joint.  A blunt dissection exposed the trapezius, which was then morselized and removed in its entirety taking care to protect the underlying flexor carpi radialis tendon.  The radial beak of the trapezius was then osteotomized from the head of the scaphoid.  The proximal metacarpal was then fenestrated with a 4.5 mm drill bit.  Four fingers close to the wrist flexion crease, a small incision was made over the FCR tendon, and a blunt dissection introduced the FCR tendon into this incision.  The FCR tendon was divided and this incision was closed with 4-0 nylon sutures.  Attention was returned to the trapezoidal wound where longitudinal traction on the FCR tendon brought the FCR tendon into the wound. The FCR tendon was then threaded through the metacarpal fenestration.  A bone anchor was then placed distal to the metacarpal fenestration.  The FCR tendon was then pulled distally and the metacarpal reduced to an anatomical position.  The FCR tendon was then sutured to the metacarpal using the previously placed bone anchor.  The remaining FCR tendon was then anchored and placed into the scaphotrapezoid and trapezoid defect.  The MP joint was extended and the capsule closed using interrupted 3-0 Tycron sutures. Attention was focused on the MCP joint where the MP joint was brought to 15 degrees of flexion and pinned with a single 0.035 Kirschner wire.  The pin was cut at skin level. All incisions were closed with a subcutaneous stitch with Prolene 3-0. Sterile dressings were then applied.  The tourniquet was deflated.  The patient was awakened from anesthesia and returned to the recovery room in satisfactory condition after having tolerated the procedure well.
