transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Right lumbosacral radiculopathy secondary to lumbar spondylolysis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Right lumbosacral radiculopathy secondary to lumbar spondylolysis.,OPERATION PERFORMED:,1.  Right L4 and L5 transpedicular decompression of the right distal L4 and L5 nerve roots.,2.  Right L4-L5 and L5-S1 laminotomy, medial facetectomies and foraminotomies, decompression of right L5 and S1 nerve roots.,3.  Right posterolateral L4-S1 fusion with local bone graft.,4.  Left L4 to S1 segmental pedicle screw instrumentation.,5.  Preparing for local bone graft harvest.,ANESTHESIA:, General endotracheal.,PREPARATION:, Povidone iodine.,INDICATION:, This is a male with right-sided lumbosacral radiculopathy, MRI revealed and lateral recess stenosis at the L4-5, L5-S1 foraminal stricture of the L4 and L5 roots.  The patient was considered a candidate for decompression stabilization by pulling a distraction between the screws to relieve radicular pain.  The patient understood the major risks and complications such as death and paralysis apparently rare, the main concern is a 10-15% failure rate to respond to surgery for which additional surgery may or may not be indicated, low risk of wound infection, cerebrospinal fluid leak.  The patient is understanding and agreed to continue and signed the consent.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was brought to the operating room, peripheral venous lines were placed.  General anesthesia was induced.  The patient was intubated.  A Foley catheter was in place.  The patient was lying face down on the OSI table using 6 uprights, pressure points were carefully padded; the back was shaved, sterilely prepared and draped.  An anterior incision was infiltrated locally and incised with a scalpel.  The posterior column on the right side was routinely exposed as well as the transverse processes at L4-L5 in the sacral wing.  Laminotomy was then performed at L4-L5 and L5-S1 in the same manner using a Midas Rex drill with an AM8 drill bit, lower part of the blade below and upper part of the blade above, and the medial facet was drilled to the thin bony shelf.  The thin bone shelf along the ligamentum flavum moved piecemeal with 2 and 3 mm of Kerrison, bone was harvested throughout to be used for bone grafting.  The L5 and S1 roots were completely exposed in the lateral cavity working lateral to the markedly hypertrophied facet joints.  Transpedicular approaches were performed for the L4 and L5 roots working lateral to medial and medial to lateral with foraminotomies, the L4-L5 roots were extensively decompressed.  The pars interarticularis was maintained.  Using a 2 mm hypertrophied Kerrison ligamentum flavum, the superior facet of S1 and L5 was resected by increasing the dimensions of the foramen from lateral to medial and medial to lateral without further compromise.  Pedicle screws were placed L4-L5 and S1 on the right side.  The initial hole was started with a Midas Rex drill, deepened with a gear shift and with a 4.5 mm tap, palpated with a pedicle probe.  It showed no penetration outside the pedicle vertebral body.  At levels L4-L5, 5.5 x 45 mm screws were placed and at S1 a 5.5 x 40 mm screw was placed.  A good bone purchase was obtained.  Gelfoam was placed laterally on the roots, corticated transverse processes and lateral facet joints were prepared, a small infused sponge was placed posterolaterally on the right side, then local bone grafting from L4 to S1.  Traction was applied between the L4-L5 screws, the L5-S1 locking screws were tightened, the heads were rotated and fractured. Traction of approximately 2 to 3 mm was applied to each side, further opening the foramen for the exiting roots.  Before BMP placement, the wound was irrigated with antibiotic irrigation.  A medium Hemovac drain was placed deep into the wound, brought out through a separate incision.  The deep fascia was closed with Vicryl No. 1, the subcutaneous fascia with Vicryl No. 1, and the subcutaneous fascia with Vicryl 2-0.  The skin was stapled.  The drain was sutured in pla

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT:, Decreased ability to perform activities of daily living secondary to right knee surgery., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a 61-year-old white female who underwent a right total knee replacement secondary to degenerative joint disease performed by Dr. A at ABCD Hospital on 08/21/2007.  The patient received 2 units of autologous blood postoperatively.  She received DVT prophylaxis with a combination of Coumadin, Lovenox, SCD boots, and TED stockings.  The remainder of his postoperative course was uneventful.  She was discharged on 08/24/2007 from ABCD Hospital and admitted to the Transitional Care Unit of XYZ Services for evaluation and rehabilitation.  The patient reports that her last bowel movement took place on 08/24/2007, just before her discharge from ABCD hospital.  She denies any urological symptoms such as dysuria, incomplete bladder emptying or other voiding difficulties.  She reports having pain in her right knee, which is more intense in a “certain position”.  The patient is unable to specify which "certain position" causes her the most discomfort.,ALLERGIES:, NKDA.,MEDICAL HISTORY: Hypertension, hypothyroidism, degenerative joint disease, GERD, anxiety disorder, Morton's neuroma of the feet bilaterally and a distant history of migraines approximately 30 years ago.,DRUGS:, On transfer, Celebrex, Coumadin, Colace, Synthroid, Lovenox, Percocet, Toprol XL, niacin and trazodone.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: temperature 96.5, blood pressure 127/72, pulse 70, respiratory rate 20, O2 saturation 95% on room air.,GENERAL: No acute distress at time of examination except as mentioned above, complains of pain in the right knee in “certain position”.,HEENT: normocephalic.  Nonicteric sclera.  EOMI.  Teeth in good condition.  The tongue is in the midline with no signs of thrush. NECK: No thyroid enlargement.  Trachea is midline.,LUNGS: clear on auscultation.,HEART: regular rate and rhythm.  Normal S1 and S2.,ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender and non-distended.  No organomegaly.,EXTREMITIES: The incision of the right knee is intact.  The Steri-Strips are in place.  There is diffuse edema of the right knee as well as limited bruising.  No calf tenderness bilaterally.  Pedal pulses are palpable bilaterally.,MENTAL STATUS: The patient appeared mildly anxious during interview and examination, but was alert and oriented.,HOSPITAL COURSE: As mentioned above, the patient was admitted on 08/24/2007 to the Transitional Care Unit of XYZ Services for evaluation and rehabilitation.  She was seen in consultation by a physiotherapist and an occupational therapist and began rehabilitation until recovery.  The patient had received appropriate instructions regarding the use of the CPM device and was also asked to limit each CPM session to two hours.  Very early in her hospitalization, the patient enthusiastically used the CPM for well over two hours and consequently experienced increased pain in her right knee.  She noticed she had a better degree of flexibility, but she reported an increased need for pain management.  Additionally, she needed Ativan, and at one point she scheduled the Ativan doses to treat her known history of an anxiety disorder.  On the fourth day of hospitalization, skin rashes were noted on the right upper limb and the right side of the abdomen.  The patient reported that this rash was itchy.  She reports that she had done quite a bit of gardening just before the surgery and that it was most likely contact dermatitis, probably due to her pre-op gardening activities.  She was treated with betamethasone cream applied to the rash twice a day.  The patient's therapy had progressed and she continued to make good progress.  At one point, the patient reported some insomnia due to right knee pain. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Pathological insufficiency.,2.  Fracture of T8 and T9 vertebrae.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Pathological insufficiency.,2.  Fracture of T8 vertebra and T9 vertebra.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  Reduction of the fracture with insertion of a T8 prosthetic device with kyphoplasty.,2.  Vertebroplasties at T7 and T9 with insertion of a prosthetic device.,ANESTHESIA:, Local with sedation.,SAMPLE:, T8 vertebra bone.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,SURGICAL INDICATIONS:, The patient is an 80-year-old woman who had a history of compression fractures.  She had recently suffered an additional compression fracture of the T8 vertebra.  She was in extreme pain.  This pain interfered with activities of daily living and was not improved by conservative treatment modalities.  It includes the risks, benefits and potential complications as well as all treatment alternatives.  The patient gave informed consent.,OPERATIVE TECHNIQUE: The patient was taken to operating room #2 where she was placed face down on the Jackson spinal table.  He was given a sedative.  The thoracodorsal spine was then prepared sterile and draped in the usual manner.  Biplanar image intensification was used to locate the T8, T7, and T9 vertebrae.  A local anesthetic of 1% marcaine with epinephrine and lidocaine was mixed 50:50. About 7 cc was instilled on the left side.  It was directly on the posterior aspect of the pedicle on the left.  Once this was located, the right side was also located.  Incisions were then made bilaterally on the pedicles of T8.  Jamshidi needles were then placed percutaneously.  Their position was verified on AP and lateral images.  They progressed slowly under direct image intensification in a biplanar manner.  Once these were satisfactorily placed, the internal trocar was removed and a guidewire was inserted deep into the T7 vertebrae.  The Jamshidi needles were then removed.  A biopsy was then performed with a biopsy trocar placed in the T8 vertebra.  This bone was then removed and sent to the laboratory.  The injection cannulas were then placed over the guidewires and their position was verified on the AP and lateral images.  Once this operation was completed, a second Jamshidi needle was placed at the T7 vertebra on the left at the entrance to the pedicle.  This was advanced under direct image intensification in a biplanar manner.  Once this was deemed satisfactory, it was impacted.  The internal trocar was removed and a guidewire was then placed.  An injection cannula was then placed over the guidewire in the body of T7.  Similarly, T9 was also dressed on the left side.  A guidewire was then placed into the Jamshidi needle, which was verified on AP and lateral images.  The cement injection cannula was then placed on it entering the T9 vertebral body.  Attention was then focused on the kyphoplasty portion of the procedure at the T8 vertebrae.  The balloons were inserted bilaterally.  The balloons were then inflated under direct image intensification and pressurized to approximately 200 mmHg.  These were able to expand and reduce the fracture.  Once this was completed, the balloons were deflated and removed.  The inner cannulas of the four entrance holes were removed and approximately 1.5 cc of cement was injected into each of the cannulas.  This was done directly under image intensification.  Once this was completed, additional cement was injected into T9 as there was a larger vertebra.  The cement was allowed to harden.  The cannula was removed and definitive radiographs were obtained.  The incisions were then cleaned with water and antibiotic irrigation.  The wounds were then approximated with #4-0 nylon in an interrupted fashion.  Compression dressings were applied and secured with adhesive tape.  She was roused and moved to her hospital bed.  She moved all four extremities without deficit.  She had no significant pain.

transcription: PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: , After obtaining written consent from the patient, the patient was brought back to the operating room and identified.  The patient was placed on the operating table in the supine position and received general anesthesia. 1 g of Ancef was administered as infectious prophylaxis.  Once the patient was under general anesthesia, the knee was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  Once the knee was fully prepared and draped, we then created 2 standard medial and lateral portals.  Through the side portal, the camera was placed.  Through the medial portal, tools were placed.  We examined the scars of the patellofemoral joint.  We then probed the patellofemoral joint.  A chondroplasty was performed using a razor.  We then went down to the side gutter.  Some loose bodies were found using a razor and dissection.  We went down the medial gutter.  No plica found. We entered the medial joint; we found that the medial meniscus was intact.  We moved to the lateral joint and found that the lateral meniscus was intact.  Photos were taken.  We drained the knee and washed the knee with large amounts of sterile saline.  The instruments were removed.  The 2 portals were closed using a 3-0 nylon suture.  Xeroform, 4 x 4, Kerlix x2 and TED stockings were placed.  The patient was successfully extubated and brought to the recovery room in stable condition.  I then discussed with the family the case, postoperative instructions, and follow-up care.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Left knee pain and stiffness., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Patient is a 57-year-old man with severe bilateral knee DJD, left greater than right, with significant pain and limitations due to both.  He is able to walk about 800 meters a day, but is limited by his knees.  Stairs are negotiated 1 at a time.  His bilateral knee DJD issues have been well documented.  He underwent arthroscopy during the two periods 1991/199 for these two cases.  He has been on conservative treatment for a long time, including nonsteroidal drugs, narcotics, and injections.  At this point, due to his progressive and persistent limitations, he opted for total joint surgery on the left side.  He has other arthritis issues, including multiple back surgeries for spinal stenosis, including decompression and epidural steroids.  Significant pain is treated with narcotic medications. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Failed right total knee arthroplasty.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Failed right total knee arthroplasty.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Revision right total knee arthroplasty.,FIRST ANESTHESIA: , Spine.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , Approximately 75 cc.,TOURNIQUE DURATION:, 123 minutes.  Then, it was relaxed for approximately 15 minutes and then reinflated for an additional 26 minutes for a total of 149 minutes.,COMPONENTS: A Zimmer NexGen Legacy Size D Straight Stem Femoral Component was used.  A NexGen femoral component with an augmented distal femoral block, size 5 mm.  A 3 mm size NexGen tibial component was used.  A 14mm constrained polyethylene surface was also used.  The original patellar component that the patient had was retained.,COMPLICATIONS:,None.,BRIEF HISTORY:,The patient is a 68-year-old female with a 13-year history of knee pain.  She had previously undergone a total knee replacement and revision at an outside facility.  She suffered from persistent pain, clicking, misalignment, difficulty walking and giving up and wanted to undergo additional revision surgery.,PROCEDURE:The patient was taken to the operating room and placed on the operating table.  The anesthesia department administered the spinal anesthesia.  Once properly anesthetized, the patient was placed in the supine position.  Care was provided and it was properly secured and well padded.  Once this was achieved, the right lower extremity was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  The tourniquet was inflated to approximately 325 mmHg on the right thigh.  At this point, an incision was made over his anterior knee scar, bringing it down to the subcutaneous tissue of the overlying retinaculum.  A medial parapatellar arthrotomy was then performed using a second knife and this was taken both distally and proximally to allow us to subluxate the patella on the lateral aspect to allow exposure of the articular surface.  No signs of purulence or macroscopic clinical appearance of infection were noted, however, intraoperative cultures were also performed to assess this.  At this point, the previous articular surface was then removed using an osteotome until it was left free and then removed.  This was done without difficulty.  Attention then shifted to removal of the femoral component.  The osteotome was taken around each of the edges until it was gently lifted, then a femoral extractor was placed around it and this was pulled down until it was easily removed.  After that, attention then shifted to the tibial component.  An osteotome was again inserted around the surface and this came off easily.  This was noted to pose minimal difficulty and did not appear to have adequate cement fixation.  This has been evaluated.  The bone stem seemed adequate, however, there were some deficits where we needed to cut the cement, so we chose to proceed with a stem component.  Attention was first focused on the femur and the femoral canal was opened and superficially reamed to a proximal 18 mm for the Zimmer stem component.  At this point, the distal femoral cut was evaluated with an intramedullary guide and it was noted that it was cut in varus, leaving us with a significant deficit in the medial femoral cut.  We chose, due to this large amount of retic, to remove the medial condyle to correct this varus cut to a six degree valgus cut.  We chose to increase the medial aspect and remove only 5 mm of the lateral condyle instead of 10 to 12 mm. At this point, the distal femoral cutting guide based on the intramedullary head was then placed.  Care was taken to ensure that it was aligned in correct rotation with the external epicondylar axis.  Once this was fixed in position, a valgus cut of approximately six degrees was then made.  This allowed part of the medial condyle to be removed distally.  The anterior cut was then checked using intramedullary gu

She underwent a total right knee replacement.  She tolerated this well.  She was taken to the recovery room. Routine postoperative antibiotics were administered.  Hemovac drainage was discontinued on the second postoperative day.  Physical therapy was initiated.  Continuous Passive Movement (CPM) was also initiated.  She was able to urinate spontaneously.  She switched to oral painkillers.  The incision remained clean, dry, and intact during hospitalization.  No pain when compressing the calf.  She was deemed ready to go home on 03/19/2007., DISPOSITION:, Discharged home., FOLLOW-UP:, Follow-up with Dr.  Prescriptions were written for Percocet and Coumadin.,INSTRUCTIONS:,Physical therapy at home and PT and INR to be established at home to adjust Coumadin dosage.,

transcription: ADMISSION DIAGNOSIS: , Left hip fracture., CHEF'S COMPLAINT: , Decreased function, secondary to the above., HISTORY: , This pleasant 70 year old gentleman had a fall to ground level at his home on 05/05/03 and was brought to ABCD Medical Center, evaluated by Dr.  He then had a left hip fracture identified on x-rays.  The pain and inability to move took his toll. He was evaluated and then also underwent a medical consultation, where they discovered a history of pneumonia, hypertension, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, congestive heart failure, hypothyroidism, depression, anxiety, seizures and chronic kidney failure, as well as anemia.  His medical problems are well under control.  The patient underwent fixation of the left femoral neck with hemiarthroplasty on this left side on 06/05/03.  The patient had medical problems including respiratory failure, perioperative anemia, pneumonia, and hypertension.  Cardiology followed closely and the patient responded well to medical treatment, as well as physical and occupational therapy.  He is gradually tolerating more activities with less difficulty, has made good progress, and has tolerated more consistent and prolonged interventions.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Positive for congestive heart failure, chronic renal failure, azole, hyperglycemia, coronary artery disease, history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation.  Distant history of subdural hematoma precluding use of Coumadin.  History of depression, panic attacks while taking Doxépine.  Perioperative anemia.  Difficulty swallowing.,ALLERGIES: Zyloprim, penicillin, Vioxx, NSAIDs.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS,1.  Heparin.,2.  Albuterol inhaler.,3.  Combivent.,4.  Aldactone.,5.  Doxepin.,6.  Xanax.,7.  Aspirin.,8.  Amiodarone.,9.  Tegretol.,10.  Synthroid.,11.  Colace.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Lives in a one-story house with 1 step down; the woman is there.  Speech and language pathology following current swallowing dysfunction.  It is minimal assistance with activities of daily living, mobility in bed.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS:, Currently negative for headache, nausea and vomiting, fevers, chills or shortness of breath or chest pain.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,HEENT: Oropharynx clear.,CV: Regular rhythm and rhythm without murmurs, rubs or gallops.,LUNGS: Clear to bilateral auscultation.,ABDOMEN: Not tender, not distended.  Bowel appears positive.,EXTREMITIES: No clubbing, cyanosis or edema.,NEUROLOGICAL: There are no focal motor or sensory losses in the lower extremities.  Normal volume and tone in the lower limbs.  The wound site healed well without the staples.,PRINT,1.  Status after left hip fracture and hemiarthroplasty.,2.  History of panic attack, anxiety, depression.,3.  Myocardial infarction with stenting.,4.  Hypertension.,5.  Hypothyroidism.,6.  Subdural hematoma.,7.  Seizures.,8.  History of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.  Recent respiratory failure.,9.  Renal failure.,10.  Recent pneumonia.,11.  O2 requiring.,12.  Perioperative anemia.,PLAN:,rehabilitation transfer as soon as medically cleared.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , Left hip fracture.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a pleasant 53-year-old woman with a known history of sciatica, apparently presented to the emergency room due to severe pain in the left lower extremity and unable to bear weight.  The history was obtained from the patient.  According to her history, she reported that she had back pain and left leg pain for 4 weeks.  She uses a walker to get around due to debilitating pain in her left thigh and lower back.  She was seen by her GP and was due for an MRI yesterday.  However, she was walking and her right foot got stuck in some sort of mat, causing excessive weight to be placed on her left lower extremity to prevent her from falling.  Since then, she has been unable to move around.  The patient called for help and was transported to the emergency room.  She denied any story of falling.  She reported that she had gone the wrong way, which made the pain worse.  She complains of severe pain in her lower limbs and back pain.  Denies any tingling or numbness.  Denies any neurological symptoms.  Denies any bowel or bladder incontinence. X-rays were obtained, notable for a left hip fracture.  An orthopedic consultation was requested for further evaluation and management.  Upon further interview with the patient, it is noted that she has a history of malignant melanoma, diagnosed approximately 4 to 5 years ago.  She underwent surgery at that time and subsequently she was found to have spread to the lymphatic system and lymph nodes for which she underwent surgery in 3/2008.,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,Sciatica and melanoma.,SURGICAL HISTORY: ,As noted above, surgery for melanoma and hysterectomy.,ALLERGIES: NONE.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Denies any tobacco or alcohol. to use.  She is divorced and has 2 children.  She lives with her son.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: The patient is well developed, well nourished and presents with mild distress secondary to the left lower limb and back pain.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Examination of the left lower limb, there is presence of apparent shortening and external rotation deformity.  Tenderness to palpation is present.  Leg rolling is positive for severe pain in the left proximal hip.  Further examination of the spine is incomplete due to severe leg pain.  She is unable to lift her right leg.  EHL/EDL 5/5.  More than 2 pulses are present distally.  The calf is gentle and not sensitive.  Homan's sign is negative.  Light touch sensation is intact.,IMAGING: The AP view of the hip is reviewed.  Only one limited view is obtained.  This is a poor quality x-ray with lots of soft tissue shadows.  This radiograph is significant in cases of basicervical fracture of the femoral neck.  The lesser trochanter is intact.  This is a high intertrochanteric/basicervical fracture.  There is a lytic lesion around the femoral neck, which is not well demarcated on this particular radiograph.  We need to order repeat x-rays including the AP pelvis, femur and knee.,LABORATORY:,were examined.,ASSESSMENT:,The patient is a 53 year old female with a probable pathologic fracture of the left proximal femur.,DISCUSSION AND PLAN:,The nature and progress of the diagnosis were discussed with the patient.  Based on his presentation with no history of falls or obvious trauma and history of malignant melanoma, it appears to be a pathologic fracture of the left proximal hip.  At this time, I would recommend getting a bone scan and repeat x-rays, which will include the AP pelvis, femur, hip and knee.  She denies any pain elsewhere.  She does have a history of back pain and sciatica, but at present it appears to be a metastatic bone lesion with pathologic fracture.  I discussed the case with Dr. X and recommended an oncology consultation. With the above fracture and presentation, she requires a left hip hemiarthroplasty versus a calcar hemiarthroplasty, cemented type.  Indication, risk and being

The patient was admitted to Orthopedics and consulted Medicine.  The patient was actually taken to the operating room, consent signed by durable power of attorney, taken on 06/08/2007, had a hemiarthroplasty of the right hip, recovered without incident.  The patient suffered from persistent confusion and dementia, which is apparently his baseline condition secondary to his Alzheimer's disease.  Brief elevation of white count after surgery, which subsided.  Studies, UA and blood cultures were negative.  Patient was stable and was discharged to Heartland.,DISCHARGE STATUS:,Stable.,DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:,Transfer to ABC for rehabilitation and continued care.  Diabetic diet.  Activity, ambulate as tolerated with caution regarding the posterior hip.  Rehabilitation Potential Fair.  He will need consultations in nursing, social work, physiotherapy and nutrition. transcription: REASON FOR VISIT: , Patient returned for right hand pain. , HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , Patient is a 28-year-old right hand dominant male, who hit the wall 3 days prior to presentation.  He complained of pain in his right hand on the ulnar side and was seen in the emergency room.  It appears that some of the joints in his hand were pushed back and placed by someone in the emergency room.  Today, he admits that his pain is much better.  Currently, since that time, he has been wearing a splint with minimal pain.  He had no numbness, tingling, or other concerning symptoms.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Negative.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,The patient is a non-smoker and does not use illegal drugs.  Drinks occasionally.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION: , A 12-point systems exam is negative.,MEDICATIONS:, None.,ALLERGIES: , No known drug allergies.,FINDINGS: ,On physical examination, he has swelling and tenderness over the ulnar dorsum of the hand.  It has a normal waterfall.  It has 70 degrees of MCP flexion and full IP flexion and extension.  He has 3-5 points of strength in his grip and intrinsics.  It has intact sensation to light touch in the radial, ulnar, and medial nerve distribution.  Two plus radial pulses.,X-rays taken today were reviewed, including three views of the right hand.  They show possible small fractures of the base of the fourth and third metacarpals.  The joint appears to be localized.  A 45-degree oblique view was obtained and confirmed adduction of the CMC joints of the fourth and fifth metacarpals.  His injury films from 09/15/07 were reviewed and demonstrated what appear to be CMC dislocations of the third and fourth metacarpals.,ASSESSMENT:,Status after carpometacarpal dislocations of the right third and fourth metacarpals.,PLANS:,The patient was placed in a short arm cast and an intrinsic plus one.  I would like him to wear this for 2 weeks and then follow up with us.  At that time we will transition him to an OT splint and begin finger and wrist range of motion activities.  We should see him again in 2 weeks, at which time he should get three views of the right hand and a 45 degree oblique view out of cast.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left wrist node.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left wrist node.,OPERATION: ,Excision of the node.,ANESTHESIA: ,General.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: ,Less than 5 mL.,OPERATION: ,After successful anesthesia, the patient was positioned on the operating table.  A tourniquet applied to the upper arm.  The extremity was prepared in the usual manner for surgery and draped.  The superficial vessels were bled with an elastic band and the tourniquet was then inflated to usual arm pressure.  A curved incision was made over the ganglion present on the dorsal aspect of the wrist.  By sharp and sharp dissection it was dissected below the extensor tendons and the shaft appeared to originate from the distal radiocapitellar joint and the dorsal capsule was excised along with the ganglion and the specimen was removed and submitted.  The small superficial vessels were electrocoagulated and instilled after closing the skin with Prolene 4-0, in the area was approximately 6-7 mL of Marcaine 0.25 with epinephrine.  A Jackson-Pratt drain was inserted and then, once the tourniquet was released, it was kept deflated until at least 5 to 10 minutes had passed, then it was activated and then removed in the recovery room.  The dressings applied to the hand were those of Xeroform, 4x4s, ABD, Kerlix and an elastic band on a fiberglass volar splint.  The tourniquet has been released.  Circulation has returned to the fingers.  The patient was then able to wake up and left the operating room in good condition.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Left carpal tunnel syndrome.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Left carpal tunnel syndrome.,OPERATIONS PERFORMED:, Endoscopic release of carpal tunnel.,ANESTHESIA:, I.V. sedation and local (1% lidocaine).,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, Zero.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,With the patient under adequate anesthesia, the upper limb was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  The arm was bloodless.  The tourniquet was elevated at 290 mm/Hg.  Construction lines were drawn on the left palm to identify the annular radius.  A transverse incision was made at the wrist, between FCR and FCU, at one finger near the interval between the glabrous skin of the palm and the normal skin of the forearm.  Blunt dissection exposed the antebrachial fascia.  Hemostasis was achieved by bipolar cauterization.  A distal window in the antebrachial fascia was then made.  Precautions have been taken to protect the underlying content.  A proximal forearm fasciotomy was performed under direct vision.  A synovial elevator was used to palpate the underside of the transverse carpal ligament, and the synovium was elevated relative to this underside.  The hamate sounds were then used to palpate the hamate hook.  The endoscopic instrument was then inserted into the proximal incision.  The transverse carpal ligament was easily visualized through the gate.  Using palmar pressure, the transverse carpal ligament was held against the gate while the instrument was inserted along the transverse carpal ligament to the distal end. The distal end of the transverse carpal ligament was then identified in the window.  The blade was then elevated and the endoscopic instrument was removed, dividing the transverse carpal ligament under direct vision.  After complete division of the transverse carpal ligament, the instrument was reinserted.  The radial and ulnar edges of the transverse carpal ligament were identified and complete release was confirmed. The wound was then closed with a running subcuticular stitch.  Steri-Strips were applied and a sterile dressing was applied over the Steri-Strips.  The tourniquet was deflated.  The patient was awakened from anesthesia and returned to the recovery room in satisfactory condition, having tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left elbow with retained hardware.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left elbow with retained hardware.,PROCEDURE: , ,1.  Manipulation of the left elbow.,2.  Hardware removal of left elbow.,ANESTHESIA:,Surgery was performed under general anesthesia.,COMPLICATIONS:,There were no intraoperative complications.,DRAINS:,None.,SAMPLES:,None.,INTRAOPERATIVE FINDING:Preoperatively, the patient has a range of motion of 40 to 100 degrees with limited supination and pronation of approximately 20 degrees.  We increased his extension and flexion to approximately 20 to 120 degrees and his pronation and supination to approximately 40 degrees.,LOCAL ANESTHETICS: 10 ml of 0.25% marcaine.,HISTORY AND PHYSICS:,The patient is a 10 year old right-handed dominant male, who threw himself from a quad bike on 10/10/2007.  The patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation of his dislocated left elbow fracture.  The patient also suffered a nondisplaced right glenoid neck fracture.  The patient's fracture healed uneventfully, although he has significant postoperative stiffness for which he is undergoing physical therapy, as well as the use of a Dynasplint.  The patient is neurologically intact distally.  Since his fracture healed, surgery was recommended to remove the hardware to decrease his irritation from elbow extension from the hardware.  The risks and benefits of surgery were discussed.  Risks of surgery included risk of anesthesia, infection, bleeding, changes in sensation and movement of the extremities, inability to remove hardware, inability to relieve pain, and persistent postoperative stiffness.  All questions were answered and the parents agreed to the above plan.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating table.  General anesthesia was then administered.  The patient's left upper extremity was then prepared and draped in a standard surgical manner.  Using fluoroscopy, the patient's K pin was located.  An incision was made over his previous scar.  Subcutaneous dissection then took place in the plane between the subcutaneous fat and the muscles.  The K wires were easily palpable.  A small incision was made in the triceps, which allowed visualization of the two pins, which were removed without incident.  The wound was then irrigated.  The triceps split was now closed with Vicryl #2-0.  The subcutaneous tissue was also closed with Vicryl #2-0 and the skin with Monocryl #4-0.  The wound was clean and dry and dressed with Steri-Strips, Xeroform and 4x4s, as well as bias tape.  A total of 10 ml of 0.25% marcaine was injected into the incision as well as the joint line.  Early in the case, before hardware removal, the arm underwent intense manipulation with improvement in extension to 20 degrees, flexion to 130 degrees, and supination to pronation to approximately 40 degrees.,DIAGNOSTIC IMPRESSION: ,Postoperative films showed no fractures, no retained hardware.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was subsequently taken to the recovery room in stable condition.,POSTOPERATIVE PLAN: The patient will resume physiotherapy and Dynasplint in 3 days.  The patient should be followed up in 1 week for wound control.  The patient was given Tylenol #3 for pain relief.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Dorsal ganglion, right wrist.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Dorsal ganglion, right wrist.,OPERATIONS PERFORMED:, Excision of the dorsal ganglion, right wrist.,ANESTHESIA:, Monitored anesthesia care with regional anesthesia applied by the surgeon.,GUARANTEE DURATION:, minutes.,DESCRIPTION OF THE PROCEDURE:, With the patient under adequate anesthesia, the upper limb was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  The arm was bled and the tourniquet was raised to 290 mm/Hg.  A transverse incision was made over the dorsal ganglion.  Using blunt dissection, the dorsal ulnar sensory nerve branches and radial sensory nerve branches were dissected and retracted out of the surgical field.  The extensor retinaculum was then incised and the extensor tendon was dissected and retracted out of the surgical field.  The ganglion was then dissected to its origin from the dorsal distal scapholunate interosseous ligament and excised in its entirety.  Precautions were taken to protect the integrity of the ligaments.  The reactive synovium was then removed using the soft tissue rongeur technique.  The wound was then infiltrated with 0.25% marcaine.  The tendons were able to return to their normal anatomical position.  The skin was closed with a Prolene 3-0 subcuticular stitch.  Sterile dressings were applied.  The tourniquet was deflated.  The patient was awakened from anesthesia and returned to the recovery room in satisfactory condition after having tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,Stenosing tenosynovitis of Quervain's wrist.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,Stenosing tenosynovitis of Quervain's wrist.,TITLE OF INTERVENTIONS,1. Liberation of Quervain.,2.  1st dorsal compartment fascial lengthening flap.,ANESTHESIA:, MAC.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,After administration of MAC anesthesia and appropriate antibiotics, the upper limb was prepared and draped in the usual standard manner.  The arm was bled with an Esmarch and the tourniquet inflated to 250 mmHg. I made a transverse incision just distal to the radial styloid.  The dissection was carried out directly to the 1st dorsal compartment with the superficial radial nerve identified and protected.  Meticulous hemostasis was maintained with bipolar electrocautery. I dissected the sheath superficially free of any other structures, particularly the superficial radial nerve.  I then incised it under direct vision dorsal to its axis and I incised it proximally and distally.  The EPB subcladding was also released. I carefully irrigated the wound.  To prevent subluxation of the tendon, I then cut back the dorsal and volar leaves of the sheath so that I could close them in the extended, recumbent position.  I did this with 3-0 Vicryl.  I then ran an instrument underneath to check and make sure the sheath wasn't too tight.  I then irrigated and closed the skin, then dressed and splinted the wrist appropriately.  The patient was sent to the recovery room in good condition, having tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: TITLE OF OPERATION: Lateral and plantar condylectomy, left fifth metatarsal.,PRE-OPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Hypertrophy of the prominent, lateral and plantar condyles, left fifth metatarsal.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Hypertrophy of the prominent, lateral and plantar condyles, left fifth metatarsal.,ANAESTHESIA:,Anesthesia care monitored with 10 mL of a 1:1 mixture of 0.5% marcaine and 1% regular lidocaine.,HAEMOSTASIS: 30 minutes, left ankle tourniquet set at 250 mmHg.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , Less than 10 mL.,MATERIALS USED: , 3-0 Vicryl and 4-0 Vicryl.,INJECTABLES:, Ancef 1 g IV 30 minutes. preoperatively.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:,The patient was brought to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  After achieving adequate sedation by the anesthesia team, the anesthetic mixture mentioned above was infiltrated directly into the patient's left foot to anesthetize future surgical sites.  The left ankle was covered with a padding cast and an 18-inch ankle tourniquet was placed around the left ankle and set at 250 mmHg.  The left foot was then prepared, scrubbed and draped using normal sterile technique.  The left ankle tourniquet was swollen.  Attention was then focused on the dorsolateral aspect of the left fifth metatarsophalangeal joint where a 4 cm linear incision was made on the left fifth metatarsophalangeal joint, parallel and lateral to the course of the extensor digitorum longus to the left fifth toe.  The incision was deepened through the subcutaneous tissues.  All bleeding was identified, cut, clamped and cauterized.  The incision was deepened to the level of the capsule and periosteum of the left fifth metatarsophalangeal joint.  All tendon and neurovascular structures were identified and removed from the site to be preserved.  Using sharp and blunt dissection, the soft tissue attachments across the left fifth metatarsal head were mobilized.  The lateral and plantar aspects of the left fifth metatarsal head were properly exposed and, using the sagittal saw, a lateral and plantar condylectomy of the left fifth metatarsal head was then performed.  The bony prominences were removed and transferred off the operating table to be sent to pathology for identification.  The remaining sharp edges of the left fifth metatarsal head were then smoothed using a dental rasp.  The area was rinsed thoroughly with saline solution.  Then, Vicryl 3-0 and Vicryl 4-0 suture materials were used to approximate the periosteal, capsular, and subcutaneous tissues, respectively.  The incision was reinforced with Steri-Strips.  The range of motion of the left fifth metatarsophalangeal joint was tested and found to be excellent and without inhibition.  The patient's left ankle tourniquet was then deflated.  Immediate hyperemia was noted throughout the left lower extremity upon deflation of the cuff.  The patient's incision was covered with Xeroform, large amounts of lint and Kling, jersey and Ace bandage and the patient's left foot was placed in a surgical shoe.  The patient was then transferred to the recovery room under the care of the anesthesia team with her vital signs stable and her vascular status at appropriate levels.  The patient was given painkillers and instructions on how to monitor her postoperative progress.  She was discharged from the hospital according to nursing protocol and will follow up with Dr. X in a week for her first post-operative appointment.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Left knee pain., SUBJECTIVE: This is a 36-year-old white female who presented to the office today with a complaint of left knee pain.  She is about five days after a third injection of Synvisc.  She states that her knee is 35-40% better, but she continues to experience constant pain when bearing full weight, cannot stand having her knee in flexion, and has a reduced range of motion with extension.  Rates his knee pain at 10/10.  It alternates ice and heat.  She uses Tylenol No. 3 p.r.n. and ibuprofen OTC p.r.n. with minimal relief.,ALLERGIES,1.  PENICILLIN.,2.  KEFLEX.,3.  BACTRIM.,4.  SULPHA.,5.  ACE BANDAGES.,MEDICATIONS,1.  Toprol.,2.  Xanax.,3.  Reviews.,4.  Ventolin.,5.  Tylenol No. 3.,6.  Advil., SYSTEMS REVIEW:, will start Medifast diet, discussed with her PCP, who encouraged her to have gastric bypass, but patient would like to try this Medifast diet first.  Other than that, denies any other problems with his eyes, ears, nose, throat, heart, lungs, gastrointestinal system, gastrointestinal system, musculoskeletal and nervous system, except as noted above and below.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION, VITAL SIGNS: Pulse 72, Blood Pressure 130/88, Respirations 16, Height 5'6.5 inches.,GENERAL: This is a 36 year old white female who is A&O x3, no apparent distress with pleasant effect.  She is well developed, well nourished, appears to be her stated age.,EXTREMITIES: Orthopedic evaluation of the left knee reveals the presence of well-healed portholes.  She has medial joint line swelling.  Negative ballot.  She feels significant pain on palpation of the medial joint space, but not the lateral joint space.  She has no pain on palpation in the popliteal fossa.  Range of motion is approximately -5 degrees at 95 degrees of flexion.  It should be noted that she exhibits extreme right hyperextension with over 95 degrees of right flexion.  She clicks with McMurray.  Negative anteroposterior drawer.  No varus or valgus instability was noted.  Positive patellar crush test.  The calf is gentle and not sensitive.  The gait is stable and analgesic on the left.,EVALUATION,1.  Osteochondral defect, torn meniscus, left knee.,2.  Obesity.,PLAN: ,I encouraged the patient to work on weight reduction as this will only benefit her knee.  I discussed treatment options with the patient at length, but I think the best plan for her would be to work on weight reduction.  She wonders if she needs a total knee; I don't think she needs a total knee replacement.  However, she may need an arthroscopy at some point.  I encouraged her to begin formal physical therapy and a home exercise program.  Will use ice or heat p.r.n.  I gave him refills of Tylenol #3, Flector patch and Relafen not to take with other anti-inflammatories.  She has some abdominal discomfort from the anti-inflammatories, she started taking Nexium 20 mg once orally. every day.  She will follow up in our office in four weeks.  If she has not achieved any relief through formal physical therapy and the treatments mentioned above, we will discuss with Dr. X whether she would benefit from another knee arthroscopy.  The patient shows good understanding of this treatment plan and agrees.

His brother died at the age of 70 from bone and throat cancer.  She has two healthy sons aged 37 and 38.  She has two daughters, aged 60 and 58, both of whom have cancer.  She describes cancer, hypertension, nervous disorders, kidney disease, lung disease, and depression in her family.,SOCIAL HISTORY: She is married and has support at home.  Denies use of tobacco, alcohol, and illicit drugs.,ALLERGIES:, Aspirin.,MEDICATIONS:,Patient does not mention any current medications.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Hypertension, depression, and osteoporosis.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,She has had four back surgeries in her life and in 2005 had an anterior cervical discectomy and a fusion of C3 to C7 by Dr. L. She is G10, P7, no c-section.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: , HEENT: Headache, vision changes, dizziness and sore throat.  GI: Difficulty swallowing.  Musculoskeletal: She is right-handed and suffers from joint pain, stiffness, decreased range of motion, and arthritis.  Respiratory: Shortness of breath and cough.  Cardiac: Chest pain and swelling of the feet and ankle.  Psychiatric: Anxiety and depression.  Urinary: Negative and non-contributory.  Hem-Onc: Negative and non-contributory.  Vascular: Negative and non-contributory.  Genital: Negative and non-contributory.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: On physical examination, she is 5 feet 10 inches tall and currently weighs 110 pounds; A year ago my weight was 145 pounds.  BP 138/78, pulse 64. General: Female well developed, well nourished, without acute distress.  HEENT examination, the head is atraumatic and normocephalic.  The eyes and sclera are anicteric.  Teeth, she has bad teeth.  She says she needs to have some teeth pulled out in the bottom of her mouth.  Cranial nerves II, III, IV and VI, vision is intact and visual fields are full for confrontation.  The MOEs are full bilaterally.  The pupils are equal, round and responsive to light.  Cranial nerves V and VII, normal facial sensation and symmetrical facial movements.  Cranial nerve VIII, hearing is intact, although diminished bilaterally on the right, worse than on the left.  Cranial nerves IX,  Cranial nerve XI, strong, symmetrical shoulder that shrugs against resistance.  Cardiac, regular rate and rhythm.  The chest and lungs are clear bilaterally.  The skin feels hot and dry.  Normal turgor and texture.  No rash or lesions are noted.  The general musculoskeletal examination reveals no macroscopic deformation, fasciculations or atrophy.  Peripheral vascular, no cyanosis, clubbing or edema.  She has tremors in her bilateral arms, as she said.  Strength testing reveals difficulty during testing due to the patient experiencing a lot of pain, but she appears to be fairly equal in bilateral upper extremities with no obvious weakness noted.  She has about a 4+/5 in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist flexors, wrist extensors, dorsal interossei and grip strength. It's much more painful for her on the left.  Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ bilaterally only in the biceps, triceps and brachioradialis, knees and ankles.  No ankle clonus is obtained.  Hoffmann's is negative bilaterally.  The feeling is intact.  She moves with small, slow steps.  No spastic gait is noted.  She has proper posture and gait, without an assistive device, although she states that she is supposed to use a cane.  She does not bring any with her today.,FINDINGS: Patient brings cervical spine x-rays and she had an MRI but is not bringing it with her today.  She will get this as well as x-rays, which showed the C3, C4, C5, C6 and C7 cervical plates forward with some elevation, with the most raised area being at the C3 level. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Right carpal tunnel syndrome.,2.  Tenosynovitis of the right index and middle fingers.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Right carpal tunnel syndrome.,2.  Tenosynovitis of the right index and middle fingers.,PROCEDURES PERFORMED:,1.  Release of the right carpal tunnel.,2.  The right index and middle fingers release the A1 pulley.,GUARANTEE DURATION: 70 minutes.,BLOOD LOSS:,Minimal.,GROSS INTRAOPERATIVE FINDINGS:,1.  A compressed median nerve at the carpal tunnel, which was flattened.,2.  Stenosing tenosynovitis of the A1 pulley of the right index and middle fingers.  After the A1 pulley was released, there was evidence of synovitis as well as fraying of the flexor digitorum profundus tendons as well as the flexor digitorum superficialis tendons.,HISTORY:,This is a 78 year old male complaining of pain and numbness in the right hand with decreased ROM of the middle index and right middle fingers complaining of catching and locking.  The patient was diagnosed with carpal tunnel syndrome on bilateral hands, with the right being worse than the left.  He had positive EMG findings as well as clinical findings.  The patient underwent an injection, which only gave him temporary relief and for this reason he consented to undergo the above-mentioned procedure.,All risks as well as complications were discussed with the patient and consent was obtained.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was brought back to Operating Room No.1 of ABCD General Hospital on 29/08/03.  He was placed supine on the operating table.  Next, a nonsterile tourniquet was placed on the right forearm, but not inflated.  At this time, 8 cc of 0.25% marcaine with epinephrine was instilled into the carpal tunnel region of the volar aspect of the wrist for anesthesia.  Additionally, an additional 2 cc was used on the superficial skin of the volar palm above the A1 pulley of the right index and middle fingers.  At this time, the extremity was then prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner for this procedure.  First, we opted for carpal tunnel release.  An incision of approximately 2.5 cm was made on the palmar aspect of the wrist, at the level of the carpal tunnel region.  First, skin dissection into the superficial fascia was performed with a self-retractor placed in addition to the Ragnells retracting proximally and distally.  The palmaris brevis muscle was then identified and clearly divided.  At this time, we identified the transverse carpal tunnel ligament and a #15 blade was used to sharply and carefully release this fascia.  Once the transverse carpal ligament fascia was divided, the identification of the median nerve was visualized.  Ligament resection was performed both proximally and distally to ensure complete release and was carefully checked.  At that time, neurolysis was performed and no evidence of space-occupying lesions was identified in the carpal tunnel.  At that time, copious irrigation was used to irrigate the wound.  The wound was suctioned and dried.  At this time, we proceeded to release the A1 pulleys.  An incision of approximately 1.5 cm was made on the A1 pulley at the level of the palmar aspect of the palm of the right index and middle fingers.  First, we opted for the index.  Once the skin incision was made, Metzenbaum scissors were used to dissect the subcutaneous tissue longitudinally and with Ragnell retractors we identified the A1 pulley.  A #15 blade was used to make a longitudinal slot with the A1 pulley and the Littler scissors were used to release the A1 pulley proximally and distally.  Once this was done, a tendon hook was then used to wrap the tendon and release the tendons both proximally and distally, then they were removed from the wound to check their integrity.  There was evidence of synovitis in addition to fraying of the deep and superficial tendons.  Once complete release was achieved, copious irrigation was required.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Carpal tunnel syndrome, bilateral.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Carpal tunnel syndrome, bilateral.,ANESTHESIA: , General,NAME OF OPERATION: , Bilateral open carpal tunnel release.,FINDINGS AT OPERATION: , The patient had identical, very thick, transverse carpal ligaments with synovium dull.,PROCEDURE:,Under satisfactory anesthesia, the patient was prepared. and draped routinely over both upper limbs.  The right upper limb was bled and the tourniquet inflated.  A curved incision was made at the ulnar base, passing through the subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia, to the transverse carpal ligament.  This was divided under direct vision along its ulnar margin and the wound was closed with interrupted nylon.  The wound was injected and a dry, sterile dressing was applied.  An identical procedure was performed on the opposite side.  The patient left the operating room in satisfactory condition.

transcription: PROCEDURE: Carpal tunnel release with transverse carpal ligament reconstruction.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After administering appropriate antibiotics and MAC anesthesia, the upper limb was prepared and draped in the usual standard manner.  The arm was bled with Esmarch and the tourniquet inflated to 250 mmHg. A longitudinal incision was made in alignment with the fourth ray, from Kaplan's cardinal line proximally to 1 cm distal to the palmar crease of the wrist.  The dissection continued to the superficial aponeurosis.  The subcutaneous fat was dissected radially for 2-3 mm and the superficial aponeurosis was cut on that side to leave a longer ulnar sheet. The ulnar sheet of the cut superficial aponeurosis was ulnarly dissected, and the distal edge of the transverse carpal ligament was identified with a hemostat.  The hemostat was gently placed under the transverse carpal ligament to protect the contents of the carpal tunnel, and the ligament was cut on its ulnar side with a knife directly on the hemostat.  The antebrachial fascia was cut proximally under direct vision with scissors. After irrigating the wound with large amounts of normal saline, the radial leaf of the cut transverse carpal ligament was repaired to the ulnar leaf of the cut superficial aponeurosis with 4-0 Vicryl.  Care was taken to avoid entrapment of the motor branch of the median nerve in the suture.  A hemostat was placed under the repair to ensure the median nerve was not compressed.  The skin was repaired with 5-0 interrupted nylon stitches. Marcaine and epinephrine were injected into the wound, which was then dressed and splinted.  The patient was sent to the recovery room in good condition, having tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Left carpal tunnel syndrome (354.0).,2.  Entrapment of the left ulnar nerve at the elbow (354.2).,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Left carpal tunnel syndrome (354.0).,2.  Entrapment of the left ulnar nerve at the elbow (354.2).,OPERATIONS PERFORMED:,1.  Left Carpal Tunnel Release (64721).,2.  Anterior submuscular transposition of the left ulnar nerve at the elbow (64718).,3.  Lengthening of the pronator flexor muscle mass of the proximal forearm to accommodate the submuscular position of the ulnar nerve (25280).,ANESTHESIA: , General anesthesia with intubation.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS: , This patient is insulin-dependent diabetic.  He also suffers from end-stage renal failure and chronic hemodialysis.  Additionally, the patient has previously undergone a heart transplant.  He was evaluated for ischemic problems in the lower extremities and potentially the left upper extremity.  However, we assert that the primary problem in this patient's left upper extremity is likely neuropathic ulcers due to a complete lack of sensation along the ulnar border of the left little finger.  Initially, these were unrecognized paper clippings.  Additionally, the patient appears to have neurogenic pain primarily affecting areas innovated by the median nerve, but also in the little finger.  Finally, this patient does have left upper extremity occlusive arterial disease in that he has a short segment radial artery occlusion and he appears to have a narrowed segment in the ulnar artery, but the arteriogram shows distal perfusion along the ulnar margin of the hand and into the little finger.  Thus, we planned to initially perform nerve entrapment releases and possibly at a later date perform arterial reconstruction if deemed necessary.  Third, this patient does have chronic distal ischemic problems with evidence of the “ping-pong ball sign” due to fatty atrophy of the fingertips and periodic cracking and ulceration of the fingertips.  However, this patient currently has no clinical signs of tissue necrosis in the fingertips. The patient also previously had an arteriovenous shunt in the forearm, which was disabled for the past 3 weeks.  Thus, we planned to bring this patient to the operating room for left carpal tunnel release as well as anterior submuscular transposition of the ulnar nerve.  This patient underwent electrodiagnostic studies which showed severe damage to the ulnar nerve at the elbow and the medial nerve at the carpal tunnel.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION: After general anesthesia is induced and the patient is intubated, he receives Ancef intravenously.  The entire left upper limb is prepared with Betadine up to the axilla and draped in a sterile manner.  A sterile tourniquet and webril are placed higher on the arm.  The arm is then bled with an Ace bandage and a tourniquet inflated to 250 mmHg.  I started with carpal tunnel release and a longitudinal curvilinear incision is made parallel to the thenar crease and stopping before the wrist flexion crease.  The dissection continued through the subcutaneous tissue to the palmer aponeurosis, which is divided longitudinally from distal to proximal.  I then encountered the transverse carpal ligament, which in turn is also divided longitudinally from distal to proximal, and the most proximal division of the transverse carpal ligament is under direct vision in the distal forearm.  After confirming complete release of the transverse carpal ligament, I then assessed the contents of the carpal tunnel.  The synovium was somewhat thickened, but not excessively.  There was erythema along the entire length of the median nerve, indicating chronic compression.  The motor branch of the median nerve was clearly identified.  The contents of the carpal tunnel were retracted in a radial direction and the bottom of the tunnel assessed and no other extrinsic compressive pathology was identified.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Left calcaneal valgus cleft.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Left calcaneal valgus cleft.,PROCEDURES: ,1.  Lengthening osteotomy of the left calcaneus with allograft.,2.  Partial plantar fasciotomy.,3.  Subtalar and posterior tibiotalar capsulotomy.,4.  Short leg cast placed.,ANESTHESIA: , Surgery performed under general anesthesia.,GUARANTEED DURATION: , 69 minutes.,The patient is under local anesthesia of 20 ml of ordinary marcaine 0.25%.,COMPLICATIONS: , No intraoperative complications.,DRAINAGES: , None.,SAMPLES: , None.,HISTORY AND PHYSICS: , The patient is aged 13 years old. woman who has already undergone bilateral foot correction at the age of 1 year.  Since then, the patient has developed a significant calcaneal valgus deformity accompanied by significant pain.  X-rays confirmed the collapse of the spinal arch, as well as the valgus position of the foot.  Considering the patient's symptoms, surgery is recommended for calcaneal osteotomy and Achilles lengthening.  The risks and benefits of surgery were discussed with the mother.  Risks of surgery include the risk of anesthesia; infection; bleeding; changes in sensation in most limbs; hardware failure; need to subsequently remove the material; possible nonunion; possible failure to correct all deformities; and the need for further surgical interventions.  The patient must be strictly non-weight bearing for at least 6 weeks and wear a corset for a maximum of 6 months.  All questions were answered and the parents agreed to the above surgical plan.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:,The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating table.  General anesthesia was then administered.  The patient received Ancef preoperatively.  A bump was placed under the left buttock.  A nonsterile tourniquet was placed on the upper aspect of the left thigh.  The extremity was then prepared and draped in standard surgical fashion.  The patient had previously undergone an incision along the lateral calcaneocuboid aspect of the foot.  This was marked and extended proximally through the Achilles tendon.  The end was wrapped in Esmarch.  Tourniquet inflation was 250 mmHg.  The decision was then made to protect the sural nerve.  There was a sensory nerve branch running through the field, although it was later heavily ligated because it was in the way.  The dissection was carried down to the Achilles tendon, which was then elongated and the distal half was carried out along the lateral side of the thigh.  The proximal end was sewn with Ethibond 0 suture and then repaired end to end lengthwise with the heel in neutral.  Dissection was then performed on the lateral edge of the foot with identification of the peroneus longus and valgus tendons, which were removed from the sheath and retracted dorsally.  At this time, we also noted that the calcaneocuboid joint appeared fused.  The area between the anterior and middle facets was creased under fluoroscopy for planned osteotomy.  This was done with a saw.  After a partial plantar fasciotomy, this was freed from a minimi abductor digit.  The osteotomy was completed with an osteotome and distracted with the blade retractor.  A tricortical allograft was then shaped and impacted in this area.  Final positioning was verified with multiple fluoroscopy views.  It was then secured using a 0.94 K pin and drilled from the heel forward.  A pin was then bent and cut at skin level.  The wound was then irrigated with normal saline.  The Achilles was repaired with this tie.  Note that during the case, it was noted that the patient had significant persistent stiffness despite the lengthening of the Achilles tendon.  A posterior capsulotomy of the tibiotalar and subtalar joints was performed with dorsiflexion increased by 10 degrees.  The wound was then closed with Vicryl #2-0 and Monocryl #4-0.  The surgical field was irrigated with 0.25% marcaine and then injected with more marcaine at the end of the case.  The wound was clean and dry.

Arthroplasty of left second toe.,HISTORY:,This 39-year-old woman presents to ABCD General Hospital with the above chief complaint.  The patient states that she has had a bunion for several months.  It gradually becomes more and more painful at this point.  The patient has attempted conservative treatment including wider shoes without long-term relief of symptoms and desires surgical treatment.,PROCEDURE:,An IV was started by the anesthesia department in the preoperative waiting area.  The patient was transported to the operating room and placed on the operating table in a supine position with a seat belt on her knees.  A copious amount of Webril was placed around the left ankle, followed by a blood pressure cuff.  After adequate sedation was achieved by the anesthesia department, a total of 15 cc of 0.5% flat marcaine was injected into a Mayo and digital block in the left foot.  The foot was then prepared and draped in the usual sterile orthopedic manner.  The foot was elevated from the operating table and bloodless with an Esmarch bandage.  The pneumatic ankle tourniquet was inflated to 250 mmHg and the foot was lowered to the operating table.  The jersey was reflected.  The foot was cleaned with a damp and dry sponge.  Attention then focused on the first metatarsophalangeal joint of the left foot.  An incision was made in this area approximately 6 cm in length.  The incision was deepened with a #15 blade.  All vessels encountered were ligated for hemostasis.  The skin and subcutaneous tissue were then dissected from the capsule.  Precautions were taken to preserve the neurovascular bundle.  A dorsal linear capsular incision was then made.  The capsule was then reflected from the head of the first metatarsal.  Attention then shifted to the first intermediate space where a lateral release was carried out.  A combination of sharp and blunt dissections was performed until the abductor tendons were identified and divided.  A lateral capsulotomy was performed.  Attention was then redirected to the medial eminence where a sagittal saw was used to resect the prominent medial eminence.  The incision was then extended proximally with further dissection down to the level of the bone.  Two 0.45 K wires were then inserted as access guides for the SCARF osteotomy.  A standard SCARF osteotomy was then performed.  The head of the first metatarsal was then moved laterally to reduce the first interspace in the metatarsal angle.  After adequate reduction of the bunion deformity was noted, the bone was temporarily fixed with a 0.45 K pin.  A 3.0 x 12 mm screw was then inserted using the standard AO method with compression noted.  A second 3.0 x 14 mm screw was also inserted with heavy compression noted.  The remaining prominent medial eminence was then resected with a sagittal saw.  Alternating rasps were then used to smooth the sharp bony edges.  The temporary fixation wires were then removed.  The tightness of the screws was checked again, which was noted.  Attention was focused on the medial capsule where a medial capsulorrhaphy was performed.  A straight stat was used to help remove part of the capsule.  The capsule was then reapproximated medially with Vicryl #2-0.  The dorsal capsule was then reapproximated with Vicryl #3-0 in running mode.  Subcutaneous closure was performed with Vicryl No. 4-0, followed by a subcutaneous stitch with Vicryl No. 5-0.  The skin was then closed with #4-0 nylon in a horizontal, mattress-like fashion. Attention was then directed to the left second toe. The arm was bloodless.  The tourniquet was elevated at 290 mmHg.  Construction lines were drawn on the left palm to identify the annular radius.  A transverse incision was made in the palm between the FCR and FCU, one finger width proximal to the interval between the glabrous skin of the palm and the normal skin of the forearm.  Blunt dissection exposed the antebrachial fascia.  Hemostasis was achieved by bipolar cauterization.  A distal window in the antebrachial fascia was then made.  Precautions have been taken to protect the underlying content.  A synovial elevator was used to palpate the inferior surface of the transverse carpal ligament, and the synovium was elevated above this inferior surface. Hamate sounds were then used to palpate the Hamate hood.  The Agee Inside Job was then inserted into the proximal incision.  The transverse carpal ligament was easily visualized through the gate.  Using volar pressure, the transverse carpal ligament was held against the gate while the instrument was inserted along the transverse carpal ligament to the distal end.  The distal end of the transverse carpal ligament was then identified in the window.  The blade was then elevated and the Agee Inside Job was removed, dividing the transverse carpal ligament under direct vision.  After complete division of the transverse carpal ligament, the Agee Inside Job was reinserted.  The radial and ulnar edges of the transverse carpal ligament were identified and complete release was achieved.  One ml of Celestone was then introduced into the carpal tunnel and irrigated freely.  The wound was then closed with a subcutaneous stitch with Prolene 3-0.  Steri-strips were applied and a sterile dressing was applied over the Steri-strips.  The tourniquet was deflated.  The patient was awakened from anesthesia and returned to the recovery room in satisfactory condition after tolerating the procedure well. Attention was turned to the right palm where, after sterile preparation, the right middle finger flexor sheath was injected with 0.5 mL of 1% plain Xylocaine and 0.5 mL of Depo-Medrol 40 mg/mL.  A bandage dressing was then applied. The patient was then awakened from anesthesia and returned to the recovery room in satisfactory condition after tolerating the procedure well.

transcription: OPERATION TITLE: ,1.  Arthrotomy, humeral head implant removal, right shoulder.,2.  Repair of a torn subscapularis tendon (rotator cuff tendon) acute tear.,3.  Debridement of the glenohumeral joint.,4.  Biopsy and culture of the right shoulder.,INDICATION FOR SURGERY:,The patient had done well following a previous total shoulder arthroplasty performed by Dr. X. However, the patient was lifted with subsequent significant pain and an apparent tear of his subscapularis.  The risks and benefits of the procedure were discussed at length with the patient, including but not limited to infection, nerve or artery damage, stiffness, loss of range of motion, incomplete pain relief, incomplete return of function, continued instability, re-tearing of the tendon, need for revision of their joint replacement, permanent damage to the nerve or artery, etc. The patient understood and wanted to continue.  Torn subscapularis tendon, right shoulder.,2.  Total right shoulder arthroplasty (Biomet system).,POSTOP DIAGNOSIS: ,1.  Torn subscapularis tendon, right shoulder.,2.  Total right shoulder arthroplasty (Biomet system).,3.  Diffuse synovitis, right shoulder.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was anesthetized in the supine position.  A Foley catheter was placed in his bladder.  He was then placed in a beach chair position.  He was brought to the side of the table and the torso was secured with towels and tape.  His head was then placed in a neutral position, without flexion or lateral extension.  It was taped to his forehead.  Care was taken to stay away from his ear cartilages and eye sockets.  The right upper limb was then prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  The patient received antibiotics well before the start of the procedure to reduce any risk of infection.  Once it was prepped and draped with the standard prep, it was prepped a second time with a chlorhexidine-type skin prep.  This was allowed to dry and the skin was then covered with Ioban bandages also to reduce the risk of infection. Additionally, preoperatively, the defibrillator function of the patient's pacemaker was turned off during this case.  A bipolar type cautery had to be used as opposed to the monopolar cautery. The patient's deltopectoral incision was then opened and extended proximally and distally.  The patient already had a significant amount of scarring in this interval.  Once we reached the deltoid and pectoral muscles, there were no cephalic veins apparent, therefore the rotator cuff interval had to be developed through a scar area.  This created a significant amount of bleeding.  As a result, a very slow and careful dissection was performed to isolate his coracoid and then his proximal humerus.  We took care to stay above the minor pectoralis and the conjoined tendon.  The deltoid had already begun to heal the proximal humerus, requiring a very extensive dissection to free the deltoid from the proximal humerus.  Likewise, the deltoid insertion had to be released by approximately 50% of its width to allow us sufficient mobility of the proximal humerus to be able to visualize the joint or component.  It was clear that the patient had an avulsion of the subscapularis tendon because the tissue on the anterior aspect of the shoulder was very thin.  The muscular component of the subscapularis could be located approximately 1 cm from the edge of the glenoid and approximately 3 cm from the lesser tuberosity.  The soft tissues in this area were significantly scarred down to the conjoined tendon, which had to be meticulously released.  The brachial plexus was identified as well as the axillary nerve.  Once this was completed, an arthrotomy was then performed, leaving tissue attached to the lesser tuberosity in case it was needed for further closure.  This revealed bloody fluid inside the joint.  We didn't think it was infected based on the fluid coming from the joint.  Sutures for the subscapularis re

The cruciate ligaments also appeared intact on direct stress testing.,HISTORY: This is a 36-year-old Caucasian female with a history of long-standing right knee pain. She chose to proceed with the surgery.  All risks and benefits of the operation were discussed with her.  She agreed with the treatment plan.,PROCEDURE: On 9/4/03, she was taken to the operating room of ABCD General Hospital.  She was placed supine on the operating table under general anesthesia administered by the anesthesia department.  His leg was placed in a Johnson knee brace and sterilely prepared and draped in the usual manner.  An incision was made in the inferolateral and parapatellar regions. The second portal site was initiated in the infero-medial parapatellar region.  Through this, an arthroscopic razor was placed and a chondroplasty of the patella was performed and removed the free joint debris.  Then, the camera was placed through the inferomedial portal.  An arthroscopic Bovie was placed through the inferolateral portal.  A release of the lateral retinaculum was then performed using the Bovie.  Hemostasis was controlled by electrocautery.  Then the knee was suctioned.  An Esmarch was used to exsanguinate the lower limb.  The tourniquet was inflated to 325 mmHg. The lateral and medial borders of the tibial tubercle were cleared of any soft tissue debris.  Next, an osteotome was then used to cut the tibial tubercle at a 45-degree angle leaving the base of the bony incision intact.  The tubercle was then pushed forward and medially, decreasing its Q angle and anteriorizing the tibial tubercle.  It was then held in place with a Steinmann pin.  Following this, two 4.5 mm cannulated, partially threaded screws were drilled using a standard technique to facilitate fixation of the tibial tubercle.  Excellent fixation was noted.  The Q angle has been reduced to approximately 15 degrees.  It was transferred about 1 cm in length.  The wound was abundantly irrigated and suctioned.  The medial retinaculum was then folded, causing further medialization of the patella.  The retinaculum was reapproximated with Vicryl #0. The anesthesia department administered general anesthesia to the patient.  All bony prominences were then well padded.  The right lower extremity was then properly positioned in a Johnson knee support.  At this time, 1% lidocaine with 20 cc of epinephrine was administered to the right knee intra-articularly under sterile conditions.  The right lower extremity was then prepared sterilely and draped in the usual sterile manner.  Then, after identifying all bony soft tissue landmarks, an inferolateral working portal was established by making a 1 cm transverse incision at the joint line lateral to the patellar tendon.  The cannula and trocar were then inserted through this, positioning the patellofemoral joint.  An arthroscopic camera was then inserted and the knee was examined sequentially, including the patellofemoral joint, medial and lateral grooves, medial lateral joints, and femoral notch.  During examination of the patellofemoral joint, changes in the patella in grade II chondromalacia were noted.  No detached bodies were observed in the two gutters.  On examination of the medial compartment, no chondromalacia or meniscal tears were noted.  In this area, attention was paid to establishing the inferomedial instrument portal.  This was first performed using a spinal needle for localization, followed by a 1 cm transverse incision at the joint line.  A probe was then inserted through this portal and the meniscus was probed further.  Again, there is no meniscal tear.  The knee was subjected to range of motion and there was no chondromalacia.  Upon examination of the femoral incision, an intact ACL was noted with a negative drawer sign.  The PCL was also found to be intact.  When observing the lateral compartment, the presence of a large bucket handle tear involving the lateral and posterior horns was noted.  It was reduced from the spot, but the white and red area was chosen to excise the bucket handle.  An arthroscopic scissor was then inserted and the two remaining brackets, the posterior and lateral brackets, were then clipped and Schlesinger forceps were then used to remove the resected meniscus.  It was found that the meniscus was followed as a whole and the entire piece was removed from the knee.  Photos were taken both during the pre-meniscal resection and after the meniscal resection.
