She has no family history of breast cancer.,MENTAL HEALTH AND SUBSTANCE ABUSE:,The patient has a history of depression.  No history of substance abuse., ADVANCED DIRECTIVE:, Unknown., PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, GENERAL: This is a female of slim build, who is not in acute distress.  VITAL SIGNS: Temperature 36.5, blood pressure 132/89, pulse 82, and weight 104 pounds.  HEAD AND NECK: Reveals reactive pupils bilaterally.  Soft neck.  No thrush.  No lymphadenopathy.  HEART: The heart sounds S1 and S2 regularly.  No whispers.  LUNGS: Clear bilaterally on auscultation.  ABDOMEN: Soft and non-tender with good bowel sounds.  NEUROLOGICAL: She is alert and oriented x3 without focal neurological deficit.  EXTREMITIES: Peripheral pulses are felt bilaterally.  She has no pedal edema, clubbing, or cyanosis.  GU: Examination of the external genitalia is trivial.  There are no lesions.,LABORATORY DATA:, From 07/11 shows hemoglobin and hematocrit of 16 and 46. Creatinine of 0.6.  LFT within normal limits.  Viral load less than 48 and CD4 count 918.,ASSESSMENT:,1.  Human immunodeficiency virus, stable on Trizivir.,2.  Hepatitis C with stable transaminases.,3.  History of depression, stable without medication.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE: The patient is a 20-year-old Caucasian male admitted to the emergency department of ABCD Hospital for evaluation of hydrocarbon aspiration.  The patient ingested “tiki oil” (kerosene, liquid paraffin, lemongrass oil) approximately two days before admission.  He then developed progressive symptoms of dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis with nausea and vomiting.  He was seen in the ABCD hospital emergency room, presenting a toxic appearance with an abnormal chest x-ray highlighting bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, more prominent on the right.  He had a temperature of 38.3 with a tachycardia near 130. The white count was 59,300 with a marked shift to the left.  Arterial blood gases showed a pH of 7.48, pO2 of 79, and pCO2 of 35. He was admitted for further medical management., MEDICAL HISTORY:, Aplastic crisis in childhood requiring splenectomy and cholecystectomy at age 9., DRUG ALLERGIES:, NONE KNOWN., CURRENT MEDICATION: , None., FAMILY HISTORY: , Non-contributory., SOCIAL HISTORY: Patient works at a local Christmas tree farm.  He smokes approximately one pack of cigarettes per day.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION:,Examination of ten systems significant for nausea, vomiting, fever, hemoptysis, and pleuritic chest pain.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: A toxic-appearing 20-year-old Caucasian male in mild respiratory distress.,VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure 122/74, pulse 130 and regular, respirations 24, temperature 38.3, and oxygen saturation 93%.,SKIN: No rashes, petechiae or bruising.,HEENT: Within normal limits.  The pupils are also round and responsive to light and accommodation.  Clean ears.  Throat clean. NECK: Supple without thyromegaly.  The lymph nodes are not palpable.,CHEST: Decreased breathing sounds bilaterally, more prominent on the right, at the right base.,HEART: No murmur or galloping rhythm.,ABDOMEN: Slight direct diffuse tenderness without rebound.  No detectable masses, pulsations or organomegaly. EXTREMITIES: No edema.  Pulses are equal and full bilaterally.,NEUROLOGICAL: Nonfocal.,DATABASE:, Chest x-ray, bilateral lower lobe pneumonia, larger on right.  ECG, sinus tachycardia, rate 130, normal intervals, no ST changes.  Arterial blood gas on 2 L of oxygen, pH 7.48, pO2 79 and pCO2 35. BLOOD STUDIES:, hematocrit is 43, white blood cells 59,300 with a shift to the left and platelet count 394,000. Sodium is 130, potassium 3.8, chloride is 97, bicarbonate 24, BUN 14, creatinine 0.8, random blood sugar 147, and calcium 9.4.,PRINT,1.  Hydrocarbon suction.,2.  Bilateral pneumonia with secondary aspiration pneumonia.,3.  Asplenic patient.,PLAN,1.  Intensive care monitoring.,2.  O2 protocol.,3.  Hydration.,4.  Antiemetic therapy.,5.  Parenteral antibiotics.,6.  Prophylactic proton pump inhibitors.,Patient will require intensive care monitoring and pulmonary medicine evaluation pending clinical evolution.,

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 71-year-old Caucasian female with a history of diabetes, osteoarthritis, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, asthma, obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP, diabetic foot ulcer, anemia, and cellulitis of the left lower extremity. She was brought by EMS to Erlanger Emergency Department with pulseless electrical activity. Her husband says he was at home with his wife when she showed up at his house complaining of a fever and chills. She became extremely unresponsive. We noticed a deterioration in his breathing. She took several of her metered-dose inhalers, then was placed on CPAP. He went to notify emergency medical services and when he returned it was found that she was not breathing. He said she had no breathing for more than 10 minutes. He states that EMS arrived at the home and she was not breathing. The patient was intubated on scene and upon arrival at Erlanger Medical Center, she was found to have repaired and dilated pupils. She was seen by me in the emergency department and was taking neo-synephrine, dopamine with a blood pressure of 97/22 with a rapid heart rate and again, in an unresponsive state. HISTORY:, Not contributory to the above.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Mother with history of coronary artery disease.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Patient is married. It does not use ethanol, tobacco or illicit products. She has a very supportive family unit.,DRUGS:, Augmentin; Detrol LA; lisinopril.,IMMUNIZATIONS:, Vaccinations were up to date against influenza, negative for Pneumovax.,ALLERGIES:, PENICILLIN.,LABORATORY AT PRESENTATION:, White blood cell count 11, hemoglobin 10.5, hematocrit 32.2, platelets 175,000. Sodium 148, potassium 5.2, BUN 30, creatinine 2.2 and glucose 216. PT was 22.4.,RADIOLOGICAL DATA:, Chest x-ray revealed diffuse pulmonary edema.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: blood pressure 97/52, pulse 79, respirations 16, O2 saturated 100%.,HEENT: The patient's pupils were fixed again, dilated and intucted on the monitor.,CHEST: Poor airflow bilaterally with bilateral rales.,CARDIOVASCULAR: Regular rhythm and rhythm.,ABDOMEN: The abdomen was obese, non-distended and non-tender.,EXTREMITIES: The left diabetic foot had drainage of pus oozing from the foot.,GU: A Foley catheter was in place.,PRINT AND PLAN:,1. Acute cardiac arrest with pulseless electrical activity with hypotensive shock and respiratory failure: Respiratory support will continue. Will eliminate pulmonary embolism, eliminate myocardial infarction. Continue the pressers. The patient is currently on dopamine, Neo-Synephrine and Levophed.,2. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome: Ventilatory support will continue.,3. Questionable Sepsis: Will obtain blood cultures, intravenous vancomycin and Rocephin will be administered.,4. Hypotensive shock: pressures will continue. Will check random cortisol. Hydrocortisone was added. The subsequent hospital care of this patient will be provided by Dr. R. The patient's condition was discussed with her daughter and her husband. The husband states that his wife has been very ill in the past with multiple admissions, but he has never seen her as seriously ill as at this event. He states that she was absolutely not breathing and he is aware of the seriousness of her illness and the seriousness of her current prognosis. Will obtain cardiology support with this admission and continue pressure and supportive therapy. The family will make an assessment and final decision regarding their long-term management after a 24-hour period.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT:, 1-month follow-up.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is an 88-year-old Caucasian female.  She is coming here today with a friend.  The patient has no complaints.  She says she feels good.  Her knees no longer hurt at all and she no longer needs Bextra.  I think the last steroid injection she received from Dr. XYZ really helped her.  The patient denies any shortness of breath or cough.  Does not have nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain.  No diarrhea or constipation.  She says her appetite is good.  She clears her plate at lunchtime.  She had no fever, chills or sweats.  The friend who accompanied her said that she was doing very well.  It seems that he eats very well at midday, but despite this, the patient continues to lose weight.  When I asked her what she eats for breakfast and dinner, she says she doesn't really eat anything.  Her only meal she eats at the nursing home is lunch and I just don't think she eats much the rest of the time.  She states that she really isn't hungry the rest of the time, except at lunchtime.  She denies any fever, chills or sweats.  We did some lab work during the last office visit and the CBC was essentially normal.  Overall metabolism was essentially normal, as was BUN of 32 and creatinine of 1.3.  It's pretty stable for her.  Liver enzymes were normal.  TSH was normal.  Free albumin was normal at age 23. She is on different antidepressants and this can cause difficulty with unintentional weight loss.,DRUGS:,Currently these are Aricept 10 mg daily, Prevacid 30 mg daily, Lexapro 10 mg daily, Norvasc 2.5 mg daily, Milk of Magnesia 30 cc daily, and Amanda 10 mg twice daily. day.,ALLERGIES:, No known drug allergies.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Revised 5/10/2004 and unchanged except for the addition of paranoia, which is much improved with her current medications.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Patient is a widow.  She is a non-smoker and a non-drinker.  She lives independently at the Kansas Christian Home, but she actually gets a lot of help with medications, a driver to get her here and to lunch.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION:,As above in HPI.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General: This is a well-developed, pleasant Caucasian female who appears thinner, especially in her face.  Clothing in the United States is looser.,Vital signs: Weight: 123, down 5 pounds from last month and down 11 pounds from May 2004. Blood pressure: 128/62.  Pulse: 60. Respirations: 20. Temperature: 96.8.,Neck: Flexible.  Carotid arteries are silent.,Chest: Clear to auscultation.,Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm and rhythm.,Abdomen: Soft and non-tender, non-distended with positive bowel sounds.  No organomegaly or masses are appreciated.,Extremities: Free of edema.,ASSESSMENT:,1.  Unintentional weight loss.  I think it's more of a lack of calories issue, although that doesn't seem to be a medical issue, although her dementia may make it hard for her to remember to eat, and with her antidepressant medications she's taking, she may just not have much of an appetite to eat unless food is prepared for her.,2.  Depression is fine.,3.  Paranoia is fine.,4.  Dementia, stable.,5.  Knee osteoarthritis, pain is much improved.,PLAN:,1.  Continue taking your current medications.,2.  I called and spoke with a doctor at the hospital.  We discussed different options.  We decided that the patient should also have her evening meal at the nursing home and take a drinking supplement such as Ensure at breakfast.  Connie will weigh the patient once a week and I will see the patient in a month.  We can see how she is doing at that time.  If she continues to lose weight despite eating better, I think we will need to do further evaluation.

transcription: S:, XYZ is here today and hasn't felt well for three days.  Her body hurts a little.  She coughs.  Her throat hurts, especially when she coughs.  His cough produces green-colored sputum.  She had a few chills.  No vomiting.  No diarrhea.  She sleeps well.  She doesn't feel like she needs anything for her cough.  She called yesterday and received a refill for her Keflex.  She took two Keflex this morning and she feels a little better now.  She is in tears, just tired of feeling exhausted.,O:,Vital signs according to the chart.  Respirations 15. Examination: Non-toxic.  No acute distress.  Alert and oriented.  HEENT: TMs are clear bilaterally, without erythema or swelling.  Clear external channels.  Clear eardrum.  The conjunctivas are clear.  Clear nasal mucosa.  Clear oropharynx with moist mucous membranes.  THE NECK is soft and supple without lymphadenopathy.  LUNGS are coarse, without rhonchi or severe wheezing.  THE HEART has a regular rhythm without murmuring.  THE ABDOMEN is soft and not tender. Chest radiography reveals no obvious consolidation or infiltrate.  We will send the x-ray for an over reading. Flu test is negative.  Rapid strep screen is negative.,A:, Bronchitis/URI.,P: , ,1.  Motrin as needed for fever and discomfort.,2.  Pushing fluids.,3.  Continue on the Keflex.,4.  Follow up with Dr. ABC if symptoms persist or worsen, otherwise as needed.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE: The patient was recently admitted for pneumonia with a positive blood count.  She was treated with IV antibiotics and p.o. antibiotics; she improved on that.  She was home and doing pretty well for about 10-12 days when she arrived at the emergency room with a temperature of 102. She was found to have strep.  She was treated with penicillin and sent home.  She returned around 8 a.m. after vomiting and a probable convulsion.  The temperature was 104.5; she was lethargic after that.  She had an LP, which was nothing remarkable.  She had blood cultures which revealed nothing.  CSF has not developed anything at this stage. PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: She is alert, recovering from anesthesia.  The head, eyes, ears, nose and throat show no particularity.  The chest is clear on auscultation and percussion.  The abdomen is soft.  The extremities are not remarkable.,LABORATORY STUDIES: The number of blanks in the emergency room was 9.8 with a slight shift.  CSF glucose was 68, protein 16 and there were no cells.  The Gram stain was unremarkable.,ASSESSMENT:,I suspect this patient is having a febrile seizure.,PLAN:,My plan is to readmit the patient to check her temperature and assess her white count.  I will observe it during the night.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT:, The patient is here for a two-month follow-up., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a 55-year-old Caucasian female.  She suffers from hypertension.  She had no difficulty experiencing chest pain.  She's a little out of breath just going up the stairs.  She only gets dizzy occasionally if she bends over and then stands up quickly.  She had no nausea, vomiting or diarrhea.  She has severe osteoarthritis in her left knee and will likely have a total knee replacement with Dr. XYZ in January next year.  The patient wants to lose weight before her operation.  She is worried about a possible risk of coronary heart disease or stroke.  She hasn't had any symptoms of heart disease other than shortness of breath from exertion, which she says is pretty stable.  She had a fairly normal lipid profile, last checked on 11/26/2003.  Cholesterol was 194, triglycerides 118, HDL 41 and LDL 129. The patient is a non-smoker.  In November 2003, his fasting blood sugar was within normal limits, at 94. His fasting insulin level was normal.  Repeat non-fasting blood sugar was 109 on 06/22/2004.  She has no history of diabetes.  She does not exercise regularly and is unable to do so because of knee pain.  She also had problems with lower back pain.  Lower back x-ray showed a mild compression fracture of L1.  She did not suffer any falls that could have contributed to a compression fracture.  She had a normal DEXA exam on 07/11/2003 which is not really consistent with a compression fracture of the lumbar spine; however, it is possible that arthritis contributes to falsely high bone density on DEXA analysis.  She wants to consider treatment to prevent further compression fractures and possible osteoporosis.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily, Prozac 20 mg daily, Vioxx 25 mg daily, vitamin C 250 mg daily, vitamin E three to four tablets daily, calcium with D 1500 mg daily, multivitamin daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, Monopril 40 mg daily, Celexa p.r.n.,ALLERGIES:,Bactrim, which causes nausea and vomiting, and duct tape.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Hypertension.,2.  Depression.,3.  Myofascitis of the feet.,4.  Severe osteoarthritis of the knee.,5.  Removal of melanoma from the right thigh in 1984.,6.  Breast biopsy in January 1997, which was benign.,7.  Holter monitor history showing ectopic beat.  The echocardiogram was normal.  It was in 1998.,8.  Compression fracture of L1, cause unknown.  She had no injuries.  Interestingly, the DEXA scan was normal on 07/11/2003, which is somewhat contradictory.,SOCIAL HISTORY: The patient is married.  She is a non-smoker and non-drinker.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION:,As per HPI.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General: This is a well-developed, well-nourished, pleasant Caucasian female who is overweight.,Vital Signs: Weight: Denied.  Blood pressure: 148/82, rechecked by myself with a large cuff, it was 125/60.  Pulse: 64. Respirations: 20. Temperature: 96.3., Neck: Flexible.  Carotid arteries are silent.,Chest: Clear to auscultation.,Cardiovascular: Revealed regular rate and rhythm with no murmur, S3 or S4.,Extremities: Revealed no edema.,Neurologic: Grossly intact.,RADIOLOGY: EKG revealed normal sinus rhythm, rate 61, borderline first degree AV block, and poor R wave progression in leads previous.,EVALUATION:,1.  Hypertension, well controlled.,2.  Family history of stroke.,3.  Compression fracture of L1, mild.,4.  Osteoarthritis of the knee.,5.  Slightly abnormal chest x-ray.,PLAN:,1.  We will obtain cardiac C-reactive protein.,2.  We discussed weight loss options.  I would recommend Weight Watchers or possibly have him see a dietician.  She will consider these options.  She is not able to exercise regularly at the moment due to knee pain.,3.  We recommend a screening colonoscopy.  She states that we have discussed this in the past and she canceled her appointment to make this happen.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Monitoring for hypertension and high cholesterol.,SUBJECTIVE: This is a 78 year old man who recently underwent a right knee replacement as well as back surgery approximately a year and a half ago.  He did well with that.  He does most of the things he wants to do.  He travels whenever he gets the chance and has just returned from a cruise.  He denies any type of chest pain, heaviness, tightness, pressure, shortness of breath on stairs only, cough or palpitations.  He sees Dr. Ferguson.  He is known to have Crohn's disease and he takes care of it for him.  He sees Dr. Roszhart for his prostate exam.  He is a non-smoker and denies that his ankles are swollen.,DRUGS:, refer to the table.,ALLERGIES:, refer to the table.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,,Vital Vitals: weight; 172 lbs, up to 2 lbs, B/P; 150/60, T; 96.4,P; 72 and regular.  ,General: A 78-year-old man who does not appear to be in acute distress.  Glasses.  Good teeth.,CV: Distant S1, S2 without murmuring or galloping. No carotid sounds. P: 2+ all around.,Lungs: Decreased with increasing AP diameter. ,Abdomen: Soft, active bowel sounds x 4 quadrants. No tenderness, distension, masses or organomegaly noted. Extremities: Well healed surgical scar on right knee.  No edema.  Hand grips are strong and even.,Back: surgical scar in the lower back.,Neuro: intact.  A&O. Moves all four without focal motor or sensory deficits.,PRINT:,1.  Hypertension.,2.  Hypercholesterolemia.,3.  Osteoarthritis.,4.  Fatigue.,PLAN:,We will check a BMP, lipid, liver, CPK and CBC profile.  Renew your medications x 3 months.  I gave him a copy of Partners in Prevention.  Increase its Altace to 5 mg daily for better blood pressure control.  Diet, exercise and weight loss, and we'll see him again in three months and p.r.n.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 43-year-old black man with no apparent past medical history who presented to the emergency department with the chief complaint of weakness, malaise, and dyspnea on exertion for approximately one month.  The patient also reports a weight loss of 15 pounds.  He denies having a fever, chills and sweats.  He denies cough and diarrhea.  He has mild anorexia.,MEDICAL HISTORY: Mostly unremarkable, except for chest wall cysts that were apparently biopsied by a dermatologist in the past, and he was diagnosed as benign.  He had a recent PPD which was negative in August 1994.,DRUGS:, None.,ALLERGIES:,No known drug allergies.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,He drinks occasionally and is a non-smoker.  The patient participated in homosexual activities in Haiti in 1982, which he described as "very active." Denies intravenous drug use.  Patient is currently employed.,FAMILY HISTORY:,Unremarkable.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: He is a thin, black male, cachectic, speaking in full sentences with oxygen.,VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure 96/56, heart rate 120. No change with orthostatics.  Temperature 101.6 degrees Fahrenheit.  Respirations 30.,HEENT: Fundus examination normal.  He suffers from oral thrush.,LYMPH: He has marked lymphadenopathy including bilateral right and posterior cervical epitrochlear lymph nodes.,NECK: no goiter, no jugular venous distension.,CHEST: bilateral basilar crackles and egophony in the right and left middle lung fields.,HEART: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs or scratches. canter.,ABDOMEN: Soft and not tender.,GENITOURINARY: Normal.,RECTAL: Unremarkable.,SKIN: Patient has multiple subcutaneous mobile nodules on the chest wall that are not painful.  He has very pale palms., LABORATORY AND X-RAY DATA:, Sodium 133, potassium 5.3, BUN 29, creatinine 1.8.  Hemoglobin 14, white count 7100, platelet count 515. Total protein 10, albumin 3.1, AST 131, ALT 31. Urinalysis shows 1+ protein, traces of blood.  Total bilirubin 2.4, direct bilirubin 0.1.  Arterial blood gas: pH 7.46, pC02 32, p02 46 on room air.  The electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm.  Chest x-ray shows bilateral alveolar and interstitial infiltrates.,PRINT:,1.  Bilateral pneumonia; suspect atypical pneumonia, exclude Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia and tuberculosis.,2.  Thrush.,3.  Elevated unconjugated bilirubins.,4.  Hepatitis.,5.  High globulin fraction.,6.  Renal failure.,7.  Subcutaneous nodules.,8.  Risky sexual behavior in 1982 in Haiti.,PLAN:,1.  Sputum induced,excludes Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia and tuberculosis.,2.  Start Bactrim and erythromycin intravenously.,3.  Start prednisone.,4.  Oxygen.,5.  Nystatin swish and swallow.,6.  Dermatological biopsy of the lesions.,7.  Check HIV and RPR.,8.  Administer Pneumovax, tetanus vaccine and Heptavax if indicated.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE: The patient is coming in today for a complete evaluation.  She is well known to me. I'll have him check a lipid profile as mentioned, and call him with that.  A screening mammogram will be performed.  She then refused a sigmoidoscopy.  I look forward to seeing her again in a year and as needed.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 70-year-old woman with a past medical history of chronic kidney disease, stage 4; history of diabetes mellitus; diabetic nephropathy; peripheral vascular disease, recent post-PTA condition of right leg, admitted to hospital due to swelling of right hand and left foot.  The patient said that the right hand was very swollen, very painful, that he could not move his fingers, and that the left foot was also very swollen and very painful, and that again he could not move his toes. He arrived at the emergency room and was diagnosed with gout and gout attacks.  I was asked to see the patient regarding chronic kidney disease.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Type 2 diabetes mellitus.,2.  Diabetic nephropathy.,3.  Chronic kidney disease, stage 4.,4.  Hypertension.,5.  Hypercholesterolemia and hyperlipidemia.,6.  Peripheral vascular disease, post-recent status, last week PTA of right lower extremity.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Negative for smoking and alcohol consumption.,CURRENT HOME MEDICATIONS:, NovoLog 20 units with each meal, Lantus 30 units at bedtime, Crestor 10 mg daily, Micardis 80 mg daily, Imdur 30 mg daily, Amlodipine 10 mg per day, Coreg 12.5 mg. twice daily, Lasix 20 mg daily, Ecotrin 325 mg daily, and calcitriol 0.5 mcg daily.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS:,The patient denies any complaints, states that the right hand and left foot were very swollen and very painful and presented to the emergency department.  Additionally, she couldn't urinate and states that as soon as they put Foley in, 500 ml of urine came out.  They also put her on steroids and colchicine, and the pain is getting better and the swelling is getting better.  Deny all fever and chills.  Denies any dysuria, frequency or hematuria.  Reports that urine output decreased significantly and she could not urinate.  Denies cough, hemoptysis or sputum production.  Denies any chest pain, orthopnea, or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General: Patient is alert and oriented, without acute distress.,Vital signs: Blood pressure 126/67, temperature 97.9, pulse 71, and respirations 20. Patient's weight is 105.6 kg.,Head: Normocephalic.,Neck: Supple.  No JVD.  No lymphadenopathy.,Chest: Symmetric.  No retraction.,Lungs: clear.,Heart: RRR without breath.,Abdomen: obese, soft and non-tender.  No bounce.  No protection.,Extremity: She has 2+ bilateral pretibial edema in lower extremity, but also left foot, dorsum of left foot and right hand are swollen and very tender to movement of toes and also fingers in these extremities.,LABORATORY TESTS:, have shown urine culture to be negative to date.  The patient's white blood cell is 12.7, hematocrit 26.1.  Patient has 90% segments and 0% bands.  serum sodium 133, potassium 5.9, chloride 100, bicarb 21, glucose 348, BUN 57, creatinine is 2.39, calcium 8.9 and uric acid yesterday was 10.9.  Sed level was 121. BNP was 851. Urinalysis showed 15-20 white blood cells, 3+ protein, 3+ blood with 25-30 red blood cells as well.,PRINT:,1.  Urinary tract infection.,2.  Acute gout attack.,3.  Diabetes mellitus with diabetic nephropathy.,4.  Hypertension.,5.  Hypercholesterolemia.,6.  Peripheral vascular disease,condition after recent right-sided PTA.,7.  Chronic kidney disease, stage 4.,PLAN: ,At present, I agree with the treatment.  We will add 50 mg of allopurinol per day.  This is secondary because the patient is already taking colchicine, and we will also stop Micardis, increase Lasix to 40 twice daily, and monitor lab results.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Abdominal pain and discomfort for 3 weeks., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 38-year-old white female with no known medical problems who complains of abdominal pain and discomfort for 3 weeks. She was in her normal state of health when she began experiencing diffuse abdominal pain and localized discomfort primarily in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant. She also complained of indigestion and pain in her right shoulder blade during this same period. None of these complaints are alleviated or aggravated by food. She denies any use of NSAIDs. The patient went to an outside hospital where a right upper quadrant ultrasound revealed no gallbladder disease but suspected a liver mass. A CT scan and MRI of the abdomen and pelvis showed a right adrenal mass measuring 12.5 x 10.9 x 11.1 cm and an intrahepatic mass measuring 7.1 x 5.4 x 6.5 cm at the dome of the liver. Chest CT scan revealed multiple small (<5 mm) bilateral pulmonary nodules. Total bone scintigraphy revealed no abnormal uptake. She was transferred to Methodist for further care. The patient states that she has a good appetite and denies any weight loss. She denies having a fever or chills. She noticed increasing dyspnea with moderate exercise, but not at rest. She denies having palpitations. She sometimes has nausea, but not vomiting, constipation or diarrhea. Over the past 2 months, she has noticed an increase in facial hair and mustache. There is a strong family history of colon cancer and other cancers in his family. She was told there was a genetic abnormality in her family, but she doesn't remember the name of the syndrome. She had a colonoscopy and polyp removal when she was 14. Her last colonoscopy was 2 months ago and was unremarkable. PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: None.  No history of hypertension, diabetes, heart disease, liver disease or cancer.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, Bilateral tubal ligation in 2001, colon polyp removed at age 14.,GYN HISTORY:, Gravida 2, Para 2, Ab 0. Menstrual periods have been regular, last menstrual period was almost a month ago. No menorrhagia. I have never had a mammogram. Has annual Pap tests which have all been normal.,FAMILY HISTORY: Mother is 61 years old and brother is 39 years old, both alive and in good health. His father died at age 48 of colon cancer and questionable pancreatic cancer. A paternal uncle died at age 32 of colon cancer and bile duct cancer. A paternal uncle had colon cancer in his 40s. Around thirty cancers are seen on the paternal side of the family, many of them colon; two women had breast cancer. The family was told there was a genetic syndrome in the family, but no one remembers the name of the syndrome.,SOCIAL HISTORY: No use of tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drugs. The patient was born and raised in Oklahoma. No known exposure. Married with 2 children.,MEDICATIONS:, None.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:, No headaches. No visual, hearing or swallowing difficulties. No cough or hemoptysis. No chest pain, PND, orthopnea. No changes in bowel or urinary habits. Otherwise, as indicated in HPI.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VS: T 97.6 BP 121/85 P 84 R 18 O2 Sat 100% on ambient air,GEN: Pleasant, slim woman in slight distress secondary to abdominal pain and discomfort.,HEENT: Pupils also round and reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact. Anicteric. Clear sclerae. Pink conjunctiva. Moist mucous membranes. No oropharyngeal lesions.,NECK: Supple, no masses, jugular venous distension or bruits.,LUNGS: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.,HEART: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmuring, galloping, rubbing.,BREASTS: Symmetrical, no skin changes, no discharge, no masses,ABDOMEN: Soft with active bowel sounds. There is minimal diffuse tenderness on examination. No masses palpated. There is fullness in the upper right quadrant with a negative Murphy sign. No bounce or guard. The span of the liver is 12 cm per percussion, but is not palpable under the costal margin. No splenomegaly., PELVIC: not done, EXT: no clubbing, cyanosis or edema. 2+ bilateral impulses.,NEURO: cranial nerves in

He says he was doing his usual job when he felt a foreign body sensation.  He tried to push the pain away at work, but he felt a persistent pain that gradually got worse throughout the day.  He has no significant blurred vision or photophobia.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE: This is a 2-year-old girl who comes just to recheck her weight, her respiratory status and her diet.  The patient is in foster care, has a long history of prematurity, was born at 22 weeks.  She has chronic lung disease, is on a ventilator, but does sprints, is doing very well, doing up to 4 1/2 hours of sprints twice a day and can increase by 15 minutes every three days or so; which she tolerated quite well as long as they distracted her towards the end, otherwise she became sort of tachypnoeic.  She is taking 2 1/2 liters of oxygen and needs it.  His diet fluctuated.  They tried to find what worked best with her.  She has been taking Pediasure for the calorie boost, but it is really making her abdomen distended and constipated.  They ate more pureed foods and that seemed to relax her, so they ate more Isomil 24 cal and baby food and less Pediasure.  She was hospitalized a few weeks ago for abdominal distension.  Dr. XYZ has been working with his G-tube, increasing the size of his mic button, but also doing some applications of silver nitrate, and he is going to evaluate it again next week, but they are happy with the appearance of his G-tube.  She has also seen Dr. Eisenbaum, just got a new pair of glasses this week and is seeing him in a few weeks for re-evaluation.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:,Flagyl, vitamins, Zyrtec, albuterol and Colace.,DRUG ALLERGIES:,None.,FAMILY SOCIAL HISTORY:,As mentioned, she is in foster care.  The foster mom is actually going to be out of town for a week from the 19th to the 23rd, so she will likely be hospitalized in respite care because there are no other foster care situations that can handle the patient.  The biological mother and grandmother visit her on Thursday for about an hour.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: The patient eats fairly well, sleeps well and does her sprints well.  Some difficulty with her hard stools versus soft stools as mentioned with the dietary situation in HPI.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vital Signs: She weighs 28 pounds 8 ounces today and is 33-1/2 inches tall.  She is on 2-1/2 liters, but she is not on ventilation currently, she is doing her sprints, and her respiratory rate is around 40.,HEENT: Sclerae and conjunctivae are clear.  The TMs are clear.  The nares are patented.  The oropharynx is clear.  Tracheal site shows no signs of infection.,Chest: Coarse.  She wheezes a little, but she moves air pretty well. Abdomen: Positive, gentle bowel sounds.  The G-tube site seems pretty clean and healthy today.  No signs of infection.  His tone is good.  Capillary refill lasts less than three seconds.,ASSESSMENT:,A 2-year-old girl with chronic lung disease, doing sprints, some intestinal difficulties, as well as weight gain problems due to the high energy expenditure from the sprints she does.,PLAN:,At this point, continue with Isomil and pureed baby food, a little Pediasure.  They are going to see Dr. XYZ towards the end of this month and follow up with Dr. Eisenbaum.  I would like to see her again in about six weeks, but we need to monitor her weight closely and call her first if there are any problems.  She's just making a wonderful progression in her development.  Every time I see her I am very impressed, this passes on to the adoptive mother.  About 25 minutes spent with the patient, most of it in counseling.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Weak and frail.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 75-year-old Caucasian female who presents today complaining of feeling weak and shaky.  When questioned further, she described shortness of breath mainly from walking.  She denies having chest pain.  She denies cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea and wheezing.  She denies any syncope, presyncope or palpitations.  Her symptoms have lasted for quite a long time but have gotten worse recently.,MEDICAL HISTORY: She has a fairly long medical history, but she is a somewhat poor historian and is unable to provide details about her history.  She states that she has an underlying history of heart disease, but is unable to give significant details.  She also has a history of hypertension and type II diabetes, but is not currently taking any medications.  She also had a history of pulmonary embolism approximately four years ago, hyperlipidemia, peptic ulcer disease, and recurrent urinary tract infections.  Surgical procedures include appendectomy, cesarean section, cataract, and hernia repair.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:, She takes two different medications, the name of which she does not remember or why she is taking them.,ALLERGIES:, She has no known medical allergies.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Notable for coronary artery disease, stroke, and heart failure congestive.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, She is a widow and lives alone.  Denies any tobacco or alcohol use.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Dyspnea on exertion.  No chest pain or tightness, fever, chills, sweating, cough, hemoptysis, wheezing, or swelling of lower extremities.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General: She is alert but appears somewhat confused and is unable to provide specific details of her history.,Vital signs: Blood pressure: 146/80.  Pulse: 68. Weight: 147 pounds.,HEENT: Nothing remarkable.,Neck: Supple without JVD, lymphadenopathy or bruit.,Chest: Clear on auscultation.,Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm.,Abdomen: Soft.,Extremities: No edema.,LABORATORY:,O2 saturated at 100% at rest and with exercise.  The electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm.  Non-specific changes in the S-T segment.  Chest x-ray pending.,ASSESSMENT/PLAN:,1.  Dyspnea on exertion, uncertain etiology.  The mother would be concerned about the possibility of coronary artery disease given the patient's underlying risk factors.  We will ask the patient to sign a release of records so that we can review her history.  Consider preparing for a stress test.,2.  Hypertension, blood pressure is acceptable today.  I don't know exactly what, if the patient is taking antihypertensive agents.  We will have to ask her to call us what the names of her medications are, so we can see exactly what she is taking.,3.  History of diabetes.  Again, I don't know if the patient is taking anything for this particular problem during her last hemoglobin A1C test.  I must obtain additional history and review records before proceeding with treatment recommendations.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION:, Left flank pain, ureteral stones.,BRIEF HISTORY:, The patient is a 76-year-old woman who was referred to us by Dr.  The patient presented with a left ureteral stone measuring approximately 1.3 cm, according to his history.  The patient has been experiencing abdominal and back pain for four to five days.  The patient has nausea and vomiting.  The patient wants something done about the stone.  The patient denies any hematuria, dysuria, burning or pain.  Patient denies any fever.,MEDICAL HISTORY: , Negative.,SURGICAL HISTORY: ,Years ago she had surgery that she does not remember.,MEDICATIONS: , None.,ALLERGIES: , None.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: ,Denies any seizure disorder, chest pain, denies any shortness of breath, denies any dysuria, burning or pain, denies any nausea or vomiting this time.  The patient has a history of nausea and vomiting, but is improving.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: The patient is not febrile.  Vital signs are stable.,HEART: Regular rate and rhythm.,ABDOMEN: Mild left flank pain and left lower abdominal pain.,The remainder of the examination is benign.,LABORATORY DATA: White count 7.8, hemoglobin 13.8, and platelets 234,000. Patient's creatinine is 0.92.,ASSESSMENT:,1.  Pain in left flank.,2.  Left ureteral stone.,3.  Nausea and vomiting.,SCHEDULE:,Schedule laser lithotripsy tomorrow.  Options such as watchful waiting, laser lithotripsy, and shock wave lithotripsy were discussed.  The patient has a fairly enlarged stone.  Failure of the procedure if the stone is significantly impacted in the ureteral wall has been discussed.  The patient understood that the success of the operation may or may not be 100%, that she may need shock wave lithotripsy if we are unable to remove the entire stone in one session.  The patient understood all the risks and benefits of the procedure and wanted to continue.  The need for a stent was also discussed with the patient.  The patient will be operated on tomorrow.  Plan for continued antibiotics, obtain a urinalysis and culture, and plan for KUB to evaluate the exact location of the stone before surgery tomorrow.

Rule out any urinary tract infections.,PLAN:,1.  No further intervention from my point of view.,2.  Agree with discharge and outpatient follow-up.  Further intervention will depend on his clinical condition.  She understood and accepted perfectly.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: , Chest pain and fever. , HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , This 48-year-old married white woman presents to the emergency department after two days of increasing fever with a recent diagnosis of urinary tract infection during outpatient treatment with nitrofurantoin.  The patient noted that she had started feeling unwell earlier on the day of admission and had an episode of substernal chest discomfort associated with nausea, dizziness, and sweating.  The patient has a medical history of diabetes and hypertension.  In addition, the patient complained of some discomfort in her neck and head for which she underwent a lumbar puncture in the emergency room; it was normal, the causes turned out to be normal too.  Patient denies nosebleeds, visual changes, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or changes in bowel habits.  She did not present any musculoskeletal or neurological deficit. transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is an 88-year-old white female, ambulant at home with a walker, who presented to the emergency department this morning after an accidental fall at home.  The patient states that she was on the ladder on Saturday and came down after the ladder.  I felt some pain in my left hip.  She then fell, injuring her left shoulder.  It is unclear how long she was on the ground.  She was taken by EMS to the hospital where x-rays revealed she had a left proximal humerus fracture and a non-displaced left hip fracture.  An orthopedist was consulted.  Given the nature of the injury and unclear events, a thorough workup was performed, including a CT scan of the head and a CT scan of the abdomen, which identified no signs of intracranial injury and only kidney stones.  She is currently complaining of pain in her left shoulder.  She states that she also experiences hip pain when moving her leg.  She denies any numbness or paresthesia.  She says that before that, she was relatively active in her household.  She takes care of her daughter who is a mess.  The patient denies any other injuries.  Denies back pain.,PREVIOUS MEDICAL HISTORY:, Extensive, including coronary artery disease, peripheral vascular disease, post-MI status, history of COPD, diverticular disease, irritable bowel syndrome, GERD, PMR, depressive disorder, and hypertension.,PREVIOUS SURGICAL HISTORY:, Includes repair of intertrochanteric fracture of the right femur.,ALLERGIES,1.  PENICILLIN.,2.  SULPHA.,3.  ACE INHIBITOR.,CURRENT DRUGS,1.  Lipitor 20 mg qd.2.  Metoprolol 25 mg twice daily,3.  Plavix 75 mg once a day.,4.  Aspirin 325 mg.,5. Citalopram 20 mg q.d.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, She denies alcohol or tobacco use.  She takes care of her daughter, who is widowed and lives at home.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Not available.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:, The patient is hard of hearing.  She also has vision problems.  Denies headache syndrome.  Currently he denies chest pain or shortness of breath.  She denies having abdominal pain.  Currently, she is experiencing pain in her left hip and left shoulder.  No urinary frequency or dysuria.  No skin lesions.  She has had swelling in both lower limbs for several weeks.  She denies endocrinopathies.  Psychiatric problems include chronic depression.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: The patient is alert and responsive.,EXTREMITIES: In the left upper extremity, there is moderate swelling and bruising in the brachial compartment.  It is diffusely painful over the proximal humerus.  She is unable to actively raise her arm due to the pain.  The neurovascular examination of the left upper limb is otherwise intact with a 1+ radial pulse.  She presents with chronic degenerative changes in the MP and IP joints of both hands.  Lower left extremity, the thigh compartment is flexible.  She has pain with tenderness when rolling the log at the greater trochanter.  The patient feels pain with any attempt to flex the hip, passively or actively.  Knee range of motion is between 5 and 60 degrees with no specific point tenderness, no joint effusion, and an intact extensive mechanism.  She presents 2 to 3+ bilateral pretibial and pedal edemas.  The patient has a weak motor response in the left lower limb.  She has a dorsal pulse of 1+.  His sensory examination is intact plantarly and dorsally on the foot.  There is no evidence of dislocation.  There was a series of AP pelvis as well as left hip, which identified a type 1, non-displaced femoral neck fracture with valgus impaction.  There is also evidence of severe degenerative disc disease with degenerative scoliosis of the LS spine.

transcription: DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:,1. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with acute hypercapnic respiratory failure.,2. Chronic atrial fibrillation with prior ablation performed under Coumadin treatment.,3. Mitral stenosis.,4. Distant history of lung cancer with previous resection of the left upper lobe.,5. Anxiety and depression.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,Details are present in the dictated report.,BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE:,The patient is a 71 year old lady who presented with increased shortness of breath lasting for a day. She denied having chest pain, fever, or cough with purulent sputum at that time. She was treated empirically with a course of Avelox antibiotics for ten days. She also received steroids, prednisolone 60 mg, and respiratory treatments with albuterol, ipratropium and her bronchodilator treatment was also optimized with theophylline. She continued to receive Coumadin for her chronic atrial fibrillation. His heart rate was monitored and maintained in the 60s-70s. On the third day of admission, she developed respiratory failure which worsened with fatigue and therefore required intubation and ventilation. She was placed on mechanical ventilation from 01/29 to 02/06/06. She was extubated on 2/6 and put on BI-PAP. Pressures were gradually increased from 10 and 5 to 15 of BI-PAP and 5 of E-PAP with FIO2 of 35% at the time of transfer to Kindred. His bronchospasm also responded to aggressive bronchodilation and corticosteroid therapy.,DISCHARGE DRUGS: Prednisolone 60 mg orally once daily, Albuterol 2.5 mg nebulized every 4 hours, Atrovent Respules nebulized every 6 hours, Pulmicort 500 micrograms nebulized twice every 8 hours, Coumadin 5 mg orally oral once daily, magnesium oxide 200 mg orally. once a day.,TRANSFER INSTRUCTIONS:,The patient should be strictly maintained on two-level PAP of 15 I-PAP/E-PAP of 5 cm and FIO2 of 35% most of the time during the day. She can be placed on a nasal cannula at a rate of 2 to 3 liters per minute with an O2 saturation of 90 to 92% at mealtimes only, and this should be limited to 1 to 2 hours at each meal. On admission, his potassium level had increased slightly to 5.5 and therefore ACE inhibitor treatment had to be stopped. We can do it again later once his blood pressure control is better, if necessary.

Hypertension.,DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Serotonin syndrome secondary to high doses of Prozac.,2.  Atypical chest pain with myocardial infarction excluded.,3.  Diabetes mellitus.,4.  Hypertension.,5.  Diarrhea resolved.,ADMISSION SUMMARY:,The patient is a 53-year-old female with a history of hypertension, diabetes, and depression.  Unfortunately, her husband left her 10 days before her admission and she developed severe anxiety and depression.  She suffered from chest pain and severe vomiting and diarrhea.  It is worth noting that it underwent a nuclear strength test in February this year, which was normal.  She was readmitted to the hospital to rule out myocardial infarction and for further evaluation.,PHYSICAL ADMISSION:,Significant for her to be afebrile.  Apparently a slightly elevated temperature was recorded at 100. His blood pressure was 140/82, heart rate was 83, and his oxygen saturation was 100%.  She was in tears.  HEART: Heart sounds were regular.  LUNGS: Clear.  ABDOMEN: Soft.  Apparently there was some level of agitation and akathexia.  She was also pacing., ADMISSION LABS: Showed CBC with white count 16.9, hematocrit 46.9, platelets 318,000. She had 80% neutrophils, no bands.  The UA of 02/05 was negative.  The chemical panel shows sodium 138, potassium 3.5, creatinine 0.6, calcium 8.3, lactate 0.9, ALT was 39, AST 38, total bilirubin 0.6.  His initial CK came out at 922. CK-MB was weak.  Troponin was 0.04.  She had normal amylase and lipase.  Previous TSH a few days ago was normal.  Chest X-ray was negative.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,1.  Serotonin syndrome.  After reassessment of the patient, including evaluation of laboratory abnormalities, it was felt that she likely had serotonin syndrome with obvious agitation, increased bowel activity, restlessness, and elevated white counts and CPK.  She had no fever, tremors, or hyperreflexia.  His CPK improved with IV fluids.  She improved considerably after stopping her Prozac.  His white count returned to normal.  By the time she was discharged, she really felt back to normal.,2.  Depression and anxiety with history of panic attacks exacerbated by her husband's departure 2 weeks ago.  We discussed it.  I have also discussed the situation with a psychiatrist who is available on Friday and have discussed the situation with the patient.  As for her medications, we stop the Prozac and she is re-evaluated by Dr. X Monday or Tuesday.  Cymbalta has been recommended as a good alternative medication for her.  The patient has a counselor.  It's going to be difficult for her to go home alone.  I discussed resources with her.  She has a daughter coming to town in a few weeks, but she has a friend she can call and stay with for the next few days.,3.  Hypertension.  She will continue to take her usual medications.,4.  Diabetes mellitus.  She will continue to take her usual medications.,5.  The diarrhea is gone.  His electrolyte abnormalities disappeared.  She had received fluid rehydration.,DISPOSITION:,She was sent home.  She will stay with a friend for a few days.  She will follow up with Dr. X on Monday or Tuesday.  Apparently, Dr. Y has already discussed the situation and the plan with her.  She will continue to take her regular medications, with the exception of stopping Prozac.,DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS: ,Include,1.  Omeprazole 20 mg per day.,2.  Temazepam 15 mg in the evening.,3.  Ativan 1 mg one-half to three times daily as needed.,4.  Cozaar 50 per day.,5.  Prandin 1 mg before meals.,6.  Aspirin 81 mg.,7.  Multivitamin daily.,8.  Lantus 60 units at bedtime.,9.  Percocet 10/325 one or two in the evening for chronic pain.  She doesn't have any left, so we're calling in a prescription for #10 of them.

transcription: ADMISSION DIAGNOSIS:, Hyperglycemia, cholelithiasis, obstructive sleep apnea, diabetes mellitus and hypertension.,DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:, Hyperglycemia, cholelithiasis, obstructive sleep apnea, diabetes mellitus, hypertension and cholecystitis.,PROCEDURE:, Laparoscopic cholecystectomy.,SERVICE:, Surgery.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, Mrs. ABC is a 57-year-old woman.  She is morbidly obese.  She also suffers from diabetes and obstructive sleep apnea.  She was evaluated at the Bariatric Surgical Center for band placement.  During her examination, she was found to have signs of cholelithiasis.  It was felt that the patient would benefit from having her gallbladder removed prior to banding due to her diabetes and the risk of infection from the band.  The patient was scheduled for her procedure on 12/31/09; however, when checking blood sugar, the patient was hyperglycemic, her sugar level was 438. She was admitted to the hospital for treatment of her hyperglycemia.,HOSPITAL COURSE: Ms. ABC was admitted to the hospital.  She was seen by Dr. A. He gave her an insulin drip.  Her sugar levels gradually returned to normal between 115 and 134. The next day, she was then taken to the operating room, where she underwent her laparoscopic cholecystectomy.  Intubation was noted to be difficult for the procedure.  There were some indications of chronic cholecystitis, mild edema, mild edema, and adhesions of the omentum around the gallbladder.  She underwent the procedure.  She tolerated it without difficulty.  She was recovered in the post-operative care unit and then brought back to the ground.  Her post-procedure blood sugar was 233. She was started again on sliding scale insulin.  She continued to do well and was deemed stable for discharge after the procedure. EXIT INSTRUCTIONS: To return to the Medifast diet.  Continue with your blood sugar.  She needs to follow up with Dr. B and she will see me Friday next week.  We will determine whether we will proceed with his tour at that time.  She can shower.  She must keep her wounds clean and dry.  No major work.  Prohibition of driving with narcotic painkillers.  She should continue to use her CPAP machine and continue to monitor her blood sugar.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 60-year-old female patient who, on and off for the past 10 to 12 months, has experienced diarrhea, nausea, and inability to eat almost daily.  She underwent an EGD and colonoscopy with Dr. ABC a few days before this admission.  Colonoscopy revealed diverticulosis and EGD showed bile retention and possible gastritis.  Biopsies were taken.  The patient presented to our emergency department with worsening abdominal pain as well as swelling of the right lower leg.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Extensive and well documented in prior records.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,The abdomen was diffusely tender.  The lungs are clear.  Blood pressure 129/69 on admission.  At the time of admission, she had only a trace of bilateral lower edema.,LABORATORY STUDIES:,white count 6.7, hemoglobin 13, hematocrit 39.3.  Potassium 3.2 on 08/15/2007., HOSPITAL COURSE: , Dr. ABC apparently could not advance the endoscope into the cecum and therefore warranted a barium enema.  This was done and didn't really show what the cecum was doing on the barium enema.  There was some stool retention in this area and the patient underwent somewhat prolonged hospital treatment with the remaining barium from the colon.  She had enemas.  She had persistent nausea, headaches, and neck pain throughout this hospitalization.  Eventually, her condition improved enough to the point where she was able to return home.,DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS: , Nausea and abdominal pain of uncertain etiology.,SECONDARY DIAGNOSIS: , Migraine.,COMPLICATIONS: , None., DISCHARGE CONDITION: , Guarded.,DISCHARGE PLAN: , Follow up with me in the office in 5-7 days to resume all pre-admission medications.  Diet and activity as tolerated.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Heroin Detoxification.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , This is a 52 year old male with a long history of heroin abuse, who continues to relapse, presents again, trying to stop using heroin, his last use shortly before his arrival, including cocaine.  The patient has a history of alcohol abuse, but is most preoccupied with heroin abuse.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Notable for chronic pain.  He suffered multiple stab wounds, gunshot wounds and various other injuries that caused him chronic pain and he states that this is what led him to take heroin to try to get rid of the pain.  He has previously been followed by ABC but has not seen him for several years.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: ,Patient states that he used heroin as well as cocaine earlier today and feels under the influence.  Denies any headache or visual complaints.  No hallucinations.  No chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain or back pain.  Denies any abscess.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, The patient is a smoker.  Admits to using heroin and abusing alcohol.  Also now admits to cocaine use.,FAMILY HISTORY:,Non-contributory.,MEDICATIONS:,He has previously taken painkillers and analgesics chronically.  Apparently he just got out of prison.  He also previously took Klonopin and lithium.  He was previously on codeine for this pain.,ALLERGIES: , NONE.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , VITAL SIGNS: The patient is afebrile.  He is markedly hypertensive, 175/104 and pulse 117, probably due to the cocaine on board.  His respiratory rate is normal at 18. GENERAL: The patient is somewhat nervous but lucid, alert, and oriented to the person, place, time, and situation.  HEENT: Nothing remarkable.  The pupils are in fact moderately dilated, around 4 to 5 mm, but reactive.  The extraoculars are intact.  His oropharynx is clear.  NECK: Soft.  Its trachea is median.  LUNGS: Clear.  He has good breathing and no wheezing.  No groans or rhonchi.  Good air circulation and no coughing.  HEART: Without murmur.  ABDOMEN: Soft and non-tender.  He has several track marks, several tattoos, but no abscesses.  NEUROLOGICAL: Non-focal.,IMPRESSION: ,MEDICAL EXAMINATION FOR PATIENT WHO WILL BE IN DETOX FROM HEROIN.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ,At this time, I believe the patient can be followed up at XYZ.  I wrote a prescription for clonidine and Phenergan for symptom relief and it was faxed to the pharmacy.  I don't think he needs any further testing at this time.  He is otherwise released in a stable condition.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , Sepsis, NOT possible.,CURRENT ILLNESS HISTORY: , This is a 53-year-old Hispanic man with diabetes, morbid obesity, hepatitis C, cirrhosis, history of alcohol and cocaine abuse, who presented to the emergency room on 07/01/09 for a fall to ground level secondary to knee weakness.  He complained of bilateral knee pain, but also had other symptoms, including hematuria and epigastric pain, for at least a month.  He ran out of prescription medication 1 month ago.  In the emergency room, he was initially afebrile, but later rose to 101.3 with a heart rate of 130 and respiratory rate of 24. The white blood cell count was slightly low at 4 and the platelet count was only 22,000. Abdominal ultrasound showed mild to moderate ascites.  He received 1 dose of Zosyn, then started levofloxacin and Flagyl last night.  Dr. leg and hepatic encephalopathy.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Patient is a former smoker, reported to have stopped in 2007. He has used cocaine in the past, reportedly stopped in 2005. He also has a history of alcohol abuse, but apparently stopped more than 10 years ago.,ALLERGIES: None known.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Vancomycin, doripenem, thiamine, Protonix, potassium chloride p.r.n., magnesium p.r.n., Zofran. p.r.n., noradrenaline drip and vitamin K.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION:, Unobtainable as patient is drowsy and confused.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,CONSTITUTIONAL/VITAL SIGNS: Heart rate 101, respiratory rate 17, blood pressure 92/48, temperature 97.5, and oxygen saturation 98% on nasal cannula of 2 L.,GENERAL APPEARANCE: The patient is drowsy.  Morbid obesity.  Height 5 feet 8 inches, body weight 182 kilos.,EYE: Slightly pale conjunctiva, icteric sclera.  Pupils equal, vivid reaction to light. EARS, NOSE, MOUTH AND THROAT: Overall hearing intact.  Moist oral mucosa.  No oral lesions.,NECK: No palpable cervical masses.  The thyroid is not enlarged on inspection.,RESPIRATORY: Regular inspiratory effort.  No crackling or wheezing. CARDIOVASCULAR: Regular heart rate.  No groans or friction.  Positive bipedal edema, 2+, right worse than left. GASTROINTESTINAL: globular abdomen.  Soft.  No guard, no rigidity.  Tender on palpation of the right upper quadrant and epigastric region.  Slightly tender to palpation of the right upper quadrant and epigastric region.,LYMPHATIC: no cervical lymphadenopathy.,SKIN: positive diffuse jaundice.  No palpable subcutaneous nodules.,PSYCHIATRIC: poor judgment and insight.,LABORATORY DATA:,WBC count 08/01/09 is 9 with 68% neutrophils, 20% bands, H&H 9.7/28.2, platelet count 24,000. INR 3.84, PTT greater than 240. BUN and creatinine 26.8/1.2.  AST 76, ALT 27, alkaline phosphatase 48, total bilirubin 17.85.  Total CK 1198.6, LDH 873.2.  Troponin 0.09, myoglobin 2792. Urinalysis dated 07/01/09 shows small leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, 1-3 leukocytes, 0-1 red blood cells, 2+ bacteria.  Two series of blood cultures from 07/01/09 are still pending. RADIOLOGY: Chest x-ray on 07/01/09 showed no pathological abnormalities of the heart, mediastinum, lung fields, bony structures or soft tissues.  X-rays of the left knee on 07/01/09 showed advanced osteoarthritis.  Abdominal ultrasound on 07/01/09 showed mild to moderate ascites, slight prominence of the gallbladder with a thickened ball, and pericholecystic fluid.  Preliminary CT report of the abdomen showed changes consistent with liver cirrhosis and portal hypertension with mild ascites, splenomegaly, and dilated portal/splenic and superior mesenteric vein.  The appendix was not clearly visible, but there was no sign of pericecal inflammation.,PRINT:,1.  Septic shock.,2.  Possible urinary tract infection.,3.  Ascites,

No seizure disorder.,ENDOCRINE/HEMATOLOGICAL: not significant.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,VITAL SIGNS: pulse 93, blood pressure 158/93, afebrile and respiratory rate 16 per minute.,HEENT: atraumatic and normocephalic.,NECK: neck veins are flat.  No significant carotid sounds., LUNGS: Air entry is decreased bilaterally., HEART: PMI is displaced.  S1 and S2 are regular.,ABDOMEN: Soft and not sensitive.  Bowel sounds are present.,EXTREMITIES: No edema.  The pulses are palpable.  No clubbing or cyanosis.  The patient moves all extremities; however, the patient presents with tremor.,RADIOLOGICAL DATA:,ECG reveals normal sinus rhythm with underlying nonspecific ST-T changes secondary to the tremor.,LABORATORY DATA:,H&H stable.  White count of 14. BUN and creatinine are within normal limits.  The cardiac enzyme profile is negative.  Ammonia level is elevated at 69. CT angiogram of the chest, no signs of pulmonary embolism.  Chest x-ray is negative for acute changes.  CT of the head, chronic and unremarkable skin changes.  Liver enzymes are within normal limits.,PRINT:,1.  The patient is a 69-year-old man, admitted with a syncopal episode and a possible seizure disorder.

transcription: DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS,1.  Multiple extensive subcutaneous abscesses, right thigh.,2.  Massive open wound, right thigh, condition after right excision of multiple subcutaneous abscesses, right thigh.,PROCEDURES PERFORMED,1.  On 03/05/08, by Dr. X, a massive debridement of the soft tissues took place, right lateral thigh and hip.,2.  Soft tissue debridement on 03/16/08 of the right thigh and hip by Dr.  Split thickness skin graft on right thigh and right hip, massive open wound on 04/01/08 by Dr. Y., REASON FOR ADMISSION:, The patient is a 62 year old male with a history of drug abuse.  He had previously received heroin injections in his bilateral thighs.  Unfortunately, he developed chronic abscesses, open sores on his bilateral thighs, much worse on the right than the left.  Decision was made to do a radical excision then it is followed by reconstruction.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,The patient was admitted on 05/03/08 by Dr.  He underwent a massive resection of multiple subcutaneous abscesses, remnants of heroin, which left huge open wounds on his right thigh and hip.  This led to a prolonged hospital stay.  The patient was initially treated with local wound care.  He was treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics.  He ended up growing different species of Clostridium.  An infectious disease consultation was obtained from Dr. Z. He contributed additional antibiotic coverage during the remainder of his hospitalization.  The patient also had significant hypoalbuminemia and decreased nutrition.  Given his significant injuries, he ended up receiving a feeding tube, and prior to the transplant, he received significant feeding tube supplementation to help him get adequate nutrition for healing.  The patient had this upper area that appeared to be more necrotic and infected soft tissue.  He returned to the operating room on 3/16/08 and a further resection was performed by Dr.  He is being treated with a VAC wound.  Slowly but gradually, his injury made significant progress.  I went from a very poor looking sore to a mostly grainy red sore.  We thought he was ready to undergo a skin graft.  Note that the patient underwent serial ultrasounds given his high risk of DVT from this massive injury and his need to decrease activities.  These were negative.  He was treated with SCDs to help reduce his risk.  On 04/01/08 the patient was taken to the operating room, he was judged to have an adequate ________ graft.  He had a skin graft on his right thigh and a huge open wound on his hip.  Donor sites have been truncated.  Postoperatively, the patient was left with a large majority of skin graft samples.  Unable to take, he was kept on IV antibiotics, strict bed rest, and limited range of motion in his hip.  He continues to receive a VAC dressing.  The graft gradually improved with this therapy.  I had another ultrasound, which came back negative for DVT.  The patient was mobilized out of bed.  Recommendations on infectious diseases were obtained.  The plan was to follow another 10 days of antibiotics upon discharge.  These will be oral antibiotics.  I would monitor his left side, which has significantly reduced inflammation and irritation or infection thanks to the antibiotic coverage.  So the decision was not made to remove this, but rather to monitor.  As of 04/11/08, his graft appeared to be in good condition.  It was pink and filling. He looked stable for the release.  The patient was discharged home.,DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:,Discharged home.,CONDITION:,Stable.,The script for Augmentin XR antibiotic was written.  He can take a shower.  Instructional orders for dressing the donor site and graft site were given to Home Health and the patient.  His follow-up was organized with Dr.

transcription: REASON FOR HOSPITALIZATION: ,Suspicious calcifications of the upper outer quadrant, left breast.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,The patient is a 78-year-old woman who underwent a routine screening mammogram on 06/04/08.  This study revealed the presence of punctate calcifications that appeared to be distributed in clusters in the left breast mound at the 2 o'clock position.  Additional imaging studies confirmed the suspicious nature of these calcifications.  The patient underwent a stereotactic needle biopsy of the left breast at 2 a.m. on 06/17/08.  Final histologic diagnosis of tissue removed during this procedure revealed focal fibrosis.  No calcifications could be identified upon examination of the biopsy material, including the x-ray taken of the preserved tissue. Two days after the stereotactic needle biopsy, however, the patient returned to the central breast with severe swelling, pain, and mass in the left breast.  She underwent ultrasound evaluation and was noted to develop a pseudoaneurysm at the site of the stereotactic needle biopsy.  I was called to see the patient at the emergency clinic at the breast center.  At the same time, Dr. Y was consulted in Interventional Radiology.  Drs Z and Y were able to identify the neck of the pseudoaneurysm in the left breast mound and this was injected under ultrasound guidance with thrombin material.  This resulted in immediate occlusion of the pseudoaneurysm.  The patient was seen at my office for a follow-up appointment on 06/24/08.  At this time, the patient continued to have evidence of significant hematoma and extensive bruising, resulting from the stereotactic needle biopsy.  There was, however, no evidence of reformation of the pseudoaneurysm.  There was no evidence of a pulsatile mass in the left breast mound or on the left chest wall. I discussed these issues with the patient and her husband.  The underlying problem is that the suspicious calcifications, which had been identified on the mammogram, had not been adequately sampled with the stereotactic needle biopsy; therefore, histological diagnosis does not explain the imaging findings.  For this reason, the patient was advised to undergo an excisional biopsy of this area with guidewire localization.  Because the breast mound had been significantly disrupted by the stereotaxic needle biopsy, the decision was to postpone any surgical intervention for at least three to four months.  The patient now returns to undergo excision of the left breast tissue with preoperative guidewire localization to identify the location of suspicious calcifications. The patient has a history of stereotactic needle biopsy of the left breast, which was performed on 01/27/04.  This revealed benign histological findings.  The family history is positive, involving a daughter who was diagnosed with breast cancer at age 40. Apart from her age, the patient has no other risk factors for developing breast cancer.  She is not receiving any hormone replacement therapy.  She had five children, the first pregnancy of which occurred at the age of 24. Apart from her daughter, no other family member has breast cancer.  There are no family members with a history of ovarian cancer.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Other hospitalizations have occurred for asthma and pneumonia.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,Colon resection in 1990 and sinus surgeries in 1987, 1990 and 2005.,MEDICATIONS CURRENT:,1.  Plavix.,2.  Arava.,3.  Nexium.,4.  Fosamax.,5.  Advair.,6.  Singular.,7.  Spiriva.,8.  Lexapro.,DRUG ALLERGIES:, ASPIRIN, PENICILLIN, IODINE AND CODEINE.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Positive for heart disease, hypertension and stroke.  Family history is positive for colon cancer affecting his father and a brother.  The patient has a daughter who was diagnosed with breast cancer at the age of 40.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,The patient does not smoke.  She has an occasional

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This 59-year-old white male is seen for a comprehensive annual health maintenance exam on 02/19/08, although this patient is in excellent general health.  Medical problems include chronic tinnitus in the left ear with moderate hearing loss for many years with no recent changes, dyslipidemia well controlled with niacin, history of hemorrhoids with occasional external hemorrhages although no problems in the last 6 months, and history of bullous concha of the left nostril followed by ENT associated with mild septal deviation.  There are no other medical problems.  He has no symptoms at this time and remains in excellent health.,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,Otherwise non-contributory.  There are no operations, serious illnesses, or injuries other than those noted above.,ALLERGIES:,There are no known allergies.,FAMILY HISTORY:,Father died of an MI at age 67 with COPD and was a heavy smoker.  Her mother is 88 years old, she is living and doing well, she has post-resection status for lung cancer.  Two brothers, alive and in good health.  A sister died at the age of 20 months from pneumonia.,SOCIAL HISTORY: The patient is married.  The woman is living and doing well.  He jogs or cross-country 5 times a week and weights twice a week.  Do not smoke or consume a lot of alcohol.  He is an allergy/immunology physician.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Otherwise non-contributory.  He does not present any gastrointestinal, cardiopulmonary, genitourinary or musculoskeletal symptoms.  No symptoms other than those described above.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: He appears alert, oriented, and in no acute distress with excellent cognitive function.  VITAL SIGNS: He is 6 feet 2 inches tall, his weight is 181.2, his blood pressure is 126/80 in the right arm, 122/78 in the left arm, his pulse is 68 and regular and his respirations are 16. SKIN: Hot and dry.  There is no pallor, cyanosis or jaundice.  HEENT: Benign tympanic membranes.  The pharynx is benign.  The nasal mucosa is intact.  The pupils are round, regular and equal, reacting equally to light and accommodation.  MOE intact.  Fundi reveals flat discs with clear margins.  Normal vascular system.  No hemorrhages, exudates or microaneurysms.  No thyroid enlargement.  There is no lymphadenopathy.  LUNGS: Clear on percussion and auscultation.  Normal sinus rhythm.  No premature beats, murmurs, S3 or S4.  Heart sounds are of good quality and intensity.  The carotid, femoral, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulsations are brisk, equal and active bilaterally.  ABDOMEN: Benign without protection, rigidity, tenderness, mass or organomegaly.  NEUROLOGICAL: Grossly intact.  EXTREMITIES: Normal.  GU: normal genitalia.  There is no inguinal hernia.  There are slight hemorrhoids in the anal canal.  The prostate is small, even normal to slightly enlarged, with discrete, symmetrical margins without significant palpable abnormality.  There is no rectal mass.  Stools are Hemoccult negative.,PRINT:,1.  Comprehensive annual health maintenance exam.,2.  Dyslipidemia.,3.  Tinnitus, left ear.,4.  Hemorrhoids.,PLAN:,At that time, continue niacin 1000 mg in the morning, 500 mg at noon, and 1000 mg in the evening; aspirin 81 mg daily; multivitamins; vitamin E 400 units per day; and vitamin C 500 mg per day.  Consider adding lycopene, selenium and flaxseed to his diet.  All appropriate labs will be obtained today.  Monitoring of fasting lipid profile and ALT in 6 months.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , Possibility of free air under the diaphragm., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a 77-year-old woman who cannot give any information.  She was sedated with Ativan and arrived at the emergency room dazed and unable to tell her story.  On a chest x-ray for what appeared to be shortness of breath, she was found to have what was thought to be free air under her right diaphragm.,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,Significant for alcohol abuse.  Unable to really gather any other information because she is so obsessed.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,Looking at the medical records, she has had an appendectomy, a right hip fracture from a fall in 2005, and TAH/BSO.,DRUGS:,Unable to assess.,ALLERGIES:,UNABLE TO ASSESS.,HISTORY SOCIAL:,Significant history of alcohol abuse, according to emergency physician, who sees her regularly.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION: ,Unobtainable.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,VITAL SIGNS: Temp 98.3, heart rate 82, respiratory rate 24 and blood pressure 141/70.,GENERAL: She is a very obtunded woman who, when awake, is not not able to provide any useful information.,HEENT: atraumatic.,NECK: soft and supple.,LUNGS: bilaterally diminished.,HEART: regular.,ABDOMEN: soft and with deep palpation, unable to rouse patient, unable to elicit any tenderness.,LABORATORY STUDIES:, Show normal white blood cell count without shift.  AST elevated 138, with normal ALT 38. Alkaline phosphatase 96, bilirubin 0.8.  Sodium is 107, with chloride 68 and potassium 2.8. Chest x-ray shows possibility of free air; therefore, a CT scan was obtained due to the patient's physical examination, which showed no signs of intra-abdominal pathology.  The etiology of the air under the diaphragm is actually colic air antero-superior to the dome of the diaphragm, close to the dome of the liver.,ASSESSMENT: ,No intra-abdominal pathology.,PLAN:,Admit her to the medical department for treatment of her hyponatremia.

transcription: PROCEDURE:, Upper endoscopy with foreign body removal.,PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS (ES):, Esophageal foreign body.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS (ES):, Penny in proximal esophagus.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, None.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE: ,After informed consent was obtained, the patient was taken to the pediatric endoscopy suite. After appropriate sedation by the anesthesia staff and intubation, an upper endoscope was inserted into the mouth, over the tongue, into the esophagus, at which time the foreign body was encountered. It was grasped with a coin removal forcep and removed with an endoscope. At that time, the endoscope was reinserted, advanced to the level of the stomach and stomach was evaluated and was normal. The esophagus was normal with the exception of some mild erythema, where the coin had been sitting. There were no erosions. The stomach was decompressed of air and fluid. The scope was removed without difficulty.,SUMMARY:, The patient underwent endoscopic removal of esophageal foreign body.,PLAN:, To discharge home, follow up as needed.

transcription: PROCEDURE:,  Upper endoscopy with removal of food impaction.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , A 92-year-old lady with history of dysphagia on and off for two years.  She comes in this morning with complaints of inability to swallow anything including her saliva.  This started almost a day earlier.  She was eating lunch and had beef stew and suddenly noticed inability to finish her meal and since then has not been able to eat anything.  She is on Coumadin and her INR is 2.5.,OPERATIVE NOTE: , Informed consent was obtained from patient.  The risks of aspiration, bleeding, perforation, infection, and serious risk including need for surgery and ICU stay particularly in view of food impaction for almost a day was discussed.  Daughter was also informed about the procedure and risks.  Conscious sedation initially was administered with Versed 2 mg and fentanyl 50 mcg.  The scope was advanced into the esophagus and showed liquid and solid particles from mid esophagus all the way to the distal esophagus.  There was a meat bolus in the distal esophagus.  This was visualized after clearing the liquid material and small particles of what appeared to be carrots.  The patient, however, was not tolerating the conscious sedation.  Hence, Dr. X was consulted and we continued the procedure with propofol sedation.,The scope was reintroduced into the esophagus after propofol sedation.  Initially a Roth net was used and some small amounts of soft food in the distal esophagus was removed with the Roth net.  Then, a snare was used to cut the meat bolus into pieces, as it was very soft.  Small pieces were grabbed with the snare and pulled out.  Thereafter, the residual soft meat bolus was passed into the stomach along with the scope, which was passed between the bolus and the esophageal wall carefully.  The patient had severe bruising and submucosal hemorrhage in the esophagus possibly due to longstanding bolus impaction and Coumadin therapy.  No active bleeding was seen.  There was a distal esophageal stricture, which caused slight resistance to the passage of the scope into the stomach.  As this area was extremely inflamed, a dilatation was not attempted.,IMPRESSION: , Distal esophageal stricture with food impaction.  Treated as described above.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,  IV Protonix 40 mg q.12h.  Clear liquid diet for 24 hours.  If the patient is stable, thereafter she may take soft pureed diet only until next endoscopy, which will be scheduled in three to four weeks.  She should take Prevacid SoluTab 30 mg b.i.d. on discharge.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Gastrostomy (gastrocutaneous fistula).,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Gastrostomy (gastrocutaneous fistula).,OPERATION PERFORMED: , Surgical closure of gastrostomy.,ANESTHESIA: , General.,INDICATIONS: , This 1-year-old child had a gastrostomy placed due to feeding difficulties.  Since then, he has reached a point where he is now eating completely by mouth and no longer needed the gastrostomy.  The tube was, therefore, removed, but the tract has not shown signs of spontaneous closure.  He, therefore, comes to the operating room today for surgical closure of his gastrostomy.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE: , After the induction of general anesthetic, the abdomen was prepped and draped in the usual manner.  An elliptical incision was made around the gastrostomy site and carried down through skin and subcutaneous tissue with sharp dissection.  The tract and the stomach were freed.  Stay sutures were then placed on either side of the tract.  The tract was amputated.  The intervening stomach was then closed with interrupted #4-0 Lembert, Nurolon sutures.  The fascia was then closed over the stomach using #3-0 Vicryl sutures.  The skin was closed with #5-0 subcuticular Monocryl.  A dressing was applied, and the child was awakened and taken to the recovery room in satisfactory condition.
