transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Gallstone pancreatitis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Gallstone pancreatitis.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Laparoscopic cholecystectomy.,ANESTHESIA: , General endotracheal and local injectable Marcaine.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , Minimal.,SPECIMEN: , Gallbladder.,COMPLICATIONS:  ,None.,OPERATIVE FINDINGS: , Video laparoscopy revealed dense omental adhesions surrounding the gallbladder circumferentially.  These dense adhesions were associated with chronic inflammatory edematous changes.  The cystic duct was easily identifiable and seen entering into the gallbladder and clipped two proximally and one distally.  The cystic artery was an anomalous branch that was anterior to the cystic duct and was identified, clipped with two clips proximally and one distally.  The remainder of the evaluation of the abdomen revealed no evidence of nodularity or masses in the liver.  There was no evidence of adhesions from the abdominal wall to the liver.  The remainder of the abdomen was unremarkable.,BRIEF HISTORY:  ,This is a 17-year-old African-American female who presented to ABCD General Hospital on 08/20/2003 with complaints of intractable right upper quadrant abdominal pain.  She had been asked to follow up and scheduled for surgery previously.  Her pain had now been intractable associated with anorexia.  She was noted on physical examination to be afebrile; however, she was having severe right upper quadrant pain with examination as well as a Murphy's sign and voluntary guarding with examination.  Her transaminases were markedly elevated.  She also developed pancreatitis secondary to gallstones.  Her common bile duct was dilated to 1 cm with no evidence of wall thickening, but evidence of cholelithiasis.  She was seen by the gastroenterologist and underwent a sphincterotomy with balloon extraction of gallstones secondary to choledocholithiasis.  Following this, she was scheduled for operative laparoscopic cholecystectomy.  Her parents were explained the risks, benefits, and complications of the procedure.  She gave us informed consent to proceed with surgery.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE:  ,The patient brought to the operative suite and placed in the supine position.  Preoperatively, the patient received IV antibiotics of Ancef, sequential compression devices and subcutaneous heparin.  The abdomen was prepped and draped in the normal sterile fashion with Betadine solution.  Utilizing a #15 blade scalpel, a transverse infraumbilical incision was created.  Utilizing a Veress needle with anterior traction on the anterior abdominal wall with a towel clamp, the Veress needle was inserted without difficulty.  Hanging water drop test was performed with notable air aspiration through the Veress needle and the saline passed through the Veress needle without difficulty.  The abdomen was then insufflated to 15 mmHg with carbon-dioxide.  Once the abdomen was sufficiently insufflated, a #10 mm bladed trocar was inserted into the abdomen without difficulty.  Video laparoscope was inserted and the above notable findings were identified in the operative findings.  The patient to proceed with laparoscopic cholecystectomy was decided and a subxiphoid port was placed.  A #15 bladed scalpel was used to make a transverse incision in the subxiphoid region within the midline.  The trocar was then inserted into the abdomen under direct visualization with the video laparoscope and seen to go to the right of falciform ligament.  Next, two 5 mm trocars were inserted under direct visualization, one in the midclavicular and one in the anterior midaxillary line.  These were inserted without difficulty.  The liver edge was lifted and revealed a markedly edematous gallbladder with severe omental adhesions encapsulating the gallbladder.  Utilizing Endoshears scissor, a plane was created circumferentially to the dome of the gallbladder to allow assistance and dissection of these dense adhesions.  Next, the omental adhesions adjacent to the infundibulum were taken down and allowed to expose the cystic duct.  A small vessel was seen anterior to the cystic duct and this was clipped two proximally and one distally and noted to be an anomalous arterial branch.  This was transected with Endoshears scissor and visualized the pulsatile branch with two clips securely in place.  Next, the cystic duct was carefully dissected with Maryland dissectors and was visualized clearly both anterior and posteriorly.  Endoclips were placed two proximally and one distally and then the cystic duct was transected with Endoshears scissor.,Once the clips were noted to be in place, utilizing electrocautery another Dorsey dissector was used to carefully dissect the gallbladder off the liver bed wall.  The gallbladder was removed and the bleeding from the gallbladder wall was easily controlled with electrocautery.  The abdomen was then irrigated with copious amounts of normal saline.  The gallbladder was grasped with a gallbladder grasper and removed from the subxiphoid port.  There was noted to be gallstones within the gallbladder.  Once the abdomen was re-insufflated after removing the gallbladder and copious irrigation was performed, all ports were then removed under direct visualization with no evidence of bleeding from the anterior abdominal wall.  Utilizing #0 Vicryl suture, a figure-of-eight was placed to the subxiphoid and infraumbilical fascia and this was approximated without difficulty.  The subxiphoid port was irrigated with copious amounts of normal saline prior to closure of the fascia.  A #4-0 Vicryl suture was used to approximate all incisions.  The incisions were then injected with local injectable 0.25% Marcaine.  All ports were then cleaned dry.  Steri-Strips were placed across and sterile pressure dressings were placed on top of this.  The patient tolerated the entire procedure well.  She was transferred to the Postanesthesia Care Unit in stable condition.  She will be followed closely in the postoperative course in General Medical Floor.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT:  ,Blood in toilet.,HISTORY: , Ms. ABC is a 77-year-old female who is brought down by way of ambulance from XYZ Nursing Home after nursing staff had noted there to be blood in the toilet after she had been sitting on the toilet.  They did not note any urine or stool in the toilet and the patient had no acute complaints.  The patient is unfortunately a poor historian in that she has dementia and does not recall any of the events.  The patient herself has absolutely no complaints, such as abdominal pain or back pain, urinary and GI complaints.  There is no other history provided by the nursing staff from XYZ.  There apparently were no clots noted within there.  She does not have a history of being on anticoagulants.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: , Actually quite limited, includes that of dementia, asthma, anemia which is chronic, hypothyroidism, schizophrenia, positive PPD in the past.,PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:  ,Unknown.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , No tobacco or alcohol.,MEDICATIONS: , Listed in the medical records.,ALLERGIES:,  No known drug allergies.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , VITAL SIGNS:  Stable.,GENERAL:  This is a well-nourished, well-developed female who is alert, oriented in all spheres, pleasant, cooperative, resting comfortably, appearing otherwise healthy and well in no acute distress.,HEENT:  Visually normal.  Pupils are reactive.  TMs, canals, nasal mucosa, and oropharynx are intact.,NECK:  No lymphadenopathy or JVD.,HEART:  Regular rate and rhythm.  S1, S2.  No murmurs, gallops, or rubs.,LUNGS:  Clear to auscultation.  No wheeze, rales, or rhonchi.,ABDOMEN:  Benign, flat, soft, nontender, and nondistended.  Bowel sounds active.  No organomegaly or mass noted.,GU/RECTAL:  External rectum was normal.  No obvious blood internally.  There is no stool noted within the vault.  There is no gross amount of blood noted within the vault.  Guaiac was done and was trace positive.  Visual examination anteriorly during the rectal examination noted no blood within the vaginal region.,EXTREMITIES:  No significant abnormalities.,WORKUP: , CT abdomen and pelvis was negative.  CBC was entirely within normal limits without any signs of anemia with an H and H of 14 and 42%.  CMP also within normal limits.  PTT, PT, and INR were normal.  Attempts at getting the patient to give A urine were unsuccessful and the patient was very noncompliant, would not allow us to do any kind of Foley catheterization.,ER COURSE:,  Uneventful.  I have discussed the patient in full with Dr. X who agrees that she does not require any further workup or evaluation as an inpatient.  We have decided to send the patient back to XYZ with observation by the staff there.  She will have a CBC done daily for the next 3 days with results to Dr. X.  They are to call him if there is any recurrences of blood or worsening of symptoms and they are to do a urinalysis at XYZ for blood.,ASSESSMENT: , Questionable gastrointestinal bleeding at this time, stable without any obvious signs otherwise of significant bleed.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:  ,1.  Congenital chylous ascites and chylothorax.,2.  Rule out infradiaphragmatic lymphatic leak.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES: , Diffuse intestinal and mesenteric lymphangiectasia.,ANESTHESIA: , General.,INDICATION:  ,The patient is an unfortunate 6-month-old baby boy, who has been hospitalized most of his life with recurrent chylothoraces and chylous ascites.  The patient has been treated somewhat successfully with TPN and voluntary restriction of enteral nutrition, but he had repeated chylothoraces.  Last week, Dr. X took the patient to the operating room in hopes that with thoracotomy, a thoracic duct leak could be found, which would be successfully closed surgically.  However at the time of his thoracotomy exploration what was discovered was a large amount of transdiaphragmatic transition of chylous ascites coming from the abdomen.  Dr. X opened the diaphragm and could literally see a fountain of chylous fluid exiting through the diaphragmatic hole.  This was closed, and we decided that perhaps an abdominal exploration as a last stage effort would allow us to find an area of lymphatic leak that could potentially help the patient from this dismal prognostic disease.  We met with his parents and talked to them about this, and he is here today for that attempt.,OPERATIVE FINDINGS:  ,The patient's abdomen was relatively soft, minimally distended.  Exploration through supraumbilical transverse incision immediately revealed a large amount of chylous ascites upon entering into the peritoneal cavity.  What we found which explains the chronic chylous ascites and chylothorax was a diffuse lymphangiectatic picture involving the small bowel mesentery approximately two thirds to three quarters of the distal small bowel including all of the ileum, the cecum, and the portion of the ascending colon.  It appeared that any attempt to resect this area would have been met with failure because of the extensive lymphatic dilatation all the way down towards the root of the supramesenteric artery.  There was about one quarter to one third of the jejunum that did not appear to be grossly involved, but I did not think that resection of three quarters of the patient's small bowel would be viable surgical option.  Instead, we opted to close his abdomen and refer for potential small intestine transplantation procedure in the future if he is a candidate for that.,The lymphatic abnormality was extensive.  They were linear dilated lymphatic channels on the serosal surface of the bowel in the mesentery.  They were small aneurysm-like pockets of chyle all along the course of the mesenteric structures and in the mesentery medially adjacent to the bowel as well.  No other major retroperitoneal structure or correctable structure was identified.  Both indirect inguinal hernias were wide open and could be palpated from an internal aspect as well.,DESCRIPTION OF OPERATION:  ,The patient was brought from the Pediatric Intensive Care Unit to the operating room within an endotracheal tube im place and with enteral feeds established at full flow to provide maximum fat content and maximum lymphatic flow.  We conducted a surgical time-out and reiterated all of the patient's important identifying information and confirmed the operative plan as described above.  Preparation and draping of his abdomen was done with chlorhexidine based prep solution and then we opened his peritoneal cavity through a transverse supraumbilical incision dividing both rectus muscles and all layers of the abdominal wall fascia.  As the peritoneal cavity was entered, we divided the umbilical vein ligamentum teres remnant between Vicryl ties, and we were able to readily identify a large amount of chylous ascites that had been previously described.  The bowel was eviscerated, and then with careful inspection, we were able to identify this extensive area of intestinal and mesenteric lymphangiectasia that was a source of the patient's chylous ascites.  The small bowel from the ligament of Treitz to the proximal to mid jejunum was largely unaffected, but did not appear that resection of 75% of the small intestine and colon would be a satisfactory tradeoff for The patient, but would likely render him with significant short bowel and nutritional and metabolic problems.  Furthermore, it might burn bridges necessary for consideration of intestinal transplantation in the future if that becomes an option.  We suctioned free all of the chylous accumulations, replaced the intestines to their peritoneal cavity, and then closed the patient's abdominal incision with 4-0 PDS on the posterior sheath and 3-0 PDS on the anterior rectus sheath.  Subcuticular 5-0 Monocryl and Steri-Strips were used for skin closure.,The patient tolerated the procedure well.  He lost minimal blood, but did lose approximately 100 mL of chylous fluid from the abdomen that was suctioned free as part of the chylous ascitic leak.  The patient was returned to the Pediatric Intensive Care Unit with his endotracheal tube in place and to consider the next stage of management, which might be an attempted additional type of feeding or referral to an Intestinal Transplantation Center to see if that is an option for the patient because he has no universally satisfactory medical or surgical treatment for this at this time.

The patient has been treated somewhat successfully with TPN and voluntary restriction of enteral nutrition, but he had repeated chylothoraces. Last week, Dr. X took the patient to the operating room in hopes that with thoracotomy,...

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Ms. Connor is a 50-year-old woman who returns to the clinic for an examination of her wound.  The patient underwent APR secondary to refractory ulcerative colitis.  Subsequently, she developed an infection in the wound, which has since healed.  During our most recent visit to our clinic, she had her perineal stitches removed and is presenting today for follow-up of her perineal wound.  She describes no drainage or erythema of the buttocks.  She has a good ostomy result.  She describes no fever, chills, nausea or vomiting.  The patient describes intermittent pain under the upper part of the incision as well as in the right lower quadrant, under her stoma.  She takes Percocet to relieve this pain and it works.  Since then, she has no longer needed to try extra strength Tylenol, which occasionally relieves this intermittent pain.  She asks for additional pain medication for this occasional abdominal pain, which she still experiences. PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: Temperature 95.8, pulse 68, blood pressure 132/73 and weight 159 pounds.  She is a pleasant female, without acute distress.  The patient's abdomen is soft, nontender, nondistended, with a well-healed midline scar.  There is an ileostomy in the right hemiabdomen, which is pink, patent, productive, and protruding.  There is no evidence of a mass or hernia on the patient's abdomen.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: This is a pleasant 50-year-old woman who underwent APR secondary to refractory ulcerative colitis.  Overall, her quality of life has improved significantly since having her APR.  She is working well with her ileostomy.  She had concerns or questions about her diet and we discussed the BRAT diet, which consists of foods that would slow down the digestive tract, such as bananas, rice, toast, cheese and peanut butter.  I discussed the need to monitor his ileostomy flow and the preferred amount of daily flow is 2 liters or less.  I advised him to abstain from soft drinks and fruit drinks.  I also discussed with her that this diet is additionally trial and error and that she may try some foods that are not suitable for her ileostomy, but others may and that this is something that she will just have to experiment with over the next few months until she figures out what foods she can and cannot eat with her ileostomy.  She also wondered about his occasional abdominal pain.  I told him it would probably continue to get better as the months went by and gave him a refill of Percocet for the continued occasional pain.  I told her that this would be the last time I refilled the Percocet and that if she had any lingering pain after finishing this bottle, then she should start taking ibuprofen or Tylenol if she had lingering pain.  The patient then spoke of numbness in his right hand and arm that was present after surgery and was thought to be due to positioning during surgery.  All of this is mostly gone, except for a little numbness at the tip of the third digit as well as some occasional forearm muscle cramps.  I told him I thought it would continue to improve as it has over the last two months since his surgery.  I told her to continue doing hand exercises like she was doing and it seems to be working for her.  Overall, I think she has healed from her surgery and is doing very well.  Here again, his quality of life is significantly improved.  She is satisfied with her performance.  We'll see her again in six months just for a routine check-up and see how she's doing then.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS,1.  Intestinal obstruction.,2.  The central line fell.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS,1.  Intestinal obstruction.,2.  The central line fell.,PROCEDURE: ,Insertion of a triple lumen central line through the right subclavian vein by percutaneous technique.,PROCEDURE DETAILS: ,This lady has intestinal obstruction.  She was being fed through a central catheter which, according to the patient, was just inserted yesterday and leaked.  At the bedside, after informed consent was obtained, the patient's right deltopectoral area was prepared and draped in the usual manner.  Xylocaine 1% was infiltrated and, with the patient in the Trendelenburg position, her right subclavian vein was cannulated percutaneously without any difficulty.  A Seldinger technique was used and a triple lumen catheter was inserted.  There was good flow through all three ports, which were irrigated with saline before connection to IV solutions.,The catheter was secured to the skin with sutures and then a dressing was applied.,The post-procedure chest x-ray revealed that there were no complications in the procedure and the catheter was in a good place.

transcription: EXAMINATION: , Ultrasound Abdomen., ,REASON FOR EXAMINATION: , Elevated liver function tests., ,INTERPRETATION: , The liver shows heterogeneously increased echotexture with significant fatty infiltration.  The gallbladder is surgically absent. There is no fluid collection in the cholecystectomy bed.  There is dilatation of the common bile duct up to 1 cm.  There is also a dilation of the pancreatic duct of up to 3 mm.  There is caliectasia in the right kidney.  The bladder is considerably distended and measures 937 cc in volume.  Caliectasia in the right kidney may be secondary to back pressure from the distended bladder.  The aorta is of normal caliber., ,PRINT:,1.  Dilated common duct as well as pancreatic duct as described.  Given the dilation of these two conduits, ERCP versus CPRM is recommended to exclude an obstructive mass.  The results could reflect changes related to cholecystectomy.  ,2.  Significantly distended bladder with probably caliectasia in the right kidney.  Recommended clinical correlation.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Umbilical hernia.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Umbilical hernia.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Umbilical hernia repair.,ANESTHESIA: , General.,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , Minimal.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,The patient was prepared and draped in sterile mode.  A subumbilical incision was made and descended to the fascia.  The umbilical hernia was carefully brought back into the cavity and the fascia was closed with interrupted vertical mattress sutures to approximate the fascia, then the wounds were infiltrated with 0.25% marcaine.  The skin was reattached to the fascia with 2-0 Vicryls.  The skin was approximated with 2-0 Vicryl subcutaneous stitches, then 4-0 Monocryl subcutaneous stitches, dressed with Steri-Strips and 4 x 4s. The patient was extubated and taken to the recovery room in stable condition.

transcription: HISTORY OF CURRENT ILLNESS: This is a 10 year old girl who was diagnosed with biliary atresia and had a procedure in Kasai and didn't do too well because she ended up having a liver transplant.  The patient did well after the liver transplant and the only problems started:,1.  History of food allergies.,2.  History of dental caries. At this time, the patient came for follow-up and complained of pain in the upper left molar.  There are no other complaints.,FOOD: , Lactose restricted diet.,MEDICATION: , Please see MRC form.,ALLERGIES: , There are no allergies.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Patient lives with parents in Lindsay, California and has a good environment.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Negative for gastrointestinal disease except one brother or a sister has ulcerative colitis.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION: ,The system exam was only positive for molar pain, but the rest of the 13 systems exams were negative to date.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,MEASUREMENTS: Height 135 cm and weight 28.1 kg.,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature 98.9 and blood pressure 105/57.,GENERAL: A well-developed, well-nourished child without acute distress.,HEENT: Atraumatic and normocephalic.  The pupils are equal, round and responsive to light.  Complete MOEs.  The conjunctivas and sclera are clear.  TMs show normal landmarks.  The nasal mucosa is pink and moist.  Teeth and gums are in good condition.  The pharynx is clear.,NECK: Supple, without thyromegaly and without masses.,LYMPHATIC: No lymphadenopathy.,LUNGS: Clear on auscultation, without retractions.,CORONARY: Regular rhythm without murmur.  S1 and S2 are normal.  Pulses are full and symmetrical bilaterally.,ABDOMEN: Normal bowel sounds.  No hepatosplenomegaly, no masses and no tenderness.,GENITALS: Normal female on inspection.,SKIN: No unusual lesions.,BACK: No scoliosis, hairy patch, lipoma or sacral dimple.,EXTREMITIES: No cyanosis, clubbing or edema.,CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM: Age-appropriate development.  DTRs are 2+ and symmetrical.  The toes descend bilaterally.  Motor and sensory without asymmetry.  Cranial nerves II to XII are generally intact.,LABORATORY DATA:,Laboratory data from 12/30/2007 tacrolimus 3.1 and Epstein-Barr negative, CMV was not detected.,FINAL PRINT:,This is a 10 year old child with a history of:,1.  Biliary atresia.,2.  Status after orthotopic liver transplantation.,3.  Dental caries.,4.  Food allergies.,5.  History of urinary tract infections.,PLAN: ,Our plan would be to continue taking the medications as follows:,1.  Prograf 0.5 mg p.o. offers,2.  Valganciclovir 420 mg p.o. offer,3.  Labs every 2-3 months.,4.  Return to clinic in 4 months.,5.  Refer this patient to a pediatric dentist for evaluation of dental caries.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Patient AF is a 50-year-old African-American male, hepatitis C positive, with a 2-day history of abdominal pain and distension with nausea and vomiting.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,AF's symptoms started 2 days ago and he has not passed gas or stools.  He didn't eat anything and vomited 8 times.  AF reports 10/10 pain in the LLQ., PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: AF's medical history is significant for an abdominal injury during the Vietnam War that required surgery and multiple episodes of small bowel obstruction and abdominal pain.  Other elements of his history include alcoholism, cocaine abuse, alcoholic hepatitis, hepatitis C positive, acute pancreatitis, chronic pancreatitis, appendicitis, liver hematoma/contusion, Hodgkin's disease, constipation, diarrhea, paralytic ileus, anemia, multiple blood transfusions, chorioretinitis, pneumonia, and "chest pain caused by cracks.” surgical procedures including the Bill Roth Type 1 procedure (partial gastrectomy) during the Vietnam War, at least 2 exploratory laparotomies and enterolysis procedures (1993; 2000) and appendectomy,DRUGS: None.,ALLERGIES:, Iodine, IV contrast (anaphylaxis), and seafood/shellfish.,FAMILY HISTORY:, No contributory.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,AF was born and raised. in San Francisco.  His father was an alcoholic.  He currently lives with his sister and does not work; he receives a pension.,HEALTH-RELATED BEHAVIORS:,AF reports smoking 1-2 cigarettes per day and drinking 40 ounces of beer per day.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Non-contributory except patient reports weight loss of 6 pounds since onset of symptoms and reports multiple transfusions for anemia.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vital signs: T: 37.1

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Bleeding after transanal excision five days ago.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Bleeding after transanal excision five days ago.,PROCEDURE:, Examination under anesthesia with bleeding control by cautery.,ANESTHESIA:, General endotracheal.,INDICATION:, The patient is a 42-year-old man who is five days from the transanal. excision of a benign lesion of the anterior base.  He presented today with diarrhea and bleeding.  Digital examination reveals bright red blood on the finger.  He is currently being examined under anesthesia and hemorrhage control.,FINDINGS:There was an ulcer where most of the polypoid lesion had previously been excised.  The wound had almost completely opened and there was still a raw ulcerated surface between the edges of the mucosa.  There were a few discrete sites of slight oozing, which were treated with a cautery and #1 suture.  No other obvious bleeding was observed.,TECHNIQUE:,The patient was taken to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  After inducing adequate general anesthesia, the patient was then placed in a modified supine position.  Her buttocks were taped, prepped and draped in a sterile manner.  The anterior rectal wall was exposed using a Parks anal retractor.  The entire wound was visualized with a few rotations of the retractor and a few sites along the edges were observed oozing and were touched up with cautery.  There was a spot in the corner that was oozing and that might have been from just opening the retractor enough to get a good look.  This was controlled with a 3-0 Monocryl figure eight suture.  In the end, there was no bleeding, no oozing, it was completely dry, and we removed our retractor, and the patient was then turned over and extubated and taken to the recovery room in stable condition.

transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED: ,Nissen fundoplication.,DESCRIPTION OF THE PROCEDURE: ,After obtaining informed consent detailing the risks of infection, bleeding, esophageal perforation, and death, the patient was brought to the operating room and placed in the supine position on the operating room table.  General endotracheal anesthesia was induced without incident.  The patient was then placed in a modified lithotomy position with great care taken to pad all extremities.  TED and Venodynes were used as prophylaxis against deep vein thrombosis.  Antibiotics were administered prophylactically against surgical infections. A 52 French bougie was placed in the proximal esophagus by anesthesia, above the cardioesophageal junction.  A 2 cm midline incision was made at the junction of the upper two thirds and the lower third between the umbilicus and the xiphoid process.  The fascia was then removed from the subcutaneous tissue using tonsil forceps.  A 1 to 2 cm incision was then made in the fascia to enter the abdominal cavity without incident.  Two 0 Vicryl sutures were then placed up and down into the aponeurosis and then attached to the special 12mm Hasson trocar equipped with a funnel-shaped adapter to occlude the fascial opening.  Pneumoperitoneum was then established by insufflation of carbon dioxide to a stable pressure state of 16 mmHg.  A 30-degree laparoscope was inserted into this port and used to guide the remaining trocars. The remaining trocars were then placed into the abdomen taking care to make the incisions along Langer's line, spreading the subcutaneous tissue with tonsil forceps, and confirming the entry site by pressing on the abdominal wall before trocar insertion.  In total, 4 other 10/11 mm trocars were placed.  In direct vision, 1 was inserted in the right upper quadrant at the midclavicular line, in the right supraumbilical position; another in the left upper quadrant, at the midclavicular line, in the left supraumbilical position; 1 below the right costal margin in the anterior axillary line; and another laterally under the left costal margin on the anterior axillary line.  All trocars were placed without difficulty.  The patient was then placed in the reverse Trendelenburg position. The triangular ligament was sharply removed and the left lobe of the liver was retracted superolaterally using a fan retractor placed through the right lateral cannula.  The gastrohepatic ligament was then identified and incised in an avascular plane.  The dissection was performed anteromedially on the phrenoesophageal membrane.  The phrenoesophageal membrane was divided on the anterior surface of the hiatal opening.  This incision was extended to the right to allow identification of the right crura.  Then, along the inner aspect of the crus, the right esophageal wall was released by dissecting the cleavage plane. Release of the posterior aspect of the esophagus was begun by extending the dissection along the entire length of the right diaphragmatic crus.  The pars flaccida of the lesser omentum was opened, preserving the hepatic branches of the vagus nerve.  This allowed free access to the crura, left and right, to the right posterior aspect of the esophagus and to the posterior vagus nerve. Attention then turned to the left anterolateral aspect of the esophagus.  At its left border, the left cru has been identified.  The dissection plane between it and the left side of the esophagus was freed.  The gastrorenic ligament was incised, beginning mobilization of the gastric pouch.  By dissecting the intramediastinal part of the esophagus, we lengthened the intra-abdominal segment of the esophagus and reduced the hiatal hernia. The next step was mobilization of the gastric pouch.  This required ligation and division of the gastrosplenic ligament and several short gastric vessels using the harmonic scalpel.  This dissection began on the stomach at the point where the vessels of greatest curvature turned toward the sp

transcription: PRESENTATION:,A 16-year-old male presents to the emergency department (ED) with rectal bleeding and pain with defecation.,HISTORY:,A 16-year-old African American male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of rectal bleeding and pain with defecation.  The patient states that he was doing well until approximately three days before his presentation, when he began experiencing some pain while defecating.  The next day, he noted increasing pain and first noticed blood on the surface of his stool.  The pain worsened the next day with increased bleeding as well as mucopurulent anal discharge.  The patient denies any history of rectal bleeding or pain.  He also denies any previous sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) and states that he was tested for HIV eight months ago and was negative.  The patient states that he has not felt well for a week.  He claims to have felt “feverish” on several occasions but did not take his temperature.  He also complained of some abdominal pain accompanied by nausea and diarrhea as well as generalized myalgia and fatigue.  He thinks he lost a few pounds, but he hasn't weighed himself to determine the exact amount of weight loss. The patient states that he has been sexually active since the age of 13. He admits to having had eight previous partners and says he "usually" uses a condom.  Upon further questioning, the patient states that of his eight partners, three were women and five were men.  His most recent sexual partner was a 38-year-old man with whom he had been living for six months.  He states that he has been tested for STDs before, but states that he only gave urine and blood for testing.  He is unaware of his partner's HIV status but assumes his partner is not infected because he looks healthy.  The patient also acknowledges an episode of sexual abuse at the age of 8 by a family friend.  Because the man was a member of the family church, the patient never felt comfortable telling any of the adults in his life.  He is very concerned about disclosing his sexual behavior to his family, as their family has expressed very negative comments about men who have sex with men.  He accesses emergency care unaccompanied by an adult.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, Thin but non-toxic young man with obvious discomfort.,Pulse = 105,RR = 23,BP = 120/62,HEENT: Multiple areas of white plaque-like material on the oral mucosa.,Neck: Multiple anterior/posterior cervical nodes in the anterior and posterior chains - 1-2 cm. diameter.,Lungs: clear to auscultation.,Cardiac: precordium quiet.,Nl S1/S2 with a II/VI systolic murmur. ,Abdomen: soft without hepatosplenomegaly.,GU: male Tanner V without external penile lesions.,Lymph: axillary lymph nodes 2 to 3 cm bilaterally.,Epitrochlear lymph nodes 1 to 2 cm.,Multiple inguinal nodes 1 to 2 cm.,Rectal: extremely painful digital examination.,+ macroscopic blood and mucous membranes.,ASSESSMENT IN LABORATORY:,Hbg = 12.5 g/dL, Hct = 32%, WBC = 3.9 tu/µL, Platelets = 120,000 tu/µL, 76% neutrophils, 19% lymphocytes, 1% eosinophils, 4% monocytes, ALT = 82 U/L, AST = 90 U/L, Sedimentation rate erythrocyte = 90, Electrolytes = normal, Gram stain of anus swab: numerous leukocytes, DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: , This patient presents with acute rectal pain with bleeding and anal discharge.  The patient also has some constitutional symptoms, including fever, fatigue, abdominal discomfort, and lymphadenopathy on physical examination.  The following are part of the differential diagnosis: Acute Proctitis and Proctocolitis., ACUTE HIV SEROCONVERSION:, This subject is sexually active and reports inconsistent condom use.  Gastrointestinal symptoms have recently been frequently reported in patients with a history of HIV seroconversion.  Rectal symptoms of bleeding and pain are not common with HIV and an alternative diagnosis would be necessary.,PERIRECTAL ABSCESS:,A patient with a history of receptive anal intercourse is at risk of developing a perirectal abscess.

transcription: HISTORY: Reason for follow-up in ICU today is acute anemia secondary to upper GI bleeding with melena with drop in hemoglobin from 11 to 8, post-transfusion status of 2 units of PRBC with EGD performed earlier today by Dr. productive, infection and shortness of breath.  Please see the transfer note dictated to the ICU yesterday detailing the need for an emergent transfer transfusion and EGD in this patient.  During the last 24 hours, the patient received 2 units of packed red blood cells and his hematocrit and hemoglobin returned to their baseline value of approximately 11, appropriate for the hemoglobin value.  He also underwent EGD earlier today with Dr.  Dr. X recommended increasing the doses of his proton pump inhibitor and avoiding NSAIDs in the future.  The patient now complains of still having problems with shortness of breath, wheezing, and productive cough, now producing yellow-brown sputum with increasing frequency, but he has had no further episodes of melena since his transfer to the intensive care unit.  He also complains of some laryngitis and pharyngitis, but denies any abdominal pain, nausea, or diarrhea.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION, VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure is 100/54, heart rate 80, and temperature 98.8.  Is and Os negative water balance of 1.4 liters in the last 24 hours.,GENERAL: This is a drowsy 68 year old man, who wakes up to voice, wakes up, seems to have a good appetite, has a continuous cough.  Pallor has improved.,EYE: Conjunctivas are now pink.,ENT: Oropharynx is clear.,CARDIOVASCULAR: Reveals distant heart tones with regular rhythm and frequency.,LUNGS: Have coarse breathing sounds with wheezing, rhonchi and slight crackles in the bases.,ABDOMEN: Soft and non-tender with no organomegaly appreciated.,EXTREMITIES: showed no clubbing, cyanosis or edema.  Capillary refill time is now normal at the fingertips.,NEUROLOGICAL: Cranial nerves II through at 8.6 and 24.7.  The platelets are stable.  Sodium is 134, Potassium 4.0, Chloride 101, Bicarb 26, BUN 19, Creatinine 1.0, Glucose 73, Calcium 8.4, INR 0.96, Iron 13%, Saturations 4%, TIBC 312, TSH 0.74, CEA high at 8.6, ferritin 27.5 and occult blood positive.  EGD, final results pending per Dr.  Melena secondary to ulcerative esophagitis.  We will continue to monitor the patient overnight to make sure there is no further bleeding.  If there are no further episodes of melena and the hemoglobin is stable or unchanged in the morning, the patient will be transferred to the medical floor for continued treatment of their chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation.,2.  Exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.  The patient is doing well, he is taking PO.  We will continue to give him his oral Omnicef ​​and azithromycin as well as his continued breathing treatments.  We will add guaifenesin and N-acetyl-cysteine ​​in the hope of mobilizing some of his secretions.  It seems to be improving.  His white count is normalized and I hope we can release him on oral antibiotics in the next 24-48 hours if there are no further complications.,3.  High CEA.  The patient will need an outpatient colonoscopy.  He refused it today.  We would like to encourage him to do so.  It should be noted that the patient, upon arrival, was following a bloodless protocol, but accepted the transfusion, under pressure.  Likewise, I hope that with proper counseling the patient will face problems.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Acute cholecystitis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Acute gangrenous cholecystitis with cholelithiasis.,OPERATION PERFORMED:, Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with cholangiography.,FINDINGS:,The patient had essentially a dead gallbladder with stones and positive large bile/pus coming from the gallbladder biliary.,COMPLICATIONS: ,None.,EBL: ,Rare.,SAMPLE WITHDRAWN: ,Gallbladder with stones.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION: ,The patient was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner under general anesthesia.  A curvilinear incision was made below the umbilicus.  Through this incision, the camera port could be placed in the peritoneal cavity under direct visualization.  Once this was completed, insufflation began.  Once insufflation was adequate, additional ports were placed in the epigastrium as well as the right upper quadrant.  Once all four ports were placed, the upper right quadrant was then explored.  The patient had significant adhesions from the omentum and colon to the liver, gallbladder undoubtedly constituting acute cholecystitis.  This was removed using the Bovie cautery to free visualization of the gallbladder.  The gallbladder was very thick and edematous and showed frank necrosis of most of the anterior gallbladder wall.  Adhesions were then removed between the omentum, colon, and gallbladder, starting slowly upward and working downward toward the cystic duct area.  Once the adhesions were completely removed, cholangiography was performed and showed no signs of dilation or obstruction of the common bile duct.  At this point, because the patient's gallbladder was very necrotic, it was felt that a drain should be placed on the patient.  The cystic duct and cystic artery were cut and divided serially.  The gallbladder was removed from the gallbladder fossa, thereby removing the entire gallbladder.  Adequate hemostasis with Bovie cautery was achieved.  The gallbladder was then placed in a bag and removed from the peritoneal cavity via the camera port.  A JP drain was then passed through the anterior port and out of one of the trochar sites and secured to the skin using a 3-0 nylon suture.  Then, the right upper quadrant was abundantly irrigated using the suction irrigator.  Once this was completed, the additional ports could be removed.  The fascial opening at the umbilicus was reinforced by closing it using a figure 8 0 Vicryl suture. All skin incisions were injected with plain 1/4 percent marcaine.  The skin was further reapproximated using 4-0 Monocryl sutures in a subcutaneous technique.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was able to be transferred to the recovery room in stable condition.

Additionally, there was a notable liver cyst.  The remainder of the abdomen remained free of any adhesions.,BRIEF HISTORY: This is a 66-year-old Caucasian female who presented to ABCD General Hospital for an elective cholecystectomy.  The patient complained of nausea, vomiting and intractable abdominal bloating after eating fatty foods.  She had had several attacks in the past following these complaints.  On examination, she was found to have pain in her right upper quadrant.  In addition, she underwent an ultrasound on 04/08/2003 which revealed cholelithiasis.  The patient was recommended to undergo laparoscopic cholecystectomy due to her recurrent symptoms.  She was explained the risks, benefits, and complications of the procedure and gave informed consent to continue.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE:The patient was brought to the operating room and placed in the supine position.  The patient received preoperative antibiotics with Kefzol.  The abdomen was prepared and draped in a normal sterile manner with Betadine solution.  The patient underwent general endotracheal anesthesia.  Once adequate sedation was achieved, a supraumbilical transverse incision was made with a #10 blade scalpel.  Using a Veress needle, the Veress needle was inserted intra-abdominally and hooked to the CO2 insufflation.  The abdomen was insufflated at 15 mmHg.  Once adequate insufflation was achieved, the laparoscopic camera was inserted into the abdomen and to visualize a distended gallbladder as well as an omental adhesion adjacent to the gallbladder.  The decision to proceed with laparoscopic cystectomy was made.  A subxiphoid transverse incision was created with a #10 blade scalpel and using a 12 mm blade trocar, the trocar was inserted under direct visualization into the abdomen.  Two 5 mm ports were placed, one at the midclavicular line, 2 cm below the costal margin, and a second at the axillary line, approximately a hand's length below the costal margin.  All ports were inserted with a 5 mm blade trocar and then under direct visualization.  Once all trocars were inserted, the gallbladder was grasped at the fundus and retracted upward and toward the left shoulder.  Adjacent adhesions were removed with a Maryland dissector.  Once this was accomplished, the gallbladder infundibulum was grasped and retracted laterally and anteriorly.  This made it possible to better delineate the cystic duct as well as the cystic artery.  Using the Maryland dissector, careful dissection of the cystic duct and the cystic artery was carried out behind each of them.  Using Endoclips, clips were placed on the cystic duct and cystic artery, one near the gallbladder and two at the distal end.  Using endoscissors, the cystic duct and cystic artery were ligated.  Then, using an electrocautery, the gallbladder was carefully dissected from the liver bed.  An electrocautery was used to stop any bleeding encountered along the way.  The gallbladder was perforated during dissection and released, with the bile contents draining into the abdomen.  No trace of stones was seen.  Once the gallbladder was completely excised from the liver bed, an EndoCatch was placed and the gallbladder was inserted into the EndoCatch and removed from the subxiphoid port.  This was sent as a specimen, a gallstone was identified in the gallbladder.  Then, using large amounts of irrigation, the abdomen w

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Cholecystitis and cholelithiasis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Cholecystitis and cholelithiasis.,PROCEDURE TITLE,1.  Laparoscopic cholecystectomy.,2.  Intraoperative cholangiography.,ANESTHESIA: ,General.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: ,The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position under general endotracheal anesthesia.  The patient received 1 g of Ancef IV intravenously.  The abdomen was prepared and draped in a routine sterile manner. A 1 cm incision was made at the umbilicus and a Veress needle was inserted.  A saline test was performed.  Satisfactory pneumoperitoneum was obtained by insufflation of CO2 at a pressure of 14 mmHg.  The Veress needle has been removed.  A 10 to 11 mm cannula was inserted.  Inspection of the peritoneal cavity revealed a soft gallbladder without adhesions.  It was largely mobile.  The liver had a normal appearance as did the peritoneal cavity.  A 5 mm cannula was inserted into the anterior axillary line of the right upper quadrant.  A second 5 mm cannula was inserted into the subcostal space.  A 10 to 11 mm cannula was inserted into the upper midline. The gallbladder was reflected in a cephalad direction.  The gallbladder was pierced with the aspiration needle and, under C-arm fluoroscopy, filled with contrast material, filling the intra- and extrahepatic biliary trees, which appeared normal.  Additional contrast material was aspirated and the aspiration needle was removed.  The bulb was grasped with a second forceps, opening Calot's triangle.  The cystic duct was dissected and exposed at its junction with the ampulla, controlled with a hemoclip, digitally controlled with two clips, and divided.  This was done while the common channel was in full viewing.  The cystic artery was also checked and divided.  The gallbladder was dissected from its bed and separated from the liver, brought outward through the upper midline cannula, and removed. The subhepatic and subphrenic spaces were carefully irrigated with saline.  There was some oozing and bleeding at the lateral site of the 5 mm cannula, but this stopped spontaneously with removal of the cannula.  The subphrenic and subhepatic spaces were again carefully irrigated with saline until clear.  Hemostasis was excellent.  The CO2 was evacuated and the camera removed.  The umbilical fascia was closed with 2-0 Vicryl, the subcus with 3-0 Vicryl, and the skin was closed with 4-0 nylon.  Sterile dressings were applied.  The number of sponges and needles was correct.

transcription: The patient's abdomen was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A subumbilical skin incision was made.  The Veress needle was inserted and the patient's abdominal cavity was insufflated at all times with moderate pressure.  A subumbilical trocar was inserted.  The camera has been inserted into the panoramic view.  The abdomen had some inflammation around the gallbladder.  A 10 mm midepigastric trocar was inserted.  A. 2mm and 5mm trocars were inserted.  The most lateral trocar grasping forceps was inserted and grasped the bottom of the gallbladder and placed in tension at the edge of the liver. Using the dissector, the cystic duct was identified and double hemoclips were invited well away from the cystic duct-common duct junction.  The cystic artery was identified and double Hemoclips were applied.  The gallbladder was removed from the liver bed using Endoshears and an electrocautery.  Hemostasis was achieved.  The gallbladder was removed from the midepigastric trocar site without difficulty.  The trocars were removed and the skin incisions were approximated using 4-0 Monocryl.  Steri-Strips and a sterile dressing were placed.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was taken to the recovery room in stable condition.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,Right colon tumor.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Right colon cancer.,2.  Ascites.,3.  Adhesion.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  Exploratory laparotomy.,2.  Lysis of adhesions.,3.  Right hemicolectomy.,ANESTHESIA: , General.,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , Less than 200 cc.,URINARY FLOW: , 200 cc.,CRYSTALLOIDS ADMINISTERED: , 2700 cc.,INDICATIONS FOR THIS PROCEDURE: ,The patient is an African-American woman of 53 years old who presented with a quasi-obstruction lesion at the hepatic angle.  The patient underwent a colonoscopy which revealed this lesion and biopsies were performed proving invasive adenocarcinoma.  The patient was decompressed NG preoperatively and prepared for surgery.  The need for colon cancer removal has been explained at length.  The patient agreed to proceed with the surgery and signed a preoperative informed consent.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position under general anesthesia by the anesthesia department and the NG and Foley catheters were placed preoperatively.  She received three IV antibiotics.  Due to his almost obstructive symptoms, a formal ________ was not performed. The abdomen was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A midline laparotomy incision was made with a No. 10 blade scalpel, and the subcutaneous tissues were separated by electrocautery down to the anterior abdominal fascia.  Once divided, the intra-abdominal cavity was accessible and the intestine was protected while the remainder of the abdominal wall was opened in the midline.  Abundant fluid was observed upon entry into the abdomen, ascitic fluid, which was clear straw-colored and was sampled for cytology.  Then, the small intestine was retracted with digital exploration and there were signs of hepatic inflection, colonic mass, adherent to the surrounding tissues.  With mobilization of the colon along Toldt's line to the right groove, the entire ileocecal region up to the transverse colon was mobilized in the field.  Next, a window was made 5 inches from the ileocecal valve and a GIA-75 was fired through the ileum.  Next, a second GIA device was pulled through the proximal transverse colon, just protecting the middle colic artery.  The dissection was then carried out along the mesenter, up to the root of the mesenter.  Several lymph nodes were carefully sampled and small radiopaque clips were applied along the base of the mesenter.  The mesentered vessels are hemostatic and tied sequentially with #0-Vicryl suture, ligated in between.  Once this specimen was submitted for pathology, the wound was inspected.  There were no signs of bleeding from the suture sites.  Then, a side-to-side anastomosis was performed between the transverse colon and the terminal ileum.  A third GIA-75 was pulled side by side and the GIA-55 was used to close the anastomosis.  A patent anastomosis was palpated.  The anastomosis was then protected with #2-0 Vicryl #0 muscle suture.  Then, the mesenteric root was closed with #0-Vicryl suture to avoid any risk of internal herniation.  The suture sites were inspected and there was no evidence of leaking.  Then, the intra-abdominal cavity was carefully irrigated with sterile saline, and the anastomosis was transported to the right lower gutter.  The omentum was used to cover intestines that appeared dilated and indurated because of the nearby obstruction.  Then, the abdominal wall was reapproximated and the fascial layer using two looped PDS sutures meeting in the middle with good approximation of the two abdominal fascia.  Additional sterile saline was used to irrigate the subcutaneous fat, and then the skin was closed with sequential sterile staples. A sterile dressing was applied and the skin was cleaned and the patient was awakened from anesthesia without difficulty and extubated in the operating room and she was transferred to the recovery room in a stable condition and will continue to be monitored on the telemetry Flo.

transcription: PROCEDURE: , Gastroscopy.,PRE-OPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Dysphagia, possible stenosis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Gastroparesis.,MEDICATION: , MAC.,DESCRIPTION OF THE PROCEDURE: , The Olympus gastroscope was introduced into the hypopharynx and carefully passed through the esophagus, stomach and the duodenum.  The hypopharynx was normal.  The esophagus showed a normal upper esophageal sphincter, normal contour throughout, and a normal gastroesophageal junction seen 39 cm from the incisors.  There was no evidence of stenosis or extrinsic narrowing following his previous hiatal hernia repair.  There was no evidence of reflux esophagitis.  Upon entering the gastric lumen, a large bezoar containing undigested food occupied a large portion of the gastric floor and body.  It had a diameter of 2 to 3 mm.  This was broken up using a scope into smaller pieces.  There was no gastric fluid retention.  The antrum appeared normal and the pylorus was patent.  The endoscope passed easily into the duodenum, which was normal until the second part.  Upon removal of the endoscope, additional views of the cardia were obtained and there was no evidence of tumor or stricture.  The scope has been removed.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was sent to the recovery room.,FINAL DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Normal postoperative hernia repair.,2.  Retained gastric contents forming a partial bezoar, suggestive of gastroparesis.,3.  Otherwise, normal upper endoscopy to the descending duodenum.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,1.  Continue proton pump inhibitors.,2.  Use Reglan 10 mg three to four times a day.

transcription: PROCEDURE: , Gastroscopy.,PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Gastroesophageal reflux disease.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Barrett's esophagus.,MEDICATIONS: , MAC.,PROCEDURE: , The Olympus gastroscope was introduced into the oropharynx and carefully passed through the esophagus, stomach and duodenum to the transverse duodenum.  The preparation was excellent and all surfaces were clearly visible.  The hypopharynx appeared normal.  The esophagus had normal contour and normal mucosa throughout its length, but at the distal end there was a moderate-sized hiatal hernia.  The GE junction was visible at 40 cm and the hiatus was noted at 44 cm from the incisors.  Above the GE junction, there were three fingers of columnar epithelium extending upward, a distance of approximately 2 cm.  This appears to be consistent with Barrett's esophagus.  Multiple biopsies were taken from many areas of this region.  There was no ulceration or active inflammation and no stenosis.  The hiatal hernia sac had normal mucosa except for a small erosion at the hiatus.  The gastric body had normal mucosa throughout.  Numerous small fundic gland polyps were observed, measuring 3 to 5 mm and entirely benign in appearance.  Biopsies were taken from the antrum to exclude Helicobacter pylori.  A retroflex view of the cardia and fundus confirmed the small hiatal hernia and demonstrated no additional lesions.  The scope passed through the pylorus, which was obvious and normal.  The duodenal mucosa in the first, second and third parts was completely normal.  The instrument was removed and the patient was sent to the recovery room.  He tolerated the procedure well.,FINAL DIAGNOSIS:,1.  A short segment Barrett's esophagus.,2.  Hiatal hernia.,3.  Accidental polyps of the fundic glands in the gastric body.,4.  Otherwise, normal upper endoscopy to the transverse duodenum.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,1.  Follow-up biopsy report.,2.  Continue PPI treatment.,3.  Follow up with Dr. X if necessary.,4.  Surveillance endoscopy for Barrett in 3 years (if pathology confirms this diagnosis).

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,Refractory dyspepsia.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Hiatal hernia.,2.  Reflux esophagitis.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with pseudo and esophageal biopsy.,ANESTHESIA:,Conscious sedation with Demerol and Versed.,SAMPLE: ,Esophageal biopsy.,COMPLICATIONS: ,None.,HISTORY:,The patient is a 52-year-old black female with morbid obesity who has long history of reflux. and GERD-like symptoms, including complications such as hoarseness and chronic cough.  She has followed several medical regimens and continues to experience dyspeptic symptoms.,PROCEDURE: After obtaining appropriate informed consent, the patient was brought to the endoscopy room.  She was placed in the left lateral position and received IV Demerol and Versed for sedation.  Once the adequate level of sedation was achieved, the gastroscope was inserted into the hypopharynx and the esophagus was easily intubated.  At the GE junction, a hiatal hernia was present.  There were mild inflammatory changes consistent with reflux esophagitis.  The telescope was then inserted into the stomach.  It was insufflated and the telescope was aimed along the greater curvature to the antrum.  The pylorus was patent.  There were signs of bile reflux in the antrum.  The duodenal bulb and scan were examined and showed no signs of mass, ulceration, or inflammation.  The endoscope was then brought back into the antrum. Retroflexion was attempted several times, however, the patient had difficulty retaining air and an adequate retroflexion view was not visualized.  The gastroscope was then slowly removed.  No other fundus or body abnormalities were noted.  Still at the GE junction, an esophageal biopsy was performed.  The perimeter was then completely removed.  The patient tolerated the procedure and was transferred to the recovery room in stable condition.  She will return to the general medical floor.  We will continue proton pump inhibitor treatment twice daily as well as dietary restrictions.  She should also attempt to lose weight significantly.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Gastroesophageal reflux.,2.  Hiatal hernia.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Gastroesophageal reflux.,2.  Hiatal hernia.,3.  Enterogastritis.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,Esophagogastroduodenoscopy, photography and biopsy.,GROSS FINDINGS:,The patient has a history of epigastric abdominal pain, persistent in nature.  She has a history of severe gastroesophageal reflux disease and frequently takes Pepcid.  She had a history of hiatal hernia.  She is currently being evaluated for disease progression.  She doesn't get much response from Protonix. On endoscopy, the gastroesophageal junction measures approximately 40 cm.  There appeared to be some inflammation at the gastroesophageal junction and a small 1-2 cm hiatal hernia.  There is no advancement of the gastric mucosa to the lower third of the esophagus.  However, as noted previously, there appears to be inflammation at the gastroesophageal junction.  There was slight inflammation in the antrum of the stomach.  The fundus of the stomach was within normal limits.  The cardia presented some laxity at the level of the lower esophageal sphincter.  The pylorus is concentric.  The duodenal bulb and scan are within normal limits.  No ulcers or erosions.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE:,The patient is taken to the endoscopy room, prepared and draped in the left lateral decubitus position.  The patient received IV sedation using Demerol and Versed.  The Olympus videoscope was inserted into the hypopharynx and, upon swallowing, passed into the esophagus.  Using air insufflation, the panendoscope was advanced along the esophagus to the stomach along the greater curvature of the stomach through the pylorus to the duodenal bulb and was scanned and the above gross findings were noted.  The panendoscope was slowly withdrawn while carefully examining the bowel lumen.  Photographs were taken with the pathology present.  A biopsy of the antrum of the stomach was obtained along with a CLO test.  Biopsy is also obtained from the gastroesophageal junction at the 12, 3, 6, and 9 o'clock positions to exclude occult Barrett's esophagitis.  Air was aspirated from the stomach and the panendoscope was removed.  The patient was sent to the recovery room in stable condition.

transcription: PROCEDURE:, Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsy.,REASON FOR PROCEDURE:, The child has a history of irritability and diarrhea with gastroesophageal reflux.  Eliminate reflux esophagitis, allergic enteritis and ulcer, as well as celiac disease.  He took Prevacid 7.5 mg p.o. offer. with suboptimal control of this irritability. Consent history and physical examinations were performed.  The procedure, indications, available alternatives and complications, i.e. bleeding, perforation, infections, adverse drug reactions, possible need for blood transfusion and complications associated with surgery, were discussed with the mother who understood and indicated this.  The opportunity to ask questions was offered and informed consent was obtained.,MEDICATIONS:,General anesthesia.,INSTRUMENT:,Olympus GIF-XQ 160.,COMPLICATIONS:,None.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:,Less than 5mL.,FINDINGS:,With the patient in the supine position intubated under general anesthesia, the endoscope was inserted without difficulty into the hypopharynx.  The proximal, middle, and distal esophagus showed normal mucosal staining and vascular pattern.  The lower esophageal sphincter appeared normal and was located 25 cm from the central incisors.  A Z line was identified in the lower esophageal sphincter.  The endoscope was advanced into the stomach, which was distended by excess air.  The rough folds have completely flattened.  The gastric mucosa was completely normal.  No hiatal hernia was observed and the pyloric valve appeared normal.  The endoscope was advanced into the first, second, and third parts of the duodenum, which showed normal mucosal staining and fold pattern.  Vater's blister was identified and found to be normal.  Biopsies were obtained x2 from the second part of the duodenum, antrum and distal esophagus 22 cm from the central incisors for histology.  Two additional antral biopsies were obtained for CLO testing.  Excess air was expelled from the stomach.  The endoscope was removed from the patient who tolerated the procedure well.  The patient was taken to the recovery room in satisfactory condition.,PRINT:,Normal esophagus, stomach and duodenum.,PLAN:,Histological evaluation and CLO test.  Continue Prevacid 7.5 mg p.o. offer.  I will contact the parents next week with the biopsy results and further management plans will be discussed at that time.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Recurrent pain in the right upper quadrant with failure of antacid medical treatment.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Normal esophageal gastroduodenoscopy.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with bile aspiration.,ANESTHESIA:, IV Demerol and Versed in a titrated manner.,INDICATIONS:, This 41-year-old woman presents to the surgical office. with history of recurrent right upper quadrant abdominal pain.  Despite antacid treatment, the patient's pain persists.  Additional findings were concerning regarding the possibility of a biliary etiology.  The patient was explained the risks and benefits of an EGD as well as a Meltzer-Lyon test where a bile aspiration was performed.  The patient agreed to the procedure and informed consent was obtained. GROSS FINDINGS: No evidence of neoplasia, mucosal changes, or ulcers on examination.  Bile aspiration was performed after administration of 3 mcg of Kinevac.,PROCEDURE DETAILS:,The patient was placed in the supine position.  After obtaining appropriate anesthesia, an Olympus gastroscope inserted from the oropharynx through the second part of the duodenum.  Prior to this, 3 mcg of Kinevac IV was administered to the patient to facilitate bile stimulation.  At that time, the patient also complained of epigastric discomfort and nausea.  This pain was similar to his previous pain. Bile was aspirated with a trap to allow collection of fluid.  This fluid was then sent to the laboratory for crystal evaluation.  Subsequently, photodocumentation obtained and retraction of the gastroscope through the antrum revealed no other signs of disease, retroflexion revealed no signs of hiatal hernia or other mass and after straightening the gastroscope and suction ________, the gastroscope was retracted.  The gastroesophageal junction was noted at 20 cm.  No other evidence of illness was appreciated here.  Retraction of the gastroscope pulled back through the esophagus, out of the oropharynx, removed from the patient.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.  We will wait for the evaluation of the bile aspiration.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Stomach pain for 2 weeks., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 45-year-old Mexican man with no significant medical history who presents to the emergency department with complaints of mid-epigastric and right upper quadrant abdominal pain for the past 14 days. The pain was initially crampy and burning and was relieved by food. He also reports that this was initially associated with a sour taste in the mouth. He went to his GP who prescribed 400 mg cimetidine qhs x 5 days; however, this did not relieve his symptoms. In fact, the pain has worsened to the point where it now radiates to the back, but its duration waxes and wanes. It is relieved when standing and walking and exacerbated when lying on the back. He reports a decreased appetite associated with a weight loss of 4 pounds over the past 2 weeks. He has nausea with a single episode of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting on the day of admission. He reports a 2-week history of subjective fever and sweating. He denies any diarrhea, constipation, dysuria, melena or hematochezia. Her last bowel movements were on the morning of admission and were normal. He denies any travel in the last 9 years and any sick contact., MEDICAL HISTORY: Removal of right inguinal groin cyst 15 years ago. Unknown etiology. No recurrence., SURGICAL HISTORY:, Fracture of the neck of the left femur with prosthesis secondary to a fall that occurred 4 years ago., FAMILY HISTORY:, Diabetic mother. No history of liver disease. No malignancy.,SOCIAL HISTORY: Patient was born in central Mexico but moved to the United States 9 years ago. He is on disability due to his anterior femoral fracture. He denies any use of tobacco or illicit drugs. He only drinks alcohol for social purposes, no more than one drink every few weeks. He is married and the father of 3 healthy children. He denies any tattoos or risky sexual behavior.,ALLERGIES:, NKDA.,MEDICATIONS:, Tylenol prn (1-2 tablets every other day for the last 2 weeks), Cimetidine 400 mg po qhs x 5 days.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:, No headaches, vision changes. No shortness of breath. No chest pain or palpitations.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vitals: T 100.9-102.7 BP 136/86 Pulse 117 RR 12 98% sitting in room air,Gen: Well developed, well nourished, no apparent distress.,HEENT: Pupils equal, round and reactive to light. Anicteric. Oropharynx clear and moist.,Neck: Soft. No lymphadenopathy or carotid bruits. No thyromegaly or masses.,CHEST: clear to auscultation bilaterally.,CV: tachycardic but regular rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs/rubs/gallops.,Abd: soft, active bowel sounds. Tender in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant with palpation associated with light guarding. No rebound tenderness. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly.,Rectal: Stools were brown and negative for guaiac.,Ext: No cyanosis/clubbing/edema.,Neurological: He was alert and oriented x3. CN II-XII intact. Normal 2+ DTR. No focal neurological deficit.,Skin: No jaundice. No rashes or lesions.,IMAGING DATA:,CT Abdomen with contrast (11/29/03): There is a 6x6 cm multilobular hypodense mass observed at the hepatic hilum and caudate lobe which results in a mass effect with dilation of intrahepatic radicals of the left lobe of the liver. The rest of the liver parenchyma is homogeneous. The gallbladder, pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands and kidneys are within normal limits. Retroperitoneal vascular structures are within normal limits. There is no evidence of lymphadenopathy, free fluid, or fluid collection., HOSPITAL COURSE:, The patient was admitted to the hospital for further evaluation. A diagnostic procedure was carried out.

transcription: OPERATION,1.  Ivor-Lewis esophagogastrectomy.,2.  Feeding the jejunostomy.,3.  Placement of two chest tubes #28-French on the right side.,4.  Right thoracotomy.,ANESTHESIA: ,General endotracheal anesthesia with a double-lumen tube.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: ,After obtaining informed consent from the patient, including a thorough explanation of the risks and benefits of the above-mentioned procedure, the patient was taken to the operating room and general endotracheal anesthesia was administered.  Before general anesthesia was administered, the patient underwent epidural anesthesia.  In addition, he was placed with a double-lumen endotracheal tube.  The patient was placed in a supine position to begin the procedure.  His abdomen and chest were prepared and draped using the standard surgical method.  After applying sterile dressings, a #10 blade scalpel was used to make an upper midline incision from the level of the xiphoid to just below the umbilicus.  Dissection was performed along the line using a Bovie electrocautery.  The abdomen was open.  Next, a Balfour retractor was positioned as well as a mechanical retractor.  Next, our attention turned to releasing the stomach.  To attempt to do this, we identified the right gastroepiploic artery and arcade.  We incised the omentum and removed it from the stomach and gastroepiploic arch.  The omentum was divided using suture ligation with 2-0 silk.  We did this along the greater curvature and then moved to the lesser curvature where the short gastric arteries were removed by ligation using 2-0 silk.  Next, we turned our attention to performing a Kocher maneuver.  This was done and the stomach was released.  We dismantled the falciform ligament as well as the caudate attachment to the diaphragm.  We widened the diaphragmatic hiatus so that we could place approximately 3 fingers in the chest.  We also performed part of the dissection of the esophagus from the abdomen to the chest.  The esophagus and esophageal hiatus were identified in the abdomen.  We then turned our attention to the left gastric artery.  The left gastric artery was identified at the base of the stomach.  We first harvested the left gastric vein by ligating and dividing it using 0 silk ties. The left gastric artery was then harvested using suture ligation with silk ties, followed by 2-0 stick tie reinforcement.  At this point, the stomach was freely mobile.  We then turned our attention to creating our jejunostomy feeding tube.  A 2-0 Vicryl bursa was placed in the jejunum approximately 20 cm distal to the ligament of Treitz.  We then used the Bovie electrocautery to open the jejunum at this site.  We placed a 16 French red rubber catheter through this site.  We tied ourselves in place.  We then used 3-0 silk sutures to create a Witzel.  Next, the jejunum loop was secured to the abdominal wall using 2-0 silk ties.  After doing this and removing the feeding jejunostomy through the skin and securing it appropriately, we turned our attention to closing the abdomen.  This was done with Prolene No. 1.  We put a 2nd layer of Vicryl 2-0.  The skin was closed with Monocryl 4-0. Next, we turned our attention to performing the thoracic portion of the procedure.  The patient was placed in left lateral decubitus.  The right chest has been prepared and draped appropriately.  We then used a scalpel with a No. 10 blade to make a posterolateral incision, without sparing the muscles.  The dissection was carried out up to the level of the ribs with the Bovie electrocautery.  Then the ribs were counted and the 5th space in between was entered.  The lung was deflated.  We placed standard chest retractors.  Next, we incised the peritoneum above the esophagus.  We dissected the esophagus just above the azygos vein.  The azygos vein, in fact, was harvested with 0 silk ligatures and reinforced with 2-0 stick ties.  As mentioned, we dissected the esophagus proximally and distally to the level of the hiatus.  After doing this, we

transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , EGD with biopsy.,INDICATION: , Ms. ABC is a pleasant 45-year-old woman with a history of severe diabetic gastroparesis, who had a gastrojejunal feeding tube placed radiologically approximately 2 months ago.  She was admitted for recurrent nausea and vomiting, with displacement of the GEJ feeding tube.  A CT scan yesterday revealed traces of leftover feeding tube still visible in the stomach.  Endoscopy is performed to confirm this and rule it out, as well as to determine if there are other causes explaining his symptoms.  The physical examination performed before the procedure was unremarkable, except for upper abdominal tenderness.,MEDICATIONS:, Fentanyl 25 mcg, Poured 2 mg, 2% lidocaine spray into the pharynx.,INSTRUMENT:, GIF 160.,PROCEDURE REPORT:, Informed consent was obtained from Ms. ABC's sister, after the risks and benefits of the procedure were carefully discussed explained, which included, but was not limited to, bleeding. infection, perforation and allergic reaction to medications.  Consent was not obtained from Ms. Morales due to her recent administration of narcotics.  Conscious sedation was achieved with the patient lying in the left lateral decubitus position.  The endoscope was then passed through the mouth, into the esophagus, stomach, where retroflexion was performed, and it was advanced into the second part of the duodenum.,FINDINGS:,1.  ESOPHAGUS: There was evidence of grade C esophagitis, with multiple white-based ulcers seen from the distal to proximal esophagus, 12 cm in length.  Several biopsies were obtained from this region and placed in jars #1.,2.  STOMACH: A small hiatal hernia was observed in the cardia of the stomach.  There was an indentation/scar following placement of the previous PEG tube and suture material was noted in the body and antrum of the stomach.  The remainder of the gastric examination was normal.  No remnants of a feeding tube were observed in the stomach.,3.  DUODENUM: It was normal.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,ASSESSMENT:,1.  Grade C esophagitis observed in the distal, middle and proximal esophagus.,2.  Small hiatal hernia.,3.  Evidence of scarring at the site of the previous feeding tube, as well as suture material seen in the body and antrum of the stomach.,PLAN: Follow-up biopsy results and radiology will replace her gastrojejunal feeding tube.

Biopsies of the distal duodenum, gastric antrum, and distal esophagus were taken and sent for pathologic evaluation.  The endoscope and insufflated air were slowly withdrawn from the upper gastrointestinal tract.  Repeat examination of the involved structures showed no visible abnormalities except for the biopsy sites. The patient tolerated the procedure with excellent comfort and stable vital signs.  After a period of recovery in the endoscopy room, the patient is discharged to continue his recovery under the care of his family at home.  The family knows to contact me today if there are any concerns about the patient's recovery after the procedure.  They will contact me later this week to obtain the results of the biopsies and CLO tests so that further appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic plans can be made.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  protein-calorie malnutrition.,2.  Intractable nausea, vomiting and dysphagia.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Protein-calorie malnutrition.,2.  Intractable nausea, vomiting and dysphagia.,3.  Enterogastritis.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , EGD with placement of a PEG tube using the Russell technique.,ANESTHESIA: , IV sedation with 1% lidocaine for local.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: ,None.,COMPLICATIONS: ,None.,BRIEF HISTORY: , This is a 44-year-old African-American woman well known to this service.  She was hospitalized several times for intractable nausea, vomiting and dehydration.  She states that her PO has decreased. consumption has gradually deteriorated.  She was admitted to Dr. Lang's department and was evaluated by Dr. Wickless, all of whom agreed that the best option for supplemental nutrition for this patient was placement of a PEG tube.,PROCEDURE: After explaining the risks, complications, and benefits to the patient and obtaining informed consent, the patient was taken to the operating room.  She was placed in a supine position.  The area was prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  After obtaining adequate IV sedation through anesthesia, esophagogastroduodenoscopy was performed.  The esophagus, stomach and duodenum were visualized without difficulty.  There was no clear evidence of any malignancy.  There was enterogastritis which was noted on examination.  The appropriate location was noted on the anterior wall of the stomach.  This area was localized externally with 1% lidocaine.  A large gauge needle was used to enter the lumen of the stomach under visualization.  A guidewire was then passed back under visualization and the needle was then removed.  A scalpel was used to make a small incision, next to the guidewire, ensuring that the underlying fascia was also cut.  A dilator with a detachable sheath was then inserted over the guidewire and, under direct visualization, was observed to enter the stomach lumen without difficulty.  The guidewire and dilator were then removed again under visualization and the PEG tube was placed through the detachable sheath and visualized into the stomach lumen.  The balloon was then inflated and the detachable sheath was then removed.  Correct placement of the tube was ensured through visualization with a scope.  The tube was then sutured using nylon suture.  An appropriate sterile dressing was applied.,DISPOSITION: ,The patient was transferred to the recovery department in stable condition.  She was then taken back to her room on the general medical floor.  Previous orders will be resumed.  We will inform the nursing staff that the PEG tube can be used at 5 p.m. tonight for medications if needed and bolus feeds.
