transcription: CC:, Episodic mental status change and RUE numbness, and chorea (found on exam).,HX:, This 78y/o RHM was referred for an episode of unusual behavior and RUE numbness. In 9/91, he experienced near loss of consciousness, generalized weakness, headache and vomiting. Evaluation at that time revealed an serum glucose of >500mg/dL and he was placed on insulin therapy with subsequent resolution of his signs and symptoms. Since then, he became progressively more forgetful, and at the time of evaluation, 1/17/93, had lost his ability to perform his job repairing lawn mowers. His wife had taken over the family finances.,He had also been "stumbling," when ambulating, for 2 months prior to presentation. He was noted to be occasionally confused upon awakening for last several months. On 1/15/93, he was lying on a couch when he suddenly began throwing pillows and blankets for no apparent reason. There had been no change in sleep, appetite, or complaint of depression.,In addition, for two months prior to presentation, he had been experiencing 10-15minute episodes of RUE numbness. There was no face or lower extremity involvement.,During the last year he had developed unusual movements of his extremities.,MEDS:, NPH Humulin 12U qAM and 6U qPM. Advil prn.,PMH:, 1) Traumatic amputation of the 4th and 5th digits of his left hand.  2) Hospitalized for an unknown "nervous" condition in the 1940's.,SHX/FHX:, Retired small engine mechanic who worked in a poorly ventilated shop. Married with 13 children. No history of ETOH, Tobacco or illicit drug use. Father had tremors following a stroke. Brother died of brain aneurysm. No history of depression, suicide, or Huntington's disease in family.,ROS:, no history of CAD, Renal or liver disease, SOB, Chest pain, fevers, chills, night sweats or weight loss. No report of sign of bleeding.,EXAM:, BP138/63 HR65 RR15 36.1C,MS: Alert and oriented to self, season; but not date, year, or place. Latent verbal responses and direction following. Intact naming, but able to repeat only simple but not complex phrases. Slowed speech, with mild difficulty with word finding. 2/3 recall at one minute and 0/3 at 3 minutes. Knew the last 3 presidents. 14/27 on MMSE: unable to spell "world" backwards. Unable to read/write for complaint of inability to see without glasses.,CN: II-XII appeared grossly intact. EOM were full and smooth and without unusual saccadic pursuits. OKN intact. Choreiform movements of the tongue were noted.,Motor: 5/5 strength throughout with Guggenheim type resistance. there were choreiform type movements of all extremities bilaterally. No motor impersistence noted.,Sensory: unreliable.,Cord: "normal" FNF, HKS, and RAM, bilaterally.,Station: No Romberg sign.,Gait: unsteady and wide-based.,Reflexes: BUE 2/2, Patellar 2/2, Ankles Trace/Trace, Plantars were flexor bilaterally.,Gen Exam: 2/6 Systolic ejection murmur in aortic area.,COURSE:, No family history of Huntington's disease could be elicited from relatives. Brain CT, 1/18/93: bilateral calcification of the globus pallidi and a high attenuation focus in the right occipital lobe thought to represent artifact. Carotid duplex, 1/18/93: RICA 0-15%, LICA 16-49% stenosis and normal vertebral artery flow bilaterally. Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE),1/18/93: revealed severe aortic fibrosis or valvular calcification with "severe" aortic stenosis in the face of "normal" LV function. Cardiology felt the patient the patient had asymptomatic aortic stenosis. EEG, 1/20/93, showed low voltage Delta over the left posterior quadrant and intermittent background slowing over the same region consistent with focal dysfunction in this quadrant. MRI Brain, 1/22/93: multiple focal and more confluent areas of increased T2 signal in the periventricular white matter, more prominent on the left; in addition, there were irregular shaped areas of increased T2 signal and decreased T1 signal in both cerebellar hemispheres; and age related atrophy; incidentally, there is a cavum septum pellucidum et vergae and mucosal thickening of the maxillary sinuses. Impression: diffuse bilateral age related ischemic change, age related atrophy and maxillary sinus disease. There were no masses or areas of abnormal enhancement. TSH, FT4, Vit B12, VDRL, Urine drug and heavy metal screens were unremarkable. CSF,1/19/93: glucose 102 (serum glucose 162mg/dL), Protein 45mg/dL, RBC O, WBC O, Cultures negative. SPEP negative. However serum and CSF beta2 microglobulin levels were elevated at 2.5 and 3.1mg/L, respectively. Hematology felt these may have been false positives. CBC, 1/17/93: Hgb 10.4g/dL (low), HCT 31% (low), RBC 3/34mil/mm3 (low), WBC 5.8K/mm3, Plt 201K/mm3. Retic 30/1K/mm3 (normal). Serum Iron 35mcg/dL (low), TIBC 201mcg/dL (low), FeSat 17% (low), CRP 0.1mg/dL (normal), ESR 83mm/hr (high). Bone Marrow Bx: normal with adequate iron stores. Hematology felt the finding were compatible with anemia of chronic disease. Neuropsychologic evaluation on 1/17/93 revealed significant impairments in multiple realms of cognitive function (visuospatial reasoning, verbal and visual memory, visual confrontational naming, impaired arrhythmatic, dysfluent speech marked by use of phrases no longer than 5 words, frequent word finding difficulty and semantic paraphasic errors) most severe for expressive language, attention and memory. The pattern of findings reveals an atypical aphasia suggestive of left temporo-parietal dysfunction. The patient was discharged1/22/93 on ASA 325mg qd. He was given a diagnosis of senile chorea and dementia (unspecified type). 6/18/93 repeat Neuropsychological evaluation revealed moderate decline in all areas tested reflecting severe dementia.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,  Brain tumor left temporal lobe.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,  Brain tumor left temporal lobe - glioblastoma multiforme.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE:,1.  Left temporal craniotomy.,2.  Removal of brain tumor.,OPERATING MICROSCOPE: , Stealth.,PROCEDURE: , The patient was placed in the supine position, shoulder roll, and the head was turned to the right side.  The entire left scalp was prepped and draped in the usual fashion after having being placed in 2-point skeletal fixation.  Next, we made an inverted-U fashion base over the asterion over temporoparietal area of the skull.  A free flap was elevated after the scalp that was reflected using the burr hole and craniotome.  The bone flap was placed aside and soaked in the bacitracin solution.,The dura was then opened in an inverted-U fashion.  Using the Stealth, we could see that this large cystic mass was just below the cortex in the white matter just anterior to the trigone of the ventricle.  We head through the vein of Labbe, and we made great care to preserve this.  We saw where the tumor almost made to the surface.  Here we made a small corticectomy using the Stealth for guidance.  We left small corticectomy entered large cavity with approximately 15 cc of yellowish necrotic liquid.  This was submitted to pathology.  We biopsied this very abnormal tissue and submitted it to pathology.  They gave us a frozen section diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme.  With the operating microscope and Greenwood bipolar forceps, we then systematically debulked this tumor.  It was very vascular and we really continued to remove this tumor until all visible tumors was removed.  We appeared to get two gliotic planes circumferentially.  We could see it through the ventricle.  After removing all visible tumor grossly, we then irrigated this cavity multiple times and obtained meticulous hemostasis and then closed the dura primarily with 4-0 Nurolon sutures with the piece of DuraGen placed over this in order to increase our chances for a good watertight seal.  The bone flap was then replaced and sutured with the Lorenz titanium plate system.  The muscle fascia galea was closed with interrupted 2-0 Vicryl sutures.  Skin staples were used for skin closure.  The blood loss of the operation was about 200 cc.  There were no complications of the surgery per se.  The needle count, sponge count, and the cottonoid count were correct.,COMMENT:  ,Operating microscope was quite helpful in this; as we could use the light as well as the magnification to help us delineate the brain tumor - gliotic interface and while it was vague at sometimes we could I think clearly get a good cleavage plane in most instances so that we got a gross total removal of this very large and necrotic-looking tumor of the brain.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Right chronic subdural hematoma.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Right chronic subdural hematoma.,TYPE OF OPERATION: , Right burr hole craniotomy for evacuation of subdural hematoma and placement of subdural drain.,ANESTHESIA: , General endotracheal anesthesia.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , 100 cc.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE:,  In preoperative identification, the patient was taken to the operating room and placed in supine position.  Following induction of satisfactory general endotracheal anesthesia, the patient was prepared for surgery.  Table was turned.  The right shoulder roll was placed.  The head was turned to the left and rested on a doughnut.  The scalp was shaved, and then prepped and draped in usual sterile fashion.  Incisions were marked along a putative right frontotemporal craniotomy frontally and over the parietal boss.  The parietal boss incision was opened.  It was about an inch and a half in length.  It was carried down to the skull.  Self-retaining retractor was placed.  A bur hole was now fashioned with the perforator.  This was widened with a 2-mm Kerrison punch.  The dura was now coagulated with bipolar electrocautery.  It was opened in a cruciate-type fashion.  The dural edges were coagulated back to the bony edges.  There was egress of a large amount of liquid.  Under pressure, we irrigated for quite sometime until irrigation was returning mostly clear.  A subdural drain was now inserted under direct vision into the subdural space and brought out through a separate stab incision.  It was secured with a 3-0 nylon suture.  The area was closed with interrupted inverted 2-0 Vicryl sutures.  The skin was closed with staples.  Sterile dressing was applied.  The patient was subsequently returned back to anesthesia.  He was extubated in the operating room, and transported to PACU in satisfactory condition.

transcription: REASON FOR NEUROLOGICAL CONSULTATION: , Cervical spondylosis and kyphotic deformity.  The patient was seen in conjunction with medical resident Dr. X. I personally obtained the history, performed examination, and generated the impression and plan.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,The patient is a 45-year-old African-American female whose symptoms first started some one and a half years ago with pain in the left shoulder and some neck pain.  This has subsequently resolved.  She started vigorous workouts in November 2005.  In March of this year, she suddenly could not feel her right foot on the bathroom floor and subsequently went to her primary care physician.  By her report, she had a nerve conduction study and a diagnosis of radiculopathy was made.  She had an MRI of lumbosacral spine, which was within normal limits.  She then developed a tingling sensation in the right middle toe.  Symptoms progressed to sensory symptoms of her knees, elbows, and left middle toe.  She then started getting sensory sensations in the left hand and arm.  She states that she feels a little bit wobbly at the knees and that she is slightly dragging her left leg.  Symptoms have been mildly progressive.  She is unaware of any trigger other than the vigorous workouts as mentioned above.  She has no associated bowel or bladder symptoms.  No particular position relieves her symptoms.,Workup has included two MRIs of the C-spine, which were personally reviewed and are discussed below.  She saw you for consultation and the possibility of surgical decompression was raised.  At this time, she is somewhat reluctant to go through any surgical procedure.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Ocular migraines.,2.  Myomectomy.,3.  Infertility.,4.  Hyperglycemia.,5.  Asthma.,6.  Hypercholesterolemia.,MEDICATIONS: , Lipitor, Pulmicort, Allegra, Xopenex, Patanol, Duac topical gel, Loprox cream, and Rhinocort.,ALLERGIES: , Penicillin and aspirin.,Family history, social history, and review of systems are discussed above as well as documented in the new patient information sheet.  Of note, she does not drink or smoke.  She is married with two adopted children.  She is a paralegal specialist.  She used to exercise vigorously, but of late has been advised to stop exercising and is currently only walking.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: , She does complain of mild blurred vision, but these have occurred before and seem associated with headaches.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , On examination, blood pressure 138/82, pulse 90, respiratory rate 14, and weight 176.5 pounds.  Pain scale is 0.  A full general and neurological examination was personally performed and is documented on the chart.  Of note, she has a normal general examination.  Neurological examination reveals normal cognition and cranial nerve examination including normal jaw jerk.  She has mild postural tremor in both arms.  She has mild decreased sensation in the right palm and mild decreased light touch in the right palm and decreased vibration sense in both distal lower extremities.  Motor examination reveals no weakness to individual muscle testing, but on gait she does have a very subtle left hemiparesis.  She has hyperreflexia in her lower extremities, worse on the left.  Babinski's are downgoing.,PERTINENT DATA:  ,MRI of the brain from 05/02/06 and MRI of the C-spine from 05/02/06 and 07/25/06 were personally reviewed.  MRI of the brain is broadly within normal limits.  MRI of the C-spine reveals large central disc herniation at C6-C7 with evidence of mild cord compression and abnormal signal in the cord suggesting cord edema.  There is also a fairly large disc at C3-C4 with cord deformity and partial effacement of the subarachnoid space.  I do not appreciate any cord edema at this level.,IMPRESSION AND PLAN:  ,The patient is a 45-year-old female with cervical spondylosis with a large C6-C7 herniated disc with mild cord compression and signal change at that level.  She has a small disc at C3-C4 with less severe and only subtle cord compression.  History and examination are consistent with signs of a myelopathy.,Results were discussed with the patient and her mother.  I am concerned about progressive symptoms.  Although she only has subtle symptoms now, we made her aware that with progression of this process, she may have paralysis.  If she is involved in any type of trauma to the neck such as motor vehicle accident, she could have an acute paralysis.  I strongly recommended to her and her mother that she followup with you as soon as possible for surgical evaluation.  I agree with the previous physicians who have told her not to exercise as I am sure that her vigorous workouts and weight training since November 2005 have contributed to this problem.  I have recommended that she wear a hard collar while driving.  The results of my consultation were discussed with you telephonically.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE:,  The patient is a 75-year-old female who comes in today with concerns of having a stroke.  She states she feels like she has something in her throat.  She started with some dizziness this morning and some left hand and left jaw numbness.  She said that she apparently had something about three weeks ago where she was dizzy and ended up falling down and she saw Dr. XYZ for that who gave her some Antivert.  She said that today though she woke up in the middle of the night and her left hand was numb and she was having numbness on the left side of her face, as well as the left side of her neck.  She said she had an earache a day or so ago.  She has not had any cold symptoms.,ALLERGIES:,  Demerol and codeine.,MEDICATIONS: , Lotensin, Lopid, metoprolol, and Darvocet.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:,  The patient says that she feels little bit nauseated at times.  She denies chest pain or shortness of breath and again feels like she has something in her throat.  She has been able to swallow liquids okay.  She said that she did brush her teeth this morning and did not have any fluid dripping out of her mouth.  She does say that she occasionally has numbness in her left hand prior to today.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General:  She is awake and alert, no acute distress.,Vital Signs:  Blood pressure:  175/86.  Temperature:  She is afebrile.  Pulse:  78.  Respiratory rate:  20.  O2 sat:  93% on room air.,HEENT:  Her TMs are normal bilaterally.  Posterior pharynx is unremarkable.  It should be noted that her uvula did not deviate and neither did her tongue.  When she smiles though she has some drooping of the left side of her face, as well as some mild nasolabial fold flattening.,Neck:  Without adenopathy or thyromegaly.  Carotids pulses are brisk without bruits.,Lungs:  Clear to auscultation.,Heart:  Regular rate and rhythm without murmur.,Extremities:  Her muscle strength is symmetrical and intact bilaterally.  DTRs are 2+/4+ bilaterally and muscle strength is intact in the upper extremities.  She has a positive Tinel’s sign on her left wrist.,Neurological:  I also took monofilament and she could sense it easily when testing her sensation on her face.,ASSESSMENT:,  Bell’s Palsy.,PLAN:,  We did get an EKG showed some ST segment changes anterolaterally.  The only EKG I have here is from 1998 and she actually had bypass in 1999, but there certainly does not appear to be anything acute on his EKG.  I assured her that it does not look like she has a stroke.  If she wants to prevent a stroke, obviously quitting her smoking would help.  It should be noted she also takes Synthroid and Zocor.  We are going to give her Valtrex 1 g t.i.d. for seven days and then if she starts noticing any other drooping or worsening of her symptoms on the left side of her face, she needs to come back, but I will not start her on steroids at this time, which she agreed with.

She said that she apparently had something about three weeks ago where she was dizzy and ended up falling down and she saw Dr. XYZ for that who gave her some Antivert. She said that today though...

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  ,The patient is a 58-year-old right-handed gentleman who presents for further evaluation of right arm pain.  He states that a little less than a year ago he developed pain in his right arm.  It is intermittent, but has persisted since that time.  He describes that he experiences a dull pain in his upper outer arm.  It occurs on a daily basis.  He also experiences an achy sensation in his right hand radiating to the fingers.  There is no numbness or paresthesias in the hand or arm.,He has had a 30-year history of neck pain.  He sought medical attention for this problem in 2006, when he developed ear pain.  This eventually led to him undergoing an MRI of the cervical spine, which showed some degenerative changes.  He was then referred to Dr. X for treatment of neck pain.  He has been receiving epidural injections under the care of Dr. X since 2007.  When I asked him what symptom he is receiving the injections for, he states that it is for neck pain and now the more recent onset of arm pain.  He also has taken several Medrol dose packs, which has caused his blood sugars to increase.  He is taking multiple other pain medications.  The pain does not interfere significantly with his quality of life, although he has a constant nagging pain.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: , He has had diabetes since 2003.  He also has asthma, hypertension, and hypercholesterolemia.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS: , He takes ACTOplus, albuterol, AndroGel, Astelin, Diovan, Dolgic Plus, aspirin 81 mg, fish oil, Lipitor, Lorazepam, multivitamins, Nasacort, Pulmicort, ranitidine, Singulair, Viagra, Zetia, Zyrtec, and Uroxatral.  He also uses Lidoderm patches and multiple eye drops and creams.,ALLERGIES:,  He states that Dyazide, Zithromax, and amoxicillin cause him to feel warm and itchy.,FAMILY HISTORY:,  His father died from breast cancer.  He also had diabetes.  He has a strong family history of diabetes.  His mother is 89.  He has a sister with diabetes.  He is unaware of any family members with neurological disorders.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,  He lives alone.  He works full time in Human Resources for the State of Maryland.  He previously was an alcoholic, but quit in 1984.  He also quit smoking cigarettes in 1984, after 16 years of smoking.  He has a history of illicit drug use, but denies IV drug use.  He denies any HIV risk factors and states that his last HIV test was over two years ago.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: , He has intermittent chest discomfort.  He has chronic tinnitus.  He has urinary dribbling.  Otherwise, a complete review of systems was obtained and was negative except for as mentioned above.  This is documented in the handwritten notes from today's visit.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vital Signs:  HR 72. RR 16.,General Appearance:  Patient is well appearing, in no acute distress.,Cardiovascular:  There is a regular rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs.  There are no carotid bruits.,Chest:  The lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally.,Skin:  There are no rashes or lesions.,NEUROLOGICAL EXAMINATION:,Mental Status:  Speech is fluent without dysarthria or aphasia.  The patient is alert and oriented to name, place, and date.  Attention, concentration, registration, recall, and fund of knowledge are all intact.,Cranial Nerves:  Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation.  Optic discs are normal.  Visual fields are full.  Extraocular movements are intact without nystagmus.  Facial sensation is normal.  There is no facial, jaw, palate, or tongue weakness.  Hearing is grossly intact.  Shoulder shrug is full.,Motor:  There is normal muscle bulk and tone.  There is no atrophy or fasciculations.  There is no action or percussion myotonia or paramyotonia.  Manual muscle testing reveals MRC grade 5/5 strength in all proximal and distal muscles of the upper and lower extremities.,Sensory:  Sensation is intact to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibratory sensation, and joint position sense.  Romberg is absent.,Coordination:  There is no dysmetria or ataxia on finger-nose-finger or heel-to-shin testing.,Deep Tendon Reflexes:  Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ at the biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellas and ankles.  Plantar reflexes are flexor.  There are no finger flexors, Hoffman's sign, or jaw jerk.,Gait and Stance:  Casual gait is normal.  Heel, toe, and tandem walking are all normal.,RADIOLOGIC DATA:,  MRI of the cervical spine, 05/19/08:  I personally reviewed this film, which showed narrowing of the foramen on the right at C4-C5 and other degenerative changes without central stenosis.,IMPRESSION:  ,The patient is a 58-year-old gentleman with one-year history of right arm pain.  He also has a longstanding history of neck pain.  His neurological examination is normal.  He has an MRI that shows some degenerative changes.  I do believe that his symptoms are probably referable to his neck.  However, I do not think that they are severe enough for him to undergo surgery at this point in time.  Perhaps another course of physical therapy may be helpful for him.  I probably would not recommend anymore invasive procedure, such as a spinal stimulator, as this pain really is minimal.  We could still try to treat him with neuropathic pain medications.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,1.  I scheduled him to return for an EMG and nerve conduction studies to determine whether there is any evidence of nerve damage, although I think the likelihood is low.,2.  I gave him a prescription for Neurontin.  I discussed the side effects of the medication with him.,3.  We can discuss his case tomorrow at Spine Conference to see if there are any further recommendations.

transcription: PROCEDURE: EEG during wakefulness demonstrates background activity consisting of moderate amplitude beta activity observed bilaterally.  The EEG background is symmetrical.  Independent, small, positive, strong wave activity is observed bilaterally in the frontotemporal regions, with sharp and slow wave discharges observed more predominantly in the right frontotemporal region of the head.  No clinical signs of involuntary movements are noted during synchronous video monitoring.  The recording time is 22 minutes and 22 seconds.  There is an attenuation of background noise, faster activity during drowsiness, and light sleep is recorded.  No sustained epileptogenic activity is evident, but bilateral independent sharp wave activity is observed intermittently.  Photic stimulation induced a bilaterally symmetric photic response.,IMPRESSION:EEG during wakefulness and light sleep is abnormal with independent,positive strong wave activity observed in both frontotemporal regions of the head, more predominant in the right frontotemporal region.  The EEG results are consistent with a potentially epileptogenic process.  A clinical correlation is warranted.

transcription: Doctor's Address, Dear Doctor:, This letter is an introduction to my patient, A, who you will see in the near future.  He is a pleasant man with a history of Wilson's disease.  He was treated with penicillamine.  He was diagnosed with this problem at the age of 14. He was on his way to a trip to South Carolina when he developed shortness of breath, palpitations and chest discomfort.  He went to the nearest hospital, in Randolph, North Carolina, and it was found that he was experiencing atrial fibrillation at a rapid rate.  He was admitted and observed there.  He spontaneously converted to normal sinus rhythm and therefore did not require any further intervention.  He started taking Lopressor, which he tolerated well.  An echocardiogram was performed, which revealed mild to moderate left atrial enlargement.  Normal ejection fraction.  No other significant valvular abnormalities.  He reported to doctors that he had cirrhosis related to his Wilson disease.  A hepatologist was therefore consulted.  There was a recommendation to avoid Coumadin secondary to his significant and questionable liver disease, so he was placed on aspirin 325 mg once daily. After discussion with Mr. A and review of his records available to me, the status of his liver disease is unclear, however, he has never had a liver biopsy, so his diagnosis of cirrhosis which was of concern in North Carolina is in doubt.  His LFTs have remained normal and his copper level is undetectable with his current dose of penicillamine. I would appreciate your contribution to the long-term management of his anticoagulation as well as any recommendations regarding rhythm control.  He is in normal sinus rhythm during my evaluation on 06/12/2008.  He tolerates his metoprolol and aspirin without difficulty.  I guess the big question remains what level of risk is involved in placing him on Coumadin treatment due to his potentially paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and signs of left atrial enlargement that would place him at increased risk for recurrent episodes. I appreciate your contribution regarding this nice gentleman.  His current medications include penicillamine 250 mg p.o. four times daily, metoprolol 12.5 mg twice daily, and aspirin 325 mg daily. If you have any questions regarding his care, please do not hesitate to call me to discuss his case.  Otherwise, I look forward to hearing from you regarding its evaluation.  Thank you as always for your care of our patient.

transcription: PROCEDURE: Subcutaneous transposition of the ulnar nerve.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:,After administering appropriate antibiotics and MAC anesthesia, the upper limb was prepared and draped in the usual standard manner.  The arm was exsanguinated with Esmarch and the tourniquet inflated to 250 mmHg. A curvilinear incision was made over the medial elbow, starting proximally at the level of the medial intermuscular septum, curving posteriorly to the medial epicondyle, and then curving anteriorly along the course of the ulnar nerve.  The dissection was carried out up to the ulnar nerve.  The branches of the medial antebrachial and medial brachial cutaneous nerves were identified and protected. Osborne's fascia was released, ulnar neurolysis performed and the ulnar nerve mobilized.  Six cm of the medial intermuscular septum was excised and the deep periosteal origin of the flexor carpi ulnaris was released to avoid twisting of the nerve as it moved forward. The subcutaneous plane just superficial to the flexor-pronator mass was developed.  Meticulous hemostasis was maintained using bipolar electrocautery.  The nerve was transposed anteriorly, superficial to the flexor-pronator mass.  The motor branches were dissected proximally and distally to avoid tying or bending the ulnar nerve. A medial semicircular flap of the flexor-pronator fascia was raised and sutured to the subcutaneous tissue so as to prevent the nerve from displacement.  The subcutaneous tissue and skin were closed with simple interrupted sutures.  Marcaine and epinephrine were injected into the wound.  The elbow was bandaged and splinted.  The patient was awakened and sent to the recovery room in good condition, having tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Rule out temporal arteritis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Rule out temporal arteritis.,PROCEDURE: , Bilateral temporal artery biopsy.,ANESTHESIA: , Local anesthesia 1% xylocaine with epinephrine.,INDICATIONS: , I was consulted by Dr. bilateral temporal headaches to exclude temporal arteritis.  I explained the procedure in detail to the patient.,PROCEDURE:,Both sides were done exactly the same way.  After infiltration of 1% Xylocaine, a 2 to 3 cm incision was made above the temporal artery.  The temporal artery was identified and was completely normal on both sides.  The proximal and distal ends were ligated with 3-0 silk suture and Hemoccult.  The temporal artery specimen was taken from both sides measuring at least 2 to 3 cm.  They were sent as separate specimens, labeled right and left.  The wound was then closed with interrupted Monocryl 3-0 subcutaneous sutures and Dermabond.  She tolerated the procedure well.

transcription: PROCEDURE:, Sleep study.,CLINICAL INFORMATION:, This patient is a 56-year-old man who presented with symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea with snoring and hypertension. The test was carried out on 01/24/06. The patient weighed 191 pounds and was five feet seven inches tall.,SLEEP QUESTIONNAIRE: By the patient's own estimation, the patient took approximately 15 minutes to fall asleep, slept for six and a half hours, and had a few dreams. I didn't wake up and sleep was less restful. He was sleepy in the morning.,STUDY PROTOCOL: An all-night polysomnogram was recorded with a Compumedics E Series digital polysomnograph. After the scalp was prepared, Ag/AgCl electrodes were applied to the scalp according to the international 10-20 system. EEG was monitored from C4-A1, C3-A2, O2-A1, and O2-A1. EOG and EMG were continuously monitored by electrodes placed on the outer canthi and chin, respectively. Nasal and oral airflow were monitored using a three-port thermistor. Respiratory effort was measured by piezoelectric technology using an abdominal and thoracic belt. Blood oxygen saturation was continuously monitored by pulse oximetry. Heart rate and rhythm were monitored by surface electrocardiography. EMG of the tibialis anterior was studied using surface-mounted electrodes spaced 5 cm apart on both legs. Body position and snoring level were also monitored.,TECHNICAL QUALITY OF STUDY:,Good.,ELECTROPHYSIOLOGICAL MEASUREMENTS:,Total recording time 406 minutes, total sleep time 365 minutes, sleep latency 25.5 minutes, REM latency 49 minutes, _____ 90%, measured sleep latency 86%. _____ period was obtained. The patient spent 10% of the time awake in bed.,Stage I: 3.8,Stage II: 50.5,Stage III: 14%,Stage REM: 21.7%,Patient had relatively good sleep architecture, except for excessive waking.,RESPIRATORY MEASUREMENTS:,Total apnea/hypopnea 75, age index 12.3 per hour. REM age index 15 per hour. Total arousal 101, arousal index 15.6 per hour. Oxygen desaturation had fallen to 88%. Longest event: 35 seconds hypopnea with FiO2 94%. Total limb movements 92, PRM index 15.1 per hour. PRM wakefulness index 8.9 per hour.,ELECTROCARDIOGRAPHIC OBSERVATIONS:, Heart rate during sleep 60 to 64 per minute, during wakefulness 70 to 78 per minute.,CONCLUSIONS:, Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome with moderately loud snoring and significant apnea/hypopnea index.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,AXIS B: Polysomnography nocturnal.,AXIS C: Hypertension. The patient should return for a nasal CPAP titration. Sleep apnea, if left untreated, can lead to chronic hypertension which can have cardiovascular consequences. Excessive daytime sleepiness, dysfunction, and memory loss may also occur.

transcription: CC: ,Headache.,HX:, This 51-year-old RHM was moving furniture several days before his presentation when he hit his head (top) against a door panel. He then rolled backwards and hit his back against a trailer hitch. There was no associated LOC but he felt “dizzy”. He has complained of HA since the accident. The next day, he began experiencing episodic dizziness lasting several minutes, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. He has been in bed most of the time since the accident. He also complained of temporary weakness of the left lower limb. The day before he was admitted, he went to his room and his girlfriend heard a loud noise. She found him on the ground, unable to speak or move his left side well. He was taken to the local emergency room. In the ER, he had a period where he looked to the right for about a minute. During this time, he was unable to speak and did not seem to understand verbal questions. This has been resolved. Emergency personnel noted decreased movement on the left side and left Babinski sign. He received 5 mg of valium and 1.0 g of DPH. HCT was performed and he was transferred to UIHC.,PMH:, DM, Coronary artery disease, Left femoral neuropathy of unknown etiology. Multiple head injuries in the past (falls/fights).,MEDS:, unknown oral medication for DM.,SHX:, 10+pack-year m/o Tobacco use; I stopped 2 years ago. Pack of 6 beers/week. No illicit drug use.,FHX:, unknown.,EXAMINATION: ,70BPM, BP144/83, 16RPM, 36.0C,MS: Alert and oriented to person, place, time. Fluent speech.,CN: left lower facial weakness with right gaze preference. Pupils 3/3 shrinking to 2/2 upon exposure to light. Flat optical disks. MOTOR: decrease in spontaneous movement of the left extremities. Strength 5/4 in upper and lower limbs. Normal muscle tone and volume.,SENSORY: Also withdraws from harmful stimulation in all four extremities. GAIT/STATION/COORDINATION: Not tested.,The general physical examination was unremarkable.,During the examination, the patient experienced a seizure in which his head was spinning and his eyes were deviating to the left, and his right hand was twitching. The entire spell lasted a minute. During the episode, he did not respond verbally. He appeared groggy and lethargic after the event.,Non-contrast HCT: 11/18/92: Right frontal skull fracture with associated minimal epidural hematoma and small subdural hematoma, as well as some adjacent subarachnoid blood and brain bruising.,LABS:, CBC, GS, PT/PTT were all WNL.,COURSE:, Patient was diagnosed with SAH/contusion right frontal and post-traumatic convulsions. DPH was continued and he received a dose of Librium for possible alcohol withdrawal. A neurosurgical consultation was obtained. He did not undergo surgery and was released on 1/12/92. Neuropsychological testing on 11/25/92 revealed: poor orientation to time or place and poor attention. Anterograde verbal and visual memory was severely impaired. Speech became slightly dysarthric when fatigued. Faulty word search. Difficulty copying 2 of 3 three-dimensional figures. Recent head trauma as well as a history of ETOH abuse and multiple prior head injuries likely contribute to his deficits.

transcription: Doctor's Address, Dear Doctor:, This letter serves as a reintroduction to my patient, A, who will see you on Thursday 06/12/2008.  As you know, he is an unfortunate gentleman who suffers from reflex sympathetic dystrophy of both lower limbs.  His current symptoms are more severe on the right and he has a persistent injury that has failed to heal on his right leg.  He visited the wound clinic to try to cure this problem, but he could not tolerate pressure dressings and was unable to achieve satisfactory healing.  He was seen by Dr. X for management of his pain and was considered for the possibility of amputation as a treatment option to help reduce his pain.  He was seen by Dr. Y of Orthopedic Associates to review this case.  However, in my discussion with Dr. Z and his assessment of Mr. A, it was felt that this might be an unwise course to take given the lack of likelihood of reduction in his pain from his RST, the questionable healing of his wound given the non-invasive studies that revealed tenuous oxygenation to his right leg, and fears of worsening of his RST symptoms on his left leg if he were to undergo amputation.  Based on the results of his transcutaneous oxygen levels and his dramatic improvement with oxygen therapy during this test, Dr. Z felt that a course of hyperbaric oxygen might be useful to help improve his wounds.  As you may or may not know, we have certainly taken aggressive and significant steps to try to improve Mr. A's pain. He went to the Cleveland Clinic for an implantable stimulator, which failed to significantly improve his pain.  He currently takes up to eight methadone tablets four times a day, morphine up to 100 mg three times a day and Dilaudid two tablets by mouth every two hours to help reduce his pain.  He is also currently taking Neurontin 1600 mg three times daily, Effexor XR 250 mg once daily, Cytomel 25 mcg once daily, Seroquel 100 mg p.o. q. day, levothyroxine 300 mcg p.o. q. day, Prinivil 20 mg p.o. q. day, and Mevacor 40 mg p.o. q day., I appreciate your help in determining whether hyperbaric oxygen is a reasonable treatment for this unfortunate situation.  Both Dr. Z and I tried to emphasize the fact that amputation can be a brutal, irreversible treatment that may not lead to a meaningful conclusion.  He was evaluated by Dr.  He agrees that a less aggressive form of therapy might be most appropriate. Thank you kindly for your quick assessment of this kind gentleman in an unfortunate situation.  If you have any questions regarding his care, please do not hesitate to call me at my office.  Otherwise, I look forward to hearing from you soon after your review.  Please feel free to call me if possible or if you have any questions about anything.

transcription: XYZ Street, City, State, Dear Dr. CD:, Thank you for seeing Mr. XYZ, a pleasant 19 year old man who saw you in 2005 for suspected seizure activity.  He comes to my office today to continue taking Dilantin 300 mg daily and has not had a seizure episode in 2 1/2 years.  He asks to leave Dilantin at this point.  After reviewing your 2005 note, there was some disagreement as to the true nature of his emergency department episodes and consideration was given to reconsidering the use of medication.  His physical, neurological examination was normal at that time.  His Dilantin level is slightly low at 12.5. I will appreciate your assessment and recommendation as to whether we should continue the Dilantin at this time.  I understand that this will likely involve repeating his EEG and so please coordinate this through the health center.  I am waiting for your response and if we should continue taking this medicine.  If you need a lab, we use ABC Diagnostic and any additional testing needed must be coordinated with the health center prior to scheduling.

transcription: PROCEDURES PERFORMED:, Phenolic neurolysis of left musculocutaneous nerve and bilateral obturator nerves.  Botulinum toxin injection left pectoralis major, left wrist flexors and bilateral knee extensors.,PROCEDURE CODES: , 64640 times three, 64614 times four, 95873 times four.,PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Spastic quadriparesis secondary to head trauma, 907.0.,DIAGNOSIS POSTOPERATIVE:,Spastic quadriparesis secondary to head trauma injury,907.0.,ANESTHESIA:,MAC.,COMPLICATIONS:,None.,DESCRIPTION OF TECHNIQUE:,Informed consent was obtained from the patient's brother.  The patient was brought to the minor procedure area and sedated according to his protocol.  The patient was positioned in the supine position.  The skin covering all injected areas was prepared with chlorhexidine.  The obturator nerves were identified lateral to the origin of the adductor longus tendon and below the femoral pulse using active EMG stimulation.  Approximately 7 ml was injected into the right side and 5 ml into the left side.  At all phenol injection sites in this area, injections were performed at the site of maximal hip adduction contraction with the least stimulus.  A negative blood sample was taken before each phenol injection.  The musculocutaneous nerve was identified in the left upper limb, above the brachial pulse, using active EMG stimulation.  Approximately 5 mL of 5% phenol was injected at this location.  Injections into this area were performed at the site of maximum elbow flexion contraction with the least stimulus.  Negative blood analysis was performed before each phenol injection. Muscles injected with botulinum toxin were identified using active EMG stimulation.  Approximately 150 units were injected bilaterally into the knee extensors, 100 units into the left pectoralis major, and 50 units into the left wrist flexors.  The total amount of botulinum toxin injected was 450 units diluted by 25 units in 1 mL.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and no complications were encountered.

transcription: CC: ,Bilateral lower extremity numbness.,HX: ,21 years old RHM complained of progressive numbness and incoordination of both lower extremities starting around 5/11/96. Symptoms peaked over a 12-24 hour period and have not changed since. Symptoms consist of tingling sensations in the distal lower extremities, approximately mid-calf height, bilaterally. He noted a decrease in coordination of both lower extremities, which he thought might be due to uncertainty about where his feet were placed in space. He denied having any bowel/bladder problems, or weakness or numbness elsewhere. Hot showers may improve his symptoms. He has not suffered from any recent flu-like illness. Medical and family history is unremarkable. He was not taking any medications.,EXAMINATION:, Nothing abnormal except for slight loss of distal vibratory sensation in the toes (R>L).,LAB:, CBC, Gen Screen, TSH, FT4, SPE, ANA were all WNL.,L-SPINE MRI:, Normal.,COURSE:, Normal examination and decrease in symptoms at next visit 4/23/93.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Squamous cell carcinoma of right temporal bone/middle ear space.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Squamous cell carcinoma of right temporal bone/middle ear space.,PROCEDURE: , Resection of right temporal bone; rectus abdominis myocutaneous free flap for reconstruction of a skull base defect; selective right neck dissection zones 2 and 3.,ANESTHESIA: , General endotracheal.,DESCRIPTION OF THE PROCEDURE: ,The patient was brought to the operating room, placed on the table in the supine position.  General endotracheal anesthesia was obtained in the usual manner.  The neurosurgery team placed the patient in pins and after positioning the patient, the right lateral scalp was prepared with Betadine after shaving as well as the abdomen.  The neck was also prepared.  After that, I made a wide ellipse of the conchal bowl with the Bovie and cut the current through the cartilage of the conchal bowl.  A wide post-auricular incision well beyond the mastoid tip extending to the right neck was then incised with the Bovie with the cutting current and a post-auricular skin flap was developed, leaving the excise conchal bolus in place while the auricle reflected anterior to the condyle.  After that, I used the Bovie to incise the soft tissue around the temporal bone from the tumor down to the mandible.  The condyle has been skeletonized to be easily visible.  The anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid was dissected and the spinal accessory nerve was identified and spared.  The contents of the neck down to the hyoid were dissected.  The hypoglossal nerve, vagus nerve, and spinal accessory nerve were dissected toward the jugular foramen.  The neck contents were collected as a separate specimen.  The external carotid artery was identified and ligated at its entry into the parotid and ligated distally with a Hemoclip for the future anastomosis.  A large posterior facial vein was identified and also cut for later use.  I then used the cutting and diamond burs to incise the skull above the external ear canal to expose the dura mater below and extended it posteriorly to the sigmoid sinus, dissecting or exposing the dura mater down to the level of the jugular bulb.  It became apparent that there were two tumors extending along the Eustachian tube medial to the condyle and so I used the router, I mean the lateral cutting bur to resect the condyle and the glenoid fossa to expose the medial extent of the Eustachian tube.  The internal carotid artery was dissected out of the parapharyngeal space into the carotid canal and I punctured the carotid canal until it was formed.  I dissected the vertical segment of the carotid artery as it entered the temporal bone until it rotated us toward the horizontal portion.  Once it was dissected, Dr. X began the procedure to complete the resection with the craniotomy.  For more details, please see his operative note. After Dr. X completed the resection, I then harvested the rectus free flap.  A skin paddle measuring approximately 4 x 4 cm was stretched next to the umbilicus.  The skin paddle was incised with the Bovie up to the anterior rectus sheath.  Sagittal incisions were made above and below the skin paddle and the rectus anterior sheath was dissected above and below the skin paddle.  The sheath was incised to the midline and a small ellipse was made around the fascia to provide blood supply to the overlying skin.  The skin paddle was then sutured to the fascia and muscle with interrupted 3-0 Vicryl.  The anterior rectus sheath was then reflected onto the rectus muscle, which was then divided superiorly with the Bovie and reflected out of the rectus sheath in an inferior direction.  The vascular pedicle could be seen entering the muscle in the usual manner.  The muscle was divided below the pedicle, then the pedicle was dissected through the groin to the external iliac artery and vein where it was ligated with two large hemoclips on each vessel.  The injury

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION:, New seizure., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a 2 1/2 year old female with a history of known febrile seizures, who was placed on Keppra oral solution 150 mg twice daily. to help prevent febrile seizures.  Although this treatment has been very effective in controlling her febrile seizures, she now has occasional brief periods of pauses and staring, during which she becomes unresponsive, but does not lose her postural tone.  According to Dad the typical spell lasts between 10-15 seconds, Mom says 3-4 minutes which probably means somewhere between 30-40 seconds.  Mom noted that an episode had recently occurred outside a store and was associated with perioral cyanosis, but no seizure activity was noted.  There have been no recent changes in her Keppra dosage and she is currently only on 20 mg/kg per day, which is overall a low dose for her.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Born at 36 weeks gestation via cesarean delivery at 8 pounds 3 ounces.  She has a history of febrile seizures and parents have reported an abdominal migraine, but upon further questioning it appears to be more of a food intolerance issue.,SURGICAL HISTORY: ,She has not had any surgery.,FAMILY MEDICAL HISTORY: ,There is a strong history of epilepsy on the maternal side of the family, including the mother with nonconvulsive seizures during childhood and additional seizures in maternal great-grandmother and a maternal great-aunt.  There is no other significant neurological history on his father's side of the family.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Currently lives with his mother, father, and two siblings.  She is at home full-time and does not attend daycare.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: ,A clear review of 10 systems was performed and revealed no additional findings other than those noted in the current illness history.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,Vital signs: Weight was 15.6 kg.  She was afebrile.  The remainder of her vital signs were stable and within normal limits for her age according to the medical record.,General: She was awake, alert and oriented.  She was not in acute distress, only slightly confused when trying to place the EEG leads. HEENT: Showed a normocephalic and atraumatic head.  His conjunctivas were nonicteric and his sclerae were clear.  His eye movements were conjugate in nature.  His tongue and mucous membranes were moist.,Neck: Trachea appeared to be in the midline.,Chest: Clear on auscultation bilaterally with no crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi.,Cardiovascular: Presented normal sinus rhythm without murmurs.,Abdomen: Showed soft, nontender, and nondistended, with good bowel sounds.  There was no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly, or other masses noted on examination. Extremities: Showed IV placement in the right upper extremity with appropriate IV restraints.  There was no evidence of blunting, cyanosis or edema.  She had no functional deformity in any of her peripheral limbs. Neurological: Neurologically, her cranial nerves were generally intact.  His strength was good in bilateral upper and lower extremities with no distal to proximal variation.  His overall resting tone was normal.  Sensory examination was generally intact, if not slightly affected, in the upper and lower limbs.  Reflexes were 1+ in the bilateral patella.  The toes descended bilaterally.  Coordination showed precise striking ability and good rapid alternating movements.  Gait examination was postponed at this time due to placement of an EEG probe.,ASSESSMENT:A 2 1/2 year old female with a history of febrile seizures, now concerned with episodes of unclear etiology, but somewhat concerning for complex partial seizures and, to a lesser extent, non-convulsive generalized seizures.,RECOMMENDATIONS,1.  For now, we'll try to capture the EEG as long as she tolerates it; however, if she needed sedation, I would defer the EEG until further adjustments were made.

transcription: PRELIMINARY DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Contusion of the frontal lobe of the brain.,2.  Closed head injury and fall history.,3.  Headache, probably secondary to a contusion.,FINAL DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Bilateral contusion of the orbital surface of the frontal lobes.,2.  Closed head injury.,3.  Story of a fall., HOSPITAL COURSE: It concerns a 29-year-old man who fell at his home.  He was seen in the emergency room due to headaches.  CT scan of the brain revealed a contusion of the frontal lobe near the falx.  The patient had no focal signs.  He was admitted to ABCD.  A neurology consultation was obtained.  Neuro checks were carried out.  The patient remained stable, despite some frontal headaches.  He underwent an MRI to exclude extension of the contusion or the possibility of bleeding and non-contrast MRI of the brain revealed findings consistent with contusion of the orbital surface of the frontal lobes bilaterally near the interhemispheric fissure.  The patient remained clinically stable and his headache resolved.  He was sent home on 11/6/2008.,PLAN:,Discharge patient home.,ACTIVITY:,As tolerated.,Patient was advised to call if headache is recurring and to take Tylenol 650 mg 1 p.o. q.6 hours. p.r.n. headache.  The patient was advised to contact me and the neurologist in approximately 1 week.

transcription: REASON FOR NEUROLOGICAL CONSULTATION: Muscle twitching, clumsiness, progressive pain syndrome, and gait disturbance.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a 62-year-old African-American male with a significant past medical history of diabetes, hypertension, a stroke in 2002 with minimal residual right-sided weakness as well as two MIs, a condition after insertion of a pacemaker who initially presented with numbness in its lower part. extremities in 2001. He says since then these symptoms have been progressive and now affect his legs above his knees as well as his hands.  More recently, he describes a burning sensation accompanied by numbness.  It has become a particular problem and of all the problems he experiences, he finds pain to be his main concern.  Over the past six months, he noticed that he could not feel hot objects in his hands and that objects would slip away from him.  He denies any weakness in oneself, just clumsiness and diminished sensation.  He also complains of brief muscle twitches, which occur in both hands and shoulders.  This is a problem that has been around for quite a long time and has become even more prevalent recently.  He has no tremors.  He denies any neck pain.  He walks with the aid of a walker due to an unstable gait. Recently, he tried gabapentin, but it was not effective in controlling the pain.  The oxycodone helps somewhat and provides pain relief for at least three hours.  Due to the pain, he has significant issues with interrupted sleep.  He claims he hasn't had a good night's sleep in many years.  About six months ago, after a heart attack and the insertion of a pacemaker, he was transferred to a nursing facility.  At this facility, his insulin was stopped.  Since then, he has only taken oral medications for his diabetes.  He denies any back pain, neck pain, changes in bowel or bladder function, or specific injury prior to these symptoms.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Diabetes, hypertension, coronary heart disease, stroke, arthritis, GERD and headache.,MEDICATIONS:,Trazodone, simvastatin, hydrochlorothiazide, Prevacid, lisinopril, glipizide and gabapentin.,FAMILY. Motor examination reveals slight atrophy of bilateral FDI, but still has strong grip.  Individual muscle strength is near normal with only subtle weakness in plantar and dorsiflexion of the ankle.  Tone and volume are normal.  Sensory examination reveals severe decrease in all modalities in her lower extremities just above the knees distally.  He has no sense of vibrations in his knees.  Likewise, there is a decrease in all sensory modalities in both of his upper extremities just above the wrist distally.  The only reflexes I could get with traces of reflexes in his biceps.  The remaining reflexes were inelicitable.  No Babinski.  The patient walks normally with the help of a cane.  He suffers from severe sensory ataxia with inability to walk unassisted.  Romberg positive with eyes open and closed.,PRINT AND PLAN:,1.  Probable painful diabetic neuropathy.  Symptoms are primarily sensory and present with severe dysfunction, with the patient unable to move independently as well as difficulty gripping and temperature differentiation in their upper limbs. transcription: REASON FOR VISIT: The patient is a 76-year-old man referred for neurological consultation by Dr. X. The patient is accompanied to the clinic today by his wife and daughter.  It provides a small part of its history; however, his family provides virtually all of this.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: He has problems with walking and balance, bladder control, thinking and memory.  When I asked him for details, he couldn't tell me much more than that he had difficulty walking and had bladder problems.  He is vaguely aware that he has memory problems. According to his family, he has had difficulty with his gait for at least three or four years.  At first they thought it was a weakness and because he was on the ground (e.g. while gardening) he was not able to get up on his own.  They tried to stop the statin he was taking at the time, but when there was no improvement after two weeks, they started the statin again.  Over time, he developed more and more difficulties.  He started to stir.  He started using a cane about two and a half years ago and has been using a walker with wheels on the front since July 2006. At this point he often, if not always, has difficulty getting in or out of the seat.  He often tends to lean back or to the side when sitting.  He often, if not always, has trouble getting in or out of a car, always drags or scrapes his feet, always has trouble turning or changing direction, always has trouble with uneven surfaces or curbs, and always has to hold on to someone or something when he walks.  It didn't fall last month.  He actually fell earlier, but there seemed to be fewer opportunities to fall.  His family recently purchased a lightweight wheelchair for use if he travels long distances.  He doesn't have stairs in his home, but his family says he wouldn't be able to use them.  His writing became smaller and more fragile. Regarding the bladder, he says, “I wet the bed.”  In speaking with his family, it appears that he is not warned that he needs to empty his bladder.  He was diagnosed with a small bladder tumor in 2005. This was treated by Dr. Y. Dr. X does not believe that the bladder tumor had anything to do with the patient's urinary incontinence.  The patient has been wearing a pad or underwear for at least a year to a year and a half.  His wife says they make two or three a day.  He was subjected to medication; however, they did not help.,He does not have headaches or a feeling of fullness of the head.,Regarding thinking and memory, at first he seemed forgetful and had problems with dates.  He now seems less spontaneous and his family says he seems to have difficulty expressing himself.  His wife went back on her medication about two years ago.  She stopped driving about three years ago.  She found out his license had expired about a year ago and she was concerned enough at that point to tell him he couldn't drive anymore.  Apparently, he did not object.  At this stage, he frequently has problems with memory, orientation and daily problems to solve at home.  He needs support for his daily activities, such as reminders to brush his teeth, get dressed, etc.  He is a retired office machine repairer.  He is currently up and active about 12 hours a day and sleeping or lying down about 12 hours a day. He did not undergo physical or occupational therapy and was not treated with medications for Parkinson's disease or Alzheimer's disease.  He was treated for his bladder.  He did not have a lumbar puncture. The medical history and 14 system review from the form they filled out for this visit that I reviewed with them is negative with the exception that he has had hypertension since 1985, high cholesterol since 1997, and diabetes since 1998. The bladder tumor was discovered in 2005 and was treated non-invasively.  He lost weight by about 200 pounds.

transcription: HISTORY: A neurologic consult was requested to evaluate and assist with his seizure medications.  The patient is a 3 year and 3 month old girl suffering from refractory epilepsy.  She had previously been under the care of XYZ, but was managed by the UCSF Epilepsy Program and recently by Dr. Y. I reviewed her previous neurological evaluations relevant to CHCC and also interviewed her mother.  She remained seizure free until 12/25/2007 when she had a seizure at home treated with Diastat.  She presented to our emergency room today with a prolonged seizure despite receiving 20 mg of Diastat at home.  Mom recorded a temperature of 103 at home.  In the emergency room, it was between 101 and 102 degrees Fahrenheit.  I reviewed the ER notes.  At 7:54 a.m., she was experiencing intermittent generalized tonic-clonic seizures despite receiving a total of 1.5 mg of lorazepam x5.  A UCSF fellow was contacted.  She received supplemental fosphenytoin and a total dose of 15 mg/kg.  Vital weight was 27. The convulsions had apparently stopped.  The valproic acid level obtained at 8:35 a.m. was 79. According to Mom, her last dose was at 6 p.m. and she didn't get her morning dose.  Other labs showed mild leukocytosis with a white blood cell count of 21,000 and a normal CMP. The previous workup here showed an EEG from 2005, which showed a left posterior focus.  MRIs from June 2007 and January 2005 were within normal limits.  Mom describes the following types of seizures:,1.  Unresponsive blinking.,2.  Look to one side.,3.  Focal motor activity in one arm and recently generalized tonic seizure. She also said she was supposed to see Dr. Y this Friday, but had rescheduled it for a later date when the genetic test results were available.  She was being treated by doctors like Dr. Z had already attended her last UCSF. She had failed most first- and second-line antiepileptic medications.  These include Keppra, Lamictal, Trileptal, phenytoin and phenobarbital.  These are caused by allergies, but she has had no real allergic reactions to them.  In fact, it led to an allergic reaction resulting in a rash and hypotension. She was also treated with Clobazam.  His best control is with his current regimen of valproic acid and Tranxen.  Further attempts to reduce Topamax, but this resulted in increased seizures.  She is also suffering from oligohidrosis this summer.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Include Diastat 20 mg; Topamax 25 mg twice daily, or 3.3 per kilogram per day; Tranxen 15 mg twice daily; Depakote 125 mg three times a day, or 25 per kilo per day.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: weight 15 kg.,GENERAL: Patient was awake, appeared sedated and postictal.,NECK: floppy.,NEUROLOGICAL: She had a few brief myoclonic jerks of the legs during drowsiness, but otherwise no overt seizures, no activity epileptic nor involuntary movements were observed. She was able to follow commands like when I ask her to kiss mom.  She recognized her doll.  The left fundus is pointed.  She withstood the rest of the exam.  There were no obvious lateralized findings. EVALUATION: Status epilepticus was resolved.  Triggered by a febrile illness, possibly viral.  Symptomatic Remote Refractory Partial Epilepsy.,IMPRESSION:,I discussed maximizing Depakote with mom and she agreed.  I recommend increasing your maintenance dose to one in the morning, one during the day, and two at bedtime.  For today, she administered 250 mg of Depacon IV and the above dosage can be continued IV until she takes medication p.o. Dr. X agreed to the changes and orders were written for this.  She can continue taking her current doses of Topamax and Tranxene.  This can be given by NG if necessary.  Topamax can potentially be increased to 25 mg in the morning and 50 mg in the evening.  I will be available if needed during

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 63-year-old left-handed man who presents for further evaluation of several neurologic symptoms.  I asked him to discuss each symptom individually because he had great difficulty describing the nature of his problems.  He first mentioned that he was suffering from neck pain.  He states that he has suffered from this problem for at least 15 years.  It's worse with movement.  This has progressed very slowly over 15 years.  It is located at the base of its neck and is of pointed quality.  It also confirms a history of gait instability.  This has been happening for a few years and is getting progressively worse.  He describes feeling unsteady on his feet and “walking like a duck.”  He's fallen about three or four times in the last year and a half. He also describes having numb feet.  When I asked him to describe this in more detail, the numbness is actually limited to his toes.  The left is slightly more affected than the right.  He denies any tingling or paresthesia.  He also described slowly losing control of his hands.  He thinks he drops things because of weakness or lack of coordination in his hands.  This has also been happening for the last one to two years.  He has noticed that buttoning his clothes is more difficult for him.  It also has no numbness or tingling in the hands.  He does indeed have a history of chronic low back pain. At the end of the visit, when I asked him which symptom bothered him the most, he actually said his fatigue was the most bothersome.  He didn't even mention it in the first part of my history.  When I asked him to describe this in more detail, he said he felt a general exhaustion.  Basically, he stays in bed all day, every day.  I asked him if he was depressed, he said he was being treated for depression.  He doesn't know if this is being handled optimally.  As I just mentioned, he stays in bed most of the day and doesn't participate in any social activities.  He doesn't think he's necessarily sad.  His appetite is good.  He never received psychotherapy for depression. When I took his history, I noticed that he was very slow in responding to my questions and also had great difficulty remembering the details of his history as well as the names of doctors he had seen in the past.  I asked him if he had ever been evaluated for cognitive difficulties and he said he had undergone testing at Johns Hopkins a few years ago.  He states that the results were normal and that he specifically did not suffer from any dementia. When I asked him when his current symptoms were first evaluated, he stated that he saw Dr.  He believes he was told he had neuropathy, but it was unclear whether it was due to his diabetes.  He told me that you evaluated him more recently after Dr. Y recommended him for this evaluation.  He also saw Dr. Z for a neurosurgical consultation a few weeks ago.  He reports that she did not think there was any indication for surgery in his neck or back at that time.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,He has had diabetes for five years.  He also suffers from high cholesterol.  He has suffered from Crohn's disease for 25 or 30 years.  He has had a colostomy for four years.  He suffers from arthritis, believed to be linked to Crohn's disease.  He suffers from hypertension and coronary artery disease and is in post-stent status.  He suffers from depression.  He had a kidney stone removed about 25 years ago.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:, He takes Actos, Ambien, baby aspirin, Coreg, Entocort, folic acid, Flomax, iron, Lexapro 20 mg q.h.s., Lipitor, Pentasa, Plavix, Protonix, Toprol, Celebrex and Zetia.,ALLERGIES:, He states that Imuran caused him to develop tachycardia.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,He previously worked in the pipeline field, but he has been on disability for five years.  He is not sure what symptoms led to his becoming disabled.  He has already smoked about two packs of cigarettes

transcription: CC:, BLE weakness.,HX:, This 82-year-old RHM was referred to the Neurology department by the Neurosurgery department for evaluation of acute paraplegia. He was in his usual healthy state until 5:30 p.m. on 4/6/95, when he developed sudden "pressure-like" epigastric discomfort associated with bilateral lower extremity weakness, SOB, dizziness, and sweating. He knelt on the ground and “fell asleep.” Emergency medical services were alert and arrived a few minutes later. He was then easily awakened, but unable to move or palpate his lower extremities. No associated upper limb or bulbar dysfunction was noted. He was taken to a local hospital where his INR was 9.1. He received 15 mg of vitamin K and was transferred to UIHC to rule out spinal epidural hemorrhage. A T-spine MRI was obtained and the preliminary reading was “normal.” The neurology department was then invited to evaluate the patient.,MEDS:, Coumadin 2 mg qd, Digoxin 0.25 mg qd, Prazosin 2 mg qd.,PMH:, 1)HTN. 2) A-Fib on coumadin. 3) Peripheral vascular disease: left s/p femoropopliteal bypass (8/94) and graft thrombosis-thrombosis (9/94). 4) Prostate adenocarcinoma: s/p TURP (1992).,FHX:, nothing remarkable.,SHX:, farmer, married, no tobacco/ETOH/illicit drug use.,EXAMINATION:, BP165/60 HR86 RR18 34.2C SAO2 98% on room air.,MS: A&O on person, place, time. In no acute distress. Lucid.,CN: banal.,ENGINE: strength 5/5 in BUE. Flaccid paraplegia in BLE, Sensory: sensory level T6 to LT/PP, bilaterally. Decreased vibrational sense in the BLE in a distribution of low, distally.,Coord: FNF and RAM intact in the BUE. Unable to do HKS.,Station: no pronator drift.,Gait: not performed.,Reflexes: 2/2 BUE, Absent in BLE, plantar responses were flexing, bilaterally.,Rectal: decreased rectal tone.,EXAMINATION GEN: No carotid sounds. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. CV: irregular rate and rhythm with slight diastolic murmur at left sternal border. Abdomen: flat, soft, non-tender, without noisy or pulsating masses. Distal pulses were strong in all extremities., COURSE:, Hgb 12.6, Hct 40%, WBC 11.7, Plt 154k, INR 7.6, PTT 50, CK 41, GS was normal. ECG showed A-Fib at 75 BPM with competing junctional pacemaker, virtually unchanged from 9/12/94. The patient was suspected to have suffered an anterior-cervico-thoracic spinal cord infarction with resulting paraplegia and T6 sensory level. An X-ray radiograph was performed in the emergency department before admission. This revealed cardiomegaly and an enlarged mediastinum. He returned from the x-ray room and suddenly became unconscious and suffered a cardiopulmonary arrest. Resuscitation measures failed. Pericardiocentesis was unremarkable. Autopsy revealed massive aortic dissection extending from the aortic root to the origin of the iliac arteries with extensive pericardial hematoma. The dissection was retrospectively observed on the T-spine on MRI.

transcription: CC:, BLE, weakness and numbness., HX:, This 59-year-old RHM was seen and released from an emergency room 1 week prior to this presentation for a 3-week history of progressive sensory and motor deficits in both lower extremities. He reported numbness beginning in the trunk and slowly progressing to the lower extremities over a 4-week period. On presentation he felt numb from the nipple line onwards. Additionally, he began experiencing progressive weakness in his lower extremities over the past week. He began using a cane 5 days before being seen and had difficulty walking and climbing stairs. He claimed he couldn't stand. He denied losing control of his bowels or bladder. However, he had not had a bowel movement in 3 days and he had not urinated in 24 hours. His lower limbs had been cold for a day. He denied any associated back or neck pain. He suffers from chronic shortness of breath, but he feels like his situation has gotten worse. Getting out of bed became a laborious task and she needed help getting to the bathroom. Ibuprofen provided minimal analgesia. In the evening, the sole of one foot was numb. She woke up the next morning, 5/9/97, with a “tingling” sensation in the BLE all the way to her buttocks. She was given Darvocet for analgesia and took a plane to the larger city in which she was based. Over the course of the hour-long flight, her BLE weakness progressed to a non-weight-bearing state (i.e., she could no longer stand). transcription: CC: Fall followed by nausea and vomiting.,HX: This 52-year-old RHM initially presented in 10/94 with a two-year history of progressive speech difficulties. He “knew what he wanted to say, but couldn’t say it.” His speech was slurred and he had difficulty controlling his tongue. Examination at this time was notable for phonemic paraphasic errors, accurate repetition of short sentences with reduced fluency, and nasal speech disturbances. He could read, but could not write. He presented with facial limb apraxia, decreased gag reflex, and positive grasp reflex. It was thought that he may have suffered from Pick's disease or degeneration of the basal cortical ganglia. On 11/18/94 he fell and was seen in the neurology clinic on 11/23/94. The EEG showed borderline background slowing and no other abnormalities. An MRI on 8/11/94 revealed slight atrophy of the left temporal lobe. Neuropsychological evaluations were obtained on 10/25/94 and 11/8/94. These were consistent with progressive aphasia and apraxia with relative economy of nonverbal reasoning. He stated that he had consumed 8 beers on the evening of 1/1/95. On 01/02/95, at 9:30 a.m., he fell forward while in his kitchen and hit his forehead on the counter, then hit his occiput on the floor. He then developed nausea and vomiting, tinnitus, dizziness, headaches and mild shortness of breath. He was taken to the UIHC ETC. Scalpel examinations were negative and he was treated with IV Compazine and IV fluid hydration and sent home. His nausea and vomiting persisted and he became generally weak. He returned to the UIHC ETC on 01/05/95. HCT analysis revealed a right frontal SDH containing signs of chronic and acute hemorrhage.,MEDS:,None.,PMH:,1) fell in 1990 from 15 feet high and landed on his feet, sustaining crush injuries to his feet and ankles. transcription: CC:, Fall.,HX:, This 67 year old RHF was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease on 1/9/95 by a local doctor. For a year before diagnosis, the patient experienced unsteady gait, falls and episodes of dizziness. She also noticed that she was slowly "losing" her voice and her handwriting was getting smaller and smaller. Two months before diagnosis, she began to have bradykinesia, but denied any tremor. She noted no improvement on Sinemet, which started in 9/95. At the time of presentation on 2/13/96, she continued to have coordination problems and an unsteady gait. Plantar responses were flexor., Gen Exam: Unremarkable. HEENT: unremarkable., COURSE:, Patient continued to take Sinemet CR 25/100 1 tablet TID and was told to monitor orthostatic BP at home. The evaluating neurologist was concerned that she might have Parkinsonism and dysautonomia. She was seen again on 05/28/96 and reported no improvement in her condition. Additionally, she complained of worsening dizziness when standing and had an episode, a week before 5/28/96, during which she was at her kitchen table and became unable to move. There were no involuntary movements or alterations in sensory/mental state. During the episode, she remembers wanting to turn around, but not being able to. Two weeks before 05/28/96, she had an episode of orthostatic syncope during which she hit her head during a fall. She stopped Sinemet 5 days before 05/28/96 and felt better. She felt like she was moving slower and her micrograph had deteriorated. She had recently had difficulty turning over in bed and sometimes fell while turning. She denied hypophonia, dysphagia, or diplopia. ON EXAMINATION: BP (supine) 153/110 with HR 88. BP (standing) 110/80 with HR 96. (+) Myerson sign and mild hypomimia, but no hypophonia. There was normal blinking and EOM. The motive force was full throughout. No tremor at rest, but slight postural tremor present. No rigidity noted. Slight reduction in finger tapping. The reflexes were symmetrical. No Babinski sign and no clonus. The gait was short with a slight anteroflexed posture. She was unable to light the spot. Retropulsion in 3-4 stages noted. Parkinsonism did not respond to Sinemet and she presented with autonomic dysfunction suggestive of Shy-Drager syndrome. It was recommended that he liberalize his dietary salt intake and lie down with the head of the bed elevated to 20-30 degrees.

There was enhancement of the meninges around the prepontine cistern and internal ear canals, as well as enhancement of a scar or inflamed mucosa of the foramen of Magendie. These changes were suggestive of bacterial or granulomatous meningitis. The patient underwent a ventriculostomy on 10/19/94. CSF collected on 10/19/94 via insertion of a V-P shunt revealed: 22 leukocytes (21 lymphocytes, 1 monocyte), 380 red blood cells, glucose 58, protein 29, GS negative, cultures (bacterial, fungal, AFB) negative, cryptococcal antigen and India ink were negative. Numerous CSF samples were collected from the lumbar region and diversion reservoir and these were consistently unremarkable except for an occasional CSF protein of up to 99 mg/dL. Serum and CSF toxoplasma titers and ACE levels were negative on several occasions. VDRL and HIV tests were unremarkable. The cultures of 10/27/94 and 10/31/94 taken from the cervical region finally gave rise to non-encapsulated crytococcus neoformans. The patient was treated with amphotericin and showed some improvement. However, scarring had likely developed by that time and the V-P shunt remained in place.

transcription: CC:, Rapidly progressive amnesia.,HX:,This 63-year-old RHM presented with a history of progressive anterograde amnesia for 1 year. During his presentation, he couldn't remember anything from one minute to the next. He also suffered from retrograde memory loss, to the extent that he could not remember the names of his grandchildren, but generally retained his intellect, language, personality and numeracy. He underwent extensive evaluation at the Mayo Clinic and an MRI there revealed increased signal on T2-weighted images in the mesiotemporal lobes bilaterally. There was no mass effect. Areas slightly enhanced with gadolinium.,PMH:, 1) CAD; MIx2 (1978 and 1979). 2) PVD; Aortic endarterectomy s/p (3/1991). 3)HTN. 4) Bilateral inguinal hernia repair., FHX/SHX:, The mother died of a stroke at age 58. The father suffered from coronary artery disease and HTN. The patient stopped smoking in 1991, but was a heavy smoker (2 to 3 ppd) for many years. He had been a feed salesman all his adult life., ROS:, Nothing remarkable. No history of cancer.,EXAMINATION:, BP 136/75 HR 73 RR12 T36.6,MS: Alert but disoriented to person, place, time. He couldn't remember his date of birth and continually asked the interviewer what year it was. He did not remember the date of his marriage, his retirement or the names of his grandchildren. He scored 18/30 on the Follutein MMSE with severe orientation and memory deficits. He had moderate difficulty naming. He repeated normally and had no constructive apraxia. Judgment remained good.,CN: unremarkable.,Motor: full strength throughout with normal muscle tone and mass.,Sensory: intact at LT/PP/PROP,Coordination: unremarkable.,Stance: no pronator drift, truncal ataxia or Romberg sign.,Gait: unremarkable.,Reflexes: 3+ throughout with descending plantar responses bilaterally.,General examination: unremarkable peculiarity.,STUDIES:,Brain MRI revealed hyperintense T2 signal in the mesiotemporal regions bilaterally, with slight enhancement on gadolinium scans. MRI, CT scan of the chest, and CT scan of the abdomen showed no evidence of lymphadenopathy or tumor. The EEG was normal during wakefulness and sleep. Antineuronal antibody screening was unremarkable. CSF studies were unremarkable and included varicella-zoster, herpes zoster, HIV, and HTLV testing and cytology. The patient underwent a stereotaxic brain biopsy at the Mayo Clinic which showed inflammatory changes, but no organism or etiology was concluded. TFT, B12, VDRL, ESR, CRP, ANA, SPEP and Folate studies were unremarkable. Neuropsychological testing revealed severe anterograde memory loss (verbal and visual) and less severe retrograde memory loss. Most other cognitive abilities were well preserved and findings were consistent with mesiotemporal dysfunction bilaterally.,IMPRESSION:,Limbic encephalitis secondary to cancer of unknown origin.,He was last seen on 07/26/96. MMSE 20/30 and mastery of category 20. Disinhibited affect. Slight right grip reflex. The clinical course was benign and nonprogressive, and unusual for such a diagnosis, although not rare.
