transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Low back pain.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Low back pain.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  L2-3 lumbar discogram.,2.  L3-4 lumbar discogram.,3.  L4-5 lumbar discogram.,4.  L5-S1 Lumbar Discogram.,ANESTHESIA: ,IV Sedation.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: ,The patient was brought to the radiology room and placed face down on a radiolucent table.  The C-arm was brought into the surgical field and AP, left right oblique, and lateral fluoroscopic images from the L1-2 to L5-S1 levels were obtained.  We then proceeded to prepare the lower back with a Betadine solution and drape it sterilely.  Using an oblique approach to the spine, the L5-S1 level was approached using an inclined C-arm with oblique projection to allow perpendicular penetration of the disc space.  A metal marker was then placed laterally and a needle entry point was determined.  A skin papule was lifted with 1% Xylocaine and a #18 gauge needle was advanced to the level of the disc space using AP, oblique, and lateral fluoroscopic projections.  A second needle, a 6-inch 22-gauge needle, was then introduced into the disc space and, with AP and lateral fluoroscopic projections, was placed centrally in the nucleus.  We then performed similar needle placement at the L4-5, L3-4, and L2-3 levels. A solution of Isovue 300 with 1 g of Ancef was then drawn into a 10 cc syringe and without informing the patient of our injection, we then proceeded to sequentially inject the disc spaces.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Intractable epilepsy, here for EEG video.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a 9 year old male who has a history of global developmental delay and infantile spasms.  In the end, the imaging study shows an MRI with absence of genu of the corpus callosum and thinning of the splenium of the corpus callosum, showing an aspect of cerebral dysgenesis.  He has severe overall developmental delay and is non-verbal.  He can track objects with his eyes, but lacks the ability to meaningfully interact with his environment.  He noted any intentional use of the hands.  He constantly has abnormal movements, more choreiform and dystonic.  He suffers from spastic quadriparesis, sometimes variable.  The patient is unable to sit or stand and receives all of his nutrition via a G tube. The patient began having seizures in childhood, presenting as infantile spasms.  I started seeing him when he was 20 months old.  By then, he had undergone tests in Seattle, Washington, and then saw Dr. X, a child neurologist in Mexico, who gave him Vigabatrin for infantile spasms.  The patient benefited from this medication and was doing well at that time regarding this type of seizure.  He was initially taking phenobarbital, which provided no benefit.  He continued to take phenobarbital; however, for a long period afterward.  The patient then began having more tonic seizures after his episodic spasms subsided and failed several drug trials, including valproic acid, Topamax, and Zonegran, at least briefly.  After he started taking Lamictal, he started to experience benefits and then actually had a year without a seizure before having an isolated seizure or two. Over the next 6 months to a year, he had only a few other seizures and was doing well in a general sense.  It was more recently that he started having new seizures which did not respond to higher doses of Lamictal up to 15 mg/kg/day.  These events manifest as tonic spells with eye deviation and postures.  The mother reports flexion of the upper limbs and extension of the lower limbs.  During this time, he is not able to cry or make any sounds.  These events last from a few seconds to a few minutes and occur at least several times a week.  There are times when he doesn't have one for a few days and other times when he has several days in a row with events.  He has another event manifesting as upper limb flexion and lower limb extension where he turns red and cries all over.  He may vomit after these episodes, then seems to calm down.  It is unknown if this is a seizure or if the patient is still responding. MEDICATIONS: The patient's medications include Lamictal for a total of 200 mg twice daily.  These are one 150 mg tablet and one 25 mg tablet.  He takes Zonegran using 25 mg capsules, 2 capsules twice a day, and baclofen 10 mg three times a day.  He is taking other medications including Xopenex and Atrovent.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: Currently any fever, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, rash, arthritis, or arthralgia are negative.  No respiratory or cardiovascular problems.  He has no change in his skills at this stage.,FAMILY HISTORY: ,Non-contributory.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: The patient is a thin, microcephalic male.  He has EEG electrodes in place and is on the EEG video at this time.,HEALTHY: His oropharynx has no lesions.,NECK: Supple with no lymphadenopathy.,CHEST: Clear on auscultation.,CARDIOVASCULAR: Rhythm and rhythm regular.  No murmur.,ABDOMEN: Benign with G tube in place.,EXTREMITIES: Reveals no clubbing, cyanosis or edema.,NEUROLOGICAL: Patient is alert and has bilateral esotropia.  He is able to briefly fixate and follow objects.  He is unable to reach objects.  It exhibits constant choreiform movements when excited.  These are more important in the upper and lower limbs.  He has a dystonic posture with bilateral wrist and finger flexion.  He also has plantar flexion

transcription: CC:, Slowing of motor skills and cognitive functions.,HX:,This 42-year-old LHM presented on 03/16/93 with progressively progressive deterioration of motor and cognitive skills over 3 years. He had difficulty holding down a job. His most recent job ended 3 years ago because he was unable to learn the correct protocols for maintaining a large treadmill. Before that, he was unable to hold a job in the mortgage department of a bank, nor could he establish and calculate property assessments. During the 6 months prior to presentation, he and his wife noted increasing speech difficulties and slowing of his motor skills (i.e., dressing and doing household chores). His walking became slower and he had difficulty balancing. He became anhedonic and disinterested in social activities, and had difficulty sleeping due to frequent awakenings and restlessness. His wife noticed “agitated movements” in his hands and feet. He underwent trials of sertraline and fluoxetine for depression 6 months prior to presentation by his local physician. These interventions do not seem to improve his mood and effects.,MEDS:, Fluoxetine.,PMH: ,1) Arthroscopic surgery on the right knee 3 years ago. 2) Vasectomy., FHX:, The mother died at the age of 60 from complications of Huntington's disease (dx to UIHC). MGM and two MAs also died from Huntington's disease. His 38-year-old sister attempted suicide twice., He and his wife have 2 adopted children., SHX: , unemployed. 2 years of college studies. Married 22 years.,ROS:,No history of use of dopaminergic or antipsychotic medications.,EXAMINATION:,Normal vital signs.,MS: A&O in relation to person, place and time. Dysarthric speech with poor respiratory control.,CN: Occasional hypometric saccades in both horizontal directions. No vertical gaze abnormality noted. Infrequent spontaneous forehead wrinkles and mouth movements. The remainder of the NC examination was unremarkable.,Motor: full strength and normal muscle tone and mass. Slight choreiform movements were noted in the hands and feet.,Sensory: unremarkable.,Coord: unremarkable.,Station/Gait: unremarkable, except that during tandem walking, slight dystonic and choreiform movements of BUE became more apparent.,Reflexes: 2/2 throughout. Plantar responses were flexing, bilateral.,There was no motor impermanence on tongue protrusion or hand grip.,COURSE:,He was thought to have early manifestations of Huntington's disease. A HCT was unremarkable. Elavil 25 mg qhs was prescribed. Neuropsychological evaluation revealed only mild anterograde memory loss. His chorea progressively worsened over the next 4 years. He developed increased motor persistence and slowed saccadic eye movements. His mood and effects became more labile. Genetic testing on 6/5/96 revealed a 45 CAg trinucleotide repeat band consistent with Huntington's disease. Brain MRI on 08/23/96 showed bilateral atrophy of the caudate nuclei.

transcription: CC:, HA and loss of vision.,HX: 71 years old, RHM developed a cataclysmic headache on 11/5/92 associated with a violent sneeze. The headache lasted 3 to 4 days. On 7/11/92 he experienced sharp pain and loss of vision in his left eye. The next day, her left pupil enlarged and her left upper eyelid began to droop. He was seen locally and a brain scan showed no signs of bleeding, but a tortuous left middle cerebral artery was visualized. Patient was transferred to UIHC on 12/11/92.,FHX:, HTN, stroke, coronary heart disease, melanoma.,SHX:, Stopped smoking 15 years ago.,MEDS:, Lanoxin, Capoten, Lasix, KCL, ASA, Voltaren, Alupent MDI,PMH: ,CHF, Atrial fibrillation, obesity, anemia, duodenal ulcer, spinal AVM. resection 1986 with residual sensory level T9, hyperreflexia and bilateral Babinski signs, COPD.,EXAMINATION: ,35.5C, BP ​​140/91, P86, RR20. Alert and oriented to person, place and time. CN: No light perception OS, Pupils: 3/7 decreasing to 2/7 upon exposure to light (i.e. fixed/dilated pupil OS). In neutral gaze, the left eye deviated laterally and inferiorly. There was complete OS ptosis. Looking down, there was an intortion operating system. The left eye could not move upward, middle or effectively downward, but could move sideways. The MOEs were completely OD. The rest of the CN exam was uneventful. Motor skills, coordination, standing and gait tests were unremarkable. Sensory examination revealed decreased pinpricks and light touch below T9 (old). Muscle stretch reflexes were increased (3+/3+) in both lower limbs and there were bilateral (old) Babinski signs. Upper limb reflexes were symmetrical (2/2). Cardiovascular examination revealed an irregular rhythm and coarse lung sounds bilaterally. The rest of the general examination was unremarkable.,LAB:, CBC, PT/PTT, general screen were unremarkable with the exception of a BUN 21 mg/DL. CSF: protein 88 mg/DL, glucose 58 mg/DL, red blood cells 2800/mm3, white blood cells 1/mm3. ANA, RF, TSH, FT4 were WNL.,PRINT:,CN3 palsy and vision loss. Differential diagnosis: temporal arteritis, aneurysm, intracranial mass.,COURSE:,External brain CT revealed a tortuous left MCA. A four-vessel cerebral angiogram revealed a dolichoectatic basilar artery and a tortuous LICA. There was no sign of an aneurysm. Transesophageal echocardiography revealed only atrial enlargement. Neuroophthalmologic evaluation revealed: loss of color vision and visual acuity OS, RAPD OS, bilateral optic disc pallor (OS > OD), CN3 palsy and bilateral temporal field loss, OS >> OD. ESR, CRP, and MRI were recommended to exclude temporal arteritis and intracranial mass. ESR 29 mm/h, CRP 4.3 mg/DL (high), patient was placed on prednisone. Temporal artery biopsy showed no evidence of vasculitis. MRI could not be obtained due to the patient's weight. A Sellar CT scan was performed instead: coronal sections revealed sellar hypertrophy and an upward curvature of the diaphragm saddle suggesting a pituitary mass. Retrospectively, sellar hypertrophy could be observed on the angiography radiographs. Differential consideration was given to cystic pituitary adenoma, noncalcified craniopharyngioma, or Rathke cyst with solid component. The patient refused surgery. He was seen at the neuroophthalmology clinic on 02/18/93 and was noted to have slight recovery of vision and improved visual fields. Aberrant reinnervation of the 3rd nerve was noted due to constriction of the pupil (OS) during adduction, downgaze, and upgaze. The upper eyelid, OS, raised in adduction and the gaze down, OS. EOM movements were otherwise complete and there was no evidence of ptosis. In retrospect, he was thought to have suffered a pituitary apoplexy in 11/92.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 61-year-old right-handed man who presents for further evaluation of foot and hand cramps.  He says he has been experiencing cramps in his feet and hands for six months.  He describes that foot cramps are much more noticeable than hand cramps.  He reports that he is developing twitching muscles in the toes of both feet.  These occur exclusively at night.  They can occur around three times a week.  When he develops these cramps, he gets up to relieve the discomfort.  He notices that the toes are in an extended position.  He walks on the ground and they seem to “fall into place”.  He develops calf pain after feeling the cramp.  Sometimes they wake him up from his sleep. He also developed cramps in his hands, although they were less severe and less frequent than those in his legs.  These do not occur at night and are completely random.  He notices that his thumb takes a flexed position and that he sometimes has to open it to relieve the cramp. He has never had such symptoms in the past.  He started taking Bactrim about nine months ago.  He had taken it briefly in the past, but never took it as long as he did today.  He can't think of any other factors that could be contributing to his symptoms. He has a history of HIV for 21 years.  He was taking antiretroviral drugs, but he stopped about six or seven years ago.  He reports that he could not tolerate the medication due to severe stomach upset.  He has a CD4 count of 326. He states that he has never developed AIDS.  He plans to resume antiretroviral treatment.,MEDICAL HISTORY: He has diabetes, but it is well controlled.  He also has Hepatitis C and HIV.,CURRENT MEDICATION: ,He takes insulin and Bactrim.,ALLERGIES: ,He has no known drug allergies.,SOCIAL HISTORY: ,He lives alone.  He recently lost his partner.  This happened about six months ago.  He denies using alcohol, tobacco or illicit drugs.  He is now retired.  He is very active and walks about four miles every few days.,FAMILY HISTORY: ,His father and mother suffered from diabetes.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: ,A complete systems review was obtained and was negative except as noted above.  This is documented in the handwritten notes from today's visit.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vital signs: Blood pressure 130/70.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: Essential tremors and torticollis. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 62-year-old white woman, right-handed, now left-handed, presenting with tremors since the 5th year.  She remembers that the tremors started in her right hand around this time, later in her early 20s she was put on propranolol for the tremors and more recently in the last 10 years she was put on primidone and clonazepam.  She thinks her clonazepam helps a lot, especially with anxiety and stress, and it helps with the tremors.  She has great difficulty writing due to the tremors, but does not report as many problems with other activities of daily living, such as drinking from a cup and doing daily activities.  For about 6-7 years she has had head tremors, which are mostly "no-no's" and occasional voice tremors as well.  Additionally, the patient was diagnosed with migraines without aura, which were very distant and closely spaced.  She also suffers from stress incontinence.  The last brain MRI was done in 2001 and was reported to be normal.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:,1.  Klonopin 0.5 mg twice daily.,2.  Primidone 100 mg twice daily,3.  Long-acting propranolol 80 mg once in the morning.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Essential tremor, cervical dystonia, endometriosis, migraines without aura, sensorineural deafness in the left ear and resection of basal cell carcinoma of the nose.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, L5-S1 lumbar laminectomy in 1975, laparotomy exploratory in 1967, tonsillectomy. and adenoidectomy and anal fissure surgery in 1975.,FAMILY HISTORY: Both parents suffer from ET and hypertension.  Maternal cousin with lupus.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Denies any smoking or alcohol.  She has been married for 44 years and has 3 children.  She worked as a labor and delivery nurse until early 2001 when she retired.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: No fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting.  No visual complaints.  She complains of decreased hearing on the left.  No chest pain or shortness of breath.  No constipation.  She gives a history of urge incontinence.  No rash.  No depressive symptoms.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure is 131/72, pulse is 50, and weight is 71.3 kg.  HEENT: PERRLA.  EOMI.  CARDIOVASCULAR: S1 and S2 normal.  Regular price and rhythm.  She has a rash on her right ankle and a basal cell carcinoma has already been resected.  NEUROLOGICAL: Alert and oriented x4.  Speech occasionally shows voice tremor.  The language is intact.  Cranial nerves II to XII intact.  Motor examination showed 5/5 power in all extremities with minimal increase in tone.  Sensory examination was intact to light touch.  Reflexes were brisk bilaterally, but they were equal and both toes were down.  His coordination showed minimal intentional component compared to bilateral finger-nose.  The gait was intact.  Lots of swing from Romberg.  The patient actually had a tremor in both upper limbs, more on the right than on the left.  She had a head tremor, which was not a variety, and she had minimal torticollis with her head twisted to the left.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: This 62-year-old white woman has essential tremor and mild torticollis.  Tremors are not bothersome for most activities of daily living, but she has great difficulty writing, which is completely illegible.  The patient did not wish to change any of her medication doses at this point.  We will check the brain with MRI and we will get the films later.  We will see her again in 3 months.  Additionally, the patient has refused any possible Botox for the slight torticollis she is suffering from at this stage.

transcription: NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES: Bilateral ulnar sensory responses are absent.  Medial bilateral distal sensory latencies are prolonged with severely attenuated evoked response amplitude.  The left radial sensory response is normal and robust.  The left sural response is absent.  Left medial distal motor latency is prolonged with attenuated evoked response amplitude.  The conduction velocity through the forearm is slightly slowed.  Right medial motor distal latency is prolonged with normal evoked response amplitude and conduction velocity.  Left ulnar distal motor latency is prolonged with severely attenuated evoked response amplitude below and above the elbow.  Conduction velocities through the forearm and elbow are prolonged.  Conduction velocity near the elbow is normal.  Right medial distal motor latency is normal with normal evoked response amplitudes at the wrist and normal evoked response amplitude at the wrist. The F waves are prolonged.,NEEDLE EMG: Needle EMG was performed on the left arm, lumbosacral and cervical paraspinal muscles as well as the mid thoracic muscles using a disposable concentric needle.  It revealed spontaneous activity in the lower cervical paraspinals, left abductor pollicis brevis, and first dorsal interosseous muscles.  There was evidence of chronic reinnervation in the triceps, extensor digitorum pollicis longus, flexor pollicis longus, and first dorsal interosseous and abductor pollicis brevis muscles.,PRINT:,This electrical study is abnormal. This is probably also the carpal tunnel, although a definitive localization cannot be made.,4.  Right ulnar neuropathy at the elbow, mild.,5.  Right median neuropathy at the wrist consistent with carpal tunnel syndrome, moderate.,6.  Left C8 radiculopathy (double crush syndrome).,7.  There is no evidence of thoracic radiculitis. The patient has responded very well to his abdominal pain since starting Neurontin.  He still has mild allodynia and is awaiting approval to obtain insurance coverage for his Lidoderm patch. transcription: FILE DESCRIPTION: This tracing was obtained using 27 gold-plated surface disc electrodes bonded and placed according to the international 10-20 system.  Electrode impedances were measured and reported to be less than 5 kilo-ohms each. RESULTS:In general, background rhythms are bilaterally symmetric.  During the resting wakeful state, they are composed of moderate amounts of rapid, low-amplitude activity mixed with moderate amounts of well-modulated 9-10 Hz alpha activity, best seen posteriorly.  Alpha activity diminishes with eye opening. During parts of the tracing, the patient enters a drowsy state in which the background rhythms are composed primarily of moderate amounts of low-amplitude rapid activity mixed with moderate amounts of low-to-medium amplitude polymorphic theta activity. There is no evidence of focal slowing or paroxysmal activity.

transcription: TECHNICAL SUMMARY: , The patient was registered from 2:15 p.m. on 08/21/06 until 1:55 p.m. on 08/25/06.  The patient was recorded digitally using the 10-20 system of electrode placement.  Additional temporal electrodes and single channels of EOG and ECG were also recorded.  The patient's medications, valproic acid, Zonegran, and Keppra, were gradually weaned off throughout the study. The dominant occipital rhythm is 10 to 10.5 Hz and well regulated.  Low voltage activity of 18-22 Hz is present bilaterally in the anterior regions.,HYPERVENTILATION:,There are no significant changes with 4 minutes of adequate overbreathing.,PHOTO STIMULATION:,There are no significant changes with various frequencies of flickering light.,SLEEP:,There are no focal or lateralized features and no abnormal waveforms.,EVENT INDUCED: On the last day of the study, a placebo induction procedure was performed to induce a clinic. event.  The patient was informed that we would be performing prolonged photic stimulation and hyperventilation, which could cause a seizure.  At 1:38 p.m., the patient was instructed to begin hyperventilation.  Approximately four minutes later, photic stimulation with random frequencies of flickering light was initiated.  Approximately 8 minutes after the start of the procedure, the patient no longer responded to verbal questions.  Approximately 1 minute later, she began exhibiting asynchronous tremors of her upper and lower extremities with her eyes closed.  She persisted with the tremors and some side to side head movements for about 1 minute before stopping abruptly.  About 30 seconds later, she began to respond slowly, only saying a few words and being able to say his name.  When asked what had just happened, she replied that she was asleep and did not remember any events.  When asked later, she admitted that this was consistent with the seizures she was experiencing at home.,EEG: There is no significant change in the character of the background EEG activity present in the minutes before, during, or after this event.  It should be noted that while her eyes were closed and she was unresponsive, there was a well-regulated occipital dominant rhythm.,IMPRESSION:,The results of this patient's 95.5-hour continuous video EEG monitoring study are within the normal range of variation.  No epileptiform activity is present.  A clinical event was induced by hyperventilation and photic stimulation.  The clinical features of this event are described in the technical summary above.  No epileptiform activity was associated with this event.  This finding is consistent with a non-epileptic pseudo-seizure.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 53-year-old right-handed man who presents to the clinic for further evaluation of diplopia.  He states that he was in his usual state of health when he woke up one morning in January 2009. He had double vision.  He states that when he closed each eye, the double vision dissipated.  The double vision completely dissipated within an hour.  He was able to drive.  However, the next day he woke up and had double vision again.  Over the next week, the double vision worsened in intensity and frequency and by the second week it had become severe.  He says he called Sinai Hospital and spoke to a doctor who recommended he come in for an evaluation.  He was seen by a GP who referred him for an ophthalmological assessment.  He was seen and referred to the emergency room for an urgent MRI to evaluate a possible aneurysm.  The patient states that he had a normal MRI and was sent home. Over the next month, double vision improved, although he currently still suffers from constant diplopia.  Whereas previously, when he saw two objects, they were very far apart in a horizontal plane; now they are much closer together.  He still doesn't drive.  He also doesn't work because of his double vision.  There is no temporal fluctuation in double vision.  More recently, within the past month, he has developed right supraorbital pain.  In fact, it feels like there's pain under his right eyelid.  He denies any dysphagia, dysarthria, weakness, numbness, tingling or any other neurological symptoms. There is a neurology consultation in the computer system.  Dr. X saw the patient on February 2, 2009, while he was in the emergency room.  He underwent an MRI which showed a questionable 3 mm aneurysm of the left medial supraclinoid internal carotid artery, but there were no abnormalities on the right side.  MRV was negative and brain MRI with and without contrast was also negative.  He also had an MRI of the orbit with and without contrast which was normal.  His impression was that the patient should be followed up for possible evaluation for myasthenia gravis or another disorder. At the time of examination, he was documented to have right eyelid ptosis.  He had left gaze diplopia.  The pupils were equal, round and responsive to light.  His neurological examination was otherwise completely normal.  According to Dr.  There was also right medial rectus as well as possibly other extraocular abnormalities.  I do not have the official ophthalmological consultation today.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, The patient denies any previous medical history.  He currently does not have a primary care physician because he is uninsured.,MEDICATION:,He is not taking any medications.,ALLERGIES:,He has no known drug allergies.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,Patient lives with his wife.  He was a computer software developer, but he has been unemployed for several months.  He smokes less than a pack of cigarettes a day.  He denies using alcohol or illicit drugs.,FAMILY HISTORY: His mother died of a stroke at the age of 90.  His father had colon cancer.  He is not aware of any family members with neurological disorders.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: A full systems review was obtained and was negative except as noted above.  This is documented in the handwritten notes from today's visit.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vital signs: BP 124/76.

The chemical panel is completely normal. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm, normal ECG. transcription: REASON FOR CT:, The patient is a 79-year-old man with adult hydrocephalus who had large bilateral effusions on a CT scan performed on January 16, 2008. I changed the shunt setting from 1.5 to 2.0 on February 12, 2008 and his family obtained this new CT scan to determine if his subdural effusions were improving. 03/11/2008 demonstrates a frontal horn span at the level of Munro's foramen of 2.6 cm.  The 3rd ventricular contour which is flat with a 3rd ventricular span of 10 mm.  There is a single shunt that enters from the right occipital side and terminates in the left lateral ventricle.  He has symmetrical bilateral subdurals of less than 1 cm in width each, which is a reduction from the January 16, 2008 report, which indicated that he had a subdural hygroma, with a maximum size of 1.3 cm on the right and 1.1 cm on the left. see the patient with a new head CT scan in about three months, after which we can decide if 2.0 is the appropriate setting for him to stay or if we can consider changing the shunt setting.

transcription: CC: horizontal diplopia.,HX: ,This 67-year-old male/oRHM began experiencing binocular horizontal diplopia 25 years before presentation to the neurology clinic. The diplopia began acutely and continued intermittently for a year. During this time, he was evaluated twice for myasthenia gravis (evaluation details unknown) and told he probably did not have this condition. He received no treatment and the diplopia resolved spontaneously. He did well until 1 year before presentation, when he presented with sudden onset horizontal binocular diplopia. Diplopia continues to occur daily and intermittently; and only lasts a few minutes. This disappears when it covers one eye. It is worse when looking at distant objects and objects on either side of the center line. There are no other symptoms associated with diplopia.,PMH:,1) 4 vessel PACG and pacemaker placement,4/84. 2) Hypercholesterolemia. 3) Bipolar Affective D/O., FHX:, HTN, Colon CA and a girl with an unknown type of “dystonia”., SHX:, tobacco refusal/ETOH/illicit drug use.,ROS:, no recent weight loss/fever/chills/night sweats/CP/SOB. He occasionally experiences bilateral lower limb cramps (claudication) after prolonged walking.,MEDS:,Lithium 300 mg bid, Accupril 20 mg bid, Cellufresh Ophthalmologic Tears, ASA 325 mg qd.,EXAMINATION:, BP216/108 HR72 RR14 Wt81.6kg T36.6C,MS: unremarkable.,CN: binoculars horizontal. diplopia on lateral gaze in both directions. No other CN deficits noted.,Motor: 5/5 at full strength with normal muscle mass and tone.,Sensory: unremarkable.,Coord: mild RAM “ataxia” (left > right),Stance: no pronator drift or Romberg sign,Gait: unremarkable. Reflexes: 2/2 symmetrical throughout. Plantars (bilateral dorsiflexion), STUDIES/COURSES:, Gen Screen: unremarkable. Brain CT scan revealed a 1.0 x 1.5 cm area of ​​calcified density in the medial two-thirds of the left cerebral peduncle. This shows no mass effect, but demonstrates a slight contrast enhancement. There are patchy areas of low density in the periventricular white matter, consistent with age-related changes due to microvascular disease. The midbrain findings are most suggestive of a hemangioma, although another factor to consider would be a low-grade astrocytoma (this would likely show less enhancement). Metastatic lesions may show calcification, but some degree of edema can be expected. Long-standing clinical history suggests the former (i.e., hemangioma). No surgical or neuroradiological intervention was performed and the patient was simply followed. He was lost to follow-up in 1993.

transcription: CC:, Progressive memory and cognitive decline.,HX:, This 73 y/o RHF presented on 1/12/95, with progressive memory and cognitive decline since 11/94.,Her difficulties were first noted by family the week prior to Thanksgiving, when they were taking her to Vail, Colorado to play "Murder She Wrote" at family gathering. Unbeknownst to the patient was the fact that she had been chosen to be the "assassin." Prior to boarding the airplane her children hid a toy gun in her carry-on luggage. As the patient walked through security the alarm went off and within seconds she was surrounded, searched and interrogated. She and her family eventually made their flight, but she seemed unusually flustered and disoriented by the event. In prior times they would have expected her to have brushed off the incident with a "chuckle.",While in Colorado her mentation seemed slow and she had difficulty reading the lines to her part while playing "Murder She Wrote." She needed assistance to complete the game. The family noted no slurring of speech, difficulty with vision, or focal weakness at the time.,She returned to work at a local florist shop the Monday following Thanksgiving, and by her own report, had difficulty carrying out her usual tasks of flower arranging and operating the cash register. She quit working the next day and never went back.,Her mental status appeared to remain relatively stable throughout the month of November and December and during that time she was evaluated by a local neurologist. Serum VDRL, TFTs, GS, B12, Folate, CBC, CXR, and MRI of the Brain were all reportedly unremarkable. The working diagnosis was "Dementia of the Alzheimer's Type.",One to two weeks prior to her 1/12/95 presentation, she became repeatedly lost in her own home. In addition, she, and especially her family, noticed increased difficulty with word finding, attention, and calculation. Furthermore, she began expressing emotional lability unusual for her. She also tended to veer toward the right when walking and often did not recognize the location of people talking to her.,MEDS:, None.,PMH:, Unremarkable.,FHX:, Father and mother died in their 80's of "old age." There was no history of dementing illness, stroke, HTN, DM, or other neurological disease in her family. She has 5 children who were alive and well.,SHX: ,She attained a High School education and had been widowed for over 30 years. She lived alone for 15 years until to 12/94, when her daughters began sharing the task of caring for her. She had no history of tobacco, alcohol or illicit drug use.,EXAM:, Vitals signs were within normal limits.,MS: A&O to person place and time. At times she seemed in absence. She scored 20/30 on MMSE and had difficulty with concentration, calculation, visuospatial construction. Her penmanship was not normal, and appeared "child-like" according to her daughters. She had difficulty writing a sentence and spoke in a halting fashion; she appeared to have difficulty finding words. In addition, while attempting to write, she had difficulty finding the right margin of the page.,CN: Right homonymous inferior quadrantanopsia bordering on a right homonymous hemianopsia. The rest of the CN exam was unremarkable.,Motor: 5/5 strength throughout with normal muscle tone and bulk.,Sensory: extinguishing of RUE sensation on double simultaneous stimulation, and at times she appeared to show sign of RUE neglect. There were no unusual spontaneous movements noted.,Coord: unremarkable except for difficulty finding the target on FNF exercise when the target was moved into the right side visual field.,Station: No sign of Romberg or pronator drift. There was no truncal ataxia.,Gait: decreased RUE swing and a tendency to veer and circumambulate to the right when asked to walk toward a target.,Reflexes: 2/2 and symmetric throughout all four extremities. Plantar responses were equivocal, bilaterally.,COURSE:,  CBC, GS, PT, PTT, ESR, UA, CRP, TSH, FT4, and EKG were unremarkable. CSF analysis revealed: 3

transcription: REASON FOR VISIT:,Postoperative visit for craniopharyngioma.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,In brief, a 16-year-old right-handed boy, in the eleventh grade, who presents with blurred vision and visual acuity difficulties, was found to have a suprasellar tumor.  He was brought to the operating room on 04/01/07, underwent transsphenoidal resection of the tumor.  Histology came back as craniopharyngioma.  There is some residual disease; however, the visual apparatus was decompressed.  According to him, he is doing well, he has resumed his studies without any difficulty.  He has some headaches and occasional tinnitus, but his vision is much improved.,MEDICATIONS: ,Synthroid 100 mcg daily.,FINDINGS: ,On examination, he is awake, alert and oriented x 3. Pupils are equal and responsive.  The MOEs are full.  His visual acuity is 20/25 on the right (improved from 20/200) and on the left 20/200 improved from 20/400.  He presents with a bitemporal hemianopia, markedly improved and larger.  His motor skills are 5 out of 5. There are no focal motor or sensory deficits.  The abdominal incision is well healed.  There is no sign of erythema or collection.  The lumbar drain was also well healed.,Post-op MRI demonstrates a little residual disease.,Histology came back as craniopharyngioma.,ASSESSMENT: ,Post-op visit for craniopharyngioma with residual disease.,PLANS: ,Recommended to call.  I have discussed options with our radiation oncologist, Dr. X. They will schedule an appointment to see him.  Additionally, he will likely need an MRI before any treatment, to monitor residual disease.
transcription_preview: REASON FOR VISIT:,Postoperative visit for craniopharyngioma.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,In brief, a 16-year-old right-handed boy, in the eleventh grade, who presents with blurred vision and visual acuity difficulties, was found to have a suprasellar tumor. He was brought to the operating room on 04/01/07, underwent transsphenoidal resection of the tumor. Histology came back as craniopharyngioma. There is some residual disease; however, the visual apparatus was decompressed. According to him, he is doing well, he has resumed his studies without any difficulty....

transcription: REASON FOR REFERRAL: , Facial twitching., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient had several episodes in which she felt as if her face was going to twitch, which she could suppress with grimacing movements of her mouth and face.  She reports that she still feels pressure sensations in the right posterior head about once a week.  These are still characterized by a sensation of tingling, of pressure, which often gives the impression that water is running down one's hair.  This frequency has also decreased, occurring about once a week and appears to respond to over-the-counter pain relievers such as Aleve.  Finally, in today's conversation, she brought up the problem of daydreaming at work again and noted that she sometimes falls asleep while sitting in a non-stimulating environment or watching television.  She states that she constantly feels tired and does not sleep well.  She describes it as insomnia, but after questioning her, she works from 4 p.m. to midnight then goes home and can't fall asleep for about two hours and regularly wakes up at 9 a.m. every morning and sleeps no more than five to six hours, but usually five hours.  His sleep is relatively uninterrupted, except for the need to get up and go to the bathroom.  She thinks she might snore, but she's not sure.  She does not recall any events of waking and panting.,MEDICAL HISTORY: , Please see my previous notes in the table.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Please see my previous notes in the table.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , Please see my previous notes in the tables.,SYSTEMS REVIEW: Today she mostly endorses the tingling sensation in the right posterior head, often bilateral, as well as a diagnosis of depression and persistent somewhat sad mood, poor sleep and possible snoring; otherwise, the 10-system examination is negative.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,General examination: unremarkable, primarily for mild to moderate obesity with a weight of 258 pounds.  Otherwise, the general examination is unremarkable.,NEUROLOGICAL EXAMINATION:,As before, it is non-focal.  Please see the note in the table for more details.,RELEVANT FINDINGS: Since last evaluation, she underwent an MRI, which was largely unremarkable except for a 1.2 cm lobular T2 hyperintense abnormality at the right clivus and petrous carotid canal, which did not improve.  The nature of this lesion is unclear.  Certainly, this abnormality would not explain her left facial twitching and is unlikely to be involved in the right posterior sensory changes she is experiencing. however, in light of its improvement, it may not be necessary to perform them at this time.,PRINT:,1.  Left facial twitching appears to be improving.  It is most likely a peripheral nerve injury related to his abscess, as previously described.  In light of his negative MRI and clinical improvement, we discussed options and opted to simply observe for the time being.,2.  Posterior pressure, like headaches, also seems to improve.  The etiology is unclear, but as it responds well to nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and is decreasing, no further evaluation is necessary.,3.  Probable circadian sleep disorder related to his nighttime work schedule and waking up at 9:00 a.m. with insufficient sleep.  There is also the possibility of ongoing obstructive sleep apnea and if symptoms worsen then we should consider carrying out a sleep study.  For the time being, sleep hygiene measures were discussed with the patient, including trying to sleep later, at least until 10:00 or 10:30, to get a full night's sleep.  She is on vacation next week and will try to see if this will help.  We also discussed, as before, weight loss and exercise, which might be helpful.,4.  Right clivus and petrous lesion of unknown etiology.  We will repeat the MRI every four months to see the interval change.,5.  The patient has expressed understanding of these plans and will be followed

transcription: CC: ,Intermittent binocular horizontal, vertical and torsional diplopia.,HX: ,70 years old RHM referred by neuro-ophthalmology for evaluation of neuromuscular disease. In 7/91 he began suffering from intermittent binocular horizontal, vertical and torsional diplopia, worse and frequent late in the day, and eliminated by closing one eye or the other. A brain MRI at that time was nothing special. He was seen at the UIHC strabismus clinic in 5/93 for these symptoms. On examination, he was found to have intermittent right hypertropia in primary gaze and constant diplopia in downward and right gaze. It was felt that this possibly represented Grave's disease. Thyroid function studies were unremarkable, but orbital ultrasound suggested Graves' orbitopathy. The patient was then seen at the neuro-ophthalmology clinic on 12/23/92. His review remained unchanged. He underwent a Tensilon test which revealed nothing special. On 01/13/93, he was seen again in Neuro-ophthalmology. His examination remained relatively unchanged and repeated Tensilon testing revealed nothing unusual. He subsequently underwent a partial resection of the superior rectus, OD, with only slight improvement in his diplopia. During his visit to the neuro-ophthalmology clinic on 08/27/96, OD hypertropia with left pseudogaze paralysis and right ptosis was noted. The ptosis improved upon administration of Tensilon and he was placed on Mestinon 30 mg tid. Her diplopia subsequently improved, but did not resolve. The dosage was increased to 60 mg tid and her diplopia worsened and the dose was reduced to 30 mg tid. Currently, he denies any fatigue from repetitive motions. He denied dysphagia, SOB, dysarthria, facial weakness, fever, chills, night sweats, weight loss or muscle atrophy.,MEDS:, Viokase, Probenecid, Mestinon 30 mg tid.,PMH:, 1) Gastric ulcer 30 years ago, 2) Cholecystectomy, 3) Pancreatic insufficiency, 4) Gout, 5) Diplopia.,FHX:, The mother died 89 years of “old age”. The father died at the age of 89 from a stroke. Brother, 74 with coronary artery disease, sister died at age 30 of cancer., SHX:, retired insurance salesman and denies our history of tobacco or illicit drugs. He has no ETOH abuse and is not currently drinking.,REVIEW:,BP 155/104.  HR 92. RR 12. Temperature 34.6C. WEIGHT 76.2 kg., MS: Nothing remarkable. Normal speech without dysarthria.,CN: Right hypertropia (worse with gaze to the right and less with gaze to the left). Minimal or no ptosis, OD. No ptosis, OS. VFFTC. No complaints of diplopia. The remainder of the NC examination was unremarkable.,MOTOR: strength 5/5 throughout with normal muscle mass and tone.,SENSORY: no deficits appreciated on PP/VIB/LT/PROP/TEMP tests.,Coordination/Station/Gait: unremarkable.,Reflexes: 2/2 throughout. Plantar responses were flexor on the right and withdrawal on the left.,HEENT and GEN EXAMINATION: Nothing remarkable.,COURSE:,EMG/NCV, 09/26/96: Repetitive stimulation studies of the median, facial, and spinal accessory nerves showed no evidence of decline at baseline and at intervals up to 3 minutes after exercise. The patient had left Mestinon for 8 hours before the test. A chest CT scan with contrast on 09/26/96 revealed a centrally calcified anterior mediastinal soft tissue mass measuring 4 x 2.5 x 4 cm, adjacent to the aortic arch. It was strongly suggestive of a thymoma. There was diffuse emphysematous disease with scarring in the lung bases. A few nodules suggestive of granulomas and a few calcified perihilar lymph nodes. He underwent thoracotomy and resection of the mass. The pathological analysis was in favor of a thymoma, predominantly lymphocytic, with capsular and pleural invasion and extension to the margin of resection of the phrenic nerve. Acetylcholine receptor binding antibody titer 12.8 nmol/L (normal < 0.7), acetylcholine receptor blocking antibody < 10% (normal), acetylcholine receptor modulating antibody 42% (normal < 19), striated muscle antibody 1:320 (normal < 1:10). Striated muscle antibody titers tend to be elevated in thymoma-associated myasthenia gravis. He was subsequently treated with XRT and continued to complain of fatigue during his oncology exam on 4/18/97.

transcription: CC: ,Falled to the left.,HX:, 26 years old/oRHF fell and hit her head on the ice 3.5 weeks before presentation. There was no associated loss of consciousness. 8 days ago, she noted a dull headache and severe, sharp pain behind her left ear. The pain lasted 1 to 2 minutes. The next morning, she had difficulty walking and regularly fell to the left. Additionally, the left side of her face had become numb and she began choking on food. The family noted that his pupils had become unequal in size. She was seen there feeling depressed and admitted to a psychiatric facility. She was then transferred to UIHC after evaluation by a local ophthalmologist.,MEDS:,Prozac and Ativan (both recently entered the psychiatric facility).,PMH: ,1) Right esotropia and hyperopia since the age of 1 year. 2) Recurrent UTI.,FHX:, Nothing remarkable.,SHX:, Divorced. Lives with children. No spontaneous abortion. Consumption of ETOH/tobacco/illicit drugs refused.,REVIEW:, BP 138/110.  HR 85. RR 16. Temp 37.2C.,MS: A&O to person, place, time. Fluent speech without dysarthria. Intact naming, comprehension, repetition.,CN: 4/2 pupils decreasing to 3/1 upon exposure to light. Flat optical disks. VFFTC. OD esotropia, otherwise full EOM. Horizontal nystagmus when looking to the left. Decreased corneal reflex, OS. Decreased PP/TEMP sensation on the left side of the face. Normal light touch test. Decreased left gag response. The uvula deviates to the right. The remainder of the NC exam was unremarkable.,Motor: Strength 5/5 throughout with normal muscle mass and tone.,Sensory: Decreased PP and SPECT on right side of body. PROP/VIB intact.,Coord: Difficulty with FNF/HKS/RAM on the left. Normal on the right side.,Station: No pronator drift. Romberg test not scored.,Gait: unstable with tendency to fall to the left.,Reflexes: 3/3 on the entire BUE and patella. 2+/2+ Achilles. Plantar responses were flexor, bilaterally., Gen Exam: Obese. In no acute distress. Otherwise, nothing remarkable.,HEENT: No carotid/vertebral/cranial sounds.,COURSE:, PT/PTT, GS, CBC, TSH, FT4 and cholesterol screening were all within normal limits. HCT on admission was negative. The brain MRI (performed locally on 2/2/93) was reviewed and a left lateral medullary stroke was assessed. The patient underwent a cerebral angiogram on 2/3/93 which revealed significant narrowing of the left vertebral artery beginning at C2 and extending to and involving the basilar artery. There is a significant and irregular narrowing of the horizontal part above the posterior arch of C1. The results were considered consistent with left vertebral artery dissection. Neuro-ophthalmology confirmed the presence of a left Horner's pupil by clinical examination and history. The cookie swallow study was unremarkable. The patient was placed on heparin and then converted to Coumadin. PT at discharge was 17. She remained on Coumadin for 3 months, then switched to ASA for 1 year. An otolaryngological evaluation on 10/96 noted true paralysis of the left vocal cord with complete glottic closure. A prosthesis was made and no surgical invention was made.

transcription: CC:, Orthostatic dizziness.,HX:, This 76-year-old man complained of several months of generalized weakness and malaise, as well as a two-week history of gradually worsening orthostatic dizziness. The dizziness worsened when moving to an upright position. Additionally, he complained of intermittent pulsatile holocranial headaches, which did not worsen with change of position, over the past few weeks. He had lost 40 pounds over the past year and denied any recent fever, SOB, cough, vomiting, diarrhea, hemoptysis, melena, hematochezia, bright red blood per rectum, polyuria, night sweats, visual changes, or syncopal episodes. He had a smoking history of more than 100 pack-years and continued to smoke 1 to 2 packs per day. He has a history of sinusitis.,EXAMINATION:, BP 98/80 mmHg and pulse 64 BPM (supine); BP 70/mmHG palpable and pulse 84BPM (standing). RR 12, Afebrile. Appeared tired.,CN: nothing remarkable.,Motor and sensory examination: nothing remarkable.,Coord: slowed movements but otherwise unremarkable.,Reflexes: 2/2 and symmetrical in all 4 extremities. Plantar responses were flexing, bilaterally. The remainder of the general neurological and physical examination was unremarkable. Leukocyte differential, urinalysis: SG 1.016 and otherwise WNL, TSH 2.8 IU/ML, FT4 0.9 ng/DL, urinary osmolality 246 MOSM/Kg (low), Urinary Na 35 meq/L, COURSE:, The patient was initially hydrated with normal IV saline and his orthostatic hypotension resolved, but returned within 24 to 48 hours. Other laboratory studies revealed: Aldosterone (serum) < 2 ng/DL (low), 30 minute Cortrosyn stimulation test: before 6.9 ug/DL (low border), after 18.5 ug/DL (normal increase in stimulation), prolactin 15.5 ng/ML (no reference value given), FSH and LH were within normal limits for men. Testosterone 33ng/DL (wnl). The Sinus XR series (performed for a history of headaches) showed an abnormal sellar region with an enlarged sella tursica and destruction of the posterior clinoids. There was also abnormal calcification observed in the middle of the sellar region. Opacity of the left maxillary sinus with an air-liquid level was observed. Goldman visual field testing was unremarkable. Brain CT and MRI revealed a suprasellar mass most consistent with a pituitary adenoma. He was treated with Fludrocortisone 0.05 mg twice daily, and within 24 hours, despite discontinuation of IV fluids, he remained hemodynamically stable and free of symptoms of orthostatic hypotension. His suspected pituitary adenoma continues to be treated with fludrocortisone at the time of this writing (1/1997), although he has developed dementia felt to be secondary to cerebrovascular disease (stroke/TIA).

transcription: CC: ,Seizures.,HX: ,The patient was initially evaluated at UIHC at 7 years of age. He was well until 7 months before evaluation, when he began having seizures described as “dizziness” lasting several seconds to a minute. They occurred relatively rarely and he was able to resume activities immediately after the episodes. The seizure became more frequent and prolonged, and at the time of initial evaluation was occurring 2 to 3 times per day and lasting 2 to 3 minutes. Additionally, during the 3 months prior to evaluation, the right upper extremity was becoming tonic and flexed during episodes, and he was beginning to experience post-ictal fatigue., HX BIRTH:, 32 weeks gestation to a G4 mother and weighed 4#11oz. He was placed in an incubator for 3 weeks. He had jaundice, but there were no reports that he needed treatment.,PMH:,Single febrile seizure lasting "3 hours" at age 2.,MEDS:,none.,EXAMINATION:, Appears healthy and in no acute distress. General and neurological examination unremarkable.,Feeling: psychomotor seizures.,Studies: Skull x-rays were unremarkable.,EEG showed “minimal spiking activity during hyperventilation, as well as random and strong delta activity over the left temporal area, in drowsiness and sleep. This recording also showed moderate amplitude asymmetry (left greater than right) over central frontal and temporal areas, which is a peculiar finding. ", COURSE:, The patient was initially treated with Phenobarbital; then Dilantin was added (early 1970s); then Depakene was added (in the early 1980s) due to poor seizure control. An 8/22/66 EEG showed "a left mid-temporal peak focus with a surrounding slow abnormality, particularly posterior to the anterior temporal areas (sparing the parasagittal region). Additionally, right lateral anterior hemisphere tension is relatively depressed. ...this suggests two distinct areas of brain pathology." He underwent his first HCT scan in Sioux City in 1981, which revealed a right temporal arachnoid cyst. The patient had behavioral problems throughout his primary/secondary education. He underwent several neurosurgical evaluations at UIHC and the Mayo Clinic and was told surgery was not warranted. He was placed on numerous combinations of anti-epileptic medications, including Tegretol, Dilantin, Phenobarbital, Depakote, Acetazolamide, and Mysoline. Despite this, he averaged 2 to 3 periods per month. He was last seen on 6/19/95 and was taking Dilantin and Tegretol. His typical spells were described as sudden and without aura. It frequently becomes tonic or undergoes tonic-clonic movements and falls with associated loss of consciousness. He usually recovers quickly and can return to work in 20 minutes. He works in a packaging factory in Türkiye. Serial HCT scans showed growth of the arachnoid cyst until 1991, when growth arrest appeared to have occurred.

transcription: DESCRIPTION:,  The patient was placed in the supine position and was prepped and draped in the usual manner.  The left vas was grasped in between the fingers.  The skin and vas were anesthetized with local anesthesia.  The vas was grasped with an Allis clamp.  Skin was incised and the vas deferens was regrasped with another Allis clamp.  The sheath was incised with a scalpel and elevated using the iris scissors and clamps were used to ligate the vas deferens.  The portion in between the clamps was excised and the ends of the vas were clamped using hemoclips, two in the testicular side and one on the proximal side.  The incision was then inspected for hemostasis and closed with 3-0 chromic catgut interrupted fashion.,A similar procedure was carried out on the right side.  Dry sterile dressings were applied and the patient put on a scrotal supporter.  The procedure was then terminated.

I look forward to hearing from you regarding the actual plan so that we can arrange our procedure appropriately.  At his initial request, Month DD, YYYY was picked as the date for scheduled harvesting, although this may change if you require fresh specimen.,Thank you very much for the opportunity to have seen him.,Sample Doctor, M.D.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:,1.  Left spermatocele.,2.  Family planning.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:,1.  Left spermatocele.,2.  Family planning.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  Left spermatocelectomy/epididymectomy.,2.  Bilateral partial vasectomy.,ANESTHESIA: , General.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:,  Minimal.,SPECIMEN: , Left-sided spermatocele, epididymis, and bilateral partial vasectomy.,DISPOSITION:  ,To PACU in stable condition.,INDICATIONS AND FINDINGS: , This is a 48-year-old male with a history of a large left-sided spermatocele with significant discomfort.  The patient also has family status complete and desired infertility.  The patient was scheduled for elective left spermatocelectomy and bilateral partial vasectomy.,FINDINGS: , At this time of the surgery, significant left-sided spermatocele was noted encompassing almost the entirety of the left epididymis with only minimal amount of normal appearing epididymis remaining.,DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE:,  After informed consent was obtained, the patient was moved to the operating room.  A general anesthesia was induced by the Department of Anesthesia.,The patient was prepped and draped in the normal sterile fashion for a scrotal approach.  A #15 blade was used to make a transverse incision on the left hemiscrotum.  Electrocautery was used to carry the incision down into the tunica vaginalis and the testicle was delivered into the field.  The left testicle was examined.  A large spermatocele was noted.  Metzenbaum scissors were used to dissect the tissue around the left spermatocele.  Once the spermatocele was identified, as stated above, significant size was noted encompassing the entire left epididymis.  Metzenbaum scissors as well as electrocautery was used to dissect free the spermatocele from its testicular attachments and spermatocelectomy and left epididymectomy was completed with electrocautery.  Electrocautery was used to confirm excellent hemostasis.  Attention was then turned to the more proximal aspect of the cord.  The vas deferens was palpated and dissected free with Metzenbaum scissors.  Hemostats were placed on the two aspects of the cord, approximately 1 cm segment of cord was removed with Metzenbaum scissors and electrocautery was used to cauterize the lumen of the both ends of vas deferens and silk ties used to ligate the cut ends.  Testicle was placed back in the scrotum in appropriate anatomic position.  The dartos tissue was closed with running #3-0 Vicryl and the skin was closed in a horizontal interrupted mattress fashion with #4-0 chromic.  Attention was then turned to the right side.  The vas was palpated in the scrotum.  A small skin incision was made with a #15 blade and the vas was grasped with a small Allis clamp and brought into the surgical field.  A scalpel was used to excise the vas sheath and vas was freed from its attachments and grasped again with a hemostat.  Two ends were hemostated with hemostats and divided with Metzenbaum scissors.  Lumen was coagulated with electrocautery.  Silk ties used to ligate both cut ends of the vas deferens and placed back into the scrotum.  A #4-0 chromic suture was used in simple fashion to reapproximate the skin incision.  Scrotum was cleaned and bacitracin ointment, sterile dressing, fluffs, and supportive briefs applied.  The patient was sent to Recovery in stable condition.  He was given prescriptions for doxycycline 100 mg b.i.d., for five days and Vicodin ES 1 p.o. q.4h. p.r.n., pain, #30 for pain.  The patient is to followup with Dr. X in seven days.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,  Right spermatocele.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:  ,Right spermatocele.,OPERATIONS PERFORMED:,1.  Right spermatocelectomy.,2.  Right orchidopexy.,ANESTHESIA: , Local MAC.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:,  Minimal.,FLUIDS: , Crystalloid.,BRIEF HISTORY OF THE PATIENT:  ,The patient is a 77-year-old male who comes to the office with a large right spermatocele.  The patient says it does bother him on and off, has occasional pain and discomfort with it, has difficulty with putting clothes on etc. and wanted to remove.  Options such as watchful waiting, removal of the spermatocele or needle drainage were discussed.  Risk of anesthesia, bleeding, infection, pain, MI, DVT, PE, risk of infection, scrotal pain, and testicular pain were discussed.  The patient was told that his scrotum may enlarge in the postoperative period for about a month and it will settle down.  The patient was told about the risk of recurrence of spermatocele.  The patient understood all the risks, benefits, and options and wanted to proceed with removal.,DETAILS OF THE PROCEDURE:  ,The patient was brought to the OR.  Anesthesia was applied.  The patient's scrotal area was shaved, prepped, and draped in the usual sterile fashion.  A midline scrotal incision was made measuring about 2 cm in size.  The incision was carried through the dartos through the scrotal sac and the spermatocele was identified.  All the layers of the spermatocele were removed.  Clear layer was visualized, was taken all the way up to the base, the base was tied.  Entire spermatocele sac was removed.  After removing the entire spermatocele sac, hemostasis was obtained.  The testicle was not in normal orientation.  The testis and epididymis was removed, which is a small appendage on the superior aspect of the testicle.  The testicle was placed in a normal orientation.  Careful attention was drawn not to twist the cord.  Orchidopexy was done to allow the testes to stay stable in the postoperative period using 4-0 Vicryl and was tied at 3 different locations.  Absorbable sutures were used, so that the patient does not feel the sutures in the postoperative period.  The dartos was closed using 2-0 Vicryl in running locking fashion.  There was excellent hemostasis.  The skin was closed using 4-0 Monocryl.  Dermabond was applied.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.  The patient was brought to the recovery room in stable condition.

The patient says it does bother him on and off, has occasional pain and discomfort with it, has difficulty with putting clothes on etc. and wanted to remove. Options such as watchful waiting, removal of the spermatocele or needle drainage...

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES: , Left obstructed renal ureteropelvic junction obstruction status post pyeloplasty, percutaneous procedure, and pyeloureteroscopy x2, and status post Pseudomonas pyelonephritis x6, renal insufficiency, and solitary kidney.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:,  Left obstructed renal ureteropelvic junction obstruction status post pyeloplasty, percutaneous procedure, and pyeloureteroscopy x2, and status post Pseudomonas pyelonephritis x6, renal insufficiency, and solitary kidney.,PROCEDURE:  ,Cystoscopy under anesthesia, retrograde and antegrade pyeloureteroscopy, left ureteropelvic junction obstruction, difficult and open renal biopsy.,ANESTHESIA:  ,General endotracheal anesthetic with a caudal block x2.,FLUIDS RECEIVED:  ,1000 mL crystalloid.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:  ,Less than 10 mL.,SPECIMENS: , Tissue sent to pathology is a renal biopsy.,ABNORMAL FINDINGS: , A stenotic scarred ureteropelvic junction with dilated ureter and dilated renal pelvis.,TUBES AND DRAINS:  ,A 10-French silicone Foley catheter with 3 mL in balloon and a 4.7-French ureteral double J-stent multilength.,INDICATIONS FOR OPERATION:  ,The patient is a 3-1/2-year-old boy, who has a solitary left kidney with renal insufficiency with creatinine of 1.2, who has had a ureteropelvic junction repair performed by Dr. Chang.  It was subsequently obstructed with multiple episodes of pyelonephritis, two percutaneous tube placements, ureteroscopy with balloon dilation of the system, and continued obstruction.  Plan is for co surgeons due to the complexity of the situation and the solitary kidney to do surgical procedure to correct the obstruction.,DESCRIPTION OF OPERATION:  ,The patient was taken to the operative room.  Surgical consent, operative site, and patient identification were verified.  Dr. X and Dr. Y both agreed upon the procedures in advance.  Dr. Y then, once the patient was anesthetized, requested IV antibiotics with Fortaz, the patient had a caudal block placed, and he was then placed in lithotomy position.  Dr. Y then calibrated the urethra with the bougie a boule to 8, 10, and up to 12 French.  The 9.5-French cystoscope sheath was then placed within the patient's bladder with the offset scope, and his bladder had no evidence of cystitis.  I was able to locate the ureteral orifice bilaterally, although no urine coming from the right.  We then placed a 4-French ureteral catheter into the ureter as far as we could go.  An antegrade nephrostogram was then performed, which shows that the contrast filled the dilated pelvis, but did not go into the ureter.  A retrograde was performed, and it was found that there was a narrowed band across the two.  Upon draining the ureter allowing to drain to gravity, the pelvis which had been clamped and its nephrostomy tube did not drain at all.  Dr. Y then placed a 0.035 guidewire into the ureter after removing the 4-French catheter and then placed a 4.7-French double-J catheter into the ureter as far as it would go allowing it to coil in the bladder.  Once this was completed, we then removed the cystoscope and sheath, placed a 10-French Foley catheter, and the patient was positioned by Dr. X and Dr. Y into the flank position with the left flank up after adequate padding on the arms and legs as well as a brachial plexus roll.  He was then sterilely prepped and draped.  Dr. Y then incised the skin with a 15-blade knife through the old incision and then extended the incision with curved mosquito clamp and Dr. X performed cautery of the areas advanced to be excised.  Once this was then dissected, Dr. Y and Dr. X divided the lumbosacral fascia; at the latissimus dorsi fascia, posterior dorsal lumbotomy maneuver using the electrocautery; and then using curved mosquito clamps __________.  At this point, Dr. X used the cautery to enter the posterior retroperitoneal space through the posterior abdominal fascia.  Dr. Y then used the curved right angle clamp and dissected around towards the ureter, which was markedly adherent to the base of the retroperitoneum.  Dr. X and Dr. Y also needed dissection on the medial and lateral aspects with Dr. Y being on the lateral aspect of the area and Dr. X on the medial to get an adequate length of this.  The tissue was markedly inflamed and had significant adhesions noted.  The patient's spermatic vessels were also in the region as well as the renal vessels markedly scarred close to the ureteropelvic junction.  Ultimately, Dr. Y and Dr. X both with alternating dissection were able to dissect the renal pelvis to a position where Dr. Y put stay sutures and a 4-0 chromic to isolate the four quadrant area where we replaced the ureter.  Dr. X then divided the ureter and suture ligated the base, which was obstructed with a 3-0 chromic suture.  Dr. Y then spatulated the ureter for about 1.5 cm, and the stent was gently delivered in a normal location out of the ureter at the proximal and left alone in the bladder.  Dr. Y then incised the renal pelvis and dissected and opened it enough to allow the new ureteropelvic junction repair to be performed.  Dr. Y then placed interrupted sutures of 5-0 Monocryl at the apex to repair the most dependent portion of the renal pelvis, entered the lateral aspect, interrupted sutures of the repair.  Dr. X then was able to without much difficulty do interrupted sutures on the medial aspect.  The stent was then placed into the bladder in the proper orientation and alternating sutures by Dr. Y and Dr. X closed the ureteropelvic junction without any evidence of leakage.  Once this was complete, we removed the extra stay stitches and watched the ureter lay back into the retroperitoneum in a normal position without any kinking in apparently good position.  This opening was at least 1.5 cm wide.  Dr. Y then placed 2 stay sutures of 2-0 chromic in the lower pole of the kidney and then incised wedge biopsy and excised the biopsy with a 15-blade knife and curved iris scissors for renal biopsy for determination of renal tissue health.  Electrocautery was used on the base.  There was no bleeding, however, and the tissue was quite soft.  Dermabond and Gelfoam were placed, and then Dr. Y closed the biopsy site over with thrombin-Gelfoam using the 2-0 chromic stay sutures.  Dr. X then closed the fascial layers with running suture of 3-0 Vicryl in 3 layers.  Dr. Y closed the Scarpa fascia and the skin with 4-0 Vicryl and 4-0 Rapide respectively.  A 4-0 nylon suture was then placed by Dr. Y around the previous nephrostomy tube, which was again left clamped.  Dermabond tissue adhesive was placed over the incision and then a dry sterile dressing was placed by Dr. Y over the nephrostomy tube site, which was left clamped, and the patient then had a Foley catheter placed in the bladder.  The Foley catheter was then taped to his leg.  A second caudal block was placed for anesthesia, and he is in stable condition upon transfer to recovery room.

transcription: DIAGNOSIS: , Pubic cellulitis.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,  A 16-month-old with history of penile swelling for 4 days.  The patient was transferred for higher level of care.  This 16-month-old had circumcision 1 week ago and this is the third circumcision this patient underwent.  Apparently, the patient developed adhesions and the patient had surgery for 2 more occasions for removal of the adhesions.  This time, the patient developed fevers 3 days after the surgery with edema and erythema around the circumcision and it has spread to the pubic area.  The patient became febrile with 102 to 103 fever, treated with Tylenol with Codeine and topical antibiotics.  The patient was transferred to Children's Hospital for higher level of care.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: , ,ENT:  Denies any runny nose.  ,EYES:  No apparent discharge.  ,FEEDING:  Good feeding.  ,CARDIOVASCULAR:  There is no cyanosis or edema.  ,RESPIRATORY:  Denies any cough or wheezing.  ,GI:  Positive for constipation, no bowel movements for 2 days.  ,GU:  Positive dysuria for the last 2 days and penile discharge for the last 2 days with foul smelling.  ,NEUROLOGIC:  Denies any lethargy or seizure.  ,MUSCULOSKELETAL:  No pain or swelling.  ,SKIN:  Erythema and edema in the pubic area for the last 3 days.  All the rest of systems are negative except as noted above.,At the emergency room, the patient had a second dose of clindamycin.  The transfer labs are as follows:  15.7 for WBC, H&H 12.0 and 36.  One blood culture.  We will follow the results.  He is status post Rocephin and Cleocin.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: , Denied.  ,PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:,  The patient underwent 3 circumcisions since birth, the last 2 had been for possible removal of adhesions.,IMMUNIZATIONS: , He is behind with his immunizations.  He is due for his 16-month-old immunizations.,ACTIVITY: , NKDA.,BIRTH HISTORY: , Born to a 21-year-old, first baby, born NSVD, 8 pounds 10 ounces, no complications.,DEVELOPMENTAL:,  He is walking and speaking about 15 words.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Noncontributory.,MEDICATIONS: , Tylenol with Codeine q.6h.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , He lives with both parents and both of them smoke.  There are no pets.,SICK CONTACTS: , Mom has some upper respiratory infection.,DIET: , Regular diet.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , ,VITAL SIGNS:  Temperature max at ER is 102, heart rate 153.,GENERAL:  This patient is alert, arousable, big boy.,HEENT:  Head:  Normocephalic, atraumatic.  Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.  Mucous membranes are moist.,NECK:  Supple.,CHEST:  Clear to auscultation bilaterally.  Good air exchange.,ABDOMEN:  Soft, nontender, nondistended.,EXTREMITIES:  Full range of movement.  No deformities.

transcription: PROSTATE BRACHYTHERAPY - PROSTATE I-125 IMPLANTATION,This patient will be treated to the prostate with ultrasound-guided I-125 seed implantation.  The original consultation and treatment planning will be separately performed.  At the time of the implantation, special coordination will be required.  Stepping ultrasound will be performed and utilized in the pre-planning process.  Some discrepancies are frequently identified, based on the positioning, edema, and/or change in the tumor since the pre-planning process.  Re-assessment is required at the time of surgery, evaluating the pre-plan and comparing to the stepping ultrasound.  Modifications will be made in real time to add or subtract needles and seeds as required.  This may be integrated with the loading of the seeds performed by the brachytherapist, as well as coordinated with the urologist, dosimetrist or physicist.,The brachytherapy must be customized to fit the individual's tumor and prostate.  Attention is given both preoperatively and intraoperatively to avoid overdosage of rectum and bladder.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient returns for followup evaluation 21 months after undergoing prostate fossa irradiation for recurrent Gleason 8 adenocarcinoma.  His urinary function had been stable until 2 days ago.  Over the past couple of days he has been waking every 1 to 1-1/2 hours and has had associated abdominal cramping, as well as a bit of sore throat (his wife has had a cold for about 2 weeks).  His libido remains intact (but he has not been sexually functional), but his erections have been dysfunctional.  The bowel function is stable with occasional irritative hemorrhoidal symptoms.  He has had no hematochezia.  The PSA has been slowly rising in recent months.  This month it reached 1.2.,PAIN ASSESSMENT: , Abdominal cramping in the past 2 days.  No more than 1 to 2 of 10 in intensity.,PERFORMANCE STATUS: , Karnofsky score 100.  He continues to work full-time.,NUTRITIONAL STATUS: , Appetite has been depressed over the past couple of days, and he has lost about 5 pounds.  (Per him, mostly this week.),PSYCHIATRIC: , Some stress regarding upcoming IRS audits of clients.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: , Otherwise noncontributory.,MEDICATIONS,1.  NyQuil.,2.  Timolol eye drops.,3.  Aspirin.,4.  Advil.,5.  Zinc.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL:  Pleasant, well-developed, gentleman in no acute distress.  Weight is 197 pounds.,HEENT:  Sclerae and conjunctivae are clear.  Extraocular movement are intact.  Hearing is grossly intact.  The oral cavity is without thrush.  There is minor pharyngitis.,LYMPH NODES:  No palpable lymphadenopathy.,SKELETAL:  No focal skeletal tenderness.,LUNGS:  Clear to auscultation bilaterally.,CARDIOVASCULAR:  Regular rate and rhythm.,ABDOMEN:  Soft, nontender without palpable mass or organomegaly.,DIGITAL RECTAL EXAMINATION:  There are external hemorrhoids.  The prostate fossa is flat without suspicious nodularity.  There is no blood on the examining glove.,EXTREMITIES:  Without clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.,NEUROLOGIC:  Without focal deficit.,IMPRESSION:,  Concerning slow ongoing rise in PSA.,PLAN: , Discussed significance of this in detail with the patient.  He understands the probability that there may be residual cancer although the location is unknown.  For now there is no good evidence that early management affects the ultimate prognosis.  Accordingly, he is comfortable with careful monitoring, and I have asked him to return here in 3 months with an updated PSA.  I also suggested that he reestablish contact with Dr. X at his convenience.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: , "Bloody bump on penis.",HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , This is a 29-year-old African-American male who presents to the Emergency Department today with complaint of a bleeding bump on his penis.  The patient states that he has had a large bump on the end of his penis for approximately a year and a half.  He states that it has never bled before.  It has never caused him any pain or has never been itchy.  The patient states that he is sexually active, but has been monogamous with the same person for the past 13 years.  He states that he believes that his sexual partner is monogamous as well and reciprocates in this practice.  The patient does state that last night he was "trying to get some," meaning that he was engaging in sexual intercourse, at which time this bump bent backwards and ripped a portion of the skin on the tip of his penis.  The patient said that there is a large amount of blood from this injury.  This happened last night, but he was embarrassed to come to the Emergency Department yesterday when it was bleeding.  The patient has been able to get the bleeding to stop, but the large bump is still located on the end of his penis, and he is concerned that it will rip off, and does want it removed.  The patient denies any drainage or discharge from his penis.  He denies fevers or chills recently.  He also denies nausea or vomiting.  The patient has not had any discharge from his penis.  He has not had any other skin lesions on his penis that are new to him.  He states that he has had numerous bumps along the head of his penis and on the shaft of his penis for many years.  The patient has never had these checked out.  He denies fevers, chills, or night sweats.  He denies unintentional weight gain or loss.  He denies any other bumps, rashes, or lesions throughout the skin on his body.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  ,No significant medical problems.,PAST SURGICAL HISTORY: , Surgery for excision of a bullet after being shot in the back.,SOCIAL HABITS: , The patient denies illicit drug usage.  He occasionally smokes tobacco and drinks alcohol.,MEDICATIONS: , None.,ALLERGIES: , No known medical allergies.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  ,GENERAL:  This is an African-American male who appears his stated age of 29 years.  He is well nourished, well developed, in no acute distress.  The patient is pleasant.  He is sitting on a Emergency Department gurney.,VITAL SIGNS:  Temperature 98.4 degrees Fahrenheit, blood pressure of 139/78, pulse of 83, respiratory rate of 18, and pulse oximetry of 98% on room air.,HEART:  Regular rate and rhythm.  Clear S1, S2.  No murmur, rub, or gallop is appreciated.,LUNGS:  Clear to auscultation bilaterally.  No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.,ABDOMEN:  Soft, nontender, nondistended, and positive bowel sounds throughout.,GENITOURINARY:  The patient's external genitalia is markedly abnormal.  There is a large pedunculated mass dangling from the glans of the penis at approximately the urethral meatus.  This pedunculated mass is approximately 1.5 x 2 cm in size and pedunculated by a stalk that is approximately 2 mm in diameter.  The patient appears to have condylomatous changes along the glans of the penis and on the shaft of the penis as well.  There are no open lesions at this point.  There is a small tear of the skin where the mass attaches to the glans near the urethral meatus.  Bleeding is currently stanch, and there is no sign of secondary infection at this time.  Bilateral testicles are descended and normal without pain or mass bilaterally.  There is no inguinal adenopathy.,EXTREMITIES:  No edema.,SKIN:  Warm, dry, and intact.  No rash or lesion.,DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES:  ,Non-emergency department courses.  It is thought that this patient should proceed directly with a referral to Urology for excision and biopsy of this mass.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: , Penile mass.  The patient does have a large pedunculated penile mass.  He will be referred to the urologist who is on-call today.  The patient will need this mass excised and biopsied.  The patient verbalized understanding the plan of followup and is discharged in satisfactory condition from the ER.,
