transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS,1.  Intrauterine pregnancy at more than 39 weeks gestation.,2.  Gestational hypertension.,3.  Thick meconium.,4.  Failed attempt at vacuum delivery.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS,1.  Intrauterine pregnancy at more than 39 weeks gestation.,2.  Gestational hypertension.,3.  Thick meconium.,4.  Failed attempt at vacuum delivery.,OPERATION PERFORMED: , Spontaneous vaginal delivery.,ANESTHESIA: , Epidural was placed x2.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: 500 ml.,COMPLICATIONS: , Thick meconium.  Severe variables, Apgars were 2 and 7. Respiratory therapy and ICN nurse at delivery.  The baby went to the newborn nursery., RESULTS:, Male infant, cephalic presentation, ROA.  Apgars 2 and 7. Weight 8 pounds 1 ounce.  Intact placenta.  Three-vessel cord.  Third degree midline tear.,DESCRIPTION OF OPERATION:,The patient was admitted this morning for induction of labor secondary to high blood pressure, particularly over the past three weeks.  She was already 3cm dilated.  She had an artificial rupture of membranes.  Pitocin was started and she went to full dilation.  While pushing, there was a sudden appearance of thick meconium, and she presented with severe variables and several late decelerations.  When she completed +2, attempted vacuum delivery, three pop-offs were performed.  The vacuum cleaner was then no longer used after the three pop-offs.  The patient pushed a little longer and delivered, ROA, a cephalic male infant, on a third degree midline tear.  Secondary to the thick meconium, DeLee suctioned the nose and mouth before delivery of the anterior shoulder and again after delivery.  Baby received a floppy disk.  The cord was clamped x2 and cut, and the baby was released to a waiting ICN nurse and respiratory therapist.  Delivery of an intact placenta and three-vessel cord.  The third-degree midline tear was repaired with Vicryl without any complications.  At first, baby behaved well and went to the newborn nursery, where they observed him for a little longer.  Once again, mother and baby are doing well.  The mother will be going postpartum and the baby is already in the newborn nursery.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Right pelvic pain.,2.  Right ovarian mass.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Right pelvic pain.,2.  Right ovarian mass.,3.  8 cm x 10 cm right ovarian cyst with ovarian torsion.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, Right laparoscopic salpingooovariectomy.,ANESTHESIA:, General with endotracheal tube.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, Less than 50 cc.,TUBES:, None.,DRAINAGES:, None.,PATHOLOGY: , The right tube and ovary sent to pathology for examination., RESULTS: , On examination under anesthesia, a normal appearing vulva and vagina and a normally palpated cervix, a normal sized uterus, and a large right adnexal mass.  Laparoscopic findings demonstrated an 8 cm x 10 cm smooth right ovarian cyst that was twisted twice.  Otherwise, the uterus, left tube and ovary, intestine, liver margins, appendix, and gallbladder were all within normal limits.  There was no blood noted in the pelvis.,INDICATIONS FOR THIS PROCEDURE:,The patient is a 26 year old G1 P1 who presented to ABCD General Emergency with a complaint of pain in the right lower quadrant since last night, which has increased in intensity.  The pain persisted despite multiple analgesics administered in the emergency room.  The patient reports positive nausea and vomiting.  There was no bleeding or vaginal discharge.  There was no fever or chills.  His cultures done in the emergency room were on hold.  The patient did have an ultrasound which revealed an 8 cm right ovarian cyst, doubtful hemorrhagic.  The uterus and left ovary were within normal limits.  Positive flow was noted in bilateral ovaries on ultrasound.  Therefore, it was deemed appropriate to take the patient for a diagnostic laparoscopy with possible oophorectomy.,PROCEDURE: After obtaining informed consent and answering all questions to the patient's satisfaction in simple terms, she was taken to the operating room where general anesthesia was obtained without any difficulty.  She was placed in the dorsal lithotomy position using Allis slings, prepped and draped in the usual sterile manner.  Her bladder was emptied with a red Robinson catheter and she was examined under anesthesia and the results were noted as above.  She was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A weighted speculum was placed into the patient's vagina with excellent visualization of the cervix.  The cervix was grasped at 12 o'clock with a single-toothed tenaculum and pulled into the surgical field.  The uterus was then probed approximately 3.5 inches and then a uterine elevator was placed.  The vulsellum tenaculum was removed.  The weighted speculum was removed.  Attention was then shifted to the abdomen where a 1 cm infra-umbilical incision was made in the infra-umbilical fold.  The Veress needle was then placed into the abdomen while the abdomen was taut with a towel forceps.  The CO2 was then activated with unblocked flow and excellent pressures.  This was continued until a normal symmetrical pneumoperitoneum was obtained.  Then, an 11 mm stepped trocar and liner were placed into the infraumbilical port without any difficulty and the placement was confirmed by laparoscope.  Laparoscopic results are as noted above.  A suprapubic incision was made with the knife, then a trocar and a 12 mm pitch liner were placed in the suprapubic region under direct visualization.  Next, forceps were used to untwist the ovary.  Next, a #12 mm port was placed in the right flank region under direct visualization using a LigaSure vessel sealing system.  The right tube and ovary were amputated and found to be hemostatic.  The EndoCatch bag was then placed in the suprapubic port and the ovary was placed in the bag.  The ovary was too large to fit completely into the sac.  Therefore, a laparoscopic needle with a 60 cc syringe was used to aspirate the contents of the ovary while it was still inside the sac. There was approximately 200 cc of fluid aspirated.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Cervical adenocarcinoma, stage I.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Cervical adenocarcinoma, stage I.,OPERATION PERFORMED:, Exploratory laparotomy, radical hysterectomy, bilateral ovarian transposition, pelvic and obturator lymphadenectomy.,ANAESTHESIA: , General, endotracheal tube.,SAMPLES: , Uterus with attached parameters and upper vagina, right and left pelvic lymph nodes and obturators.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS: The patient recently underwent a cone biopsy during which invasive adenocarcinoma of the cervix was noted.  She was informed of treatment options, including radical hysterectomy or radiotherapy, and the former was recommended. , RESULTS: , During examination under anesthesia, the cervix healed well following a recent cone biopsy and no nodularity was noted in the supporting ligaments.  During exploratory laparotomy, there was no evidence of disease extension to the broad ligament or bladder flap.  There was no evidence of intraperitoneal spread or lymphadenopathy.  ,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE: ,The patient was brought to the operating room with an IV in place.  Anesthesia was administered, after which she was examined under anesthesia.  The vagina was then prepared and a Foley catheter was placed.  She was prepared and draped.  A Pfannenstiel incision was made three centimeters above the pubic symphysis.  The peritoneum was penetrated and the abdomen was explored with the results indicated.  The Bookwalter retractor was placed and the bowel was filled.  Clamps were placed on the broad ligament for traction.  The retroperitoneum was opened by a lateral incision parallel to the infundibulopelvic ligaments.  The round ligaments were isolated, divided and ligated.  The peritoneum covering the vesicouterine fold was incised and the bladder was mobilized by sharp dissection.  The pararectal and paravesical spaces were opened and the broad ligament was palpated without signs of suspicion or extension of the disease.  The utero-ovarian ligaments were then isolated, divided and double ligated.  The tubes and ovaries were mobilized.  The ureters were dissected from the medial leaflet of the peritoneum.  When the crossing of the uterine artery was reached, the artery was isolated at its origin, divided and ligated.  The pedicle of the uterine artery was dissected anteriorly above the ureter.  The ureter was tunneled through the broad ligament using right angle tunneling forceps, after which each pedicle was divided and ligated.  This continued to the point of insertion of the ureter into the bladder trigone.  The peritoneum passing through the cul-de-sac was divided and the rectovaginal space was opened.  Clamps were placed on the uterosacral ligaments at their point of origin.  The tissues were divided and ligated by suture.  Clamps were placed on the paravaginal tissues, which were then divided and suture ligated.  The vagina was then clamped and divided at the junction between the middle third and the upper third.  The vaginal vault was closed with interrupted figure-of-eight stitches.  Excellent hemostasis was noted. The retractors were repositioned in the retroperitoneum for lymphadenectomy.  Dissection boundaries included the bifurcation of the common iliac artery superiorly, the crossing of the deep circumflex iliac vein over the external iliac artery inferiorly, the psoas muscle laterally, and the anterior division of the hypogastric artery medially.  The obturator nerves were carefully isolated and preserved bilaterally and served as the posterior edge of dissection.  Ligaclips were applied if necessary.  After lymph node samples were collected, the pelvis was irrigated.  The ovaries were transposed above the pelvic brim using stitches.  The packs and retractors were removed and the peritoneum was closed with a running stitch.  The subcutaneous tissues were irrigated and the fascia was closed with a mass suture using a delayed absorption suture.  The subcutaneous adipose tissue was irrigated, a

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , Management of blood pressure., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a 38-year-old woman admitted following childbirth.  The patient had a cesarean section.  Following this, the patient was treated for her blood pressure.  She was sent home and returned apparently with uncontrolled blood pressure.  She takes several medications and is unable to control her blood pressure.  Cardiacly, the patient denies any symptoms of chest pain or shortness of breath.  She complains of fatigue and exhaustion.  The child had a birth defect and was taken to hospital, where he underwent surgery.  The patient is in the intensive care unit.,CORONARY RISK FACTORS: history of hypertension, history of gestational diabetes mellitus, non-smoker and normal cholesterol.  No history of established coronary heart disease or family history non-contributory to coronary heart disease.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Not significant.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,No major surgery except cesarean section.,DRUGS:,Currently, Cardizem and metoprolol have been discontinued.  I started taking 50 mg of hydralazine 3 times a day and 200 mg of labetalol 2 times a day, hydrochlorothiazide and insulin supplementation.,ALLERGIES: , None.,PERSONAL HISTORY: , Non-smoker.  Do not consume alcohol.  No history of recreational drug use.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Hypertension, gestational diabetes mellitus, pre-eclampsia, this is her third child with a miscarriage.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,CONSTITUTIONAL: No history of fever, chills or rigors.,HEENT: No history of cataracts, blurred vision or glaucoma.,CARDIOVASCULAR: No congestive heart.  No arrhythmia.,RESPIRATORY: No history of pneumonia or valley fever.,GASTROINTESTINAL: No epigastric discomfort, hematemesis or melena.,UROLOGICAL: No frequency or urgency.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: No arthritis or muscle weakness.,SKIN: Not significant.,NEUROLOGICAL: No TIA.  No CVA.  No seizure disorder.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: pulse 86, blood pressure 175/86, afebrile and respiratory rate 16 per minute.,HEENT: atraumatic and normocephalic.,NECK: neck veins are flat.,LUNGS: clear.,HEART: S1 and S2 regular.,ABDOMEN: soft and not tender.,EXTREMITIES: not edema.  Palpable pulses., LABORATORY DATA: Electrocardiogram shows sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST-T changes.  Labs were noted.  BUN and creatinine within normal limits.,PRINT:,1.  Preeclampsia, condition after cesarean delivery with uncontrolled blood pressure.,2.  No history of heart disease except borderline gestational diabetes mellitus.,RECOMMENDATIONS:,1.  We will obtain an echocardiogram to assess left ventricular function.,2.  The patient will start taking labetalol and hydralazine to see how he does.,3.  Depending on the response to the medications, we will make further adjustments.  Discussed the care plan with the patient, he understands it perfectly and agrees to it.  All questions answered in detail.

Oncologist.,A dedicated ultrasound of the underside of the left breast should be performed at 6:00 a.m., position N+5.5 cm for further evaluation of the mass. At the same time, an ultrasound scan of the remaining masses should also be performed. Please note, however, that the remaining masses have primarily benign features based on MRI criteria. However, further evaluation by ultrasound should be performed.

Bilateral tubal ligation (BTL).,TUBES: , None.,DRAINAGES: , Gravity foley.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , 600 cc.,FLUIDS: 200 cc of crystalloids.,URINARY FLOW: 300 cc of clear urine at the end of the procedure.,FINDINGS:,Operative findings demonstrated wire mesh through the anterior abdominal wall and the anterior part. fascia.  Intestinal adhesions were noted across the anterior abdominal wall.  The uterus was within normal limits.  The tubes and ovaries bilaterally were within normal limits.  The baby came out of the right anterior sacral position without any difficulty.  Apgars 8 and 9. Weight was 7.5 lbs.,INDICATIONS FOR THIS PROCEDURE:,Patient is a 23 year old G3 P 2-0-0-2 with 30 and 4/7th weeks reported for scheduled secondary cesarean to repeat x2.  She had her first C-section due to congenital hip problems.  During her second C-section, the baby had a breach, so she had to have a third C-section.  The patient also requests sterilization.  Therefore, she requested a tubal ligation.,PROCEDURE: After obtaining informed consent and answering all questions to the patient's satisfaction in simple terms, she was taken to the operating room where spinal anesthesia with Astramorph was achieved without any difficulty.  She was placed in a supine position with a tilt to the left, prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A Pfannenstiel skin incision was made to remove the old scar with a first knife, then moved down to the underlying fascia layer with a second knife.  The fascia was excised in the midline extended laterally with Mayo scissors.  The upper part of the fascial incision was then tensioned with Ochsner forceps and the underlying rectus muscle was dissected with Metzenbaum scissors.  Dense adhesions were noted at this stage as well as metal mesh.  The anterior aspect of the fascial incision was then tensioned with Ochsner forceps and the underlying rectus muscle was sharply and bluntly dissected.  The superior rectus muscle was opened with a hemostat.  The peritoneum was identified and unceremoniously captured digitally.  The peritoneal incision was then extended upward to the level of the mesh.  Then, inferiorly using the knife, the adhesions were removed and the bladder was identified and the peritoneum incision extended inferiorly to the level of the bladder.  The bladder blade was inserted and the vesicouterine peritoneum was identified and placed in place with Allis forceps and the bladder flap was created digitally with Metzenbaum scissors.  The bladder blade was then reinserted to protect the bladder and the uterine incision was made with a first knife then extended laterally with the Bandage scissors.  The amniotic fluid turned out to be clear.  At this point, after examination of the intrauterine contents, it was found that the baby had a breach.  The right foot was identified and the baby came out of the double breach position without any difficulty.  The cord was clamped and the baby was then handed over to the waiting pediatricians.  Placental cord gases were obtained and the placenta was then manually extracted from the uterus.  The uterus was exteriorized and cleared of all clots and debris.  Next, the uterine incision was then closed with Vicryl No. 0 in a double closure stitch mode, the first layer in a locking stitch mode and the second layer a nesting layer. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Intrauterine pregnancy at 38 weeks.,2.  Poor presentation.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Intrauterine pregnancy at 38 weeks.,2.  Misrepresentation.,3.  Delivery of a viable male neonate.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Primary low transverse cervical cesarean section.,ANESTHESIA: , Spinal with Astramorph.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , 300 cc.,URINARY FLOW: , 80 cc clear urine.,FLUIDS: , 2000 cc crystalloids.,COMPLICATIONS: , None., RESULTS: A viable male neonate in left occiput transverse position with Apgars of 9 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes respectively, weighing 3030 g.  No nuchal cord.  No meconium.  Normal uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries.,INDICATIONS: This patient is a 21-year-old Caucasian female, gravida 3, para 1-0-1-1, who presented to labor and delivery during labor.  His cervix formed cervical chains.  It progressed to 75% and -2, however, a raised lobular area was palpated on the fetal head.  However, on examination, it is impossible to delineate the facial structures, but fetal presentation is certain.  Fetal heart sounds began and continued to have variable decelerations with overall reassuring contractions.  The contraction pattern was inadequate.  It was discussed with the patient's family that in light of the physical examination and abnormal fetal presentation, a cesarean section would be recommended.  All questions were answered.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After obtaining informed consent in simple terms, the patient was taken back to the operating room and placed in the dorsal lithotomy position with a tilt to the left.  Before this, spinal anesthesia was administered.  The patient was then prepared and draped.  A Pfannenstiel skin incision was made with the first scalpel and carried down to the underlying layer of fascia with the second scalpel.  The fascia was then incised at the midline and extended laterally using Mayo scissors.  The superior aspect of the rectus fascia was then grasped with Ochsners, tented and the underlying layer of the rectus muscle was bluntly dissected as well as with Mayo scissors.  The upper and lower portion of the rectus fascia were identified, tented, and the underlying layer of the rectus muscle was dissected bluntly as well as with Mayo scissors.  The rectus muscle was then separated in the midline.  The peritoneum was then identified, covered with hemostatic forceps, and sharply penetrated with Metzenbaum scissors.  The peritoneum is then gently stretched.  The vesicouterine peritoneum was then identified, tented with an Allis and the bladder flap was created bluntly as well as using Metzenbaum scissors.  The uterus was entered with the second scalpel and a large transverse incision.  This was then suddenly extended upwards and laterally.  The infant then delivered atraumatically.  The nose and mouth were suctioned.  The cord was then tightened and cut.  The infant was handed over to the pediatrician who was waiting for him.  The placenta was then extracted manually.  The uterus was exteriorized and cleared of all clots and debris.  The uterine incision was then repaired using #0 chrome in a routine manner by marking a U-shaped stitch. A second layer of the same suture was used in an interlocking fashion to achieve excellent hemostasis.  The uterus was then returned to anatomical position.  The abdomen and gutters were cleared of any clots.  Again, the incision proved to be hemostatic.  The rectus muscle was then reapproximated with Vicryl #2-0 in a single interrupted stitch.  The right fascia was then repaired with Vicryl #0 in stroke mode, locking the first stitch and the penultimate stitch lateral to medial.  This was palpated and the patient was found to be flawless and intact.  The skin was then closed with staples.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.  The number of sponges, knees and needles was correct x2.  She will be followed in hospitalization by Dr.

Group B streptococcal colonization.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Pregnancy at 40 weeks.,2.  Inability to progress.,3.  Premature and prolonged rupture of membranes.,4. Delivery of a viable male neonate., PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Primary low transverse cesarean section via Pfannenstiel incision., ANESTHESIA: , Spinal., ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , 1000 cc., FLUID REPLACEMENT: , 2700 cc of crystalloid., URINE: 500 cc of light yellow urine in the Foley. catheter., INTRAOPERATIVE FINDINGS:, Uterus, tubes and ovaries appear normal.  A viable male neonate with Apgars of 9 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes respectively.  The infant's weight was 4140 g with clear amniotic fluid.  The umbilical cord was wrapped tightly around the leg x1.  The infant was in a vertex, in the right occiput anterior position.,PROCEDURE INDICATIONS:,The patient is a 19-year-old G1 P0 at 41 and 1/7 weeks of intrauterine pregnancy.  She presented at midnight on 8/22/03 complaining of spontaneous rupture of membranes, which was confirmed during labor and delivery.  The patient had a positive beta group colonization culture and was started on penicillin.  The patient was also placed on Pitocin protocol at this time.  The patient was monitored throughout the morning, showing irregular contractions every 5-6 minutes, then eventually no contractions on the monitor.  The IUPC was implemented without difficulty and the contractions seemed regular, however, they were insufficient compared to the daily units.  The patient was taken off Pitocin.  She walked and took a short shower.  The patient was then placed back on Pitocin with the IUPC in place and we were unable to achieve adequate contractions.  Maximum cervical dilation was 5 cm, 80% effaced, negative station 2 and cephalic position.  At the time of the cesarean section, the patient had been ruptured for over 24 hours and it was determined that she would not progress in her cervical dilation as macrosomia was suspected on ultrasound.  Options were discussed with the patient and her family and it was determined that we would accept her for a cesarean today.  Consent has been signed.  All questions were answered in the presence of Dr. X. PROCEDURE: The patient was taken to the operating room where spinal anesthesia was placed.  She was placed in a supine position with an upward left tilt.  She was prepared and draped in a normal sterile manner and her spinal anesthesia was deemed adequate.  A Pfannenstiel incision was made with a first scalpel and carried through the underlying layer of fascia with a second scalpel.  The fascia was incised at the midline and extended laterally using curved Mayo scissors.  The upper part of the fascial incision was grasped with Ochsner and Kocher forceps and elevated from the rectus muscles.  Attention was then shifted to the lower portion of the incision, where Kocher forceps were used to elevate the fascia of the underlying rectus muscle.  The rectus muscle was abruptly separated in the midline.  The underlying peritoneum was tensioned with Allis forceps and incised using Metzenbaum scissors.  The peritoneum is then brutally stretched.  The bladder blade was placed.  The vesicouterine peritoneum was identified, tented with that of Allis and sharply penetrated with Metzenbaum scissors.  The incision was extended laterally and the bladder flap was created digitally.  The bladder blade was then reinserted into the lower uterine segment.  A low transverse uterine incision was made with a second scalpel.  The uterine incision was extended laterally in a brutal manner.  The bladder blade was removed and the infant's head was removed using a vacuum cleaner.  The infant's nose and mouth were bulbously suctioned and the body was delivered atraumatically.  It should be noted that there was an umbilical cord tightly around the leg x1. The cord was tightened and cut.  The infant was entrusted to the pediatric waiting ward

The patient presents to labor and delivery in active preterm labor and approximately 4 to 6 cm dilated.  The decision for cesarean section was made.,PROCEDURE: The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position with a slight left lateral tilt, then she was prepared and draped in the usual manner for a low transverse incision.  The patient then received general anesthesia and, once this was over, a first knife was used to make a low transverse incision extending to the level of the fascia.  The fascia was cut centrally and extended transversely using curved Mayo scissors.  The edges of the fascia were grasped with Kocher and both blunt and sharp dissection was then carried out both caudally and cephalad.  The rectus abdominis muscle was divided centrally and extended vertically.  The peritoneum was penetrated at a high point and also extended vertically.  The bladder blade was placed.  The bladder flap was created using Metzenbaum scissors and dissected caudally.  The second knife was used to make a low transverse incision taking care to avoid presentation of the fetus.  The first fetus was the top.  The liquid was clear.  The head was delivered followed by the rest of the body.  The cord was double tightened and cut.  The newborn was handed over to the waiting pediatrician and nursery staff.  The second fluid has ruptured.  It was also the clear liquid.  The presentation part was brought back to the top.  The head was delivered followed by the rest of the body and the cord was double tightened and cut, and the newborn was handed over to the waiting pediatrician in addition to the nursery staff.  pH and cord blood were obtained from both cords with careful identification of A and B. Once this was completed, the placenta was delivered and handed over for further inspection by Pathology.  At that time, it was noted that the uterus was stuck to the abdominal wall by a uterine adhesion approximately 3 cm x 3 cm thick and needed to be freed by clean dissection.  Then there were multiple omental adhesions on the surface of the uterus itself.  This had to be released as well as on the abdominal wall so that the uterus could then be externalized.  The mucosa was cleaned of any remaining blood and placental fragments and the edges of the uterus were grasped in four quadrants with Kocher and a continuous 0 chrome locking stitch was used to approximate the uterine incision again, with the second layer used to nest the first.  The bladder flap was reapproximated with Vicryl and 3-0 Gelfoam underneath.  The right fallopian tube was grasped with a Babcock, it was double tied with 0 chromium and the joint portion was then heavily incised and cauterized. transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, Patient is a 26 year old gravida 2, para 1-0-0-1, at 28 1/7 weeks who presents to the emergency department with left lower quadrant pain, reports no bowel movements for two weeks as well as nausea and vomiting for the past 24 hours. She denies any fever, chills, or burning when urinating.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Positive for lower back pain only. transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Chronic pelvic pain, probably secondary to endometriosis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Mild pelvic endometriosis.,PROCEDURE:,1.  Attempt at laparoscopy.,2.  Open laparoscopy.,3.  Fulguration of the endometrial implant.,ANESTHESIA: , general endotracheal.,BLOOD LOSS: , minimal.,COMPLICATIONS: , none.,INDICATIONS: ,The patient is a 21-year-old single female with chronic recurrent pelvic pain who is unresponsive to birth control pills containing estrogen and progesterone, either cyclically or daily, as well as progestin medications only, who had a negative result. Recent gastrointestinal workup including colonoscopy, and desired definitive operative evaluation and diagnosis prior to initiation of 6 months of Depo-Lupron treatment.,PROCEDURE: After obtaining an adequate general anesthesia plan, the patient was placed in the dorsal lithotomy position.  She was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner for pelviolabdominal surgery.  Bimanual examination revealed a normal-sized uterus in midposition with a benign adnexal area. In the high lithotomy position, a weighted speculum was placed in the posterior vaginal wall.  The anterior lip of the cervix was grasped with a single-toothed tenaculum.  A Hulka tenaculum was placed transcervically.  The other instruments have been removed.  A Foley catheter was placed transurethrally to drain the bladder intraoperatively. In the low lithotomy position and steep Trendelenburg position, attention was directed to the infraumbilical region.  Here, a stab incision was made through which the 120 mm Veress needle was placed and approximately 3 L of carbon dioxide was used to create pneumoperitoneum.  The needle was removed, the incision slightly widened, and trocar #5 and cannula were placed.  The trocar was removed and the endoscope placed confirming preperitoneal insufflation. The space was emptied of the insufflated gas and 2 further attempts were made, which failed due to the patient's adiposity.  Attention was shifted to the vaginal region where, in the high lithotomy position, attempts at posterior vaginal apical insertion were made.  Hulka's tenaculum was removed, the posterior lip of the cervix grasped with a single-tooth tenaculum and the long Allis forceps used to grasp the posterior fornix to which traction was applied.  The first short Veress needles, then 15 cm, were attempted to be placed, but after several passes, no satisfactory pneumoperitoneum could be established also by this route.  It was chosen not to perform an intentional transcervical uterine perforation, but to return to the umbilical area.  The 15 cm Veress needle was inserted several times, but again one pneumo was preperitoneal. Finally, an open laparoscopic approach was undertaken.  The skin incision was widened with a knife blade.  A blunt dissection was used to continue the dissection down to the fascia.  This was grasped with Kocher forceps, entered suddenly and opened transversely.  Four 0 Vicryl sutures were placed as retaining sutures and labeled with hemostats and needles were cut.  Dissection continued between the rectus muscle and finally the anterior peritoneum was reached, grasped, elevated and penetrated. At this point the Hasson cannula was placed and tightly tied with the above holding sutures while creating the pneumoperitoneum, a #10 endoscope was placed confirming the intraperitoneal positioning. Under direct visualization, a 5 mm suprapubic cannula and manipulation probe were placed.  Clockwise inspection of the pelvis revealed a benign vesicouterine pouch, normal uterus and fundus, normal right tube and ovary.  In the impasse there were 3 groups of 3-5 charred type endometrial implants and those more distally in the greatest depth had created puckers and tents.  The left tube and ovary were normal.  There was no grip.  There were no signs of acute pelvic inflammatory disease. The Endoshears and then the cautery on a hook were placed and the implants fused.  Picture

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Postpartum hemorrhage.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Postpartum hemorrhage.,PROCEDURE:, Examination under anesthesia.  Removal of intrauterine clots.,ANESTHESIA:, Conscious sedation.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, Approximately 200 ml during the procedure, but at least 500 ml before and probably more than 1500 ml before that.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,INDICATIONS AND CONCERNS:, This is a 19 year old G1, P1 female, in postpartum status vaginal, which was assessed by the nurse during labor and delivery approximately four hours after her delivery.  I was called in for persistent bleeding and large clots.  I examined the patient and found that she had at least 500 ml of clots in her uterus.  She was unable to tolerate further examination due to concerns about the amount of bleeding she had already had and her inability to adequately assess it.  I advised him to recommend anesthesia, dilation and curettage.  The risks and benefits of this procedure were discussed with Misty, all her questions were adequately answered and informed consent was obtained.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was taken to the operating room where satisfactory conscious sedation was performed.  She was placed in the dorsal lithotomy position, prepared and draped in the usual manner.  Bimanual examination revealed a moderate amount of clot in the uterus.  I was able to remove most of the clots with my hands and an attempt at a short curettage was carried out, but due to the contraction of the uterus this operation could not be carried out adequately.  I was able to thoroughly examine the uterine cavity with my hand and no remaining clots, tissue or placental membrane was found.  At this point, the procedure has ended.  The bleeding at this time was minimal.  Preoperative H&H were 8.3 and 24.2.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was transported to the recovery room in good conditions.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DX:,1.  Menorrhagia,2.  Desires permanent sterilization.,POSTOPERATIVE DX:,1.  Menorrhagia,2.  Desires permanent sterilization.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE:, Hysteroscopy, Essure, tubal occlusion and ThermaChoice endometrial ablation.,ANESTHESIA: general with paracervical block.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: minimal.,FLUIDS:, At hysteroscopy, deficit of 100 ml lactated Ringer IV, 850 ml Ringer lactate. Ringer.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,PATHOLOGY:, None.,ORDER:,Stable until the recovery room.,FINDINGS:, A nulliparous cervix without lesions.  Uterine cavity ringing up to 10 cm, normal appearing tubal ostia bilaterally, fluffy endometrium, normal appearing cavity without obvious polyps or fibroids.,PROCEDURE:,The patient was taken to the operating room, where general anesthesia was found to be adequate.  She was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A speculum was placed in the vagina.  The anterior lip of the cervix was grasped with a single tooth tenaculum and a paracervical block was performed using 20 ml of 0.50% lidocaine with 1:200,000 epinephrine. The cervical vaginal junction at 4 o'clock was injected and 5 ml was instilled.  The block was performed at 8 o'clock also with 5 ml at 10 o'clock and at 2 o'clock.  Lidocaine was injected into the cervix.  The cervix was minimally dilated with the Hanks No. 17 dilator. The 5 mm 30 degree hysteroscope was then inserted under direct visualization using lactated Ringer's as a means of distension.  The uterine cavity was examined and the above normal findings were noted.  The Essure Tubal Occluder was then inserted through the surgical port and the tip of the Essure device slid easily into the right ostia.  The coil was advanced and easily placed and the device removed.  There were three coils in the uterine cavity after removal of the insertion device.  The device has been removed and recharged.  The advice was to advance under direct visualization and the tip was inserted into the left ostia.  This went easily and the device was inserted.  It was removed easily and three coils were inserted back into the uterine cavity.  The hysteroscope was then removed and ThermaChoice ablation was performed.  The uterus was then probed to 9.5 to 10 cm.  The ThermaChoice balloon was primed and the pressure was increased to minus 150. The device was then moistened and inserted into the uterine cavity and the balloon was slowly filled with 40 ml of D5W.  The pressure was increased to 170 and the cycle was initiated.  A complete eight-minute cycle was completed.  At no time was there a significant loss of catheter balloon pressure.  Once the cycle was complete, the balloon was deflated and removed.  The tenaculum was removed.  No bleeding was noted.  The patient was then awakened, transferred and taken to the recovery room in satisfactory condition.

She also had symptoms of back pain, dysmenorrhea and dysuria.  The symptoms had gotten worse over time.  It was also noted that the patient had been experiencing increasing pelvic pain for the past 8 months and had an enlarged uterus on examination.,PROCEDURE:,The patient underwent a total vaginal hysterectomy.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,The patient was admitted on 04/09/2007 to undergo a total vaginal hysterectomy.  The procedure took place as planned without complications.  The uterus was sent for pathological analysis.  The patient was followed in hospital 2 days after the operation.  She recovered fairly well and vital signs remained stable.,Laboratory studies, H&H were followed and appeared stable on 05/09/2007 with a hemoglobin level of 11.2 and hematocrit of 31.8.,Patient was ready for discharge on Monday morning 06/09/2007.,LABORATORY RESULTS: Please see chart for studies completed upon admission.,DISPOSITION:Patient was discharged home in stable condition.  She was asked to follow up in the office after the operation.

There is a twin gestation in the transverse vertex with an anterior placenta and a normal amount of amniotic fluid surrounding both twins.  The fetal biometrics of twin A are as follows.  The biparietal diameter is 4.9 cm compatible with 20 weeks and 5 days, the head circumference is 17.6 cm compatible with 20 weeks and 1 day, the abdominal circumference is 15.0 cm compatible with 20 weeks and 2 days, and the femur length is 3.1 cm compatible with 19 weeks and 5 days, and the humeral length is 3.0 cm compatible with 20 weeks and 2 days. 0 days.  The average gestational age by ultrasound is 20 weeks 1 day and the estimated fetal weight is 353 g.  The following structures are considered normal on fetal anatomical examination, shape of the fetal head, choroid plexuses, cerebellum, thickness of the nuchal fold, spinal column and fetal face, four-chamber view of the fetal heart, outflow tract of the fetal heart, stomach, kidneys and cord insertion site, bladder, extremities, genitals, cord, which appeared to have three vessels and the placenta.,Limited in views of baby A with a nasolabial region.,The following is the fetal biometry of twin B. Biparietal diameter is 4.7 cm consistent with 20 weeks and 2 days, head circumference is 17.5 cm compatible with 20 weeks and 0 days, abdominal circumference is 15.5 cm compatible with 20 weeks and 5 days, femur length is 3.3 cm compatible with 20 weeks and 3 days. days, and the humeral length is 3.1 cm corresponding to 20 weeks and 2 days, the average gestational age by ultrasound is 22 weeks and 2 days and the estimated fetal weight is 384 g.  The following structures were considered normal during fetal anatomical examination.  The shape of the fetal head, the choroid plexuses, the cerebellum, the thickness of the nuchal fold, the fetal spine and fetal face, the four-chamber view of the fetal heart, the outflow tract of the fetal heart, the stomach, kidneys and cord insertion site, the bladder, the extremities, the genitals, the cord, which appeared to have three vessels, and the placenta.  Limited to today's ultrasound, views of the nasolabial region. In summary, this is a twin gestation, which could well be monochorionic at 20 weeks and 1 day.  There is like one sex and one placenta.  Whether or not this is a monochorionic or dichorionic gestation cannot be determined with certainty from today's ultrasound. I sat down with the patient and her husband and discussed alternative outcomes and complications.  We focused our discussion today on the association between twin pregnancy and preterm birth.  We discussed that the average singleton intrauterine pregnancy delivers at 40 weeks of gestation, while the average twin delivery occurs at 35 weeks of gestation.  We mentioned the fact that 15% of twins give birth before 32 weeks of gestation.  It is twins who worry us most about the long-term prospects of prematurity.  We discussed several etiologies of preterm delivery including preterm labor, cervical incompetence, premature rupture of fetal membranes as well as early delivery due to preeclampsia and failure to thrive.  We discussed the use of serial transvaginal ultrasound to evaluate early cervical changes and the use of serial transabdominal ultrasound to evaluate normal interval growth.  We discussed the need for frequent office visits to screen for preeclampsia.  We also discussed treatment options such as cervical cerclage, bed rest, tocolytic medications.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS,1.  Pelvic mass.,2.  Suspected right ovarian cyst.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS,1. Pelvic mass.,2.  Suspected right ovarian cyst.,PROCEDURES,1.  Exploratory laparotomy.,2.  Extensive lysis of adhesions.,3.  Right salpingo-oophorectomy.,ANESTHESIA:, General.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , 200 ml,SAMPLES: ,Right tomb and ovary.,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,FINDINGS: ,Extensive adhesive disease of the omentum and intestinal wall of the entire pelvis, which required more than 45 minutes of operation to establish visualization and clean the intestine and other important structures of the ovary cyst, tube and ovary in order to eliminate them.  The large and small intestines completely enveloped a large cystic mass in the right ovary.  Normal anatomy was difficult to see due to adhesions.  The cyst ruptured accidentally intraoperatively with approximately 150 ml to 200 ml of cloudy fluid.  The cyst wall, tube and ovary were removed from the intestine.  The posterior peritoneum was also removed to completely remove the ovary and tube from the cyst wall.  Postoperative hemostasis was excellent.,PROCEDURE: ,The patient was transported to the operating room, where general anesthesia was performed without difficulty.  She was then placed in the supine position, prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A vertical midline incision was made from the umbilicus and extended to the pubic symphysis along the patient's anterior incision line.  The incision was made carefully until reaching the peritoneal cavity.  Care was taken at the entrance to the peritoneum to avoid injury to underlying structures.  At this point, extensive adhesive disease was noted, again requiring over 45 minutes of dissection in order to visualize the anatomy planned for surgery.  The omentum was carefully removed from the patient's right side to create a window.  This was extended along the lower portion of the incision, clearing the omentum of its adhesions to the anterior peritoneum and what appears to be the vesicouterine peritoneum.  It has been noted that a large mass of intestine adheres to itself, causing a rather tortuous journey.  Adhesiolysis was performed to free the intestine in order to remove it from the pelvis.  Excellent hemostasis was noted.  The intestine was then packed above the pelvis, allowing visualization of a tangled mass of large and small intestine surrounding a large ovarian cyst.  Careful adhesive lysis and dissection separated the colon from the posterior wall of the cyst.  The small intestine and part of the colon adhered anteriorly to the cyst and upon dissection of these to remove them from their attachment, the cyst ruptured.  A large quantity of cloudy liquid was observed and was evacuated.  The cyst wall was then carefully tensioned and removed from the patient's small and large intestines.  Once the bowel was freed, the remnants of the round ligament were identified, elevated, and the peritoneum was incised, opening the retroperitoneal space. The retroperitoneal space was opened following the line of the ovarian vessels, which were identified and elevated and a window was created inferior to the ovarian vessels, but superior to the course of the ureter.  This pedicle was double clamped, sectioned, and tied with a #2-0 Vicryl loose tie.  Vicryl #0 suture ligation was used to achieve hemostasis.  Excellent hemostasis was noted at this pedicle.  The posterior peritoneum and part of the remaining broad ligament were carefully dissected and peeled to remove the proboscis and ovary, which were still densely adhered to the peritoneum.  Precautions were taken with regard to the remains of uterine vessels.  However, a laceration of the uterine vessels occurred, which was clamped with right angle forceps and carefully sutured and ligated with excellent hemostasis noted. transcription: DELIVERY NOTE:, The patient is a very pleasant 22 year old primigravida receiving prenatal care with Dr.  The patient was admitted for labor and delivery on Tuesday, December 22, 2008 at 5:30 a.m. at 40 weeks and 1 day gestation for elective induction of labor since she lives a significant distance from the hospital.  Her cervix on admission was not ripe so she was given a dose of Cytotec 25 mcg intravaginally and by the afternoon she was having frequent contractions and the fetal heart tracing was reassuring.  Later, Pitocin was launched.  The next day, around 9 a.m., I checked her cervix and performed an artificial rupture of the membranes, which revealed meconium-stained amniotic fluid. An intrauterine pressure catheter was therefore placed, and the MDL infusion was started.  The patient had a labor epidural, which worked well.  Of note, the patient's recent vaginal culture for Group B Strep was negative for Group B Strep. The patient subsequently had a normal spontaneous vaginal delivery of a full-term live male neonate with Apgar scores of 7 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes respectively and a newborn weight of 7 pounds, 1.5 ounces at birth.  The intensive care unit staff were present due to the presence of meconium-colored amniotic fluid.  The DeLee suction was performed at the perineum.  A second-degree midline episiotomy was repaired in layers in the usual manner using 3-0 Vicryl.  The placenta was simply delivered and examined and found to be complete and a bimanual vaginal examination was performed and revealed that the uterus was firm.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: Approximately 300 ml.

transcription: DELIVERY NOTE:, The patient is a 29-year-old woman gravida 6, para 2-1-2-3, with an estimated due date of 01/05/2009.  The patient presented to the labor and delivery department with complaints of spontaneous rupture of membranes at 8 p.m. on 12/26/2008.  She tested positive for nitrazine and fern.  At that time, she wasn't actually contracting.  She was positive for group B streptococcus, but she was 5 cm dilated.  The patient was placed on prophylaxis against group B streptococci with ampicillin.  She received a total of three doses throughout her labor.  Her pregnancy was complicated by lack of prenatal care.  She frequently missed visits.  At 37 weeks, she claims to have had a suspicious bump on her left lips.  There appeared to be no fluid or blisters on the lesion.  Therefore, it was not cultivated by the provider; However, the patient was sent to check serum anti-HSV antibody levels, and she tested positive for both HSV1 and HSV2.  I performed a bright light examination and found no lesions on the vulva or in the vault consistent with the sterile speculum examination and consulted with Dr.  The patient requested epidural anesthesia, which she received with very good relief.  She had labor augmented by IV Pitocin and was fully dilated according to my routine exam just after 6 hours and was prepared for delivery and the patient pushed very effectively for about one and a half contractions.  She gave birth to a viable baby girl on 12/27/2008 at 6:26 a.m., giving birth to an intact perineum.  The baby was delivered in the occiput anterior position.  The baby was delivered into the mother's abdomen where she was warm, dry and stimulated.  The umbilical cord was double clamped and then cut.  The baby's Apgars were 8 and 9 years old. The placenta came out spontaneously intact.  There was a three-vessel cord with normal insertion.  The fundus was massaged for firming and Pitocin was administered via IV protocol per unit.  The perineum was inspected and found to be fully intact.  The estimated blood loss was approximately 400 ml.  The patient's blood type is A+.  She is immune to rubella and, as previously mentioned, positive for GBS and has received three doses of ampicillin.

transcription: DELIVERY NOTE: This is an 18 year old, G2, P0 at 35-4/7 weeks by an EDC declared on 01/21/09.  The patient is a patient of Dr. X. Her pregnancy is complicated by premature contractions.  She had been bedridden since her 34th week.  She also has a history of smoking and asthma.  She was admitted here and labor was confirmed with ruptured membranes.  It was initially at 5.70%, -1.  His sac was ruptured, an IUPC was placed.  She received an epidural to control the pain and a Pitocin augmentation was performed.  She progressed for several hours to finish and push, then pushed for about 15 minutes to deliver a vigorous baby girl from an OA presentation.  Delivery of the head was manually assisted.  The shoulders and the rest of the body then followed without difficulty.  Baby was sucked in and let out a vigorous cry.  The cord was clamped twice and cut and the infant was handed over to the waiting nursing team.  The placenta, then delivered spontaneously and intact, presented a three-vessel cord.  Inspection of the perineum reveals that it is intact.  There was a hymenal remnant/skin tag protruding from the vaginal introit.  I discussed it with the patient.  She chose to have it removed.  This was accomplished and I placed a single interrupted 3-0 Vicryl suture for hemostasis.  Further inspection revealed bilateral superficial labial lacerations that were hemostatic and did not require repair.  The overall EBL is 300 ml.  Mom and baby are doing well now.  Cord gas is passed due to prematurity.

However, no adnexal mass was noted on bimanual examination.  By laparoscopy, the patient presented numerous omental adhesions to the vesicouterine peritoneum in the fundus of the uterus.  There were also adhesions to the left fallopian tube and right fallopian tube.  There was a large amount of blood in the abdomen, approximately 2 liters of clotted and non-clotted blood.  There was questionable gestational tissue ________ on the left sacrospinous ligament.  There was an apparent rupture and hemorrhagic ectopic pregnancy in the isthmus portion of the right fallopian tube.,PROCEDURE: After obtaining informed consent, the patient was taken to the operating room and general anesthesia was administered.  She was then positioned in the dorsal lithotomy position, prepared and draped in a normal sterile manner.  Once anesthesia was deemed adequate, a bimanual examination was performed under anesthesia.  A weighted speculum is then placed in the vagina.  The inner wall of the vagina elevated by the uterine sound and the anterior lip of the cervix were grasped with the vulsellum tenaculum.  The cervix was then serially dilated with Hank dilators to a Hank size #20, then precise curettage was performed to obtain a moderate amount of decidual appearing tissue and the tissue was then sent to pathology.  At this point, the uterine manipulator was placed in the cervix and attached to the anterior cervix and the vulsellum tenaculum and weighted speculum were removed.  Next, attention shifted to the abdomen.  The surgeons remove all dirty gloves from the previous part of the case.  Next, a 2 cm incision was made immediately below the umbilicus.  The upper surface of the umbilicus was grasped with a towel forceps and a Veress needle was inserted through this incision.  Then, a syringe was used to inject normal saline into the Veress needle.  The normal saline solution was falling freely, so a Veress needle was connected to the CO2 gas which was started at its lowest setting.  The gas was flowing freely with normal resistance, so the CO2 gas was advanced to a higher setting.  The abdomen was insufflated until adequate distension.  Once adequate distension was achieved, the CO2 gas was disconnected.  The Veress needle was removed and a size 11 stepped trocar was placed.  The introducer was removed and the trocar was connected to CO2 gas and a camera was inserted.  Then, a 1 cm incision was made on the midline, approximately two finger breadths below the pubic symphysis after transillumination with the camera.  A Veress needle and stepped sheath were inserted through this incision.  Then, the Veress needle was removed and a size #5 trocar was inserted under direct visualization.  Next, a size #5 port was placed approximately five fingerbreadths to the left of the umbilicus in the same manner.  A size #12 port was similarly placed approximately six fingerbreadths to the right of the umbilicus and also under direct visualization.  The laparoscopic dissector was inserted through the suprapubic port and was used to bluntly dissect omental adhesions from the vesicouterine peritoneum and bilateral fallopian tubes.  Next, the Dorsey suction irrigator was used to copiously irrigate the abdomen.  Approximate total of 3 liters of irrigation w

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Enlarged fibroid uterus.,2.  Hypermenorrhea.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Enlarged fibroid uterus.,2.  Hypermenorrhea.,3.  Secondary anemia.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  Dilation and curettage.,2.  Hysteroscopy., GROSS FINDINGS: The uterus was anteverted, significantly enlarged, irregular and firm.  The cervix is ​​patulous and nulliparous without lesions.  Adnexal examination was negative for masses.,PROCEDURE: The patient was taken to the operating room where she was properly prepared and sterilely draped under general anesthesia.  After bimanual examination, the cervix was exposed with a weighted vaginal speculum and the anterior lip of the cervix was grasped with a tenaculum vulsellum.  The uterus was probed to a depth of 11 cm.  The endocervical canal was then gradually dilated with Hanks and Hegar dilators up to a Hegar #10.  The ACMI hysteroscope was then introduced into the uterine cavity using sterile saline as a distension medium and with a connected video camera.  The endometrial cavity was distended with fluids and the cavity visualized.  Multiple irregular areas of fibroid degeneration were noted throughout the cavity.  The coronal areas were visualized bilaterally with the corresponding tubal ostia.  A moderate amount of proliferative-appearing endometrium was noted.  No direct intraluminal lesions were observed.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.  Several photos were taken of the endometrial cavity and the hysteroscope was removed from the cavity. A large pointed curette was then used to obtain a moderate amount of tissue, which was sent to the pathologist for analysis.  The instrument was removed from the vaginal vault.  The patient was sent to the recovery room in satisfactory postoperative condition.

transcription: EXAMINATION:, CT examination of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast.,INDICATIONS:, Abdominal pain.,TECHNIQUE:,CT examination of the abdomen and pelvis was performed after 100 ml of intravenous administration of Isovue-300 contrast.  No oral contrast agent was administered.  There was no comparison of studies.,RESULTS,CT PELVIS:,In the pelvis, the uterus presents a thickened-appearing endometrium.  There is also a 4.4 x 2.5 x 3.4 cm hypodense mass in the cervix and lower uterine segment of uncertain etiology.  There is also a 2.5 cm intramural hypodense mass involving the dorsal uterine fundus, likely representing a fibroid.  Several smaller fibroids were also suspected. The ovaries are unremarkable in appearance.  There is no free pelvic fluid or lymphadenopathy.,CT ABDOMEN:,The appendix has a normal appearance in the right lower quadrant.  There are few scattered diverticula in the sigmoid colon without signs of diverticulitis.  Furthermore, the small and large intestines do not present any particularity.  The stomach is quite ordinary.  There is no abdominal or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.  There are no adrenal masses.  The kidneys, liver, gallbladder and pancreas have an unremarkable appearance.  The spleen contains several small calcified granulomas, but no evidence of a mass.  It's a normal size.  The lung bases are clear bilaterally.  The bony structures show no features other than slight degenerative changes of the facets at L4-L5 and L5-S1.,PRINT:,1.  Hypoattenuating mass of the lower uterine segment and cervix of uncertain etiology measuring approximately 4.4 x 2.5 x 3.4 cm.,2.  Multiple uterine fibroids.,3.  Prominent endometrium.,4.  A follow-up pelvic ultrasound is recommended.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Full-term pregnancy.,2.  Desires permanent sterilization.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Full-term pregnancy.,2.  Desires permanent sterilization.,PROCEDURE:,1.  Repeat the low transverse cesarean section.,2.  Bilateral tubal ligation.,3.  Extensive adhesiolysis of the anterior abdominal wall, uterus and bladder.,ANESTHESIA:,Spinal/epidural with good effect.,RESULTS:,Birth of a vigorous male infant from cephalic presentation.  Apgars 9/9.  Birth weight 6 pounds 14 ounces.  Infant sucked with a bulb during delivery of the head and body.  Cord clamped and cut and infant transferred to the pediatric team present.  Manually extracted complete placenta intact with three vascular cords.  Extensive anterior abdominal wall adhesions, with the anterior abdominal wall completely adhering to the anterior uterus along the entire length of the incision.  Additionally, the bladder was involved in the adhesion mass complex.  A window was surgically developed at the apical aspect of the incision allowing the finger to pass behind the dense adhesions of the anterior abdominal wall.  These adhesions were surgically severed using the Bovie cautery technique, freeing the anterior uterine attachment from the anterior abdominal wall.  Upon initial entry through the fibrous layer of the anterior abdominal wall _______ into the serosal and lightly muscular part of the anterior uterus due to the dense adhesion attachment that had occurred during previous surgical procedures.  Bilateral tubal ligation performed without difficulty via the Parkland technique.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: , 500 ml.,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,URINARY FLOW: ,According to anesthesia records.  Urine cleared after surgery., IV FLUID: , According to anesthesia records., Patient tolerated the procedure well and was taken to the recovery room in stable condition with stable vital signs., OPERATIVE TECHNIQUE: , Patient was placed in supine position after spinal/epidural anesthesia.  She was prepared and draped in the usual manner for another C-section.  A sharp knife was used to make a Pfannenstiel skin incision at the location of the previous scar.  This was carried through the subcutaneous tissue to the dense fibromuscular and fascial layer with a sharp knife.  This incision was extended laterally with Mayo scissors.  A dense fibromuscular layer was encountered during the patient's previous surgical procedures.  During entry, the incision was made in the partial serous and muscular layer of the anterior uterus, and there was no free area to enter the peritoneal cavity due to the dense fibromuscular adhesions of the entire uterus to the anterior abdominal wall along the incision.  The fascia was previously separated at the top and bottom of the muscle layer.  A surgical window was created at the apical side of the incision towards the uterine fundus.  The finger could be passed and placed behind the dense adhesions between the uterus through the anterior abdominal wall.  This adhesion complex was transposed via Bovie cauterization along its entire length, circumferentially freeing the uterus from its attachment to the anterior abdominal wall.  Inferiorly, difficulties were encountered with separation of adhesions involving the bladder in addition to the uterus and anterior abdominal wall.  These adhesions were also treated surgically by sharp, blunt dissection, and electrocautery.  This was successfully achieved without anterior entry into the bladder.  Smooth micros and Metzenbaum scissors were then used to perform a sharp dissection to separate the bladder from its attachment to the lower uterine segment, thereby allowing vesicouterine peritoneal reflection for incision of the uterus.  The uterus was then incised using a sharp knife and a low transverse incision.  This was extended with bandage scissors.  The infant emerged easily from cephalic presentation.  Pear aspiration was performed after head and body delivery.  The cord was clamped and cut and the infant was transferred to the attending pediatric team.  Cord segment and cord blood

transcription: REASON FOR HOSPITALIZATION: ,Suspicious calcifications of the upper outer quadrant, left breast.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,The patient is a 78-year-old woman who underwent a routine screening mammogram on 06/04/08.  This study revealed the presence of punctate calcifications that appeared to be distributed in clusters in the left breast mound at the 2 o'clock position.  Additional imaging studies confirmed the suspicious nature of these calcifications.  The patient underwent a stereotactic needle biopsy of the left breast at 2 a.m. on 06/17/08.  Final histologic diagnosis of tissue removed during this procedure revealed focal fibrosis.  No calcifications could be identified upon examination of the biopsy material, including the x-ray taken of the preserved tissue. Two days after the stereotactic needle biopsy, however, the patient returned to the central breast with severe swelling, pain, and mass in the left breast.  She underwent ultrasound evaluation and was noted to develop a pseudoaneurysm at the site of the stereotactic needle biopsy.  I was called to see the patient at the emergency clinic at the breast center.  At the same time, Dr. Y was consulted in Interventional Radiology.  Drs Z and Y were able to identify the neck of the pseudoaneurysm in the left breast mound and this was injected under ultrasound guidance with thrombin material.  This resulted in immediate occlusion of the pseudoaneurysm.  The patient was seen at my office for a follow-up appointment on 06/24/08.  At this time, the patient continued to have evidence of significant hematoma and extensive bruising, resulting from the stereotactic needle biopsy.  There was, however, no evidence of reformation of the pseudoaneurysm.  There was no evidence of a pulsatile mass in the left breast mound or on the left chest wall. I discussed these issues with the patient and her husband.  The underlying problem is that the suspicious calcifications, which had been identified on the mammogram, had not been adequately sampled with the stereotactic needle biopsy; therefore, histological diagnosis does not explain the imaging findings.  For this reason, the patient was advised to undergo an excisional biopsy of this area with guidewire localization.  Because the breast mound had been significantly disrupted by the stereotaxic needle biopsy, the decision was to postpone any surgical intervention for at least three to four months.  The patient now returns to undergo excision of the left breast tissue with preoperative guidewire localization to identify the location of suspicious calcifications. The patient has a history of stereotactic needle biopsy of the left breast, which was performed on 01/27/04.  This revealed benign histological findings.  The family history is positive, involving a daughter who was diagnosed with breast cancer at age 40. Apart from her age, the patient has no other risk factors for developing breast cancer.  She is not receiving any hormone replacement therapy.  She had five children, the first pregnancy of which occurred at the age of 24. Apart from her daughter, no other family member has breast cancer.  There are no family members with a history of ovarian cancer.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,Other hospitalizations have occurred for asthma and pneumonia.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,Colon resection in 1990 and sinus surgeries in 1987, 1990 and 2005.,MEDICATIONS CURRENT:,1.  Plavix.,2.  Arava.,3.  Nexium.,4.  Fosamax.,5.  Advair.,6.  Singular.,7.  Spiriva.,8.  Lexapro.,DRUG ALLERGIES:, ASPIRIN, PENICILLIN, IODINE AND CODEINE.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Positive for heart disease, hypertension and stroke.  Family history is positive for colon cancer affecting his father and a brother.  The patient has a daughter who was diagnosed with breast cancer at the age of 40.,SOCIAL HISTORY:,The patient does not smoke.  She has an occasional

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Arch prolapse.,2.  Enterocele.,PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Arch prolapse.,2.  Enterocele.,OPERATIONS:,1.  Abdominosacrocolpopexy.,2.  Enterocele repair.,3.  Cystoscopy.,4.  Lysis of adhesions.,ANESTHESIA:, General endotracheal.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:, Less than 100 ml.,SAMPLE:, None.,BRIEF HISTORY:, The patient is a 53-year-old woman with a history of hysterectomy and presenting with vaginal vault prolapse.  The patient had good support in the anterior and posterior vagina but had significant apical prolapse.  Options such as watchful waiting, pessary, abdominal surgery, robotic sacrocolpopexy versus open sacrocolpopexy were discussed. The patient already had multiple abdominal scars.  The risk of open surgery was somewhat higher for the patient.  After discussing the options, the patient wanted to proceed with a Pfannenstiel incision and sacrocolpopexy repair.  The risks of anesthesia, bleeding, infection, pain, MI, DVT, PE, erogenous exposure to mesh, and mesh-related complications were discussed.  The patient understood the risks of recurrence, etc. and wanted to continue the intervention.  The patient was asked not to lift heavy loads for 3 months, etc. The patient was prepared for bowels, preoperative antibiotics were administered.,OPERATION DETAILS:,The patient was brought to the operating room, anesthesia was applied.  The patient was placed in the dorsal lithotomy position.  The patient was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  A lower abdominal Pfannenstiel incision was made at the former incision site.  The incision was made through the subcutaneous tissue through the fascia, and the fascia was peeled away from the rectus abdominis muscle.  The muscle was split down the middle and the peritoneum was penetrated using sharp instruments.  There was no injury to the intestine upon entry.  There were significant adhesions that broke loose.  All adhesions of the right lower quadrant and left lower quadrant sigmoid colon were released, likewise the colon was mobilized.  There was little room, everything was packed, Bookwalter was then placed on the sacred bone.  The middle of the sacral bone has been identified.  The right ureter was clearly identified and was lateral to where the posterior peritoneum was open.  The ligament on the sacral or sacral __________ was easily identified, 0 Ethibond points were placed x3.  A mesh of 1 cm x 5 cm was cut.  It was a flexible Prolene mesh attached to the sacral ligament.  The bladder was clearly out of the vault area which was exposed, in the gross area 0 Ethibond points were placed x3.  The fence was attached.  The apex was clearly up. The enterocele sac was closed with 4-0 Vicryl without much difficulty.  The ureter was not involved in this process at all.  The peritoneum was closed on the mesh.  Please note that the peritoneum has been opened and placed around the mesh so that it is not exposed to the intestine.  Before closure, antibiotic irrigation was performed using Ancef solution.  The mesh was exposed in an antibiotic solution before use. After complete irrigation with L and a half of antibiotic solution.  The entire solution has been removed.  Good hemostasis was achieved.  All packaging has been removed.  The count was right.  The rectus abdominis muscle was brought together using 4-0 Vicryl.  The fascia was closed using PDS loop #1 in the fascia running on both sides and was tied in the middle.  The subcutaneous tissue was closed with 4-0 Vicryl and the skin was closed with 4-0 Monocryl subcutaneously.  A cystoscopy was performed at the end of the procedure.  Please note that the Foley was in place throughout the procedure and was carefully placed at the start of the procedure.  A cystoscopy was performed and indigo carmine was administered.  There was a good efflux of indigo carmine in both ureteral openings.  There was no injury to the rectum or bladder.  The bladder seemed completely normal.  Rectal examination was performed at the end of the procedure.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:,1.  Chronic otitis media with effusion.,2.  Conductive hearing loss.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:,1.  Chronic otitis media with effusion.,2.  Conductive hearing loss.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Bilateral tympanostomy with myringotomy tube placement _______ split tube 1.0 mm.,ANESTHESIA:  ,Total IV general mask airway.,ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:  ,None.,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,INDICATIONS FOR PROCEDURE:,  The patient is a 1-year-old male with a history of chronic otitis media with effusion and conductive hearing loss refractory to outpatient medical therapy.  After risks, complications, consequences, and questions were addressed with the family, a written consent was obtained for the procedure.,PROCEDURE:,  The patient was brought to the operative suite by Anesthesia.  The patient was placed on the operating table in supine position.  After this, the patient was then placed under general mask airway and the patient's head was then turned to the left.,The Zeiss operative microscope and medium-sized ear speculum were placed and the cerumen from the external auditory canals were removed with a cerumen loop to #5 suction.  After this, the tympanic membrane is then brought into direct visualization with no signs of any gross retracted pockets or cholesteatoma.  A myringotomy incision was then made within the posterior inferior quadrant and the middle ear was then suctioned with a #5 suction demonstrating dry contents.  A _____ split tube 1.0 mm was then placed in the myringotomy incision utilizing a alligator forcep.  Cortisporin Otic drops were placed followed by cotton balls.  Attention was then drawn to the left ear with the head turned to the right and the medium sized ear speculum placed.  The external auditory canal was removed off of its cerumen with a #5 suction which led to the direct visualization of the tympanic membrane.  The tympanic membrane appeared with no signs of retraction pockets, cholesteatoma or air fluid levels.  A myringotomy incision was then made within the posterior inferior quadrant with a myringotomy blade after which a _________ split tube 1.0 mm was then placed with an alligator forcep.  After this, the patient had Cortisporin Otic drops followed by cotton balls placed.  The patient was then turned back to Anesthesia and transferred to recovery room in stable condition and tolerated the procedure very well.  The patient will be followed up approximately in one week and was sent home with a prescription for Ciloxan ear drops to be used as directed and with instructions not to get any water in the ears.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,  Obstructive adenotonsillar hypertrophy with chronic recurrent pharyngitis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Obstructive adenotonsillar hypertrophy with chronic recurrent pharyngitis.,SURGICAL PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy.,ANESTHESIA: , General endotracheal technique.,SURGICAL FINDINGS:  ,A 4+/4+ cryptic and hypertrophic tonsils with 2+/3+ hypertrophic adenoid pads.,INDICATIONS: , We were requested to evaluate the patient for complaints of enlarged tonsils, which cause difficulty swallowing, recurrent pharyngitis, and sleep-induced respiratory disturbance.  She was evaluated and scheduled for an elective procedure.,DESCRIPTION OF SURGERY:  ,The patient was brought to the operative suite and placed supine on the operating room table.  General anesthetic was administered.  Once appropriate anesthetic findings were achieved, the patient was intubated and prepped and draped in the usual sterile manner for a tonsillectomy.  He was placed in semi-Rose ___ position and a Crowe Davis-type mouth gag was introduced into the oropharynx.  Under an operating headlight, the oropharynx was clearly visualized.  The right tonsil was grasped with the fossa triangularis and using electrocautery enucleation technique, was removed from its fossa.  This followed placing the patient in a suspension position using a McIvor-type mouth gag and a red rubber Robinson catheter via the right naris.  Once the right tonsil was removed, the left tonsil was removed in a similar manner, once again using a needle point Bovie dissection at 20 watts.  With the tonsils removed, it was possible to visualize the adenoid pads.  The oropharynx was irrigated and the adenoid pad evaluated with an indirect mirror technique.  The adenoid pad was greater than 2+/4 and hypertrophic.  It was removed with successive passes of electrocautery suction.  The tonsillar fossa was then once again hemostased with suction cautery, injected with 0.5% ropivacaine with 1:100,000 adrenal solution and then closed with 2-0 Monocryl on an SH needle.  The redundant soft tissue of the uvula was removed posteriorly and cauterized with electrocautery to prevent swelling of the uvula in the postoperative period.  The patient's oropharynx and nasopharynx were irrigated with copious amounts of normal saline contained with small amount of iodine, and she was recovered from her general endotracheal anesthetic.  She was extubated and left the operating room in good condition to the postoperative recovery room area.,Estimated blood loss was minimal.  There were no complications.  Specimens produced were right and left tonsils.  The adenoid pad was ablated with electrocautery.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left canal cholesteatoma.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Left canal cholesteatoma.,OPERATIVE PROCEDURE:,1.  Left canal wall down tympanomastoidectomy with ossicular chain reconstruction.,2.  Microdissection.,3.  NIM facial nerve monitoring for three hours.,COMPLICATIONS:  ,None.,FINDINGS:,  There is an extremely large canal cholesteatoma, which eroded most of the posterior and superior canal wall.  There was a significant amount of myringosclerosis and tympanosclerosis.  There is some mild erosion of the lenticular process of the incus.  The facial nerve was normal.  We removed the incus, removed the head of the malleus, and placed a titanium-PORP from the stapes capitulum to a cartilage graft.,PROCEDURE: , The patient was taken to the operating room, placed under general anesthetic and intubated without difficulty.  The NIM facial nerve monitoring electrodes were positioned and monitoring was performed throughout the procedure.  There was no abnormal activity during this case.  We inspected the ear canal, identified the huge defect, which was completely filled with cerumen.  Through the ear canal, we removed as much as we could and then infiltrated the canal and postauricular area with 1:100,000 of epinephrine.,We prepped and draped the ear in a sterile fashion.  We reopened the previously used postauricular incision and dissected down the mastoid cortex.  We reflected the soft tissues anteriorly to the level of the ear canal and identified where the ear canal skin entered the defect in the mastoid bone.  A #6 cutting bur was used to drill down the mastoid cortex and identified this cholesteatoma which was then carefully dissected out.  We went all the way to the mastoid antrum.  We finished a complete mastoidectomy with identification of the tegmen, sigmoid sinus.  We removed the lateral aspect of the mastoid tip.  We lowered the facial ridge.  The incudostapedial joint was already membranous in nature, we went ahead and used the joint knife and removed the incus.  We separated the incus from the stapes and then removed it.  We used a malleus head nipper to remove the head of the malleus and then we continued to saucerize the entire mastoid cavity.,There was no cholesteatoma within the middle ear space, but there was roughly 40% surface area perforation.  The remaining portion of the tympanic membrane was extremely calcified and myringosclerotic; this was removed.  There was also a large focus of tympanosclerosis between the stapes crura, which was impinging the ability of the stapes to move.  We carefully dissected this out.  This did seem to improve the mobility of the stapes somewhat.  At this point, there was a near total perforation.  There was only a minimal amount of anterior remnant of the drum left.  We tried to go ahead and harvest the temporalis fascia, but there was really only wisps of this fascia in place.  He had already had a previous tympanoplasty, but even outside the areas where the graft was taken, the temporalis muscle was quite atrophied and lumpy, and I suspect this was due to his chronic disease and long history of corticosteroid usage.  We harvested a few pieces as best as we could.  We went ahead and did a meatoplasty by making a canal incision in the 6 o'clock and 12 o'clock positions.  We excised cartilage posteriorly and inferiorly to enlarge the meatus.  This cartilage was thin and used for cartilage tympanoplasty.  We placed some Gelfoam in the middle ear space and placed the cartilage on the top of it.  We did cut a titanium-PORP of the proper side and placed on top of the stapes capitulum to interface with the cartilage cap.  A few other small pieces of temporalis fascia were used to bulge through the surrounding edges of the cartilage and make sure that it was medial to any remnant of ear canal and tympanic membrane remnants.  We placed a layer of Gelfoam lateral to the graft, closed the postauricular incision in layers and put 2 Merocel packs in the ear.  Glasscock dressing was applied.  The patient was awakened from anesthesia and taken to the recovery room in stable condition.  He will be given antibiotics and pain medicines and he will be given instructions to follow up with me in one week.
