transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , I was asked by Dr. X to see the patient in consultation for a new diagnosis of colon cancer.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,  The patient presented to medical attention after she noticed mild abdominal cramping in February 2007.  At that time, she was pregnant and was unsure if her symptoms might have been due to the pregnancy.  Unfortunately, she had miscarriage at about seven weeks.  She again had abdominal cramping, severe, in late March 2007.  She underwent colonoscopy on 04/30/2007 by Dr. Y.  Of note, she is with a family history of early colon cancers and had her first colonoscopy at age 35 and no polyps were seen at that time.,On colonoscopy, she was found to have a near-obstructing lesion at the splenic flexure.  She was not able to have the scope passed past this lesion.  Pathology showed a colon cancer, although I do not have a copy of that report at this time.,She had surgical resection done yesterday.  The surgery was laparoscopic assisted with anastomosis.  At the time of surgery, lymph nodes were palpable.,Pathology showed colon adenocarcinoma, low grade, measuring 3.8 x 1.7 cm, circumferential and invading in to the subserosal mucosa greater than 5 mm, 13 lymph nodes were negative for metastasis.  There was no angiolymphatic invasion noted.  Radial margin was 0.1 mm.  Other margins were 5 and 6 mm.  Testing for microsatellite instability is still pending.,Staging has already been done with a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis.  This showed a mass at the splenic flexure, mildly enlarged lymph nodes there, and no evidence of metastasis to liver, lungs, or other organs.  The degenerative changes were noted at L5-S1.  The ovaries were normal.  An intrauterine device (IUD) was present in the uterus.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:,  She has otherwise been feeling well.  She has not had fevers, night sweats, or noticed lymphadenopathy.  She has not had cough, shortness of breath, back pain, bone pain, blood in her stool, melena, or change in stool caliber.  She was eating well up until the time of her surgery.  She is up-to-date on mammography, which will be due again in June.  She has no history of pulmonary, cardiac, renal, hepatic, thyroid, or central nervous system (CNS) disease.,ALLERGIES: , PENICILLIN, WHICH CAUSED HIVES WHEN SHE WAS A CHILD.,MEDICATIONS PRIOR TO ADMISSION:,  None.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: , No significant medical problem.  She has had three miscarriages, all of them at about seven weeks.  She has no prior surgeries.,SOCIAL HISTORY:  ,She smoked cigarettes socially while in her 20s.  A pack of cigarettes would last for more than a week.  She does not smoke now.  She has two glasses of wine per day, both red and white wine.  She is married and has no children.  An IUD was recently placed.  She works as an esthetician.,FAMILY HISTORY:  ,Father died of stage IV colon cancer at age 45.  This occurred when the patient was young and she is not sure of the rest of the paternal family history.  She does believe that aunts and uncles on that side may have died early.  Her brother died of pancreas cancer at age 44.  Another brother is aged 52 and he had polyps on colonoscopy a couple of years ago.  Otherwise, he has no medical problem.  Mother is aged 82 and healthy.  She was recently diagnosed with hemochromatosis.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , ,GENERAL:  She is in no acute distress.,VITAL SIGNS:  The patient is afebrile with a pulse of 78, respirations 16, blood pressure 124/70, and pulse oximetry is 93% on 3 L of oxygen by nasal cannula.,SKIN:  Warm and dry.  She has no jaundice.,LYMPHATICS:  No cervical or supraclavicular lymph nodes are palpable.,LUNGS:  There is no respiratory distress.,CARDIAC:  Regular rate.,ABDOMEN:  Soft and mildly tender.  Dressings are clean and dry.,EXTREMITIES:  No peripheral edema is noted.  Sequential compression devices (SCDs) are in place.,LABORATORY DATA:,  White blood count of 11.7, hemoglobin 12.8, hematocrit 37.8, platelets 408, differential shows left shift, MCV is 99.6.  Sodium is 136, potassium 4.1, bicarb 25, chloride 104, BUN 5, creatinine 0.7, and glucose is 133.  Calcium is 8.8 and magnesium is 1.8.,IMPRESSION AND PLAN: , Newly diagnosed stage II colon cancer, with a stage T3c, N0, M0 colon cancer, grade 1.  She does not have high-risk factors such as high grade or angiolymphatic invasion, and adequate number of lymph nodes were sampled.  Although, the tumor was near obstructing, she was not having symptoms and in fact was having normal bowel movements.,A lengthy discussion was held with the patient regarding her diagnosis and prognosis.  Firstly, she has a good prognosis for being cured without adjuvant therapy.  I would consider her borderline for chemotherapy given her young age.  Referring to the database that had been online, she has a 13% chance of relapse in the next five years, and with aggressive chemotherapy (X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA) platinum-based), this would be reduced to an 8% risk of relapse with a 5% benefit.  Chemotherapy with 5-FU based regimen would have a smaller benefit of around 2.5%.,Plan was made to allow her to recuperate and then meet with her and her husband to discuss the pros and cons of adjuvant chemotherapy including what regimen she could consider including the side effects.  We did not review all that information today.,She has a family history of early colon cancer.  Her mother will be visiting in the weekend and plan is to obtain the rest of the paternal family history if we can.  Tumor is being tested for microsatellite instability and we will discuss this when those results are available.  She has one sibling and he is up-to-date on colonoscopy.  She does report multiple tubes of blood were drawn prior to her admission.  I will check with Dr. Y's office whether she has had a CEA and liver-associated enzymes assessed.  If not, those can be drawn tomorrow.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:,1.  Left breast mass.,2.  Hypertrophic scar of the left breast.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES:,1.  Left breast mass.,2.  Hypertrophic scar of the left breast.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:  ,Excision of left breast mass and revision of scar.,ANESTHESIA:  ,Local with sedation.,SPECIMEN: , Scar with left breast mass.,DISPOSITION:  ,The patient tolerated the procedure well and transferred to the recover room in stable condition.,BRIEF HISTORY:  ,The patient is an 18-year-old female who presented to Dr. X's office.  The patient is status post left breast biopsy, which showed a fibrocystic disease with now a palpable mass just superior to the previous biopsy site.  The patient also has a hypertrophic scar.  Thus, the patient elected to undergo revision of the scar at the same time as an excision of the palpable mass.,INTRAOPERATIVE FINDINGS: , A hypertrophic scar was found and removed.  The cicatrix was removed in its entirety and once opening the wound, the area of tissue where the palpable mass was, was excised as well and sent to the lab.,PROCEDURE: , After informed consent, risks, and benefits of the procedure were explained to the patient and the patient's family, the patient was brought to the operating suite, prepped and draped in the normal sterile fashion.  Elliptical incision was made over the previous cicatrix.  The total length of the incision was 5.5 cm.  Removing the cicatrix in its entirety with a #15 blade Bard-Parker scalpel after anesthetizing with local solution with 0.25% Marcaine.  Next, the area of tissue just inferior to the palpable mass, where the palpable was removed with electro Bovie cautery.  Hemostasis was maintained.  Attention was next made to approximating the deep dermal layers.  An interrupted #4-0 Vicryl suture was used and then a running subcuticular Monocryl suture was used to approximate the skin edges.  Steri-Strips as well as bacitracin and sterile dressings were applied.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to recovery in stable condition.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Cervical lymphadenopathy.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,  Cervical lymphadenopathy.,PROCEDURE: , Excisional biopsy of right cervical lymph node.,ANESTHESIA: , General endotracheal anesthesia.,SPECIMEN: , Right cervical lymph node.,EBL: , 10 cc.,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,FINDINGS:,  Enlarged level 2 lymph node was identified and removed and sent for pathologic examination.,FLUIDS: , Please see anesthesia report.,URINE OUTPUT: , None recorded during the case.,INDICATIONS FOR PROCEDURE: , This is a 43-year-old female with a several-year history of persistent cervical lymphadenopathy.  She reports that it is painful to palpation on the right and has had multiple CT scans as well as an FNA which were all nondiagnostic.  After risks and benefits of surgery were discussed with the patient, an informed consent was obtained.  She was scheduled for an excisional biopsy of the right cervical lymph node.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: , The patient was taken to the operating room and placed in the supine position.  She was anesthetized with general endotracheal anesthesia.  The neck was then prepped and draped in the sterile fashion.  Again, noted on palpation there was an enlarged level 2 cervical lymph node.,A 3-cm horizontal incision was made over this lymph node.  Dissection was carried down until the sternocleidomastoid muscle was identified.  The enlarged lymph node that measured approximately 2 cm in diameter was identified and was removed and sent to Pathology for touch prep evaluation.  The area was then explored for any other enlarged lymph nodes.  None were identified, and hemostasis was achieved with electrocautery.  A quarter-inch Penrose drain was placed in the wound.,The wound was then irrigated and closed with 3-0 interrupted Vicryl sutures for a deep closure followed by a running 4-0 Prolene subcuticular suture.  Mastisol and Steri-Strip were placed over the incision, and sterile bandage was applied.  The patient tolerated this procedure well and was extubated without complications and transported to the recovery room in stable condition.  She will return to the office tomorrow in followup to have the Penrose drain removed.

Her sister died of breast cancer at age 32 and her daughter had breast cancer at age 27.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  ,The chest was clear.  The abdomen was nontender.  Pelvic examination shows no masses.  No heart murmur.  ,HOSPITAL COURSE:  ,The patient underwent surgery on the day of admission.  In the postoperative course she was afebrile and unremarkable.  The patient regained bowel function and was discharged on the morning of the fourth postoperative day.,OPERATIONS AND PROCEDURES: , Total abdominal hysterectomy/bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with resection of ovarian fossa peritoneum en bloc on July 25, 2006.,PATHOLOGY: , A 105-gram uterus without dysplasia or cancer.,CONDITION ON DISCHARGE: , Stable.,PLAN:  ,The patient will remain at rest initially with progressive ambulation after.  She will avoid lifting, driving or intercourse.  She will call me if any fevers, drainage, bleeding, or pain.  Follow up in my office in four weeks.  Family history, social history, psychosocial needs per the social worker.,DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS: , Percocet 5 #40 one every 3 hours p.r.n. pain.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Extremely large basal cell carcinoma, right lower lid.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:,  Extremely large basal cell carcinoma, right lower lid.,TITLE OF OPERATION: , Excision of large basal cell carcinoma, right lower lid, and repaired with used dorsal conjunctival flap in the upper lid and a large preauricular skin graft.,PROCEDURE: , The patient was brought into the operating room and prepped and draped in usual fashion.  Xylocaine 2% with epinephrine was injected beneath the conjunctiva and skin of the lower lid and also beneath the conjunctiva and skin of the upper lid.  A frontal nerve block was also given on the right upper lid.  The anesthetic agent was also injected in the right preauricular region which would provide a donor graft for the right lower lid defect.  The area was marked with a marking pen with margins of 3 to 4 mm, and a #15 Bard-Parker blade was used to make an incision at the nasal and temporal margins of the lesion.,The incision was carried inferiorly, and using a Steven scissors the normal skin, muscle, and conjunctiva was excised inferiorly.  The specimen was then marked and sent to pathology for frozen section.  Bleeding was controlled with a wet-field cautery, and the right upper lid was everted, and an incision was made 3 mm above the lid margin with the Bard-Parker blade in the entire length of the upper lid.  The incision reached the orbicularis, and Steven scissors were used to separate the tarsus from the underlying orbicularis.  Vertical cuts were made nasally and temporally, and a large dorsal conjunctival flap was fashioned with the conjunctiva attached superiorly.  It was placed into the defect in the lower lid and sutured with multiple interrupted 6-0 Vicryl sutures nasally, temporally, and inferiorly.,The defect in the skin was measured and an appropriate large preauricular graft was excised from the right preauricular region.  The defect was closed with interrupted 5-0 Prolene sutures, and the preauricular graft was sutured in place with multiple interrupted 6-0 silk sutures.  The upper border of the graft was attached to the upper lid after incision was made in the gray line with a Superblade, and the superior portion of the skin graft was sutured to the upper lid through the anterior lamella created by the razor blade incision.,Cryotherapy was then used to treat the nasal and temporal margins of the area of excision because of positive margins, and following this an antibiotic steroid ointment was instilled and a light pressure dressing was applied.  The patient tolerated the procedure well and was sent to recovery room in good condition.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: , Aplastic anemia.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , This is a very pleasant 72-year-old woman, who I have been following for her pancytopenia.  After several bone marrow biopsies, she was diagnosed with aplastic anemia.  She started cyclosporine and prednisone on 03/30/10.  She was admitted to the hospital from 07/11/10 to 07/14/10 with acute kidney injury.  Her cyclosporine level was 555.  It was thought that her acute kidney injury was due to cyclosporine toxicity and therefore that was held.,Overall, she tells me that now she feels quite well since leaving the hospital.  She was transfused 2 units of packed red blood cells while in the hospital.  Repeat CBC from 07/26/10 showed white blood cell count of 3.4 with a hemoglobin of 10.7 and platelet count of 49,000.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:,  Folic acid, Aciphex, MiraLax, trazodone, prednisone for 5 days every 4 weeks, Bactrim double strength 1 tablet b.i.d. on Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays.,ALLERGIES:  ,No known drug allergies.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: , As per the HPI, otherwise negative.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Hypertension.,2.  GERD.,3.  Osteoarthritis.,4.  Status post tonsillectomy.,5.  Status post hysterectomy.,6.  Status post bilateral cataract surgery.,7.  Esophageal stricture status post dilatation approximately four times.,SOCIAL HISTORY:  ,She has no tobacco use.  She has rare alcohol use.  She has three children and is a widow.  Her husband died after they were married only eight years.  She is retired.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Her sister had breast cancer.,PHYSICAL EXAM:,VIT:

transcription: DIAGNOSIS:,  Refractory anemia that is transfusion dependent.,CHIEF COMPLAINT: , I needed a blood transfusion.,HISTORY: , The patient is a 78-year-old gentleman with no substantial past medical history except for diabetes.  He denies any comorbid complications of the diabetes including kidney disease, heart disease, stroke, vision loss, or neuropathy.  At this time, he has been admitted for anemia with hemoglobin of 7.1 and requiring transfusion.  He reports that he has no signs or symptom of bleeding and had a blood transfusion approximately two months ago and actually several weeks before that blood transfusion, he had a transfusion for anemia.  He has been placed on B12, oral iron, and Procrit.  At this time, we are asked to evaluate him for further causes and treatment for his anemia.  He denies any constitutional complaints except for fatigue, malaise, and some dyspnea.  He has no adenopathy that he reports.  No fevers, night sweats, bone pain, rash, arthralgias, or myalgias.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  ,Diabetes.,PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:,  Hernia repair.,ALLERGIES: , He has no allergies.,MEDICATIONS: , Listed in the chart and include Coumadin, Lasix, metformin, folic acid, diltiazem, B12, Prevacid, and Feosol.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , He is a tobacco user.  He does not drink.  He lives alone, but has family and social support to look on him.,FAMILY HISTORY:,  Negative for blood or cancer disorders according to the patient.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL:  He is an elderly gentleman in no acute distress.  He is sitting up in bed eating his breakfast.  He is alert and oriented and answering questions appropriately.,VITAL SIGNS:  Blood pressure of 110/60, pulse of 99, respiratory rate of 14, and temperature of 97.4.  He is 69 inches tall and weighs 174 pounds.,HEENT:  Sclerae show mild arcus senilis in the right.  Left is clear.  Pupils are equally round and reactive to light.  Extraocular movements are intact.  Oropharynx is clear.,NECK:  Supple.  Trachea is midline.  No jugular venous pressure distention is noted.  No adenopathy in the cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary areas.,CHEST:  Clear.,HEART:  Regular rate and rhythm.,ABDOMEN:  Soft and nontender.  There may be some fullness in the left upper quadrant, although I do not appreciate a true spleen with inspiration.,EXTREMITIES:  No clubbing, but there is some edema, but no cyanosis.,NEUROLOGIC:  Noncontributory.,DERMATOLOGIC:  Noncontributory.,CARDIOVASCULAR:  Noncontributory.,IMPRESSION: , At this time is refractory anemia, which is transfusion dependent.  He is on B12, iron, folic acid, and Procrit.  There are no sign or symptom of blood loss and a recent esophagogastroduodenoscopy, which was negative.  His creatinine was 1.  My impression at this time is that he probably has an underlying myelodysplastic syndrome or bone marrow failure.  His creatinine on this hospitalization was up slightly to 1.6 and this may contribute to his anemia.,RECOMMENDATIONS:  ,At this time, my recommendation for the patient is that he undergoes further serologic evaluation with reticulocyte count, serum protein, and electrophoresis, LDH, B12, folate, erythropoietin level, and he should undergo a bone marrow aspiration and biopsy.  I have discussed the procedure in detail which the patient.  I have discussed the risks, benefits, and successes of that treatment and usefulness of the bone marrow and predicting his cause of refractory anemia and further therapeutic interventions, which might be beneficial to him.  He is willing to proceed with the studies I have described to him.  We will order an ultrasound of his abdomen because of the possible fullness of the spleen, and I will probably see him in follow up after this hospitalization.,As always, we greatly appreciate being able to participate in the care of your patient.  We appreciate the consultation of the patient.

transcription: 3-DIMENSIONAL SIMULATION,This patient is undergoing 3-dimensionally planned radiation therapy in order to adequately target structures at risk while diminishing the degree of exposure to uninvolved adjacent normal structures.  This optimizes the chance of controlling tumor while diminishing the acute and long-term side effects.  With conformal 3-dimensional simulation, there is extended physician, therapist, and dosimetrist effort and time expended.  The patient is initially taken into a conventional simulator room where appropriate markers are placed and the patient is positioned and immobilized.  Preliminary filed sizes and arrangements, including gantry angles, collimator angles, and number of fields are conceived.  Radiographs are taken and these films are approved by the physician.  Appropriate marks are placed on the patient's skin or on the  immobilization device.,The patient is transferred to the diagnostic facility and placed on a flat CT scan table.  Scans are performed through the targeted area.  The scans are evaluated by the radiation oncologist and the tumor volume, target volume, and critical structures are outlined on the CT images.  The dosimetrist then evaluates the slices in the treatment-planning computer with appropriately marked structures.  This volume is reconstructed in a virtual 3-dimensional space utilizing the beam's-eye view features.  Appropriate blocks are designed.  Multiplane computerized dosimetry is performed throughout the volume.  Field arrangements and blocking are modified as necessary to provide coverage of the target volume while minimizing dose to normal structures.,Once appropriate beam parameters and isodose distributions have been confirmed on the computer scan, the individual slices are then reviewed by the physician.  The beam's-eye view, block design, and appropriate volumes are also printed and reviewed by the physician.  Once these are approved, physical blocks or multi-leaf collimator equivalents will be devised.  If significant changes are made in the field arrangements from the original simulation, the patient is brought back to the simulator where computer designed fields are re-simulated.,In view of the extensive effort and time expenditure required, this procedure justifies the special procedure code, 77470.
transcription_preview: 3-DIMENSIONAL SIMULATION,This patient is undergoing 3-dimensionally planned radiation therapy in order to adequately target structures at risk while diminishing the degree of exposure to uninvolved adjacent normal structures. This optimizes the chance of controlling tumor while diminishing the acute and long-term side effects. With conformal 3-dimensional simulation, there is extended physician, therapist, and dosimetrist effort and time expended. transcription: SUBJECTIVE: Today I am the patient for immune thrombocytopenia.  His platelets dropped to 10 on 9/1/07 and shortly after learning of this result, I increased his prednisone to 60 mg daily.  Repeat on 1/16/07 revealed platelets up to 43. No bleeding problems noted.  I spoke with his hematologist who recommends at this point decreasing his prednisone to 40 mg for 3 days, then going down to 20 mg per day.  The patient had been taking 20 mg every other day for at least some time and her platelets were at least above about 20. PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, vital signs: as in table.  The patient is alert, pleasant and cooperative.  She is not in apparent distress.  The petechial areas on her legs have disappeared.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,Patient with improvement in her platelet count following a prednisone burst.  We will decrease his prednisone to 40 mg for 3 days, then go down to 20 mg per day.  Basically eventually, over time, I might try to move it back a little further.  She is taking medications for osteoporosis including bisphosphonate and calcium with vitamin D. We will arrange for a CBC to be taken weekly.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Carcinoma of the left breast.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Carcinoma of the left breast.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, True cut needle biopsy of the breast.,RAW FINDINGS:,This 65-year-old woman on examination presented dimpling and wrinkling of the skin associated with nipple discharge.  On examination, she has visible carcinoma of the left breast with dimpling, wrinkling, and erosion of the skin.  At this time, a true cut needle biopsy was performed.,PROCEDURE: The patient was taken to the operating room, laid in the supine position, prepared sterilely, and draped in the usual manner.  The area above the left breast was infiltrated with a 1:1 mixture of 0.25% marcaine and 1% xylocaine.  Using an automatic 18-gauge true needle biopsy, five left breast biopsies were surgically performed.  Hemostasis was controlled by pressure.  The patient tolerated the procedure well, while awaiting the results of the biopsy.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 19-year-old male with sickle cell anemia.  He presented alone to the emergency room with back pain for 3 days.  He does not take any medication.  He lives with a roommate.  Appetite is diminished.  No diarrhea or vomiting.  Empty well.  The bowels have been regular.  Denies any abdominal pain.  He complains of mild headaches, but his main concern is back pain that extends from above the lower T-spine to the lumbosacral spine.  The patient is unsure about his vaccinations.  The patient does indeed have sickle cell disease and the hemoglobin is being monitored in the hematology clinic.,ALLERGIES: , THE PATIENT IS ALLERGIC TO TYLENOL WITH CODEINE, but he states that he can get morphine with Benadryl., MEDICATIONS: , He was previously taking folic acid.  None at present.,SURGICAL HISTORY: , He has not had any surgery in the past.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Positive for diabetes, hypertension and cancer.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , He denies smoking or drug use.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , VITAL SIGNS: On examination, the patient has a tympanic temperature of 37 degrees, the pulse was recorded at 37 per minute. but later it was noted to be 66 per minute, respiratory rate is 24 per minute and blood pressure is 149/66, blood pressure is 132/72.,GENERAL: He is alert, speaks in full sentences, does not appear to be in distress.,HEENT: Normal.,NECK: Soft.,CHEST: Clear.,HEART: Regular.,ABDOMEN: Soft.  He has pain in his mid and lower spine.,SKIN: Color is normal.,EXTREMITIES: He moves all extremities well.,NEUROLOGICAL: Age appropriate.,ER COURSE: ,The patient was told that I would do lab work and give him IV fluids.  Additionally, he will receive a combination of morphine and Benadryl.  The patient received one liter of NS over one hour and then was maintained on D5 semi-normal saline at 125 per hour.  The CBC performed showed 4,300 white blood cells, hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39.9%, platelets 162,000, segments 65.9, lymphs 27, monos 3.4.  Chemistry tests performed were essentially normal except for a total bilirubin of 1.6 mg/dL, which were all indirect.  The patient was first given morphine and diphenhydramine at 6:40 p.m. and this was repeated at 8 p.m.  He received 5 mg of morphine and 25 mg of Benadryl.  I then spoke to Dr. X and it was decided to admit the patient. The patient initially stated that he wanted to be observed in the emergency room and given painkillers and fluids and wanted to go home in the morning.  He said he had a job interview in the morning.  The residents' service came to evaluate it.  The residency department then spoke with Dr. X and it was decided to admit him to the Hematology department for pain control and IV hydration.  He is due to be transferred to P.O. medications around 4 a.m. and I hope he can be discharged in time for his interview tomorrow.,PRINT:,Sickle cell crisis.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:,Veno-occlusive crisis and discitis.

A 16-hour left anterior oblique imaging was performed with and without protection of the original injection site. RESULTS: There are two small foci of increased activity in the left axilla.  This is consistent with the sentinel lymph node.  No other areas of activity are visualized aside from the injection site and two axillary lymph nodes.,PRINT: Technically successful lymph node injection with two areas of increased activity in the left axilla consistent with a sentinel lymph node.

transcription: MAIN DIAGNOSIS:,1.  A 61-year-old white male with a diagnosis of mantle cell lymphoma, diagnosed in 2001, post-autologous transplant status with BEAM regimen in 04/02 followed by relapse.,2.  Allogeneic peripheral stem cell transplant from a related sibling and the patient is 53 months from transplant.,3.  Graft-versus-host disease involving the gastrointestinal tract, skin, and liver currently off immunosuppression.,4.  Diabetes.,5.  Bipolar disorder.,6.  Chronic muscle pain.,7.  Chronic edema of the lower limbs.,8.  ECOG Performance Status 1., INTERMEDIATE HISTORY:, Patient comes to clinic today for follow-up.  I see him once every 4 to 8 weeks.  He is off any immunosuppression.  He has mild chronic GVHD, but not enough to warrant treatment. The disease is under control and he is 4 and a half years after the transplant. He has several complaints.  He had hematochezia.  I referred him to gastroenterology.  They did an upper and lower endoscopy.  No evidence of ulcer or abnormality was found.  Some polyps were removed.  They were benign.  He may have a mild iron deficiency, but he is tired and has several complaints related to his activity level.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:,1.  Paxil 40 mg once daily.,2.  Cozaar.,3.  Xanax 1 mg four times daily.,4.  Prozac 20 mg daily.,5.  Lasix 40 mg per day.,6.  Potassium 10 mEq per day.,7.  Mirapex two tablets every evening.,8.  Allegra 60 mg twice daily.,9.  Avandamet 4/1000 mg per day.,10.  Nexium 20 mg per day.,11.  NovoLog 25/50.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Fatigue, occasional rectal bleeding, and obesity.  Other systems were examined and found to be unremarkable.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: Today found temperature 35.8, blood pressure 120/49, pulse 85, respirations 18. HEENT: Oral cavity, no mucositis.  NECK: No knots.  AXILLE: No knots.  LUNGS: Clear.  HEART: Regular rhythm and rhythm without murmurs.  ABDOMEN: No palpable mass.  Morbid obesity.  EXTREMITIES: Slight edema of lower extremities.  SKIN: Slight dryness.  CNS: Grossly intact., LABORATORY DATA:, white count 4.4, hemoglobin 10.1, platelet count 132,000, sodium 135, potassium 3.9, chloride 105, bicarbonate 24, BUN 15, and creatinine 0.9.  Normal alkaline phosphatase 203, AST 58 and ALT 31.,EVALUATION AND PLAN:,1.  The patient with mantle cell lymphoma who is 4 and a half years after allotransplantation.  He currently shows no signs of illness.  Since he is 4 1/2 years post transplant, I have no plans to scan him or obtain chimerisms unless there is a reason to do so.,2.  He is slightly anemic, may have iron deficiency.  He had recurrent rectal bleeding.  I told him to take a multivitamin with iron and see how that helps with anemia.,3.  Concerning the hematochezia, he had an endoscopy.  I reviewed the results of the previous endoscopy.  He appears to have polyps, but there is no evidence of graft-versus-host disease.,4.  Regarding fatigue, I just reassured him that he should increase his activity level, but I'm not sure if that's realistic.,5.  He is being followed for his diabetes by his internist.,6.  If he had a fever or anything that suggested an infection, I advised him to call me.  I will see him again in about 2 months.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION:,Glioma.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,The patient is a 71-year-old woman who was initially,diagnosed with a brain tumor in 1982. She underwent,radiotherapy for it, although craniotomy failed to achieve a,biopsy due to seizure activity during surgery.  She did well for about 10 years and developed Parkinson's disease, possibly related to radiation therapy.  She was followed by the neurologist, Dr. Z, to treat the seizures.  She put in a vagal stimulator to help control her seizures. Over the past few months, she has had increasing weakness on her right side.  She lives in a retirement home.  She has not been able to walk and has not been able to write for the past three or four years. MRI performed on 11/13/2006 showed an increase in the size of the abdominal region and the left parietal region.  There was slight improvement and the appearance was consistent with a moderate to low grade tumor anterior to the motor cortex. Surgery was performed during this admission to remove part of the posterior portion of the tumor.  She tolerated the procedure well.  She did not notice any worsening or improvement in her weakness.  Pathology shows a low to intermediate grade glioma.  Dr. A's second opinion is still pending. The patient feels well today.  She has no headaches and reports no new neurological symptoms.  She did not have leg swelling, cough, shortness of breath, or chest pain.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS: ,1.  Ambien p.r.n.  ,2.  Vicodin p.r.n.  ,3.  Actonel every Sunday.  ,4.  Colace.  ,5.  Felbatol 1200 mg twice daily,6.  Heparin injections for prophylaxis.  ,7.  Maalox p.r.n.  ,8.  Mirapex 0.5 mg three times a day,9.  Protonix 40 mg per day.  ,10.  Tylenol p.r.n.  ,11.  Zanaflex 4 mg tablet, half a tablet daily and 6 mg at bedtime.  ,12.  She takes Zofran p.r.n., an albuterol inhaler q.i.d. and Aggrenox, which she should start.,The rest of the history is mainly from the table.,ALLERGIES: ,SHE IS ALLERGIC TO PENICILLIN.,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,1.  Parkinson's, probably secondary to radiotherapy.,2.  History of previous stroke.,3.  Seizure disorder secondary to his brain tumor.,4.  History of urinary incontinence.,5.  She had two hip fractures, which required surgical pinning.,6.  Appendectomy.,7.  Cholecystectomy.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Shows that she does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol.  She lives in a nursing home.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Shows family history of breast cancer.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, ,GENERAL: Today she is sitting in the chair, alert and appropriate.  It tends to lean to the right.  The right arm and hand are significantly weaker than the left.  She is quite thin.,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature is 98.5, blood pressure is 138/75, pulse is 76, respirations are 16, and pulse oximetry is 92% on room air.,HEENT: There is a craniotomy incision on the left parietal area, clean and dry with stitches still in place.  Oropharynx shows no thrush or mucositis.,LUNGS: Clear bilaterally on auscultation.,HEART: Exam shows regular rhythm.,ABDOMEN: soft.,EXTREMITIES: No peripheral edema or signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) noted on lower extremities.,PRINT AND PLAN:, Progressive low-grade glioma, now over 20 years since initial diagnosis.  She is in post-craniotomy status for volume reduction and did well with the operation. We reviewed phase II trials that used Temodar in grade 2 gliomas. Although complete responses are rare, it is quite common to have a partial response and/or stable disease, and most patients had improved quality of life indices, including many patients benefiting from reduced seizure activity.  We discussed using Temodar after she healed from her surgery.  Toxicities would mainly include fatigue, nausea and myelosuppression.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: ,We were asked to see the patient due to a brain tumor.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,She was initially diagnosed with glioblastoma multiforme in September of this year.  She had several brain lesions and a biopsy confirmed the diagnosis.  She was seen by Dr.  Due to her living conditions, she chose to be treated by the hospital's radiotherapy department and the oncology department.  Details of his treatment are not available at the time of writing this dictation.  His family has a packet of Temodar 100 mg tablets.  She is admitted now with increasing confusion.  A CT scan shows an increase in the size of the lesions compared to the preoperative CT scan.  We are asked to comment on his treatment at this stage.  She herself is confused and unable to provide any more story.  ,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,Based on her old records: No known medical history prior to diagnosis.,SOCIAL HISTORY: ,She lived alone and now lives in an assisted living facility.  ,MEDICATIONS,1.  Dilantin 300 mg per day.,2.  Haloperidol 1 mg h.s.,3.  Dexamethasone 4 mg q.i.d. ,4.  Docusate 100 mg twice daily,5.  Pen-VK 500 mg per day.,6.  Ibuprofen 600 mg daily.,7.  Zantac 150 mg twice daily.,8.  Temodar 100 mg per day.,9.  Magic mouthwash daily.,10.  Tylenol #3 as needed.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION: Impossible.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: Elderly woman, confused.  ,HEENT: Normal conjunctiva.  Normal ears and nose.  Mouth normal., NECK: supple., CHEST: clear., HEART: normal., ABDOMEN: soft, positive bowel sounds., NEUROLOGICAL: alert, cranial nerves intact.  Left arm slightly weak.  Left leg slightly weak.,PRINT AND PLAN:, Glioblastoma multiforme, unsure of where she is in her cancer treatment.  Given the number of pills in the hands of the patient's family, it appears that she has only recently been treated and so it is not surprising that she is experiencing increased problems related to the increase in tumor size.  We will need to speak with Dr. Y at the clinic to better understand his treatment regimen.  At this point, I will hold Temodar today and consider restarting him tomorrow if we can clarify his treatment plan.

transcription: HDR BRACHYTHERAPY, The intracavitary brachytherapy applicator was placed appropriately and secured after patient identification.  Simulation films were obtained, documenting its positioning.  The 3-dimensional treatment planning process was performed using the CT-derived data.  A treatment plan was selected using sequential dwell positions in a single catheter.  The patient was taken to the treatment area.  The patient was correctly positioned and the position of the intracavitary device was verified.  Catheter length measurements were taken.  Appropriate measurements of the dimensions and assembly of the probe were also made.  The applicator was attached to the HDR postloader.  The device performed its control sequences appropriately and brachytherapy was then administered without difficulty or complication.  The brachytherapy source has been correctly removed from the brachytherapy safe located inside the device.  Radiological screening was performed with the Geiger-Muller counter before and after completion of the brachytherapy procedure and the results were considered appropriate. Once the procedure was completed, the intracavitary device was removed without difficulty.  The patient was in no apparent distress and was discharged home.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Excision of squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid with frozen section and full thickness skin grafting of the opposite eyelid.,COMPLICATIONS: ,None.,BLOOD LOSS: ,Minimal.,ANESTHESIA:,Local with sedation.,INDICATION:,The patient is a 65 year old male with a large squamous cell carcinoma on the right upper eyelid, who has previously received radiotherapy.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:,The patient was taken to the operating room, laid on his back, administered intravenous sedation, and then prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  He was anesthetized with a combination of 2% lidocaine and 0.5% marcaine with epinephrine on both upper eyelids.  The area of ​​obvious scar tissue resulting from radiotherapy for squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid was completely excised to the eyelid margin, including resection of a few upper eyelashes.  This extended essentially from the lacrimal point to the lateral corner and up to the upper eyelid.  The resection was performed through the orbicularis oculi muscle, resecting the pretarsal orbicularis oculi muscle and the lower portion of the preseptal orbicularis oculi muscle, leaving the tarsus intact and leaving the orbital septum intact.  After complete resection, the patient was able to easily open and close his eyes because the levator muscle insertion was left intact up to the tarsal plate.  The sample was sent to pathology, which revealed only fibrous tissue and no signs of residual squamous cell carcinoma.  Meticulous hemostasis was achieved with the Bovie cautery and a full-thickness skin graft from the opposite upper eyelid was harvested in a manner similar to an appropriately sized blepharoplasty for the right upper eyelid defect.  The left upper eyelid incision was closed with 6-0 rapid-absorbing interrupted intestinal sutures, and the skin graft was sutured in place with 6-0 rapid-absorbing interrupted intestinal sutures.  An eye patch was placed on the right side and the patient tolerated the procedure well and was taken to the PACU in good condition.

transcription: CONCOMITANT CHEMORADIOTHERAPY FOR CURATIVE INTENTION PATIENTS, This patient receives combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy with the aim of maximizing the chances of controlling this cancer.  Chemotherapy is administered in addition to radiotherapy, not only to act as a cytotoxic agent on its own, but also to potentiate and enhance the effect of radiotherapy on tumor cells.  It has been shown in the literature that this will maximize the chances of control. During treatment, the patient's treatment should be closely monitored by the treating physician to ensure that the appropriate chemotherapy drugs are administered at the correct time during the course of radiotherapy.  It is also important to closely monitor the patient to know when chemotherapy or radiotherapy treatment should be stopped.  This combination treatment generally produces greater side effects than either treatment alone, and these must be constantly monitored and treatment initiated in a timely manner to minimize these effects.  As a result, this requires more frequent consultations and coordination with the medical oncologist.  Therefore, it becomes a very time-consuming treatment and warrants CPT code 77470.

transcription: REASON FOR VISIT:,Postoperative visit for craniopharyngioma.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,In brief, a 16-year-old right-handed boy, in the eleventh grade, who presents with blurred vision and visual acuity difficulties, was found to have a suprasellar tumor.  He was brought to the operating room on 04/01/07, underwent transsphenoidal resection of the tumor.  Histology came back as craniopharyngioma.  There is some residual disease; however, the visual apparatus was decompressed.  According to him, he is doing well, he has resumed his studies without any difficulty.  He has some headaches and occasional tinnitus, but his vision is much improved.,MEDICATIONS: ,Synthroid 100 mcg daily.,FINDINGS: ,On examination, he is awake, alert and oriented x 3. Pupils are equal and responsive.  The MOEs are full.  His visual acuity is 20/25 on the right (improved from 20/200) and on the left 20/200 improved from 20/400.  He presents with a bitemporal hemianopia, markedly improved and larger.  His motor skills are 5 out of 5. There are no focal motor or sensory deficits.  The abdominal incision is well healed.  There is no sign of erythema or collection.  The lumbar drain was also well healed.,Post-op MRI demonstrates a little residual disease.,Histology came back as craniopharyngioma.,ASSESSMENT: ,Post-op visit for craniopharyngioma with residual disease.,PLANS: ,Recommended to call.  I have discussed options with our radiation oncologist, Dr. X. They will schedule an appointment to see him.  Additionally, he will likely need an MRI before any treatment, to monitor residual disease.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Stage IIA right breast cancer.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: She is an extremely pleasant 58 year old woman, whom I am following up on for her stage IIA right breast cancer.  She noticed a lump in her breast in November 2007. A mammogram dated 01/28/08 showed a mass in her right breast.  On 02/10/08, she underwent an ultrasound-guided biopsy.  Pathology showed Nottingham grade II infiltrating ductal carcinoma.  The tumor was ER positive, PR positive and HER-2/neu negative.  On 02/22/08 she underwent a lumpectomy and sentinel lymph node biopsy.  Pathology showed a grade I infiltrating ductal carcinoma of 3.3 cm, one sentinel lymph node was negative.  It was therefore stage IIA T2, N0, M0 breast cancer.  It should be noted that at this time she was on hormone replacement therapy which was stopped.  She underwent radiotherapy which was completed in May 2008. She then started taking Arimidex, but unfortunately she did not tolerate the Arimidex and I replaced her with Femara.  She also did not tolerate Femara and I replaced it with Tamoxifen.  She did not tolerate tamoxifen and therefore when I saw her on 11/23/09 she decided to stop taking anti-estrogen medication.  She met with me again on 2/22/10 and decided she wanted to restart with tamoxifen.  When I saw her on 4/28/10, she was doing really well on the tamoxifen.  She told me that 2 weeks after this visit, she developed toxicity from tamoxifen and therefore stopped it herself.  She will no longer take tamoxifen. Overall, she feels good.  She has good energy levels and her ECOG performance status is 0. She denies any fever, chills or night sweats.  No lymphadenopathy.  No nausea or vomiting.  No changes in bowel or bladder habits.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:, Avapro 300 mg q.d., Pepcid q.d., Zyrtec p.r.n. and calcium q.d.,ALLERGIES:, Sulfa, Betadine and IV contrast.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: , According to HPI, otherwise negative.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1. Status after cholecystectomy in 1993.,8.  She has a history of positive tuberculosis test.,9.  She obtained her status after repair of a ventral hernia in November 2008.,SOCIAL HISTORY: She does not use tobacco.  Only occasional alcohol consumption.  She does not use any illicit drugs.  She has two adult children.  She is married.  She works as a social worker dealing with adult abuse and neglect issues.  Her husband is a high school chemistry teacher.,FAMILY HISTORY: Her father had prostate cancer.  His maternal uncle suffered from Hodgkin's disease, melanoma and prostate cancer.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VIT:

He works.  He did have an episode of upper respiratory infection and fever at the end of April which improved with antibiotics.  Overall, when he compares his strength to six or eight months ago, he finds that he feels much stronger.  He has no complaints other than mild xerostomia and treatment-related hearing loss.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vital signs: height 65 inches, weight 150, pulse 76, blood pressure 112/74, temperature 95.4, respirations 18.,HEENT: extraocular muscles intact. Dry mouth noted.,Lymph: No palpable lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary areas.,Lungs: Clear.,Heart: Regular rhythm.,Abdomen: Soft, not distended.  No liver, spleen, or other palpable masses.,Lower extremities: no edema.,Neurological: awake, alert, ambulatory, oriented, cognitively intact.,I reviewed the CT images and report from the May 1 study. This showed no evidence of metabolically active malignancy. The most recent laboratory studies were performed last September and the TSH was normal.  I asked him to repeat the TSH on the first anniversary.,He is currently not taking any medications.,In summary, this 57 year old man presented with T1 N3 nasopharyngeal cancer and is now 20 months after completion of all treatments.  He recovered well.  We will continue to follow thyroid function and I have asked him to get a TSH at the first birthday in September and a CBC in follow-up.  We will see him again in six months with a PET-CT. He returns to general care and direction of Dr. ABC.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION:, Newly diagnosed cholangiocarcinoma.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a very pleasant 77-year-old woman who noted an increase in her liver function tests during routine blood work in December 2009. Ultrasound of the abdomen showed gallbladder sludge and findings in the gallbladder consistent with a adenomyomatosis.  During this ultrasound, the size of the common bile duct was noted to be 10 mm.  She then underwent a CT scan of the abdomen in July 2010, which showed intrahepatic ductal dilation, with the size of the common bile duct being 12.7 mm.  She then underwent an MRCP MRI, which revealed stenosis of the distal common bile duct.  She was then referred to gastroenterology and underwent ERCP.  On August 24, 2010, she underwent endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography.  She was found to have a narrowing mass in the mid-to-proximal common bile duct consistent with cholangiocarcinoma.  A temporary biliary stent was placed across the biliary stricture.  Blood tests were performed during hospitalization.  She was also noted to have a high CA99.  She comes to the clinic today for an initial medical oncology consultation.  After seeing me this morning, she has a follow-up consultation with a surgeon.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia.  In July, she underwent surgery on her left eye for muscle repair.  Other surgeries include left ankle surgery for a fractured ankle in 2000.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Diovan 80/12.5 mg daily, Lipitor 10 mg daily, lutein 20 mg daily, folic acid 0.8 mg daily, and multivitamins daily.,ALLERGIES:,No known drug allergies.,HISTORY FAMILY:, Notable for heart disease.  She had three brothers who died following open heart surgery.  His parents and brothers all suffered from hypertension.  His younger brother died at the age of 18 from an infection caused by butchery.  He was cutting Argentine beef and contracted an infection and died within 24 hours.  She has a living brother who suffers from angina and an 84-year-old sister with dementia.  She has two healthy adult sons. SOCIAL HISTORY: The patient has been married to her second husband for ten years.  Her first husband died in 1995. She has no history of smoking and does not drink alcohol.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,The patient reports a change in her intestines since having the stent placed.  She has noticed some weight loss, but she emphasizes that this is due to a poor diet.  She experienced mild fatigue, but before her diagnosis she had absolutely no symptoms.  As mentioned above, she was found to have abnormalities in alkaline phosphatase and total bilirubin, AST, and ALT, which prompted follow-up.  She has been having difficulty with her vision which has improved with her recent surgery.  She denies any fever, chills and night sweats.  She had loose stools.  The remainder of his systems review is negative.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITALS:

transcription: SIMULATION IN 3 DIMENSIONS, This patient undergoes radiotherapy planned in 3 dimensions in order to correctly target the structures at risk while decreasing the degree of exposure to adjacent normal structures not involved.  This optimizes the chances of controlling the tumor while decreasing acute and long-term side effects.  With conformal three-dimensional simulation, doctors, therapists and dosimetrists spend a lot of effort and time.  The patient is initially taken to a conventional simulation room where appropriate markers are placed and the patient is positioned and immobilized.  Preliminary file sizes and layouts, including gantry angles, collimator angles, and number of fields, are designed.  X-rays are taken and these films are approved by the doctor.  Appropriate marks are placed on the patient's skin or on the immobilizer. The patient is transferred to the diagnostic center and placed on a flat CT table.  The analyzes are carried out in the targeted area.  The scans are evaluated by the radiation oncologist and the tumor volume, target volume and critical structures are indicated on the CT images.  The dosimetrist then evaluates the sections in the treatment planning computer with the structures correctly marked.  This volume is reconstructed in a three-dimensional virtual space using the naked eye view features of the beam.  Suitable blocks are designed.  Multiplane computerized dosimetry is performed throughout the volume.  Field arrangements and blocking are modified as necessary to ensure coverage of the target volume while minimizing dose to normal structures. Once the appropriate beam parameters and isodose distributions have been confirmed on the computer analysis, the individual slices are then examined by the physician.  The beam view, block design and appropriate volumes are also printed and reviewed by the doctor.  Once these are approved, physical blocks or equivalents of multi-leaf collimators will be designed.  If significant changes are made to the field arrangements from the original simulation, the patient is returned to the simulator where the computer-designed fields are simulated again. Given the considerable effort and time required, this procedure warrants Special Procedure Code 77470.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE: Today I am the patient for immune thrombocytopenia.  His platelets dropped to 10 on 9/1/07 and shortly after learning of this result, I increased his prednisone to 60 mg daily.  Repeat on 1/16/07 revealed platelets up to 43. No bleeding problems noted.  I spoke with his hematologist who recommends at this point decreasing his prednisone to 40 mg for 3 days, then going down to 20 mg per day.  The patient had been taking 20 mg every other day for at least some time and her platelets were at least above about 20. PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, vital signs: as in table.  The patient is alert, pleasant and cooperative.  She is not in apparent distress.  The petechial areas on her legs have disappeared.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,Patient with improvement in her platelet count following a prednisone burst.  We will decrease his prednisone to 40 mg for 3 days, then go down to 20 mg per day.  Basically eventually, over time, I might try to move it back a little further.  She is taking medications for osteoporosis including bisphosphonate and calcium with vitamin D. We will arrange for a CBC to be taken weekly.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Carcinoma of the left breast.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Carcinoma of the left breast.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:, True cut needle biopsy of the breast.,RAW FINDINGS:,This 65-year-old woman on examination presented dimpling and wrinkling of the skin associated with nipple discharge.  On examination, she has visible carcinoma of the left breast with dimpling, wrinkling, and erosion of the skin.  At this time, a true cut needle biopsy was performed.,PROCEDURE: The patient was taken to the operating room, laid in the supine position, prepared sterilely, and draped in the usual manner.  The area above the left breast was infiltrated with a 1:1 mixture of 0.25% marcaine and 1% xylocaine.  Using an automatic 18-gauge true needle biopsy, five left breast biopsies were surgically performed.  Hemostasis was controlled by pressure.  The patient tolerated the procedure well, while awaiting the results of the biopsy.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 19-year-old male with sickle cell anemia.  He presented alone to the emergency room with back pain for 3 days.  He does not take any medication.  He lives with a roommate.  Appetite is diminished.  No diarrhea or vomiting.  Empty well.  The bowels have been regular.  Denies any abdominal pain.  He complains of mild headaches, but his main concern is back pain that extends from above the lower T-spine to the lumbosacral spine.  The patient is unsure about his vaccinations.  The patient does indeed have sickle cell disease and the hemoglobin is being monitored in the hematology clinic.,ALLERGIES: , THE PATIENT IS ALLERGIC TO TYLENOL WITH CODEINE, but he states that he can get morphine with Benadryl., MEDICATIONS: , He was previously taking folic acid.  None at present.,SURGICAL HISTORY: , He has not had any surgery in the past.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Positive for diabetes, hypertension and cancer.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , He denies smoking or drug use.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , VITAL SIGNS: On examination, the patient has a tympanic temperature of 37 degrees, the pulse was recorded at 37 per minute. but later it was noted to be 66 per minute, respiratory rate is 24 per minute and blood pressure is 149/66, blood pressure is 132/72.,GENERAL: He is alert, speaks in full sentences, does not appear to be in distress.,HEENT: Normal.,NECK: Soft.,CHEST: Clear.,HEART: Regular.,ABDOMEN: Soft.  He has pain in his mid and lower spine.,SKIN: Color is normal.,EXTREMITIES: He moves all extremities well.,NEUROLOGICAL: Age appropriate.,ER COURSE: ,The patient was told that I would do lab work and give him IV fluids.  Additionally, he will receive a combination of morphine and Benadryl.  The patient received one liter of NS over one hour and then was maintained on D5 semi-normal saline at 125 per hour.  The CBC performed showed 4,300 white blood cells, hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39.9%, platelets 162,000, segments 65.9, lymphs 27, monos 3.4.  Chemistry tests performed were essentially normal except for a total bilirubin of 1.6 mg/dL, which were all indirect.  The patient was first given morphine and diphenhydramine at 6:40 p.m. and this was repeated at 8 p.m.  He received 5 mg of morphine and 25 mg of Benadryl.  I then spoke to Dr. X and it was decided to admit the patient. The patient initially stated that he wanted to be observed in the emergency room and given painkillers and fluids and wanted to go home in the morning.  He said he had a job interview in the morning.  The residents' service came to evaluate it.  The residency department then spoke with Dr. X and it was decided to admit him to the Hematology department for pain control and IV hydration.  He is due to be transferred to P.O. medications around 4 a.m. and I hope he can be discharged in time for his interview tomorrow.,PRINT:,Sickle cell crisis.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:,Veno-occlusive crisis and discitis.

transcription: EXAMINATION:,Lymphatic scanning in nuclear medicine.,REASON FOR EXAMINATION:,Left breast cancer.,TECHNIQUE:,1.0 mCi of Technetium-99m sulfur colloid was injected into the dermis surrounding the left breast biopsy site at four locations.  A 16-hour left anterior oblique imaging was performed with and without protection of the original injection site. RESULTS: There are two small foci of increased activity in the left axilla.  This is consistent with the sentinel lymph node.  No other areas of activity are visualized aside from the injection site and two axillary lymph nodes.,PRINT: Technically successful lymph node injection with two areas of increased activity in the left axilla consistent with a sentinel lymph node.

transcription: MAIN DIAGNOSIS:,1.  A 61-year-old white male with a diagnosis of mantle cell lymphoma, diagnosed in 2001, post-autologous transplant status with BEAM regimen in 04/02 followed by relapse.,2.  Allogeneic peripheral stem cell transplant from a related sibling and the patient is 53 months from transplant.,3.  Graft-versus-host disease involving the gastrointestinal tract, skin, and liver currently off immunosuppression.,4.  Diabetes.,5.  Bipolar disorder.,6.  Chronic muscle pain.,7.  Chronic edema of the lower limbs.,8.  ECOG Performance Status 1., INTERMEDIATE HISTORY:, Patient comes to clinic today for follow-up.  I see him once every 4 to 8 weeks.  He is off any immunosuppression.  He has mild chronic GVHD, but not enough to warrant treatment. The disease is under control and he is 4 and a half years after the transplant. He has several complaints.  He had hematochezia.  I referred him to gastroenterology.  They did an upper and lower endoscopy.  No evidence of ulcer or abnormality was found.  Some polyps were removed.  They were benign.  He may have a mild iron deficiency, but he is tired and has several complaints related to his activity level.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:,1.  Paxil 40 mg once daily.,2.  Cozaar.,3.  Xanax 1 mg four times daily.,4.  Prozac 20 mg daily.,5.  Lasix 40 mg per day.,6.  Potassium 10 mEq per day.,7.  Mirapex two tablets every evening.,8.  Allegra 60 mg twice daily.,9.  Avandamet 4/1000 mg per day.,10.  Nexium 20 mg per day.,11.  NovoLog 25/50.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Fatigue, occasional rectal bleeding, and obesity.  Other systems were examined and found to be unremarkable.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: Today found temperature 35.8, blood pressure 120/49, pulse 85, respirations 18. HEENT: Oral cavity, no mucositis.  NECK: No knots.  AXILLE: No knots.  LUNGS: Clear.  HEART: Regular rhythm and rhythm without murmurs.  ABDOMEN: No palpable mass.  Morbid obesity.  EXTREMITIES: Slight edema of lower extremities.  SKIN: Slight dryness.  CNS: Grossly intact., LABORATORY DATA:, white count 4.4, hemoglobin 10.1, platelet count 132,000, sodium 135, potassium 3.9, chloride 105, bicarbonate 24, BUN 15, and creatinine 0.9.  Normal alkaline phosphatase 203, AST 58 and ALT 31.,EVALUATION AND PLAN:,1.  The patient with mantle cell lymphoma who is 4 and a half years after allotransplantation.  He currently shows no signs of illness.  Since he is 4 1/2 years post transplant, I have no plans to scan him or obtain chimerisms unless there is a reason to do so.,2.  He is slightly anemic, may have iron deficiency.  He had recurrent rectal bleeding.  I told him to take a multivitamin with iron and see how that helps with anemia.,3.  Concerning the hematochezia, he had an endoscopy.  I reviewed the results of the previous endoscopy.  He appears to have polyps, but there is no evidence of graft-versus-host disease.,4.  Regarding fatigue, I just reassured him that he should increase his activity level, but I'm not sure if that's realistic.,5.  He is being followed for his diabetes by his internist.,6.  If he had a fever or anything that suggested an infection, I advised him to call me.  I will see him again in about 2 months.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION:,Glioma.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,The patient is a 71-year-old woman who was initially,diagnosed with a brain tumor in 1982. She underwent,radiotherapy for it, although craniotomy failed to achieve a,biopsy due to seizure activity during surgery.  She did well for about 10 years and developed Parkinson's disease, possibly related to radiation therapy.  She was followed by the neurologist, Dr. Z, to treat the seizures.  She put in a vagal stimulator to help control her seizures. Over the past few months, she has had increasing weakness on her right side.  She lives in a retirement home.  She has not been able to walk and has not been able to write for the past three or four years. MRI performed on 11/13/2006 showed an increase in the size of the abdominal region and the left parietal region.  There was slight improvement and the appearance was consistent with a moderate to low grade tumor anterior to the motor cortex. Surgery was performed during this admission to remove part of the posterior portion of the tumor.  She tolerated the procedure well.  She did not notice any worsening or improvement in her weakness.  Pathology shows a low to intermediate grade glioma.  Dr. A's second opinion is still pending. The patient feels well today.  She has no headaches and reports no new neurological symptoms.  She did not have leg swelling, cough, shortness of breath, or chest pain.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS: ,1.  Ambien p.r.n.  ,2.  Vicodin p.r.n.  ,3.  Actonel every Sunday.  ,4.  Colace.  ,5.  Felbatol 1200 mg twice daily,6.  Heparin injections for prophylaxis.  ,7.  Maalox p.r.n.  ,8.  Mirapex 0.5 mg three times a day,9.  Protonix 40 mg per day.  ,10.  Tylenol p.r.n.  ,11.  Zanaflex 4 mg tablet, half a tablet daily and 6 mg at bedtime.  ,12.  She takes Zofran p.r.n., an albuterol inhaler q.i.d. and Aggrenox, which she should start.,The rest of the history is mainly from the table.,ALLERGIES: ,SHE IS ALLERGIC TO PENICILLIN.,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,1.  Parkinson's, probably secondary to radiotherapy.,2.  History of previous stroke.,3.  Seizure disorder secondary to his brain tumor.,4.  History of urinary incontinence.,5.  She had two hip fractures, which required surgical pinning.,6.  Appendectomy.,7.  Cholecystectomy.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Shows that she does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol.  She lives in a nursing home.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Shows family history of breast cancer.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, ,GENERAL: Today she is sitting in the chair, alert and appropriate.  It tends to lean to the right.  The right arm and hand are significantly weaker than the left.  She is quite thin.,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature is 98.5, blood pressure is 138/75, pulse is 76, respirations are 16, and pulse oximetry is 92% on room air.,HEENT: There is a craniotomy incision on the left parietal area, clean and dry with stitches still in place.  Oropharynx shows no thrush or mucositis.,LUNGS: Clear bilaterally on auscultation.,HEART: Exam shows regular rhythm.,ABDOMEN: soft.,EXTREMITIES: No peripheral edema or signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) noted on lower extremities.,PRINT AND PLAN:, Progressive low-grade glioma, now over 20 years since initial diagnosis.  She is in post-craniotomy status for volume reduction and did well with the operation. We reviewed phase II trials that used Temodar in grade 2 gliomas. Although complete responses are rare, it is quite common to have a partial response and/or stable disease, and most patients had improved quality of life indices, including many patients benefiting from reduced seizure activity.  We discussed using Temodar after she healed from her surgery.  Toxicities would mainly include fatigue, nausea and myelosuppression.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: ,We were asked to see the patient due to a brain tumor.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: ,She was initially diagnosed with glioblastoma multiforme in September of this year.  She had several brain lesions and a biopsy confirmed the diagnosis.  She was seen by Dr.  Due to her living conditions, she chose to be treated by the hospital's radiotherapy department and the oncology department.  Details of his treatment are not available at the time of writing this dictation.  His family has a packet of Temodar 100 mg tablets.  She is admitted now with increasing confusion.  A CT scan shows an increase in the size of the lesions compared to the preoperative CT scan.  We are asked to comment on his treatment at this stage.  She herself is confused and unable to provide any more story.  ,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,Based on her old records: No known medical history prior to diagnosis.,SOCIAL HISTORY: ,She lived alone and now lives in an assisted living facility.  ,MEDICATIONS,1.  Dilantin 300 mg per day.,2.  Haloperidol 1 mg h.s.,3.  Dexamethasone 4 mg q.i.d. ,4.  Docusate 100 mg twice daily,5.  Pen-VK 500 mg per day.,6.  Ibuprofen 600 mg daily.,7.  Zantac 150 mg twice daily.,8.  Temodar 100 mg per day.,9.  Magic mouthwash daily.,10.  Tylenol #3 as needed.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION: Impossible.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: Elderly woman, confused.  ,HEENT: Normal conjunctiva.  Normal ears and nose.  Mouth normal., NECK: supple., CHEST: clear., HEART: normal., ABDOMEN: soft, positive bowel sounds., NEUROLOGICAL: alert, cranial nerves intact.  Left arm slightly weak.  Left leg slightly weak.,PRINT AND PLAN:, Glioblastoma multiforme, unsure of where she is in her cancer treatment.  Given the number of pills in the hands of the patient's family, it appears that she has only recently been treated and so it is not surprising that she is experiencing increased problems related to the increase in tumor size.  We will need to speak with Dr. Y at the clinic to better understand his treatment regimen.  At this point, I will hold Temodar today and consider restarting him tomorrow if we can clarify his treatment plan.

transcription: HDR BRACHYTHERAPY, The intracavitary brachytherapy applicator was placed appropriately and secured after patient identification.  Simulation films were obtained, documenting its positioning.  The 3-dimensional treatment planning process was performed using the CT-derived data.  A treatment plan was selected using sequential dwell positions in a single catheter.  The patient was taken to the treatment area.  The patient was correctly positioned and the position of the intracavitary device was verified.  Catheter length measurements were taken.  Appropriate measurements of the dimensions and assembly of the probe were also made.  The applicator was attached to the HDR postloader.  The device performed its control sequences appropriately and brachytherapy was then administered without difficulty or complication.  The brachytherapy source has been correctly removed from the brachytherapy safe located inside the device.  Radiological screening was performed with the Geiger-Muller counter before and after completion of the brachytherapy procedure and the results were considered appropriate. Once the procedure was completed, the intracavitary device was removed without difficulty.  The patient was in no apparent distress and was discharged home.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED: , Excision of squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid with frozen section and full thickness skin grafting of the opposite eyelid.,COMPLICATIONS: ,None.,BLOOD LOSS: ,Minimal.,ANESTHESIA:,Local with sedation.,INDICATION:,The patient is a 65 year old male with a large squamous cell carcinoma on the right upper eyelid, who has previously received radiotherapy.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:,The patient was taken to the operating room, laid on his back, administered intravenous sedation, and then prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  He was anesthetized with a combination of 2% lidocaine and 0.5% marcaine with epinephrine on both upper eyelids.  The area of ​​obvious scar tissue resulting from radiotherapy for squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper eyelid was completely excised to the eyelid margin, including resection of a few upper eyelashes.  This extended essentially from the lacrimal point to the lateral corner and up to the upper eyelid.  The resection was performed through the orbicularis oculi muscle, resecting the pretarsal orbicularis oculi muscle and the lower portion of the preseptal orbicularis oculi muscle, leaving the tarsus intact and leaving the orbital septum intact.  After complete resection, the patient was able to easily open and close his eyes because the levator muscle insertion was left intact up to the tarsal plate.  The sample was sent to pathology, which revealed only fibrous tissue and no signs of residual squamous cell carcinoma.  Meticulous hemostasis was achieved with the Bovie cautery and a full-thickness skin graft from the opposite upper eyelid was harvested in a manner similar to an appropriately sized blepharoplasty for the right upper eyelid defect.  The left upper eyelid incision was closed with 6-0 rapid-absorbing interrupted intestinal sutures, and the skin graft was sutured in place with 6-0 rapid-absorbing interrupted intestinal sutures.  An eye patch was placed on the right side and the patient tolerated the procedure well and was taken to the PACU in good condition.

transcription: CONCOMITANT CHEMORADIOTHERAPY FOR CURATIVE INTENTION PATIENTS, This patient receives combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy with the aim of maximizing the chances of controlling this cancer.  Chemotherapy is administered in addition to radiotherapy, not only to act as a cytotoxic agent on its own, but also to potentiate and enhance the effect of radiotherapy on tumor cells.  It has been shown in the literature that this will maximize the chances of control. During treatment, the patient's treatment should be closely monitored by the treating physician to ensure that the appropriate chemotherapy drugs are administered at the correct time during the course of radiotherapy.  It is also important to closely monitor the patient to know when chemotherapy or radiotherapy treatment should be stopped.  This combination treatment generally produces greater side effects than either treatment alone, and these must be constantly monitored and treatment initiated in a timely manner to minimize these effects.  As a result, this requires more frequent consultations and coordination with the medical oncologist.  Therefore, it becomes a very time-consuming treatment and warrants CPT code 77470.

transcription: REASON FOR VISIT:,Postoperative visit for craniopharyngioma.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,In brief, a 16-year-old right-handed boy, in the eleventh grade, who presents with blurred vision and visual acuity difficulties, was found to have a suprasellar tumor.  He was brought to the operating room on 04/01/07, underwent transsphenoidal resection of the tumor.  Histology came back as craniopharyngioma.  There is some residual disease; however, the visual apparatus was decompressed.  According to him, he is doing well, he has resumed his studies without any difficulty.  He has some headaches and occasional tinnitus, but his vision is much improved.,MEDICATIONS: ,Synthroid 100 mcg daily.,FINDINGS: ,On examination, he is awake, alert and oriented x 3. Pupils are equal and responsive.  The MOEs are full.  His visual acuity is 20/25 on the right (improved from 20/200) and on the left 20/200 improved from 20/400.  He presents with a bitemporal hemianopia, markedly improved and larger.  His motor skills are 5 out of 5. There are no focal motor or sensory deficits.  The abdominal incision is well healed.  There is no sign of erythema or collection.  The lumbar drain was also well healed.,Post-op MRI demonstrates a little residual disease.,Histology came back as craniopharyngioma.,ASSESSMENT: ,Post-op visit for craniopharyngioma with residual disease.,PLANS: ,Recommended to call.  I have discussed options with our radiation oncologist, Dr. X. They will schedule an appointment to see him.  Additionally, he will likely need an MRI before any treatment, to monitor residual disease.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Stage IIA right breast cancer.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: She is an extremely pleasant 58 year old woman, whom I am following up on for her stage IIA right breast cancer.  She noticed a lump in her breast in November 2007. A mammogram dated 01/28/08 showed a mass in her right breast.  On 02/10/08, she underwent an ultrasound-guided biopsy.  Pathology showed Nottingham grade II infiltrating ductal carcinoma.  The tumor was ER positive, PR positive and HER-2/neu negative.  On 02/22/08 she underwent a lumpectomy and sentinel lymph node biopsy.  Pathology showed a grade I infiltrating ductal carcinoma of 3.3 cm, one sentinel lymph node was negative.  It was therefore stage IIA T2, N0, M0 breast cancer.  It should be noted that at this time she was on hormone replacement therapy which was stopped.  She underwent radiotherapy which was completed in May 2008. She then started taking Arimidex, but unfortunately she did not tolerate the Arimidex and I replaced her with Femara.  She also did not tolerate Femara and I replaced it with Tamoxifen.  She did not tolerate tamoxifen and therefore when I saw her on 11/23/09 she decided to stop taking anti-estrogen medication.  She met with me again on 2/22/10 and decided she wanted to restart with tamoxifen.  When I saw her on 4/28/10, she was doing really well on the tamoxifen.  She told me that 2 weeks after this visit, she developed toxicity from tamoxifen and therefore stopped it herself.  She will no longer take tamoxifen. Overall, she feels good.  She has good energy levels and her ECOG performance status is 0. She denies any fever, chills or night sweats.  No lymphadenopathy.  No nausea or vomiting.  No changes in bowel or bladder habits.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:, Avapro 300 mg q.d., Pepcid q.d., Zyrtec p.r.n. and calcium q.d.,ALLERGIES:, Sulfa, Betadine and IV contrast.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: , According to HPI, otherwise negative.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Asthma.,2.  Hypertension.,3.  GERD.,4.  Eczema.,5.  Status after three cesareans.,6.  Status after hysterectomy in 1981 for fibroids.  They also removed an ovary.,7.  Status after cholecystectomy in 1993.,8.  She has a history of positive tuberculosis test.,9.  She obtained her status after repair of a ventral hernia in November 2008.,SOCIAL HISTORY: She does not use tobacco.  Only occasional alcohol consumption.  She does not use any illicit drugs.  She has two adult children.  She is married.  She works as a social worker dealing with adult abuse and neglect issues.  Her husband is a high school chemistry teacher.,FAMILY HISTORY: Her father had prostate cancer.  His maternal uncle suffered from Hodgkin's disease, melanoma and prostate cancer.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VIT:

transcription: DIAGNOSIS:, T1 N3 M0 nasopharyngeal cancer, condition after radiotherapy with 2 cycles of high-dose cisplatin with radiotherapy, completed in June 2006; status after 2 cycles of carboplatin/5-FU administered as adjuvant therapy, completed in September 2006; hearing loss related to chemotherapy and radiotherapy; xerostomia; history of deep vein thrombosis of the left upper extremity.,PERFORMANCE STATUS:,0.,INTERVAL HISTORY:,In the interval since his last visit, he has done quite well.  He works.  He did have an episode of upper respiratory infection and fever at the end of April which improved with antibiotics.  Overall, when he compares his strength to six or eight months ago, he finds that he feels much stronger.  He has no complaints other than mild xerostomia and treatment-related hearing loss.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,Vital signs: height 65 inches, weight 150, pulse 76, blood pressure 112/74, temperature 95.4, respirations 18.,HEENT: extraocular muscles intact.  Non-icteric sclera.  Oral cavity free of exudate or ulceration.  Dry mouth noted.,Lymph: No palpable lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary areas.,Lungs: Clear.,Heart: Regular rhythm.,Abdomen: Soft, not distended.  No liver, spleen, or other palpable masses.,Lower extremities: no edema.,Neurological: awake, alert, ambulatory, oriented, cognitively intact.,I reviewed the CT images and report from the May 1 study. This showed no evidence of metabolically active malignancy. The most recent laboratory studies were performed last September and the TSH was normal.  I asked him to repeat the TSH on the first anniversary.,He is currently not taking any medications.,In summary, this 57 year old man presented with T1 N3 nasopharyngeal cancer and is now 20 months after completion of all treatments.  He recovered well.  We will continue to follow thyroid function and I have asked him to get a TSH at the first birthday in September and a CBC in follow-up.  We will see him again in six months with a PET-CT. He returns to general care and direction of Dr. ABC.
