transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a 68-year-old woman whom I have been following, who has had angina.  In any case today, she called me because she had a recurrent left arm pain after her stent, three days ago, and this persisted after two sublingual nitroglycerin when I spoke to her.  I advised her to call 911, which she did.  While waiting for 911, she was attended to by a physician who is her neighbor and he advised her to take the third nitroglycerin and that apparently relieved her pain.  By the time she presented here, she is currently pain-free and is feeling well.,PAST CARDIAC HISTORY: , The patient has been having arm pain for several months.  She underwent an exercise stress echocardiogram within the last several months with me, which was equivocal, but then she had a nuclear stress test which showed inferobasilar ischemia.  I had originally advised her for a heart catheterization but she wanted medical therapy, so we put her on a beta-blocker.  However, her arm pain symptoms accelerated and she had some jaw pain, so she presented to the emergency room.  On 08/16/08, she ended up having a cardiac catheterization and that showed normal left main 80%  mid LAD lesion, circumflex normal, and RCA totally occluded in the mid portion and there were collaterals from the left to the right, as well as right to right to that area.  The decision was made to transfer  her as she may be having collateral insufficiency from the LAD stenosis to the RCA vessel.  She underwent that with drug-eluting stents on 08/16/08, with I believe three or four total placed, and was discharged on 08/17/08.  She had some left arm discomfort on 08/18/08, but this was mild.  Yesterday, she felt very fatigued, but no arm pain, and today, she had arm pain after walking and again it resolved now completely after three sublingual nitroglycerin.  This is her usual angina.  She is being admitted with unstable angina post stent.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: , Longstanding hypertension, CAD as above, hyperlipidemia, and overactive bladder.,MEDICATIONS:,1.  Detrol LA 2 mg once a day.,2.  Prilosec for GERD 20 mg once a day.,3.  Glucosamine 500/400 mg once a day for arthritis.,4.  Multivitamin p.o. daily.,5.  Nitroglycerin sublingual as available to her.,6.  Toprol-XL 25 mg once a day which I started although she had been bradycardic, but she seems to be tolerating.,7.  Aspirin 325 mg once a day.,8.  Plavix 75 mg once a day.,9.  Diovan 160 mg once a day.,10.  Claritin 10 mg once a day for allergic rhinitis.,11.  Norvasc 5 mg once a day.,12.  Lipitor 5 mg once a day.,13.  Evista 60 mg once a day.,ALLERGIES: , ALLERGIES TO MEDICATIONS ARE NONE.  SHE DENIES ANY SHRIMP OR SEA FOOD ALLERGY.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Her father died of an MI in his 50s and a brother had his first MI and bypass surgery at 54.,SOCIAL HISTORY:  ,She does not smoke cigarettes, abuse alcohol, no use of illicit drugs.  She is divorced and lives alone and is a retired laboratory technician from Cornell Diagnostic Laboratory.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:,  She denies a history of stroke, cancer, vomiting up blood, coughing up blood, bright red blood per rectum, bleeding stomach ulcers, renal calculi, cholelithiasis, asthma, emphysema, pneumonia, tuberculosis, home oxygen use or sleep apnea, although she has been told in the past that she snores and there was some question of apnea in 05/08.  No morning headaches or fatigue.  No psychiatric diagnosis.  No psoriasis, no lupus.  Remainder of the review of systems is negative x14 systems except as described above.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL:  She is a pleasant elderly woman, currently in no acute distress.,VITAL SIGNS:  Height 4 feet 11 inches, weight 128 pounds, temperature 97.2 degrees Fahrenheit, blood pressure 142/70, pulse 47,  respiratory rate 16, and O2 saturation 100%,HEENT:  Cranium is normocephalic and atraumatic.  She has moist mucosal membranes.,NECK:  Veins are not distended.  There are no carotid bruits.,LUNGS:  Clear to auscultation and percussion without wheezes.,HEART:  S1 and S2, regular rate.  No significant murmurs, rubs or gallops.  PMI nondisplaced.,ABDOMEN:  Soft and nondistended.  Bowel sounds present.,EXTREMITIES:  Without significant clubbing, cyanosis or edema.  Pulses grossly intact.  Bilateral groins are inspected, status post as the right femoral artery was used for access for the diagnostic cardiac catheterization here and left femoral artery used for PCI and there is no evidence of hematoma or bruit and intact distal pulses.,LABORATORY DATA: , EKG reviewed which shows sinus bradycardia at the rate of 51 beats per minute and no acute disease.,Sodium 136, potassium 3.8, chloride 105, and bicarbonate 27.  BUN 16 and creatinine 0.9.  Glucose 110.  Magnesium 2.5.  ALT 107 and AST 65 and these were normal on 08/15/08.  INR is 0.89, PTT 20.9, white blood cell count 8.2, hematocrit 31 and it was 35 on 08/15/08, and platelet count 257,000.,IMPRESSION AND PLAN:  ,The patient is a 68-year-old woman with exertional angina, characterized with arm pain, who underwent recent left anterior descending percutaneous coronary intervention and has now had recurrence of that arm pain post stenting to the left anterior descending artery and it may be that she is continuing to have collateral insufficiency of the right coronary artery.  In any case, given this unstable presentation requiring three sublingual nitroglycerin before she was even pain free, I am going to admit her to the hospital and there is currently no evidence requiring acute reperfusion therapy.  We will continue her beta-blocker and I cannot increase the dose because she is bradycardic already.  Aspirin, Plavix, valsartan, Lipitor, and Norvasc.  I am going to add Imdur and watch headaches as she apparently had some on nitro paste before, and we will rule out MI, although there is a little suspicion.  I suppose it is possible that she has non-cardiac arm pain, but that seems less likely as it has been nitrate responsive and seems exertionally related and the other possibility may be that we end up needing to put in a pacemaker, so we can maximize beta-blocker use for anti-anginal effect.  My concern is that there is persistent right coronary artery ischemia, not helped by left anterior descending percutaneous coronary intervention, which was severely stenotic and she does have normal LV function.  She will continue the glucosamine for her arthritis, Claritin for allergies, and Detrol LA for urinary incontinence.,Total patient care time in the emergency department 75 minutes.  All this was discussed in detail with the patient and her daughter who expressed understanding and agreement.  The patient desires full resuscitation status.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  This is a 91-year-old female who was brought in by family.  Apparently, she was complaining that she felt she might have been poisoned at her care facility.  The daughter who accompanied the patient states that she does not think anything is actually wrong, but she became extremely agitated and she thinks that is the biggest problem with the patient right now.  The patient apparently had a little bit of dry heaves, but no actual vomiting.  She had just finished eating dinner.  No one else in the facility has been ill.,PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Remarkable for previous abdominal surgeries.  She has a pacemaker.  She has a history of recent collarbone fracture.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:  Very difficult to get from the patient herself.  She seems to deny any significant pain or discomfort, but really seems not particularly intent on letting me know what is bothering her.  She initially stated that everything was wrong, but could not specify any specific complaints.  Denies chest pain, back pain, or abdominal pain.  Denies any extremity symptoms or complaints.,SOCIAL HISTORY:  The patient is a nonsmoker.  She is accompanied here with daughter who brought her over here.  They were visiting the patient when this episode occurred.,MEDICATIONS:  Please see list.,ALLERGIES:  NONE.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  VITAL SIGNS:  The patient is afebrile, actually has a very normal vital signs including normal pulse oximetry at 99% on room air.  GENERAL:  The patient is an elderly frail looking little lady lying on the gurney.  She is awake, alert, and not really wanted to answer most of the questions I asked her.  She does have a tremor with her mouth, which the daughter states has been there for "many years".  HEENT:  Eye exam is unremarkable.  Oral mucosa is still moist and well hydrated.  Posterior pharynx is clear.  NECK:  Supple.  LUNGS:  Actually clear with good breath sounds.  There are no wheezes, no rales, or rhonchi.  Good air movement.  CARDIAC:  Without murmur.  ABDOMEN:  Soft.  I do not elicit any tenderness.  There is no abdominal distention.  Bowel sounds are present in all quadrants.  SKIN:  Skin is without rash or petechiae.  There is no cyanosis.  EXTREMITIES:  No evidence of any trauma to the extremities.,EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT COURSE:  I had a long discussion with the family and they would like the patient receive something for agitation, so she was given 0.5 mg of Ativan intramuscularly.  After about half an hour, I came back to talk to the patient and the family, the patient states that she feels better.  Family states she seems more calm.  They do not want to pursue any further workup at this time.,IMPRESSION:  ACUTE EPISODE OF AGITATION.,PLAN:  At this time, I had reviewed the patient's records and it is not particularly enlightening as to what could have triggered off this episode.  The patient herself has good vital signs.  She does not seem to have any specific acute process going on and seemed to feel comfortable after the Ativan was given, a small quantity was given to the patient.  Family and daughter specifically did not want to pursue any workup at this point, which at this point I think is reasonable and we will have her follow up with ABC.  She is discharged in stable condition.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , This is a 12-year-old male, who was admitted to the Emergency Department, who fell off his bicycle, not wearing a helmet, a few hours ago.  There was loss of consciousness.  The patient complains of neck pain.,CHRONIC/INACTIVE CONDITIONS:,  None.,PERSONAL/FAMILY/SOCIAL HISTORY/ILLNESSES:,  None.,PREVIOUS INJURIES: , Minor.,MEDICATIONS: , None.,PREVIOUS OPERATIONS: , None.,ALLERGIES:  ,NONE KNOWN.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Negative for heart disease, hypertension, obesity, diabetes, cancer or stroke.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , The patient is single.  He is a student.  He does not smoke, drink alcohol or consume drugs.,REVIEW OF SYSTEMS,CONSTITUTIONAL:  The patient denies weight loss/gain, fever, chills.,ENMT:  The patient denies headaches, nosebleeds, voice changes, blurry vision, changes in/loss of vision.,CV:  The patient denies chest pain, SOB supine, palpitations, edema, varicose veins, leg pains.,RESPIRATORY:  The patient denies SOB, wheezing, sputum production, bloody sputum, cough.,GI:  The patient denies heartburn, blood in stools, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, constipation.,GU:  The patient denies painful/burning urination, cloudy/dark urine, flank pain, groin pain.,MS:  The patient denies joint pain/stiffness, backaches, tendon/ligaments/muscle pains/strains, bone aches/pains, muscle weakness.,NEURO:  The patient had a loss of consciousness during the accident.  He does not recall the details of the accident.  Otherwise, negative for blackouts, seizures, loss of memory, hallucinations, weakness, numbness, tremors, paralysis.,PSYCH:  Negative for anxiety, irritability, apathy, depression, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances, suicidal thoughts.,INTEGUMENTARY:  Negative for unusual hair loss/breakage, skin lesions/discoloration, unusual nail breakage/discoloration.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,CONSTITUTIONAL:  Blood pressure 150/75, pulse rate 80, respirations 18, temperature 37.4, saturation 97% on room air.  The patient shows moderate obesity.,NECK:  The neck is symmetric, the trachea is in the midline, and there are no masses.  No crepitus is palpated.  The thyroid is palpable, not enlarged, smooth, moves with swallowing, and has no palpable masses.,RESPIRATIONS:  Normal respiratory effort.  There is no intercostal retraction or action by the accessory muscles.  Normal breath sounds bilaterally with no rhonchi, wheezing or rubs.,CARDIOVASCULAR:  The PMI is palpable at the 5ICS in the MCL.  No thrills on palpation.  S1 and S2 are easily audible.  No audible S3, S4, murmur, click or rub.  Abdominal aorta is not palpable.  No audible abdominal bruits.  Femoral pulses are 3+ bilaterally, without audible bruits.  Extremities show no edema or varicosities.,GASTROINTESTINAL:  No palpable tenderness or masses.  Liver and spleen are percussed but not palpable under the costal margins.  No evidence for umbilical or groin herniae.,LYMPHATIC:  No nodes over 3 mm in the neck, axillae or groins.,MUSCULOSKELETAL:  Normal gait and station.  The patient is on a stretcher.  Symmetric muscle strength and normal tone, without signs of atrophy or abnormal movements.,SKIN:  There is a hematoma in the forehead and one in the occipital scalp, and there are abrasions in the upper extremities and abrasions on the knees.  No induration or subcutaneous nodules to palpation.,NEUROLOGIC:  Normal sensation by touch.  The patient moves all four extremities.,PSYCHIATRIC:  Oriented to time, place, and person.  Appropriate mood and affect.,LABORATORY DATA:  Reviewed chest x-ray, which is normal, right hand x-ray, which is normal, and an MRI of the head, which is normal.,DIAGNOSES,1.  Concussion.,2.  Facial abrasion.,3.  Scalp laceration.,4.  Knee abrasions.,PLANS/RECOMMENDATIONS:,  Admitted for observation.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 19-year-old male with sickle cell anemia.  He presented alone to the emergency room with back pain for 3 days.  He does not take any medication.  He lives with a roommate.  Appetite is diminished.  No diarrhea or vomiting.  Empty well.  The bowels have been regular.  Denies any abdominal pain.  He complains of mild headaches, but his main concern is back pain that extends from above the lower T-spine to the lumbosacral spine.  The patient is unsure about his vaccinations.  The patient does indeed have sickle cell disease and the hemoglobin is being monitored in the hematology clinic.,ALLERGIES: , THE PATIENT IS ALLERGIC TO TYLENOL WITH CODEINE, but he states that he can get morphine with Benadryl., MEDICATIONS: , He was previously taking folic acid.  None at present.,SURGICAL HISTORY: , He has not had any surgery in the past.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Positive for diabetes, hypertension and cancer.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , He denies smoking or drug use.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , VITAL SIGNS: On examination, the patient has a tympanic temperature of 37 degrees, the pulse was recorded at 37 per minute. but later it was noted to be 66 per minute, respiratory rate is 24 per minute and blood pressure is 149/66, blood pressure is 132/72.,GENERAL: He is alert, speaks in full sentences, does not appear to be in distress.,HEENT: Normal.,NECK: Soft.,CHEST: Clear.,HEART: Regular.,ABDOMEN: Soft.  He has pain in his mid and lower spine.,SKIN: Color is normal.,EXTREMITIES: He moves all extremities well.,NEUROLOGICAL: Age appropriate.,ER COURSE: ,The patient was told that I would do lab work and give him IV fluids.  Additionally, he will receive a combination of morphine and Benadryl.  The patient received one liter of NS over one hour and then was maintained on D5 semi-normal saline at 125 per hour.  The CBC performed showed 4,300 white blood cells, hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39.9%, platelets 162,000, segments 65.9, lymphs 27, monos 3.4.  Chemistry tests performed were essentially normal except for a total bilirubin of 1.6 mg/dL, which were all indirect.  The patient was first given morphine and diphenhydramine at 6:40 p.m. and this was repeated at 8 p.m.  He received 5 mg of morphine and 25 mg of Benadryl.  I then spoke to Dr. X and it was decided to admit the patient. The patient initially stated that he wanted to be observed in the emergency room and given painkillers and fluids and wanted to go home in the morning.  He said he had a job interview in the morning.  The residents' service came to evaluate it.  The residency department then spoke with Dr. X and it was decided to admit him to the Hematology department for pain control and IV hydration.  He is due to be transferred to P.O. medications around 4 a.m. and I hope he can be discharged in time for his interview tomorrow.,PRINT:,Sickle cell crisis.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:,Veno-occlusive crisis and discitis.

transcription: PRESENTATION:,A 16-year-old male presents to the emergency department (ED) with rectal bleeding and pain with defecation.,HISTORY:,A 16-year-old African American male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of rectal bleeding and pain with defecation.  The patient states that he was doing well until approximately three days before his presentation, when he began experiencing some pain while defecating.  The next day, he noted increasing pain and first noticed blood on the surface of his stool.  The pain worsened the next day with increased bleeding as well as mucopurulent anal discharge.  The patient denies any history of rectal bleeding or pain.  He also denies any previous sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) and states that he was tested for HIV eight months ago and was negative.  The patient states that he has not felt well for a week.  He claims to have felt “feverish” on several occasions but did not take his temperature.  He also complained of some abdominal pain accompanied by nausea and diarrhea as well as generalized myalgia and fatigue.  He thinks he lost a few pounds, but he hasn't weighed himself to determine the exact amount of weight loss. The patient states that he has been sexually active since the age of 13. He admits to having had eight previous partners and says he "usually" uses a condom.  Upon further questioning, the patient states that of his eight partners, three were women and five were men.  His most recent sexual partner was a 38-year-old man with whom he had been living for six months.  He states that he has been tested for STDs before, but states that he only gave urine and blood for testing.  He is unaware of his partner's HIV status but assumes his partner is not infected because he looks healthy.  The patient also acknowledges an episode of sexual abuse at the age of 8 by a family friend.  Because the man was a member of the family church, the patient never felt comfortable telling any of the adults in his life.  He is very concerned about disclosing his sexual behavior to his family, as their family has expressed very negative comments about men who have sex with men.  He accesses emergency care unaccompanied by an adult.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, Thin but non-toxic young man with obvious discomfort.,Pulse = 105,RR = 23,BP = 120/62,HEENT: Multiple areas of white plaque-like material on the oral mucosa.,Neck: Multiple anterior/posterior cervical nodes in the anterior and posterior chains - 1-2 cm. diameter.,Lungs: clear to auscultation.,Cardiac: precordium quiet.,Nl S1/S2 with a II/VI systolic murmur. ,Abdomen: soft without hepatosplenomegaly.,GU: male Tanner V without external penile lesions.,Lymph: axillary lymph nodes 2 to 3 cm bilaterally.,Epitrochlear lymph nodes 1 to 2 cm.,Multiple inguinal nodes 1 to 2 cm.,Rectal: extremely painful digital examination.,+ macroscopic blood and mucous membranes.,ASSESSMENT IN LABORATORY:,Hbg = 12.5 g/dL, Hct = 32%, WBC = 3.9 tu/µL, Platelets = 120,000 tu/µL, 76% neutrophils, 19% lymphocytes, 1% eosinophils, 4% monocytes, ALT = 82 U/L, AST = 90 U/L, Sedimentation rate erythrocyte = 90, Electrolytes = normal, Gram stain of anus swab: numerous leukocytes, DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: , This patient presents with acute rectal pain with bleeding and anal discharge.  The patient also has some constitutional symptoms, including fever, fatigue, abdominal discomfort, and lymphadenopathy on physical examination.  The following are part of the differential diagnosis: Acute Proctitis and Proctocolitis., ACUTE HIV SEROCONVERSION:, This subject is sexually active and reports inconsistent condom use.  Gastrointestinal symptoms have recently been frequently reported in patients with a history of HIV seroconversion.  Rectal symptoms of bleeding and pain are not common with HIV and an alternative diagnosis would be necessary.,PERIRECTAL ABSCESS:,A patient with a history of receptive anal intercourse is at risk of developing a perirectal abscess.

transcription: HISTORY AND PHYSICS: The patient is a 13-year-old boy who has a history of Shone complex and presents with complete heart block.  He is on a pacemaker.  He had a coarctation of the aorta and it was repaired when he was a baby.  He has been followed in our cardiology clinic here and is doing well.  However, last night he was sleeping and he states that he felt like he was having a dream, and there was thunder in the dream that woke him up.  He then felt his defibrillator go off and it continued and he felt like his heart rate was not normal.  So his father put him in the car and transported him here.  It has been reviewed here.  He had scar tissue at one point when the internal stimulator wasn't working properly and he had to have it replaced.  It was 2 a.m. when he woke up and, again, he was brought here by private vehicle.  He was fine before going to bed.  No cough, cold, runny nose, fever.  No trauma was noted.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Shone complex, pacemaker dependent.,MEDICATIONS:, He is not currently taking any medications.,ALLERGIES:, He has no allergies.,IMMUNIZATIONS:, Up to date.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, He lives with his parents.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Negative.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEM:, Twelve questions, all negative unless otherwise noted. above.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: This is an awake and alert male, who appears to be in mild distress.,HEENT: Pupils are equal, round and responsive to light.  Extraocular movements are intact.  His TMs are clear.  His nostrils are clear.  The mucous membranes are pink and moist.  The throat is clear. NECK: Supple without lymphadenopathy or masses.  Trachea is midline.,LUNGS: clear.,HEART: shows bradycardia at 53. Has good distal pulses.,ABDOMEN: soft, not tender.  Positive bowel sounds.  No guard, no bounce.  No rash is observed.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,Initial blood pressure is 164/90.  He was transferred to room 1. He was placed on a nasal cannula.  The pulse ox was 100%, which is normal.  We put it on a monitor.  We did an ECG; it doesn't seem to be picking up his pacemaker at the moment.  Shortly after the patient arrived, the Medtronic technician came to test his pacemaker.  The Medtronic representative informed me that the lead they had in place had been recalled because it was prone to microfractures, oversensing, and automatic defibrillation.  As instructed, he was moved to room 1, placed on a monitor, pulse ox.  An IV was placed.  A standard blood test was sent.  A chest x-ray was taken showing a normal heart size, the lead appeared to be in place.  There were no signs of pulmonary edema.  His pacemaker didn't seem to be picking up.  We placed him on a transthoracic tube.  However, it is difficult to get good placement with these due to the area where his pacemaker was placed.  The Medtronic technician first turned off his defibrillation mode and lowered his sensor.  However, we were unable to get our transthoracic pacemaker to capture his heart.  When the Medtronic representative turned off the pacemaker, the heart rate seemed to drop into the 40s. The patient appeared to be in pain.  We put it back at the rate of 60 at that point.  He still has sinus bradycardia, but no signs of extrasystoles.  No widening of his QRS complex.  I spoke with Cardiology.  The cardiology department arrived and assessed him at my bedside.  Again, we increased the transthoracic pacemaker, but it doesn't seem to be speeding up yet, and his heart rate continues with the Medtronic's internal pacemaker.  So, he and the ICU doctor on call, Dr. X, agreed to take this young man to the ICU. An hour after the presentation here, the ICU was ready for bed.  I accompanied the patient to the ICU.  He remained awake and alert.  At first, he complained of severe chest pain.  Once the defibrillator was turned off, he no longer had any pain.  He was transported to the pediatric PICU and delivered in stable condition. LABORATORY DATA: CBC was normal.  Chem-20 was also normal.

He had a coarctation of the aorta and it was repaired when he was a baby. He has been followed in our cardiology clinic here and is doing well. However, last night he was sleeping and he states that he felt like he was having a dream, and there was thunder in the...

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: A 14-day-old child was seen by a private physician due to a blister.  On Friday, she was found to have a small blister near her navel.  They went to their doctor on Saturday, the culture was drawn.  It came back today, causing MRSA to grow.  She's fine.  They put bacitracin ointment near the navel.  That's pretty much cured.  However, today they noticed a small blister on his left temporal area.  They called the private doctor.  They directly called the infectious disease doctor here and asked him to come to the hospital.  Mom says she has also been diagnosed with MRSA on her butt and is on some medications. transcription: SUBJECTIVE: The patient is a 20-year-old Caucasian male admitted to the emergency department of ABCD Hospital for evaluation of hydrocarbon aspiration.  The patient ingested “tiki oil” (kerosene, liquid paraffin, lemongrass oil) approximately two days before admission.  He then developed progressive symptoms of dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis with nausea and vomiting.  He was seen in the ABCD hospital emergency room, presenting a toxic appearance with an abnormal chest x-ray highlighting bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, more prominent on the right.  He had a temperature of 38.3 with a tachycardia near 130. The white count was 59,300 with a marked shift to the left.  Arterial blood gases showed a pH of 7.48, pO2 of 79, and pCO2 of 35. He was admitted for further medical management., MEDICAL HISTORY:, Aplastic crisis in childhood requiring splenectomy and cholecystectomy at age 9., DRUG ALLERGIES:, NONE KNOWN., CURRENT MEDICATION: , None., FAMILY HISTORY: , Non-contributory., SOCIAL HISTORY: Patient works at a local Christmas tree farm.  He smokes approximately one pack of cigarettes per day.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION:,Examination of ten systems significant for nausea, vomiting, fever, hemoptysis, and pleuritic chest pain.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: A toxic-appearing 20-year-old Caucasian male in mild respiratory distress.,VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure 122/74, pulse 130 and regular, respirations 24, temperature 38.3, and oxygen saturation 93%.,SKIN: No rashes, petechiae or bruising.,HEENT: Within normal limits.  The pupils are also round and responsive to light and accommodation.  Clean ears.  Throat clean. NECK: Supple without thyromegaly.  The lymph nodes are not palpable.,CHEST: Decreased breathing sounds bilaterally, more prominent on the right, at the right base.,HEART: No murmur or galloping rhythm.,ABDOMEN: Slight direct diffuse tenderness without rebound.  No detectable masses, pulsations or organomegaly. EXTREMITIES: No edema.  Pulses are equal and full bilaterally.,NEUROLOGICAL: Nonfocal.,DATABASE:, Chest x-ray, bilateral lower lobe pneumonia, larger on right.  ECG, sinus tachycardia, rate 130, normal intervals, no ST changes.  Arterial blood gas on 2 L of oxygen, pH 7.48, pO2 79 and pCO2 35. BLOOD STUDIES:, hematocrit is 43, white blood cells 59,300 with a shift to the left and platelet count 394,000. Sodium is 130, potassium 3.8, chloride is 97, bicarbonate 24, BUN 14, creatinine 0.8, random blood sugar 147, and calcium 9.4.,PRINT,1.  Hydrocarbon suction.,2.  Bilateral pneumonia with secondary aspiration pneumonia.,3.  Asplenic patient.,PLAN,1.  Intensive care monitoring.,2.  O2 protocol.,3.  Hydration.,4.  Antiemetic therapy.,5.  Parenteral antibiotics.,6.  Prophylactic proton pump inhibitors.,Patient will require intensive care monitoring and pulmonary medicine evaluation pending clinical evolution.,

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , Left hip fracture.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a pleasant 53-year-old woman with a known history of sciatica, apparently presented to the emergency room due to severe pain in the left lower extremity and unable to bear weight.  The history was obtained from the patient.  According to her history, she reported that she had back pain and left leg pain for 4 weeks.  She uses a walker to get around due to debilitating pain in her left thigh and lower back.  She was seen by her GP and was due for an MRI yesterday.  However, she was walking and her right foot got stuck in some sort of mat, causing excessive weight to be placed on her left lower extremity to prevent her from falling.  Since then, she has been unable to move around.  The patient called for help and was transported to the emergency room.  She denied any story of falling.  She reported that she had gone the wrong way, which made the pain worse.  She complains of severe pain in her lower limbs and back pain.  Denies any tingling or numbness.  Denies any neurological symptoms.  Denies any bowel or bladder incontinence. X-rays were obtained, notable for a left hip fracture.  An orthopedic consultation was requested for further evaluation and management.  Upon further interview with the patient, it is noted that she has a history of malignant melanoma, diagnosed approximately 4 to 5 years ago.  She underwent surgery at that time and subsequently she was found to have spread to the lymphatic system and lymph nodes for which she underwent surgery in 3/2008.,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,Sciatica and melanoma.,SURGICAL HISTORY: ,As noted above, surgery for melanoma and hysterectomy.,ALLERGIES: NONE.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Denies any tobacco or alcohol. to use.  She is divorced and has 2 children.  She lives with her son.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: The patient is well developed, well nourished and presents with mild distress secondary to the left lower limb and back pain.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Examination of the left lower limb, there is presence of apparent shortening and external rotation deformity.  Tenderness to palpation is present.  Leg rolling is positive for severe pain in the left proximal hip.  Further examination of the spine is incomplete due to severe leg pain.  She is unable to lift her right leg.  EHL/EDL 5/5.  More than 2 pulses are present distally.  The calf is gentle and not sensitive.  Homan's sign is negative.  Light touch sensation is intact.,IMAGING: The AP view of the hip is reviewed.  Only one limited view is obtained.  This is a poor quality x-ray with lots of soft tissue shadows.  This radiograph is significant in cases of basicervical fracture of the femoral neck.  The lesser trochanter is intact.  This is a high intertrochanteric/basicervical fracture.  There is a lytic lesion around the femoral neck, which is not well demarcated on this particular radiograph.  We need to order repeat x-rays including the AP pelvis, femur and knee.,LABORATORY:,were examined.,ASSESSMENT:,The patient is a 53 year old female with a probable pathologic fracture of the left proximal femur.,DISCUSSION AND PLAN:,The nature and progress of the diagnosis were discussed with the patient.  Based on his presentation with no history of falls or obvious trauma and history of malignant melanoma, it appears to be a pathologic fracture of the left proximal hip.  At this time, I would recommend getting a bone scan and repeat x-rays, which will include the AP pelvis, femur, hip and knee.  She denies any pain elsewhere.  She does have a history of back pain and sciatica, but at present it appears to be a metastatic bone lesion with pathologic fracture.  I discussed the case with Dr. X and recommended an oncology consultation. With the above fracture and presentation, she requires a left hip hemiarthroplasty versus a calcar hemiarthroplasty, cemented type.  Indication, risk and being

transcription: HISTORY:, The patient is a 4 day old infant transferred here due to hyperbilirubinemia and some hypoxia.  The mother states that she took the child to the clinic this morning because the child had a yellow appearance and had a bilirubin level of 23 mg%.  The patient was then sent to the hospital where she had lab tests and was found to be hypoxic, but her oxygen had minimal supplemental oxygen.  She was also noted to be breathing periodically.  The patient is breast and bottle fed and is eating well.  There was no diarrhea or vomiting.  Empty well.  The bowels were regular.,According to the referral institution's report, because the patient had periodic breathing and was hypoxic, she was thought to be septic and she received a dose of IM ampicillin.,The patient was born at 37 weeks' gestation to a gravida 3, para 3 by repeat cesarean section.  Birth weight was 8 pounds 6 ounces and the mother's prenatal assessment was normal except for placenta previa.  The patient's mother apparently went into labor and then underwent a cesarean section.,FAMILY HISTORY: ,Positive for asthma and diabetes and there is no exposure to second-hand smoke.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,VITAL SIGNS: The patient has a rectal temperature of 36.8, pulse rate of 148 per minute, respirations of 50 per minute, oxygen saturation of 96 on room air, but dropped to 90 and the patient received 1 liter by nasal cannula. The color is pink and jaundice is also noted, scleral and cutaneous., HEENT: Normal., NECK: Soft., CHEST: Clear., HEART: Regular with a soft murmur 3/6.  The femorals are clearly palpable.  Filling of the hood is immediate.,ABDOMEN: A soft, small and reducible umbilical hernia is noted.,EXTERNAL GENITALS: Those of a female child.,SKIN: Icteric color.  Nonspecific rash on the body, which is sparse.  The patient has a cephalhematoma hematoma approximately 6 cm above the left occipitoparietal area.,EXTREMITIES: The patient moves all extremities well.  Has normal tone and good suction.,EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT COURSE:,Parents were told that I would repeat the labs and also catheterize the urine samples.  The parents were informed that the child had a murmur.  I spoke to Dr.  The case has been discussed with Dr. Y and he will admit this child for hyperbilirubinemia. CBC performed showed white count 15,700, hemoglobin 18g%, hematocrit 50.6%, platelets 245,000, 10 bands, 44 segs, 34 lymphs and 8 monos.  Chemical analyzes performed showed sodium 142 mEq/L, potassium 4.5 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, CO2 28 mmol/L, glucose 75 mg%, BUN 8 mg%, creatinine 0.7 mg% and calcium 8.0 mg%.  Total bilirubin was 25.4 mg, all unconjugated.  CRP was 0.3 mg%.  A blood culture was performed.  The catheterized urine sample was normal.  Parents were constantly kept informed of what was happening and the need for admission.  Phototherapy was instituted in the emergency room almost after the baby arrived in the emergency room.,PRINT:,Hyperbilirubinemia and heart murmur.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:,Regarded as breast milk, jaundice, ABO incompatibility, galactosemia and ventricular septal defect.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is an 88-year-old white female, ambulant at home with a walker, who presented to the emergency department this morning after an accidental fall at home.  The patient states that she was on the ladder on Saturday and came down after the ladder.  I felt some pain in my left hip.  She then fell, injuring her left shoulder.  It is unclear how long she was on the ground.  She was taken by EMS to the hospital where x-rays revealed she had a left proximal humerus fracture and a non-displaced left hip fracture.  An orthopedist was consulted.  Given the nature of the injury and unclear events, a thorough workup was performed, including a CT scan of the head and a CT scan of the abdomen, which identified no signs of intracranial injury and only kidney stones.  She is currently complaining of pain in her left shoulder.  She states that she also experiences hip pain when moving her leg.  She denies any numbness or paresthesia.  She says that before that, she was relatively active in her household.  She takes care of her daughter who is a mess.  The patient denies any other injuries.  Denies back pain.,PREVIOUS MEDICAL HISTORY:, Extensive, including coronary artery disease, peripheral vascular disease, post-MI status, history of COPD, diverticular disease, irritable bowel syndrome, GERD, PMR, depressive disorder, and hypertension.,PREVIOUS SURGICAL HISTORY:, Includes repair of intertrochanteric fracture of the right femur.,ALLERGIES,1.  PENICILLIN.,2.  SULPHA.,3.  ACE INHIBITOR.,CURRENT DRUGS,1.  Lipitor 20 mg qd.2.  Metoprolol 25 mg twice daily,3.  Plavix 75 mg once a day.,4.  Aspirin 325 mg.,5.  Combivent Aerosol two puffs twice a day.,6.  Protonix 40 mg once daily,7.  Fosamax 70 mg per week.,8.  Multivitamins including calcium and vitamin D.,9.  Hydrocortisone.,10.  Nitroglycerin.,11.  Citalopram 20 mg q.d.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, She denies alcohol or tobacco use.  She takes care of her daughter, who is widowed and lives at home.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Not available.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:, The patient is hard of hearing.  She also has vision problems.  Denies headache syndrome.  Currently he denies chest pain or shortness of breath.  She denies having abdominal pain.  Currently, she is experiencing pain in her left hip and left shoulder.  No urinary frequency or dysuria.  No skin lesions.  She has had swelling in both lower limbs for several weeks.  She denies endocrinopathies.  Psychiatric problems include chronic depression.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: The patient is alert and responsive.,EXTREMITIES: In the left upper extremity, there is moderate swelling and bruising in the brachial compartment.  It is diffusely painful over the proximal humerus.  She is unable to actively raise her arm due to the pain.  The neurovascular examination of the left upper limb is otherwise intact with a 1+ radial pulse.  She presents with chronic degenerative changes in the MP and IP joints of both hands.  Lower left extremity, the thigh compartment is flexible.  She has pain with tenderness when rolling the log at the greater trochanter.  The patient feels pain with any attempt to flex the hip, passively or actively.  Knee range of motion is between 5 and 60 degrees with no specific point tenderness, no joint effusion, and an intact extensive mechanism.  She presents 2 to 3+ bilateral pretibial and pedal edemas.  The patient has a weak motor response in the left lower limb.  She has a dorsal pulse of 1+.  His sensory examination is intact plantarly and dorsally on the foot.  There is no evidence of dislocation.  There was a series of AP pelvis as well as left hip, which identified a type 1, non-displaced femoral neck fracture with valgus impaction.  There is also evidence of severe degenerative disc disease with degenerative scoliosis of the LS spine.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Stomach pain for 2 weeks., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 45-year-old Mexican man with no significant medical history who presents to the emergency department with complaints of mid-epigastric and right upper quadrant abdominal pain for the past 14 days. The pain was initially crampy and burning and was relieved by food. He also reports that this was initially associated with a sour taste in the mouth. He went to his GP who prescribed 400 mg cimetidine qhs x 5 days; however, this did not relieve his symptoms. In fact, the pain has worsened to the point where it now radiates to the back, but its duration waxes and wanes. It is relieved when standing and walking and exacerbated when lying on the back. He reports a decreased appetite associated with a weight loss of 4 pounds over the past 2 weeks. He has nausea with a single episode of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting on the day of admission. He reports a 2-week history of subjective fever and sweating. He denies any diarrhea, constipation, dysuria, melena or hematochezia. Her last bowel movements were on the morning of admission and were normal. transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: , Chest pain and fever. , HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , This 48-year-old married white woman presents to the emergency department after two days of increasing fever with a recent diagnosis of urinary tract infection during outpatient treatment with nitrofurantoin.  The patient noted that she had started feeling unwell earlier on the day of admission and had an episode of substernal chest discomfort associated with nausea, dizziness, and sweating.  The patient has a medical history of diabetes and hypertension.  In addition, the patient complained of some discomfort in her neck and head for which she underwent a lumbar puncture in the emergency room; it was normal, the causes turned out to be normal too.  Patient denies nosebleeds, visual changes, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or changes in bowel habits.  She did not present any musculoskeletal or neurological deficit.  She denies any rash or skin lesions.,MEDICAL HISTORY: Hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, particularly high triglycerides with a slightly elevated LDL of 81 with a new LDL standard of 74, diabetics with a poor family history of cardiovascular disease like this patient and postmenopausal hot flashes. oophorectomy.,FAMILY HISTORY: ,Positive for coronary artery disease in her father and brother in his 40s.,SOCIAL HISTORY: ,She is married and does not smoke, drink or have ever drank.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,On admission, temperature 99.4 degrees F., blood pressure 137/60, pulse 90 and regular without ectopy, respiratory rate 20 without unusual respiratory effort. No distension of the neck veins is noted.  No noise is heard.  The chest is clear on percussion and auscultation.  The heart has a regular rhythm, without murmurs, friction or gallops. Chest pain of unclear etiology, excluding myocardial infarction.,3.  Neck and back pain of unclear etiology with negative lumbar puncture.,4.  Hypertension.,5.  Type II diabetes, not treated with insulin.,6.  Hyperlipidemia treated with TriCor but not with statins.,7.  Arthritis.,ADDITIONAL LABORATORY STUDIES:, B-natriuretic peptide was 26. Urine smear and culture negative at 24 and 48 hours.  Chest x-ray was negative.  Lipid panel - triglycerides 249, VLDL 49, HDL 33, LDL 81.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,The patient was placed on home treatment. transcription: ADMIT DIAGNOSIS:,Cerebrovascular accident (CVA).,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:,The patient is a 56-year-old man with a significant past medical history of nasopharyngeal cancer after radiation therapy to the pharynx and neck in 1991 who presents to the emergency department after waking up at 2:30 a.m. this morning with difficulty swallowing, difficulty breathing, and left-sided numbness and weakness.  This happened at 2:30 in the morning. His wife said he also had difficulty speaking, but gradually the symptoms disappeared, but he still complained of a headache and at that time he was brought to the emergency room.  He arrived at the emergency room here via a private ambulance at 6:30 in the morning.  At the first evaluation, he did present with left-sided weakness and complained of headaches.  He underwent tests, including a CT scan, which came back negative and his symptoms slowly began to resolve.  He was initially admitted, placed on Plavix and aspirin.  However, a few hours later his symptoms returned and he had increasing weakness in his left arm and left leg as well as slurred speech.  A repeat CT scan was reported to be negative and he was subsequently heparinized and admitted.  He also underwent an ultrasound, carotid ultrasound and lab work in the emergency room.  The wife is at the bedside and denies having had any other symptoms previously.  He denied any chest pain or palpitations.  She reports that he is taking a Z-Pak, received a cortisone shot and a decongestant from Dr. ABC on Saturday due to congestion and has improved.,ALLERGIES:,He has no known drug allergies.,CURRENT MEDICATIONS:,1.  Multivitamin.,2.  Ibuprofen p.r.n.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Nasopharyngeal cancer.  Occurred in 1991. Post XRT status of the nasopharyngeal region and its neck due to spread to lymph nodes.,2.  Lumbar disc disease.,3.  Status after discectomy.,4.  Chronic neck pain secondary to XRT.,5.  History of thalassemia.,6.  Chronic dizziness since his XRT in 1991.,SURGICAL HISTORY: ,Lumbar discectomy, about 7-8 years ago, otherwise negative.,SOCIAL HISTORY: ,He is a non-smoker.  He drinks a beer from time to time. He has significant left upper extremity weakness of approximately 3-4/5, left lower extremity weakness is approximately 2-3/5 on the left.  The handle is about 4/5 on the left, the right side is 5/5.,LABORATORY DATA:,His initial blood test, PT was 11 and PTT was 27. CBC is within normal limits except hemoglobin 12.9 and hematocrit 39.1. transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: , Increased work of breathing., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a 2 month old girl with a 9 day history of illness.  According to mom's report, the illness started 9 days ago with a dry cough.  The patient was eating normally until about three days ago.  Mom used a spray at night, which she said helped.  The patient's cough gradually worsened and three days ago the patient had a cough that was getting significantly worse.  At this time, the patient also had significant and increasing congestion.  Two days ago, the patient was taken to the primary care physician's office and the patient was given 2 puffs of Xopenex every 4-6 hours for home treatment, but according to the mother's report, this did not improve the patient's symptoms.  On Wednesday evening, the patient's congestion and work of breathing increased and the patient was gagging after feedings.  The patient was brought to the Children's Hospital emergency room and was subsequently evaluated.  A chest x-ray was performed and found to be normal.  The patient's saturations were found to be normal and the patient was discharged home.  Last night the patient had several episodes of vomiting after feeding with cough and today there was a decrease in his activity.  The patient had a temperature of 101 Wednesday evening, but did not have a true fever.  The patient experienced a slight decrease in urine output today and secondary to the persistent increase in work of breathing, cough, and posttussive vomiting, the patient was brought to Children's Hospital for reevaluation. above., IMMUNIZATIONS:, None., MEDICAL HISTORY:, No hospitalizations.  No surgery.,BIRTH HISTORY: The patient was born to a G8, P2, A6 mother through normal spontaneous vaginal delivery.  Birth weight 6 pounds 12 ounces.  Mom said she had a uterine infection during her pregnancy and at the time of delivery, but the patient was only in the hospital for 24 hours with her mother after giving birth.  The patient was full term and the mother was found to have gestational diabetes which was controlled by diet during pregnancy.,FAMILY HISTORY: Brother, mother and father all suffer from asthma. He denies hematochezia, hematemesis or melena. He denies any frank abdominal pain or fever. ,MEDICAL HISTORY:,As above. Also, hypertension. ,ALLERGIES:, “Fleet enema.” ,MEDICATIONS:, Accupril and vitamins. ,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,SYSTEMIC: Patient denies fever or chills.,HEENT: Patient denies blurred vision, headache, or change in hearing.,NECK: Patient denies dysphagia, dysphonia, or neck pain.,RESPIRATORY: Patient denies shortness of breath, cough, or hemoptysis.,CARDIAC: Patient denies history of arrhythmia, swelling of extremities. palpitations or chest pain.,GASTROINTESTINAL: see above.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Patient denies arthritis, arthralgia, or joint swelling.,NEUROLOGICAL: Patient denies balance difficulties, numbness, or paralysis.,GENITOURINARY: Patient denies dysuria, flank pain, or hematuria.,EXAMINATION PHYSICS: VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure. 183/108, pulse 76, respirations 16, temperature 98.7. ,HEENT: The cranial nerves are generally intact. No whispers. ,ABDOMEN: Soft, not tender, not distended. Bowel sounds are diminished and high-pitched. There is a large midline laparotomy scar with staples still in place. There is no evidence of wound infection. Examination of the colostomy port reveals no obvious fecal impactions or sites of obstruction. There is no evidence of infection. The mucosa appears normal. There is a small amount of non-bloody stool in the colostomy bag. No mass or noise is noted. ,EXTREMITIES: There is no cyanosis, clubbing, or edema. Pulses are 2+ and equal bilaterally. ,NEUROLOGICAL: The patient is alert and awake with no focal motor or sensory deficits noted. ,MEDICAL DECISION MAKING: Colostomy dysfunction may represent impaction; however, I did not appreciate this during the physical examination. There may also be a proximal adhesion or impaction that I cannot reach, which can cause bowel obstruction, shunt failure, nausea, and ultimately vomiting. An abdominal series was obtained, which confirmed this possibility by demonstrating air and fluid levels and a dilated bowel. ,The CBC showed a WBC of 9.4 with a normal differential. The hematocrit is 42.6. I interpret this as normal. Amylase is currently on hold. ,I have discussed this case with Dr. S, the patient's surgeon, who agrees that there is a possibility of bowel obstruction and that the patient should be admitted for observation. Due to the patient's insurance status, he will actually be admitted to Dr. D for observation. I discussed the case with Dr. P, who is Dr. D's on-call physician. Both Dr. S and Dr. P have been informed of the patient's condition and are familiar with his situation. ,FINAL IMPRESSION:, Intestinal obstruction, condition after colostomy. ,LAYOUT:, Admission for observation. The patient's condition is good. He is hemodynamically stable.

transcription: MAIN COMPLAINT:  The patient was found by the 8-year-old sister in the bathroom.  He was lying on his side and crying and moaning.  The brother or sister went to tell the parents.  The parents rushed to the bathroom, they found him crying and he was not moving the left side of his body.  He was initially alert, but his alertness decreased.  They decided to take him to the hospital emergency room, where a head scan was performed, which showed a 4 x 4 x 2.5 cm bleed.  The emergency doctor called our emergency department and I decided to call the neurosurgery department, Mr. X, the physician assistant, who is on call for the neurosurgery services.  Collectively, they made arrangements with the ICU staff to have the child transported to our emergency room.  For a quick stop, I have an MRI and then I'm admitted to intensive care.  The story was taken away from the parents.  He had a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease, otherwise a healthy child.,MEDICATIONS:, None.,ALLERGIES:, No known drug allergies.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,He has only had tympanostomy tubes.,FAMILY MEDICAL HISTORY:,Unremarkable.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: He has was brought by our transport team.  On the way, he wasn't as alert as he used to be.  He was always oriented.  He had to be stimulated by sternal rub to wake up, and the saturation dropped to the 80s, and he was put on a nasal cannula, and code 3 was initiated, and he was rushed to our emergency room.  When I saw him, he was lethargic, but excited.  He could recognize where he was and he could also recognize his parents well.,HEENT: The pupils are reactive to direct and indirect light of 4 mm.  No signs of trauma are visible on the head.  Throat is clear.,LUNGS: Clear to auscultation.,HEART: Regular rate and rhythm.,ABDOMEN: Soft.,NEUROLOGICAL: He has left sided weakness, but his cranial nerves II to XII are grossly intact.,EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT COURSE: In the ER, by the time I saw him, Dr. Y and Dr. Z were from the ICU and the anesthesia department also arrived and they assessed the patient with me. and pretty much they took care of the patient.  They decided to give him a dose of IV mannitol.  I ordered his laboratories, typed and cross-referenced.  The white blood cell count is 15.6 and the hemoglobin is 12.8.  PT/PTT was ordered due to the bleeding, observed intracerebral.  They were 13.1 and 24.5 respectively.  Blood gas, I-STAT pH 7.36, pCO2 is 51. This was a venous sample.  The intensive care services decided to carry out a rapid sequence intubation.  This was done in our emergency room by Dr. Y and Dr. Z. The patient was sent to MRI, and from where he was going to be admitted to the ICU in critical condition.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:, Arteriovenous malformation, stroke, traumatic injury.,PRINT:, Intracerebral hemorrhage of uncertain etiology to be determined during hospitalization.,TIME SPENT:, I spent 30 minutes in intensive care with the patient. excluding all procedures.,

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION: , Possibility of free air under the diaphragm., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: , The patient is a 77-year-old woman who cannot give any information.  She was sedated with Ativan and arrived at the emergency room dazed and unable to tell her story.  On a chest x-ray for what appeared to be shortness of breath, she was found to have what was thought to be free air under her right diaphragm.,MEDICAL HISTORY: ,Significant for alcohol abuse.  Unable to really gather any other information because she is so obsessed.,SURGICAL HISTORY:,Looking at the medical records, she has had an appendectomy, a right hip fracture from a fall in 2005, and TAH/BSO.,DRUGS:,Unable to assess.,ALLERGIES:,UNABLE TO ASSESS.,HISTORY SOCIAL:,Significant history of alcohol abuse, according to emergency physician, who sees her regularly.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION: ,Unobtainable.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,VITAL SIGNS: Temp 98.3, heart rate 82, respiratory rate 24 and blood pressure 141/70.,GENERAL: She is a very obtunded woman who, when awake, is not not able to provide any useful information.,HEENT: atraumatic.,NECK: soft and supple.,LUNGS: bilaterally diminished.,HEART: regular.,ABDOMEN: soft and with deep palpation, unable to rouse patient, unable to elicit any tenderness.,LABORATORY STUDIES:, Show normal white blood cell count without shift.  AST elevated 138, with normal ALT 38. Alkaline phosphatase 96, bilirubin 0.8.  Sodium is 107, with chloride 68 and potassium 2.8. Chest x-ray shows possibility of free air; therefore, a CT scan was obtained due to the patient's physical examination, which showed no signs of intra-abdominal pathology.  The etiology of the air under the diaphragm is actually colic air antero-superior to the dome of the diaphragm, close to the dome of the liver.,ASSESSMENT: ,No intra-abdominal pathology.,PLAN:,Admit her to the medical department for treatment of her hyponatremia.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 19-year-old male with sickle cell anemia.  He presented alone to the emergency room with back pain for 3 days.  He does not take any medication.  He lives with a roommate.  Appetite is diminished.  No diarrhea or vomiting.  Empty well.  The bowels have been regular.  Denies any abdominal pain.  He complains of mild headaches, but his main concern is back pain that extends from above the lower T-spine to the lumbosacral spine.  The patient is unsure about his vaccinations.  The patient does indeed have sickle cell disease and the hemoglobin is being monitored in the hematology clinic.,ALLERGIES: , THE PATIENT IS ALLERGIC TO TYLENOL WITH CODEINE, but he states that he can get morphine with Benadryl., MEDICATIONS: , He was previously taking folic acid.  None at present.,SURGICAL HISTORY: , He has not had any surgery in the past.,FAMILY HISTORY: , Positive for diabetes, hypertension and cancer.,SOCIAL HISTORY: , He denies smoking or drug use.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: , VITAL SIGNS: On examination, the patient has a tympanic temperature of 37 degrees, the pulse was recorded at 37 per minute. but later it was noted to be 66 per minute, respiratory rate is 24 per minute and blood pressure is 149/66, blood pressure is 132/72.,GENERAL: He is alert, speaks in full sentences, does not appear to be in distress.,HEENT: Normal.,NECK: Soft.,CHEST: Clear.,HEART: Regular.,ABDOMEN: Soft.  He has pain in his mid and lower spine.,SKIN: Color is normal.,EXTREMITIES: He moves all extremities well.,NEUROLOGICAL: Age appropriate.,ER COURSE: ,The patient was told that I would do lab work and give him IV fluids.  Additionally, he will receive a combination of morphine and Benadryl.  The patient received one liter of NS over one hour and then was maintained on D5 semi-normal saline at 125 per hour.  The CBC performed showed 4,300 white blood cells, hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39.9%, platelets 162,000, segments 65.9, lymphs 27, monos 3.4.  Chemistry tests performed were essentially normal except for a total bilirubin of 1.6 mg/dL, which were all indirect.  The patient was first given morphine and diphenhydramine at 6:40 p.m. and this was repeated at 8 p.m.  He received 5 mg of morphine and 25 mg of Benadryl.  I then spoke to Dr. X and it was decided to admit the patient. The patient initially stated that he wanted to be observed in the emergency room and given painkillers and fluids and wanted to go home in the morning.  He said he had a job interview in the morning.  The residents' service came to evaluate it.  The residency department then spoke with Dr. X and it was decided to admit him to the Hematology department for pain control and IV hydration.  He is due to be transferred to P.O. medications around 4 a.m. and I hope he can be discharged in time for his interview tomorrow.,PRINT:,Sickle cell crisis.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:,Veno-occlusive crisis and discitis.

transcription: PRESENTATION:,A 16-year-old male presents to the emergency department (ED) with rectal bleeding and pain with defecation.,HISTORY:,A 16-year-old African American male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of rectal bleeding and pain with defecation.  The patient states that he was doing well until approximately three days before his presentation, when he began experiencing some pain while defecating.  The next day, he noted increasing pain and first noticed blood on the surface of his stool.  The pain worsened the next day with increased bleeding as well as mucopurulent anal discharge.  The patient denies any history of rectal bleeding or pain.  He also denies any previous sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) and states that he was tested for HIV eight months ago and was negative.  The patient states that he has not felt well for a week.  He claims to have felt “feverish” on several occasions but did not take his temperature.  He also complained of some abdominal pain accompanied by nausea and diarrhea as well as generalized myalgia and fatigue.  He thinks he lost a few pounds, but he hasn't weighed himself to determine the exact amount of weight loss. The patient states that he has been sexually active since the age of 13. He admits to having had eight previous partners and says he "usually" uses a condom.  Upon further questioning, the patient states that of his eight partners, three were women and five were men.  His most recent sexual partner was a 38-year-old man with whom he had been living for six months.  He states that he has been tested for STDs before, but states that he only gave urine and blood for testing.  He is unaware of his partner's HIV status but assumes his partner is not infected because he looks healthy.  The patient also acknowledges an episode of sexual abuse at the age of 8 by a family friend.  Because the man was a member of the family church, the patient never felt comfortable telling any of the adults in his life.  He is very concerned about disclosing his sexual behavior to his family, as their family has expressed very negative comments about men who have sex with men.  He accesses emergency care unaccompanied by an adult.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:, Thin but non-toxic young man with obvious discomfort.,Pulse = 105,RR = 23,BP = 120/62,HEENT: Multiple areas of white plaque-like material on the oral mucosa.,Neck: Multiple anterior/posterior cervical nodes in the anterior and posterior chains - 1-2 cm. diameter.,Lungs: clear to auscultation.,Cardiac: precordium quiet.,Nl S1/S2 with a II/VI systolic murmur. ,Abdomen: soft without hepatosplenomegaly.,GU: male Tanner V without external penile lesions.,Lymph: axillary lymph nodes 2 to 3 cm bilaterally.,Epitrochlear lymph nodes 1 to 2 cm.,Multiple inguinal nodes 1 to 2 cm.,Rectal: extremely painful digital examination.,+ macroscopic blood and mucous membranes.,ASSESSMENT IN LABORATORY:,Hbg = 12.5 g/dL, Hct = 32%, WBC = 3.9 tu/µL, Platelets = 120,000 tu/µL, 76% neutrophils, 19% lymphocytes, 1% eosinophils, 4% monocytes, ALT = 82 U/L, AST = 90 U/L, Sedimentation rate erythrocyte = 90, Electrolytes = normal, Gram stain of anus swab: numerous leukocytes, DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: , This patient presents with acute rectal pain with bleeding and anal discharge.  The patient also has some constitutional symptoms, including fever, fatigue, abdominal discomfort, and lymphadenopathy on physical examination.  The following are part of the differential diagnosis: Acute Proctitis and Proctocolitis., ACUTE HIV SEROCONVERSION:, This subject is sexually active and reports inconsistent condom use.  Gastrointestinal symptoms have recently been frequently reported in patients with a history of HIV seroconversion.  Rectal symptoms of bleeding and pain are not common with HIV and an alternative diagnosis would be necessary.,PERIRECTAL ABSCESS:,A patient with a history of receptive anal intercourse is at risk of developing a perirectal abscess.

transcription: HISTORY AND PHYSICS: The patient is a 13-year-old boy who has a history of Shone complex and presents with complete heart block.  He is on a pacemaker.  He had a coarctation of the aorta and it was repaired when he was a baby.  He has been followed in our cardiology clinic here and is doing well.  However, last night he was sleeping and he states that he felt like he was having a dream, and there was thunder in the dream that woke him up.  He then felt his defibrillator go off and it continued and he felt like his heart rate was not normal.  So his father put him in the car and transported him here.  It has been reviewed here.  He had scar tissue at one point when the internal stimulator wasn't working properly and he had to have it replaced.  It was 2 a.m. when he woke up and, again, he was brought here by private vehicle.  He was fine before going to bed.  No cough, cold, runny nose, fever.  No trauma was noted.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Shone complex, pacemaker dependent.,MEDICATIONS:, He is not currently taking any medications.,ALLERGIES:, He has no allergies.,IMMUNIZATIONS:, Up to date.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, He lives with his parents.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Negative.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEM:, Twelve questions, all negative unless otherwise noted. above.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,GENERAL: This is an awake and alert male, who appears to be in mild distress.,HEENT: Pupils are equal, round and responsive to light.  Extraocular movements are intact.  His TMs are clear.  His nostrils are clear.  The mucous membranes are pink and moist.  The throat is clear. NECK: Supple without lymphadenopathy or masses.  Trachea is midline.,LUNGS: clear.,HEART: shows bradycardia at 53. Has good distal pulses.,ABDOMEN: soft, not tender.  Positive bowel sounds.  No guard, no bounce.  No rash is observed.,HOSPITAL COURSE:,Initial blood pressure is 164/90.  He was transferred to room 1. He was placed on a nasal cannula.  The pulse ox was 100%, which is normal.  We put it on a monitor.  We did an ECG; it doesn't seem to be picking up his pacemaker at the moment.  Shortly after the patient arrived, the Medtronic technician came to test his pacemaker.  The Medtronic representative informed me that the lead they had in place had been recalled because it was prone to microfractures, oversensing, and automatic defibrillation.  As instructed, he was moved to room 1, placed on a monitor, pulse ox.  An IV was placed.  A standard blood test was sent.  A chest x-ray was taken showing a normal heart size, the lead appeared to be in place.  There were no signs of pulmonary edema.  His pacemaker didn't seem to be picking up.  We placed him on a transthoracic tube.  However, it is difficult to get good placement with these due to the area where his pacemaker was placed.  The Medtronic technician first turned off his defibrillation mode and lowered his sensor.  However, we were unable to get our transthoracic pacemaker to capture his heart.  When the Medtronic representative turned off the pacemaker, the heart rate seemed to drop into the 40s. The patient appeared to be in pain.  We put it back at the rate of 60 at that point.  He still has sinus bradycardia, but no signs of extrasystoles.  No widening of his QRS complex.  I spoke with Cardiology.  The cardiology department arrived and assessed him at my bedside.  Again, we increased the transthoracic pacemaker, but it doesn't seem to be speeding up yet, and his heart rate continues with the Medtronic's internal pacemaker.  So, he and the ICU doctor on call, Dr. X, agreed to take this young man to the ICU. An hour after the presentation here, the ICU was ready for bed.  I accompanied the patient to the ICU.  He remained awake and alert.  At first, he complained of severe chest pain.  Once the defibrillator was turned off, he no longer had any pain.  He was transported to the pediatric PICU and delivered in stable condition. LABORATORY DATA: CBC was normal.  Chem-20 was also normal.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: A 14-day-old child was seen by a private physician due to a blister.  On Friday, she was found to have a small blister near her navel.  They went to their doctor on Saturday, the culture was drawn.  It came back today, causing MRSA to grow.  She's fine.  They put bacitracin ointment near the navel.  That's pretty much cured.  However, today they noticed a small blister on his left temporal area.  They called the private doctor.  They directly called the infectious disease doctor here and asked him to come to the hospital.  Mom says she has also been diagnosed with MRSA on her butt and is on some medications.  The child did not have a fever.  She has not been lethargic or irritable.  She eats well up to 2 ounces at each feeding.  Eat well and sleep well.  No other changes were noted., PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: She was born full term.  No complications.  At home with mom.  No hospitalizations, surgery, allergies.,DRUGS:, As indicated.,IMMUNIZATIONS:, Up to date.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Negative.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, No sick contacts.  No movement or change in living conditions.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION: ,Ten systems were queried, all were negative except as noted above.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,GENERAL: Female awake, alert, no acute distress at this time.,HEENT: Soft, flat fontanelle.  PERRLA.  EOMI.  The conjunctivas are clear.  TMS are clear.  The nostrils are clear.  Pink and moist mucous membranes.  Clear throat.  No oral lesions.,NECK: Supple.,LUNGS: Clear.,HEART: Regular rhythm and rhythm.  Normals S1, S2.  No murmuring.,ABDOMEN: Soft, not sensitive.  Positive bowel sounds.  No guard, no bounce.  No rash observed.,ENDS: Capillary refill is rapid.  Good distal pulses.,NEUROLOGICAL: Cranial nerves II to XII intact.  Strength 5/5 in all extremities.,SKIN: His navel appears completely clear.  There is no sign of erythema.  The area indicated by the parents, where the blister was, appears to be well healed.  There is no evidence of injury noted at this time.  On the left temple and just inside the hairline, there is a small blister.  It's not a pustule.  It is almost flat and there is little liquid underneath.  There is no surrounding erythema or tenderness.  I inspected the body, from head to toe.  No other areas of injury observed.,EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT COURSE: I spoke with Dr. He states that we should treat MRSA with Bactrim p.o. There were no signs of jaundice in this little girl.  Hibiclens and Bactroban.  I spoke with Dr. X's associate to call back after Dr.  A blood culture was done here to make sure she didn't have MRSA in her blood, which she doesn't appear to have clinically.  She was discharged in stable condition.,PRINT: ,Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection.,PLAN: ,MRSA Instructions were given as above and antibiotics were prescribed.  Follow up with their doctor.

transcription: HISTORY: Reason for follow-up in ICU today is acute anemia secondary to upper GI bleeding with melena with drop in hemoglobin from 11 to 8, post-transfusion status of 2 units of PRBC with EGD performed earlier today by Dr. productive, infection and shortness of breath.  Please see the transfer note dictated to the ICU yesterday detailing the need for an emergent transfer transfusion and EGD in this patient.  During the last 24 hours, the patient received 2 units of packed red blood cells and his hematocrit and hemoglobin returned to their baseline value of approximately 11, appropriate for the hemoglobin value.  He also underwent EGD earlier today with Dr.  Dr. X recommended increasing the doses of his proton pump inhibitor and avoiding NSAIDs in the future.  The patient now complains of still having problems with shortness of breath, wheezing, and productive cough, now producing yellow-brown sputum with increasing frequency, but he has had no further episodes of melena since his transfer to the intensive care unit.  He also complains of some laryngitis and pharyngitis, but denies any abdominal pain, nausea, or diarrhea.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION, VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure is 100/54, heart rate 80, and temperature 98.8.  Is and Os negative water balance of 1.4 liters in the last 24 hours.,GENERAL: This is a drowsy 68 year old man, who wakes up to voice, wakes up, seems to have a good appetite, has a continuous cough.  Pallor has improved.,EYE: Conjunctivas are now pink.,ENT: Oropharynx is clear.,CARDIOVASCULAR: Reveals distant heart tones with regular rhythm and frequency.,LUNGS: Have coarse breathing sounds with wheezing, rhonchi and slight crackles in the bases.,ABDOMEN: Soft and non-tender with no organomegaly appreciated.,EXTREMITIES: showed no clubbing, cyanosis or edema.  Capillary refill time is now normal at the fingertips.,NEUROLOGICAL: Cranial nerves II through at 8.6 and 24.7.  The platelets are stable.  Sodium is 134, Potassium 4.0, Chloride 101, Bicarb 26, BUN 19, Creatinine 1.0, Glucose 73, Calcium 8.4, INR 0.96, Iron 13%, Saturations 4%, TIBC 312, TSH 0.74, CEA high at 8.6, ferritin 27.5 and occult blood positive.  EGD, final results pending per Dr.  Melena secondary to ulcerative esophagitis.  We will continue to monitor the patient overnight to make sure there is no further bleeding.  If there are no further episodes of melena and the hemoglobin is stable or unchanged in the morning, the patient will be transferred to the medical floor for continued treatment of their chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation.,2.  Exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.  The patient is doing well, he is taking PO.  We will continue to give him his oral Omnicef ​​and azithromycin as well as his continued breathing treatments.  We will add guaifenesin and N-acetyl-cysteine ​​in the hope of mobilizing some of his secretions.  It seems to be improving.  His white count is normalized and I hope we can release him on oral antibiotics in the next 24-48 hours if there are no further complications.,3.  High CEA.  The patient will need an outpatient colonoscopy.  He refused it today.  We would like to encourage him to do so.  It should be noted that the patient, upon arrival, was following a bloodless protocol, but accepted the transfusion, under pressure.  Likewise, I hope that with proper counseling the patient will face problems.

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, Patient is a 26 year old gravida 2, para 1-0-0-1, at 28 1/7 weeks who presents to the emergency department with left lower quadrant pain, reports no bowel movements for two weeks as well as nausea and vomiting for the past 24 hours.  She states that she has also not urinated in the last 24 hours because of the pain.  She denies any fluid leakage, vaginal bleeding, or uterine contractions.  She reports good fetal movements.  She denies any fever, chills, or burning when urinating.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Positive for lower back pain only.  Her mother reports that she is eating without difficulty and that the current nausea and vomiting are much less than when she is not pregnant.  She keeps screaming because she asked for painkillers and how "it hurts".,MEDICAL HISTORY:,1.  Irritable bowel syndrome.,2.  Urinary tract infections tripled.  Patient is unsure whether pyelo is present or not.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, Denies.,ALLERGIES:, No known drug allergies.,DRUGS:, Phenergan and Zofran twice daily.  Macrobid questionable., GYN:, History of abnormal smears, group B within normal limits.  Denies any sexually transmitted diseases.,OB HISTORY: , G1 is a term of spontaneous vaginal delivery without complications, now 6 years old.  G2 is current. Upon review by Baptist Laboratory Systems, the patient had several positive urine drug tests and as of February 2008 had a urine drug test positive for benzodiazepines, barbiturates, opiates, and marijuana and as recently as 2005 for cocaine. air, 96 degrees Fahrenheit. Urinalysis that is supposed to be a catheterization sample shows a specific gravity of 1.024, greater than 88 ketones, many bacteria, but no white blood cells or nitrites.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,The patient is a 26 year old gravida 2, para 1-0-0-1 at 28-1 weeks with left lower quadrant pain and likely constipation.  I spoke with Dr.  Transfer plans are currently being developed.  This has been discussed with Dr. Y who is in agreement with the plan.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE: The patient is a 20-year-old Caucasian male admitted to the emergency department of ABCD Hospital for evaluation of hydrocarbon aspiration.  The patient ingested “tiki oil” (kerosene, liquid paraffin, lemongrass oil) approximately two days before admission.  He then developed progressive symptoms of dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis with nausea and vomiting.  He was seen in the ABCD hospital emergency room, presenting a toxic appearance with an abnormal chest x-ray highlighting bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, more prominent on the right.  He had a temperature of 38.3 with a tachycardia near 130. The white count was 59,300 with a marked shift to the left.  Arterial blood gases showed a pH of 7.48, pO2 of 79, and pCO2 of 35. He was admitted for further medical management., MEDICAL HISTORY:, Aplastic crisis in childhood requiring splenectomy and cholecystectomy at age 9., DRUG ALLERGIES:, NONE KNOWN., CURRENT MEDICATION: , None., FAMILY HISTORY: , Non-contributory., SOCIAL HISTORY: Patient works at a local Christmas tree farm.  He smokes approximately one pack of cigarettes per day.,SYSTEMS EXAMINATION:,Examination of ten systems significant for nausea, vomiting, fever, hemoptysis, and pleuritic chest pain.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION,GENERAL: A toxic-appearing 20-year-old Caucasian male in mild respiratory distress.,VITAL SIGNS: Blood pressure 122/74, pulse 130 and regular, respirations 24, temperature 38.3, and oxygen saturation 93%.,SKIN: No rashes, petechiae or bruising.,HEENT: Within normal limits.  The pupils are also round and responsive to light and accommodation.  Clean ears.  Throat clean. NECK: Supple without thyromegaly.  The lymph nodes are not palpable.,CHEST: Decreased breathing sounds bilaterally, more prominent on the right, at the right base.,HEART: No murmur or galloping rhythm.,ABDOMEN: Slight direct diffuse tenderness without rebound.  No detectable masses, pulsations or organomegaly. EXTREMITIES: No edema.  Pulses are equal and full bilaterally.,NEUROLOGICAL: Nonfocal.,DATABASE:, Chest x-ray, bilateral lower lobe pneumonia, larger on right.  ECG, sinus tachycardia, rate 130, normal intervals, no ST changes.  Arterial blood gas on 2 L of oxygen, pH 7.48, pO2 79 and pCO2 35. BLOOD STUDIES:, hematocrit is 43, white blood cells 59,300 with a shift to the left and platelet count 394,000. Sodium is 130, potassium 3.8, chloride is 97, bicarbonate 24, BUN 14, creatinine 0.8, random blood sugar 147, and calcium 9.4.,PRINT,1.  Hydrocarbon suction.,2.  Bilateral pneumonia with secondary aspiration pneumonia.,3.  Asplenic patient.,PLAN,1.  Intensive care monitoring.,2.  O2 protocol.,3.  Hydration.,4.  Antiemetic therapy.,5.  Parenteral antibiotics.,6.  Prophylactic proton pump inhibitors.,Patient will require intensive care monitoring and pulmonary medicine evaluation pending clinical evolution.,

transcription: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is an 85-year-old man who follows Dr. A as an outpatient. He has been known to us since his last admission.
