transcription: TITLE OF OPERATION: 1. Repair of total abnormal pulmonary venous connection.,2.  Ligation of the patent ductus arteriosus.,3.  Repair the secundum type atrial septal defect (autologous pericardial patch).,4.  Subtotal thymectomy.,5.  Insertion of a peritoneal dialysis catheter.,INDICATION FOR SURGERY: This neonate was diagnosed after birth with a total anomalous pulmonary venous connection.  After initial stabilization, she was transferred to the hospital for complete correction.,PREOPERATION DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Total abnormal pulmonary venous connection.,2.  Atrial communication.,3.  Patent ductus arteriosus.,4.  Operating weight less than 4 kilograms (3.2 kilograms).,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,CROSS CLAMP TIME: , 63 minutes.,CARDIOPULMONARY BYPASS TIME MONITOR: 35 minutes, deep hypothermic circulatory arrest time (4 plus 19) is equal to 23 minutes.  Low flow perfusion 32 minutes., RESULTS: Horizontal pulmonary venous confluence with the right upper and middle veins with two veins entering the confluence on the right and multiple entry sites for the left sided veins.  Large patulous anastomosis between the posterior surface of the left atrium and the anterior surface of the pulmonary venous confluence.  Unobstructed ascending vein ligated.  Persistence of the diminutive ductus arteriosus of the left atrium with posterior atrial septal defect with deficient lower margin.  At the end of the procedure, right ventricular pressure close to half of normal systemic sinus rhythm, good biventricular function by visual inspection.,PROCEDURE: After informed consent, the patient was brought to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  During induction of general endotracheal anesthesia and establishment of indwelling arterial and venous monitoring lines.  The patient was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner, from chin to groin.  A median sternotomy incision was made.  Dissection was carried out through the deepest planes until the sternum was incised and divided with an oscillating saw.  A subtotal thymectomy was performed.  Systemic heparinization was performed and the pericardium was penetrated and shaped to the cradle.  A small portion of the anterior pericardium was removed and fixed in glutaraldehyde for patch closure of the atrial septal defect segment during the procedure.  Purse strings were deployed over the ascending aorta on the right.  Auricular appendage.  The aorta was then cannulated with an 8-French aortic cannula and the right atrium with an 18-French Polystan right-angle cannula.  With an ACT greater than 400, a larger lung bypass was started with excellent cardiac decompression and the patent ductus arteriosus was ligated with a 2-0 silk tie.  Systemic cooling was started and the head was packed and iced and systemic steroids were administered.  During cooling, a traction suture was placed at the apex of the left ventricle.  After 25 minutes of cooling, the aorta was cross-clamped and the heart stopped by administration of 30 cubic centimeters/kilogram of cold blood cardioplegia delivered directly into the aortic root after cross-clamping of the aorta.  After successful cardioplegic arrest, a low flow perfusion period was started and a 10 French catheter was inserted into the right atrial appendage replacing the 18 French Polystan venous cannula.  The heart was then rotated to the right and the venous confluence was exposed.  It was incised and enlarged and a corresponding incision in the dorsal and posterior aspect of the left atrium was made.  The two openings were then anastomosed end-to-side with several interlocking sutures to avoid the purse-string effect with a 7-0 PDS suture running.  Once the anastomosis was complete, the heart was returned to the chest and the patient's blood volume was drained into the reservoir.  A right auriotomy was then performed during the period of circulatory arrest.  The atrial septal defect was very difficult to expose, but it was sealed

transcription: HISTORY: We had the pleasure of seeing the patient today in our pediatric rheumatology clinic.  He was sent here with a chief complaint of joint pain in several joints for a few months.  This is a 7-year-old white male with no history of systemic illness, who until 2 months ago was doing well and 2 months ago he began complaining of pain in his fingers, elbows, and neck.  Right now it's better and it's almost gone, but for several months he was having pain to the point where he was crying at one point.  He is not a complainer according to his mother and he is a very active child.  There is no history of illness or gastrointestinal problems.  He has allergy problems, especially seasonal ones, and he takes Claritin for that.  Other than that, he had no other problems.  He denies any swelling, except for the swelling mentioned by the doctor in his elbow.  There is no history of rash, no stomach pain, no diarrhea, no fever, no weight loss, no ulcers in the mouth except canker sores.  No lymphadenopathy, no eye problems and no urinary problems.,MEDICATIONS: ,His medications consist only of Motrin only when needed and Claritin currently for seasonal allergies and rhinitis.,ALLERGIES: ,He has no allergies to any medications.,BIRTH HISTORY: ,Pregnancy and delivery without complications.  He has no history of hospitalization or surgery.,FAMILY HISTORY: Positive for arthritis in his grandmother.  No history of pediatric arthritis.  There is a history of psoriasis in his father.,SOCIAL HISTORY: He lives with mom, dad, brother, sister and everyone is healthy.  They live in Easton.  They have 4 dogs, 3 cats, 3 mules and no deer.  At school, he is in the second year and he does physical education without any limitations.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature is 98.7, pulse is 96, respiratory rate is 24, height is 118.1 cm, weight is 22.1 kg and blood pressure is 61/44.,GENERAL: He is alert, active, in no way distressed, very cooperative.,HEENT: He does not have a rash on his face.  No lymphadenopathy.  The oral mucosa is clear.  No tonsillitis.  His ear canals are clear and his pupils respond to light and accommodation.,CHEST: Clear to auscultation.,HEART: Regular rhythm and no murmurs.,ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, no visceromegaly.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Has no limitations in any of his joints or active swelling today.  He also has no feeling in any of his joints.  Muscle strength is 5/5 in proximal muscles.,LABORATORY DATA: Includes arthritis panel.  He has normal uric acid, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 2, rheumatoid factor of 6, and negative antinuclear antibodies and C-reactive protein of 7.1.  His mother stated that this was done while he was experiencing symptoms.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,This patient may have had reactive arthritis.  He is seen frequently and the patient has a family history of psoriatic arthritis or psoriasis.  I don't see any issues at this time on his labs or physical exam.  This may have been linked to a recent episode of viral infection or any infection.  Mother has been informed of the finding and my recommendation is to observe him and if there is a recurrence of symptoms or persistence of swelling or limitation in any of his joints, I will be happy to see him again. If you have any questions about further evaluation and plan, please do not hesitate to contact us.

transcription: CONSULTATION INDICATION:, Increasing need for oxygen.,HISTORY:, The little boy, XYZ, is an infant of 29 3/7 weeks gestation.  His mother had a premature rupture of membranes on 12/20/08.  She then presented to the labor and delivery unit with flu-like symptoms.  The baby was then induced and delivered.  The mother had previously had premature babies.  This baby was doing well, and then we had a significant increase in ambient air oxygen requirements up to 85%.  He is now on 60% FiO2.,PHYSICAL FINDINGS,GENERAL: He appeared to be pink, well perfused and slightly jaundiced.,VITAL SIGNS: pulse 156, respiratory rate 56, sat 92% and blood pressure 59/28 mmHg.,SKIN: He was pink.,He was on a high frequency ventilator with good agitation.,His echocardiogram showed normal structure. anatomy.  He presents with signs of significant pulmonary hypertension.  A large ductus arteriosus was observed with a bidirectional shunt.  Foramen ovale shunting was also noted with bidirectional shunting.  The shunt for the canal and foramen ovale was equal left to right and right to left.,IMPRESSION: My impression is that little boy, XYZ, has significant pulmonary hypertension.  The best therapy for this is to continue administering oxygen.  If the clinical condition worsens, he may need nitric oxide.  Certainly, Indocin should not be used at this time.  He must have lower pulmonary artery pressures for this to be taken into account. Thank you so much for allowing me to be involved in baby XYZ's care.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION:, New seizure., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a 2 1/2 year old female with a history of known febrile seizures, who was placed on Keppra oral solution 150 mg twice daily. to help prevent febrile seizures.  Although this treatment has been very effective in controlling her febrile seizures, she now has occasional brief periods of pauses and staring, during which she becomes unresponsive, but does not lose her postural tone.  According to Dad the typical spell lasts between 10-15 seconds, Mom says 3-4 minutes which probably means somewhere between 30-40 seconds.  Mom noted that an episode had recently occurred outside a store and was associated with perioral cyanosis, but no seizure activity was noted.  There have been no recent changes in her Keppra dosage and she is currently only on 20 mg/kg per day, which is overall a low dose for her.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Born at 36 weeks gestation via cesarean delivery at 8 pounds 3 ounces.  She has a history of febrile seizures and parents have reported an abdominal migraine, but upon further questioning it appears to be more of a food intolerance issue.,SURGICAL HISTORY: ,She has not had any surgery.,FAMILY MEDICAL HISTORY: ,There is a strong history of epilepsy on the maternal side of the family, including the mother with nonconvulsive seizures during childhood and additional seizures in maternal great-grandmother and a maternal great-aunt.  There is no other significant neurological history on his father's side of the family.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Currently lives with his mother, father, and two siblings.  She is at home full-time and does not attend daycare.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: ,A clear review of 10 systems was performed and revealed no additional findings other than those noted in the current illness history.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,Vital signs: Weight was 15.6 kg.  She was afebrile.  The remainder of her vital signs were stable and within normal limits for her age according to the medical record.,General: She was awake, alert and oriented.  She was not in acute distress, only slightly confused when trying to place the EEG leads. HEENT: Showed a normocephalic and atraumatic head.  His conjunctivas were nonicteric and his sclerae were clear.  His eye movements were conjugate in nature.  His tongue and mucous membranes were moist.,Neck: Trachea appeared to be in the midline.,Chest: Clear on auscultation bilaterally with no crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi.,Cardiovascular: Presented normal sinus rhythm without murmurs.,Abdomen: Showed soft, nontender, and nondistended, with good bowel sounds.  There was no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly, or other masses noted on examination. Extremities: Showed IV placement in the right upper extremity with appropriate IV restraints.  There was no evidence of blunting, cyanosis or edema.  She had no functional deformity in any of her peripheral limbs. Neurological: Neurologically, her cranial nerves were generally intact.  His strength was good in bilateral upper and lower extremities with no distal to proximal variation.  His overall resting tone was normal.  Sensory examination was generally intact, if not slightly affected, in the upper and lower limbs.  Reflexes were 1+ in the bilateral patella.  The toes descended bilaterally.  Coordination showed precise striking ability and good rapid alternating movements.  Gait examination was postponed at this time due to placement of an EEG probe.,ASSESSMENT:A 2 1/2 year old female with a history of febrile seizures, now concerned with episodes of unclear etiology, but somewhat concerning for complex partial seizures and, to a lesser extent, non-convulsive generalized seizures.,RECOMMENDATIONS,1.  For now, we'll try to capture the EEG as long as she tolerates it; however, if she needed sedation, I would defer the EEG until further adjustments were made.

transcription: INFORMANT: Dad on the phone.  Transferred from ABCD Memorial Hospital, rules out sepsis.,HISTORY: This is a 3 week old, Caucasian, NSVD boy transferred from ABCD Memorial Hospital to rule out sepsis and possible congenital heart disease.  The patient had a fever of 100.1 on 09/13/2006 taken rectally, and his mother, being a nurse, took the baby to the hospital and he was admitted to rule out sepsis.  All sepsis assessments were carried out, CBC, UA, LP and CMP, and as one murmur was rated 2/5, he also had an echo.  The patient was started on ampicillin and cefotaxime.  The ultrasound results came back and showed a patent foramen ovale/TSA with primary pulmonary stenosis, and then, given severe congenital heart disease, he was transferred here on vancomycin, ampicillin, and cefotaxime.  The patient was n.p.o. when he entered. He was taking 3/4 L of oxygen.  According to the note, there were subcostal retractions.  Arriving here, baby looks very healthy.  He has no subcostal retractions.  He does not require oxygen and his urine and stool tests are positive.  The stools are although green in color, and today in the morning he reached a fever of 100.1, but at the moment he does not have a fever.  ED classified this case as a direct admission.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Patient reportedly had fever, weight loss, and lack of appetite.  The day he had fever, no rash, no ear pain, no congestion, no rhinorrhea, no throat pain, no neck pain, no visual changes, no conjunctivitis, no cough, no dyspnea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, and no dysuria.  According to mom, baby felt limp on the day of fever and he also had stool every day from 4 to 6 hours, yellowish to green in color, but today the stool we noticed was green in color.  He usually urinates 4-5 times a day, but the day he had a fever his urine was also low.  Mom gave baby Pedialyte.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,None.,HOSPITALIZATIONS:,Recent transfer from ABCD to rule out sepsis and heart disease.,BIRTH HISTORY:,Born 08/23/2006 at Memorial Hospital, NSVD, no complications.  Hospital stay 24 hours.  Breastfed, no formula, no jaundice, 7 pounds 8 ounces.,FAMILY HISTORY:, None.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, None.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Lives with mom and dad.  Dad is a service manager at GMC; 4 year old son, healthy; and 2 cats, 2 dogs, 3 chickens, 1 frog.  They usually visit a ranch, but not recently.  No sick contact and no travel.,MEDICATIONS:, Took vancomycin, cefotaxime and ampicillin.,ALLERGIES:, No allergies.,FOOD:, Breastfeeds every 2 hours.,IMMUNIZATIONS:, No vaccinations.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature 99, pulse 158, respiratory rate 68, blood pressure 87/48, oxygen. 100% to room air.,MEASURES: Weight 3.725 kg.,GENERAL: Alert and comfortable and sleepy.,SKIN: No rash.,SMELLS: Extraocular movements intact.  PERRLA.  No runny nose.  No nasal cannula, but no oxygen is actively circulating and the anterior fontanelle is flat., NECK: soft, non-tender, supple., CHEST: CTAP., GI: bowel sounds present.  Non-tender, non-distended.,GU: bilaterally descended testicles.,BACK: straight.,NEUROLOGICAL: non-focal.,EXTREMITIES: no edema.  Bilateral pedal pulses are present and upper arm pulses are also present. LABORATORY DATA: As drawn on 09/13/2006 on ABCD, it showed WBC 4.2, hemoglobin 11.8, hematocrit 34.7, platelets 480,000. Sodium 140, potassium 4.9, chloride 105, bicarbonate 28, BUN 7, creatinine 0.4, glucose 80, CRP 0.5.  Neutrophils 90, bands 7, lymphocytes 27, monocytes 12, and eosinophils 4. Chest x-ray done on 09/13/2006 indicates a mild left upper lobe infiltrate, but as seen here and discussed with Dr.  UA and LP results are pending.  Blood, LP and urine cultures are also pending.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ,This is a 3 week old Caucasian baby boy admitted to rule out sepsis and congenital heart disease.,INFECTIOUS/PULMONARY DISEASE: ,Afebrile with 20 hour blood cultures so far, LP and urine cultures are negative.  W

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Arm and leg jerks.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 10 day old Caucasian female, presented here with approximately 1 minute of bilateral arm and leg jerks, which started on the first day of her life and has occurred a total of 6 times since then.  Mom denies any apnea, perioral cyanosis or color change.  These movements are done without arching the back.  They mainly occur during sleep, so mom isn't aware of rolling her eyes.  Mom is able to wake the patient during these times and stop the movements of the patient's extremities. Otherwise, this patient has been active, breastfeeding well, although she falls asleep at the breast.  She currently drinks 15-20 minutes of breast milk every 2-3 hours.  She has increased diapers up to 8 wet stools and 6 to 7 dirty yellow stools per day.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: Negative fever, negative restlessness, eye tracking, some sneezing and hiccups.  This patient developed upper airway congestion over the past day.  She had no vomiting or diarrhea.  According to mom, she doesn't spit up and mom is also unable to notice a relationship between these movements and feedings.  This patient had no rash.  Mom was informed by the nurses at birth that her temperature might be low, around 97.5 degrees Fahrenheit.  Otherwise, the above history of current illness and other examination of systems is negative., BIRTH/PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:, Patient was an 8 pound 11 ounce, 41 week old infant, born via vaginal delivery without vacuum assistance or forceps.  There were no complications during pregnancy such as diabetes or hypertension.  Prenatal care began around 3 weeks and the mother maintained all visits.  She also denies any use of tobacco, alcohol or drugs during pregnancy.  Mom was positive for GBS, but denies any other infections such as urinary tract infections.  She did not have a fever during labor and received inadequate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis.  After delivery, this patient did not receive secondary antibiotics at the “borderline laboratories”.  She had jaundice after birth and received photographic treatments.  Her bilirubin level at discharge was approximately 11. Mother and child remained in the hospital for approximately 3 1/2 days. Mom denies any history of sexually transmitted diseases in her or her father.  She specifically denies any herpetic and vesicular genital lesions.  However, she has a history of human papillomavirus (vaginal) warts, removed 20 years ago.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, Negative.,ALLERGIES:, No known drug allergies.,DRUGS:, None.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, At home live mom, dad and brothers aged 18, 16, 14, 12 and a sister of 3 years.  All residents of the house are currently sick with colds, coughs and runny noses, except mom.  Also living at home are 2 dogs and 2 outside cats.  Mom denies any recent travel, especially recent vacations and no smoke exposure.,FAMILY HISTORY: Dad is with a stepdaughter who has seizures starting at age 14 and is currently on medication.  The patient's 16-year-old brother suffers from incessant, nonsustained ventricular tachycardia.  The maternal grandmother suffers from hypertension and diabetes.  There are no other children in the family seeing a specialist and no deaths of children under 1 year of age.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

transcription: HISTORY:, The patient is a 4 day old infant transferred here due to hyperbilirubinemia and some hypoxia.  The mother states that she took the child to the clinic this morning because the child had a yellow appearance and had a bilirubin level of 23 mg%.  The patient was then sent to the hospital where she had lab tests and was found to be hypoxic, but her oxygen had minimal supplemental oxygen.  She was also noted to be breathing periodically.  The patient is breast and bottle fed and is eating well.  There was no diarrhea or vomiting.  Empty well.  The bowels were regular.,According to the referral institution's report, because the patient had periodic breathing and was hypoxic, she was thought to be septic and she received a dose of IM ampicillin.,The patient was born at 37 weeks' gestation to a gravida 3, para 3 by repeat cesarean section.  Birth weight was 8 pounds 6 ounces and the mother's prenatal assessment was normal except for placenta previa.  The patient's mother apparently went into labor and then underwent a cesarean section.,FAMILY HISTORY: ,Positive for asthma and diabetes and there is no exposure to second-hand smoke.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,VITAL SIGNS: The patient has a rectal temperature of 36.8, pulse rate of 148 per minute, respirations of 50 per minute, oxygen saturation of 96 on room air, but dropped to 90 and the patient received 1 liter by nasal cannula. GENERAL: Patient is jaundiced, well hydrated.  Has periodic breathing.  The color is pink and jaundice is also noted, scleral and cutaneous., HEENT: Normal., NECK: Soft., CHEST: Clear., HEART: Regular with a soft murmur 3/6.  The femorals are clearly palpable.  Filling of the hood is immediate.,ABDOMEN: A soft, small and reducible umbilical hernia is noted.,EXTERNAL GENITALS: Those of a female child.,SKIN: Icteric color.  Nonspecific rash on the body, which is sparse.  The patient has a cephalhematoma hematoma approximately 6 cm above the left occipitoparietal area.,EXTREMITIES: The patient moves all extremities well.  Has normal tone and good suction.,EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT COURSE:,Parents were told that I would repeat the labs and also catheterize the urine samples.  The parents were informed that the child had a murmur.  I spoke to Dr.  The case has been discussed with Dr. Y and he will admit this child for hyperbilirubinemia. CBC performed showed white count 15,700, hemoglobin 18g%, hematocrit 50.6%, platelets 245,000, 10 bands, 44 segs, 34 lymphs and 8 monos.  Chemical analyzes performed showed sodium 142 mEq/L, potassium 4.5 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, CO2 28 mmol/L, glucose 75 mg%, BUN 8 mg%, creatinine 0.7 mg% and calcium 8.0 mg%.  Total bilirubin was 25.4 mg, all unconjugated.  CRP was 0.3 mg%.  A blood culture was performed.  The catheterized urine sample was normal.  Parents were constantly kept informed of what was happening and the need for admission.  Phototherapy was instituted in the emergency room almost after the baby arrived in the emergency room.,PRINT:,Hyperbilirubinemia and heart murmur.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:,Regarded as breast milk, jaundice, ABO incompatibility, galactosemia and ventricular septal defect.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Chronic otitis media., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 14 month old child with a history of chronic recurrent episodes of otitis media, totaling 6 episodes, requiring antibiotics since birth.  Chronic nasal congestion is also associated.  There had been no episodes of spontaneous perforation of the tympanic membrane, but there had been temperature elevations up to 102 during the acute infection.  He is currently admitted for myringotomy and catheter insertion under general facemask anesthesia.,ALLERGIES:, None.,MEDICATION:, None.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Non-contributory.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Mild reflux.,PREVIOUS SURGERY:, None.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Patient is not in daycare. The left ear is still a little black.  Nose, moderate hypertrophy of the inferior turbinates.  No polyps or purulence.  Oral cavity, oropharynx 2+ tonsils.  No exudate.  Neck, no knots, masses or thyromegaly.  Lungs are clear for A&P.  Cardiac examination, regular rate and rhythm.  No whispers.  The abdomen is soft and nontender.  Positive bowel sounds.,PRINT:,Chronic Eustachian tube dysfunction, chronic otitis media with effusion, recurrent acute otitis media and wax accumulation.,PLAN:,The patient will be admitted to the operating room for myringotomy and tube insertion under general face mask anesthesia.

transcription: PROCEDURE: Delayed primary thoracic closure.,INDICATIONS:,The patient is a neonate with a diagnosis of hypoplastic left heart syndrome who, 48 hours before the current procedure, underwent a modified stage 1 Norwood operation.  Given the scale of the procedure and the size of the patient (2.5 kg), we chose to leave the chest open to facilitate postoperative care.  He is now returned to the operating room for delayed primary chest closure.,PREOP DX:, Open chest status after a modified Norwood stage 1 operation.,POSTOP DX:, Open chest status after a modified Norwood stage 1 operation.,ANESTHESIA:, General endotracheal.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,FINDINGS:, No evidence of intramediastinal purulence or hematoma.  He tolerated the procedure well.,PROCEDURE DETAILS:,The patient was brought to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  After general endotracheal anesthesia, the chest was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  The previously placed AlloDerm membrane was removed.  Mediastinal cultures were obtained, and the mediastinum was extensively irrigated and suctioned.  Both cavities were also irrigated and vacuumed.  The drains were flushed and repositioned.  About 30 cubic centimeters of blood was drawn slowly from the right ear line.  The sternum was then coated with a vancomycin paste.  The proximal aspect of the 5 mm RV-PA conduit was marked with a small titanium clip on its inferior aspect and with another on its lower right side.  The sternum was then closed with stainless steel wires, followed by closure of the subcutaneous tissues with interrupted monofilament sutures.  The skin was closed with interrupted nylon sutures and a sterile dressing was placed.  The peritoneal dialysis catheter as well as the atrial and ventricular pacing wires were removed.  The patient was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit shortly thereafter in very stable condition. I was the surgeon present in the operating room and responsible for the surgical procedure throughout the duration of the case.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE: The patient presents with mom and dad for a well-child checkup on her one-year-old child.  The family has no concerns that the patient is doing generally well since the last visit, eating a well-balanced diet of infant formula transitioning to whole milk, fruits, vegetables, proteins, and grains.  Normal urination and stool pattern.  No hearing or vision concerns.  Growth and Development: Denver II normally meets all age-appropriate developmental milestones in the areas of fine motor skills, gross motor skills, personal and social interactions, and speech and language development.  See Denver II form in table., PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Allergies: None.  Medications: Tylenol this morning in preparation for vaccines and a daily multivitamin.,FAMILY SOCIAL HISTORY:, Unchanged since last check.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:, According to HPI; otherwise negative.,TARGET: Weight 24 pounds 1 ounce.  Height 30 inches.  Head circumference 46.5 cm.  Afebrile temperature.,General: Well-developed, well-nourished, cooperative, alert and interactive one-year-old white female, smiling, happy and drooling.,HEENT: Atraumatic, normocephalic.  The anterior fontanelle is closed.  Pupils also round and reactive.  The sclerae are clear.  Red reflex present bilaterally.  Extraocular muscles intact.  TMs are clear bilaterally.  Oropharynx: The mucous membranes are moist and pink.  Good teeth.  Drooling and chewing with teething behavior today.  The neck is flexible.  No lymphadenopathy.,Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.  No wheezing.  No crackling.  Good air exchange.,Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm and rhythm.  No whispers.  Good bilateral pulses. Abdomen: soft, not tender.  Not distended.  Positive bowel sounds.  No mass.  No organomegaly.,Genitourinary: Tanner I female genitalia.  Femoral pulses are equal bilaterally.  No rash.,Extremities: Full range of motion.  No cyanosis, clubbing or edema.  Negative Ortolani and Barlow maneuver.,Back: straight.  No scoliosis.,Integument: Warm, dry and pink without lesions.,Neurological: Alert.  Good muscle tone and strength.  Cranial nerves II to XII are virtually intact.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,1.  Well, a 1 year old white female.,2.  Advance guidance.  Review of growth issues, diet development and safety as well as vaccinations.  Will receive Pediarix and HIB today.  Discussion of risks and benefits as well as possible side effects and symptomatic treatment.  Will also obtain a screening CBC and lead level today via finger prick and will call family with results as soon as they are available.  I gave mom and dad a health check-up document for a one-year-old child.,3.  Follow up for the child's health check at 15 months or, if necessary, for acute care.

transcription: SUBJECTIVE:, This 9 month old Hispanic male arrives today for a 9 month well check.  They are coming from Texas until the end of April 2004. Mom says he is fine since the last time.  He is up to date with his vaccinations according to his report.  She notes that he has developed bumps on his chest that have been there for about a week.  Two weeks ago he was diagnosed with left otitis media and was treated with antibiotics.  Mom says he's been fine ever since.  She has no concerns about it.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Significant for an uncomplicated full-term vaginal delivery.,MEDICATIONS:, None.,ALLERGIES:, None.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Lives with her parents.  Smoking is not permitted in the house.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Development is appropriate.  No fever.  No other rash.  No cough or congestion.  No vomiting or diarrhea.  Eat normally.,OBJECTIVE: His weight is 16 pounds 9 ounces.  Height is 26-1/4 inches.  The head circumference is 44.75 cm.  Pulse is 124. Respirations are 26. Temperature is 98.1 degrees.  Generally, this is a well-developed, well-nourished, active, alert and playful 9-month-old male, without acute distress. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic.  The anterior fontanelle is soft and flat.  The tympanic membranes are clear bilaterally.  The conjunctivas are clear.  Pupils equal, round and reactive to light.  Nares without turbinate edema.  The oropharynx is not erythematous., NECK: supple, without lymphadenopathy, thyromegaly, carotid sound or JVD., CHEST: clear on auscultation bilaterally., CARDIOVASCULAR: regular frequency and rhythm, without murmur., ABDOMEN: bowel sounds soft, non-tender, non-distended and normoactive.  No masses or organomegaly on palpation.,GU: normal male external genitalia.  Uncircumcised penis.  Testicles descended bilaterally.  Femoral Pulses 2/4.,ENDS: Moves all four ends equally.  Minimal tibial torsion.,SKIN: No abnormalities other than five small molluscum contagiosum with umbilical hernia noted on chest.,ASSESSMENT/PLAN:,1.  Check well child.  I'm doing well.  Will recommend a follow-up health check of the child at age 1 and vaccinations at that time.  Discussed safety issues including poisons, choking hazards, pet safety, and proper nutrition with Mom.  She receives a parental guide.,2.  Molluscum contagiosum.  Describes the viral etiology of these.  I told him they were self-limited and we would continue to monitor at this time.,3.  Left otitis media, resolved.  Continue to monitor.  We plan to follow up in three months if they are still in the area, or p.r.n.

transcription: TITLE OF OPERATION: 1. Repair of total abnormal pulmonary venous connection.,2.  Ligation of the patent ductus arteriosus.,3.  Repair the secundum type atrial septal defect (autologous pericardial patch).,4.  Subtotal thymectomy.,5.  Insertion of a peritoneal dialysis catheter.,INDICATION FOR SURGERY: This neonate was diagnosed after birth with a total anomalous pulmonary venous connection.  After initial stabilization, she was transferred to the hospital for complete correction.,PREOPERATION DIAGNOSIS:,1.  Total abnormal pulmonary venous connection.,2.  Atrial communication.,3.  Patent ductus arteriosus.,4.  Operating weight less than 4 kilograms (3.2 kilograms).,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,CROSS CLAMP TIME: , 63 minutes.,CARDIOPULMONARY BYPASS TIME MONITOR: 35 minutes, deep hypothermic circulatory arrest time (4 plus 19) is equal to 23 minutes.  Low flow perfusion 32 minutes., RESULTS: Horizontal pulmonary venous confluence with the right upper and middle veins with two veins entering the confluence on the right and multiple entry sites for the left sided veins.  Large patulous anastomosis between the posterior surface of the left atrium and the anterior surface of the pulmonary venous confluence.  Unobstructed ascending vein ligated.  Persistence of the diminutive ductus arteriosus of the left atrium with posterior atrial septal defect with deficient lower margin.  At the end of the procedure, right ventricular pressure close to half of normal systemic sinus rhythm, good biventricular function by visual inspection.,PROCEDURE: After informed consent, the patient was brought to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  During induction of general endotracheal anesthesia and establishment of indwelling arterial and venous monitoring lines.  The patient was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner, from chin to groin.  A median sternotomy incision was made.  Dissection was carried out through the deepest planes until the sternum was incised and divided with an oscillating saw.  A subtotal thymectomy was performed.  Systemic heparinization was performed and the pericardium was penetrated and shaped to the cradle.  A small portion of the anterior pericardium was removed and fixed in glutaraldehyde for patch closure of the atrial septal defect segment during the procedure.  Purse strings were deployed over the ascending aorta on the right.  Auricular appendage.  The aorta was then cannulated with an 8-French aortic cannula and the right atrium with an 18-French Polystan right-angle cannula.  With an ACT greater than 400, a larger lung bypass was started with excellent cardiac decompression and the patent ductus arteriosus was ligated with a 2-0 silk tie.  Systemic cooling was started and the head was packed and iced and systemic steroids were administered.  During cooling, a traction suture was placed at the apex of the left ventricle.  After 25 minutes of cooling, the aorta was cross-clamped and the heart stopped by administration of 30 cubic centimeters/kilogram of cold blood cardioplegia delivered directly into the aortic root after cross-clamping of the aorta.  After successful cardioplegic arrest, a low flow perfusion period was started and a 10 French catheter was inserted into the right atrial appendage replacing the 18 French Polystan venous cannula.  The heart was then rotated to the right and the venous confluence was exposed.  It was incised and enlarged and a corresponding incision in the dorsal and posterior aspect of the left atrium was made.  The two openings were then anastomosed end-to-side with several interlocking sutures to avoid the purse-string effect with a 7-0 PDS suture running.  Once the anastomosis was complete, the heart was returned to the chest and the patient's blood volume was drained into the reservoir.  A right auriotomy was then performed during the period of circulatory arrest.  The atrial septal defect was very difficult to expose, but it was sealed

transcription: HISTORY: We had the pleasure of seeing the patient today in our pediatric rheumatology clinic.  He was sent here with a chief complaint of joint pain in several joints for a few months.  This is a 7-year-old white male with no history of systemic illness, who until 2 months ago was doing well and 2 months ago he began complaining of pain in his fingers, elbows, and neck.  Right now it's better and it's almost gone, but for several months he was having pain to the point where he was crying at one point.  He is not a complainer according to his mother and he is a very active child.  There is no history of illness or gastrointestinal problems.  He has allergy problems, especially seasonal ones, and he takes Claritin for that.  Other than that, he had no other problems.  He denies any swelling, except for the swelling mentioned by the doctor in his elbow.  There is no history of rash, no stomach pain, no diarrhea, no fever, no weight loss, no ulcers in the mouth except canker sores.  No lymphadenopathy, no eye problems and no urinary problems.,MEDICATIONS: ,His medications consist only of Motrin only when needed and Claritin currently for seasonal allergies and rhinitis.,ALLERGIES: ,He has no allergies to any medications.,BIRTH HISTORY: ,Pregnancy and delivery without complications.  He has no history of hospitalization or surgery.,FAMILY HISTORY: Positive for arthritis in his grandmother.  No history of pediatric arthritis.  There is a history of psoriasis in his father.,SOCIAL HISTORY: He lives with mom, dad, brother, sister and everyone is healthy.  They live in Easton.  They have 4 dogs, 3 cats, 3 mules and no deer.  At school, he is in the second year and he does physical education without any limitations.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature is 98.7, pulse is 96, respiratory rate is 24, height is 118.1 cm, weight is 22.1 kg and blood pressure is 61/44.,GENERAL: He is alert, active, in no way distressed, very cooperative.,HEENT: He does not have a rash on his face.  No lymphadenopathy.  The oral mucosa is clear.  No tonsillitis.  His ear canals are clear and his pupils respond to light and accommodation.,CHEST: Clear to auscultation.,HEART: Regular rhythm and no murmurs.,ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, no visceromegaly.,MUSCULOSKELETAL: Has no limitations in any of his joints or active swelling today.  He also has no feeling in any of his joints.  Muscle strength is 5/5 in proximal muscles.,LABORATORY DATA: Includes arthritis panel.  He has normal uric acid, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 2, rheumatoid factor of 6, and negative antinuclear antibodies and C-reactive protein of 7.1.  His mother stated that this was done while he was experiencing symptoms.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:,This patient may have had reactive arthritis.  He is seen frequently and the patient has a family history of psoriatic arthritis or psoriasis.  I don't see any issues at this time on his labs or physical exam.  This may have been linked to a recent episode of viral infection or any infection.  Mother has been informed of the finding and my recommendation is to observe him and if there is a recurrence of symptoms or persistence of swelling or limitation in any of his joints, I will be happy to see him again. If you have any questions about further evaluation and plan, please do not hesitate to contact us.

transcription: CONSULTATION INDICATION:, Increasing need for oxygen.,HISTORY:, The little boy, XYZ, is an infant of 29 3/7 weeks gestation.  His mother had a premature rupture of membranes on 12/20/08.  She then presented to the labor and delivery unit with flu-like symptoms.  The baby was then induced and delivered.  The mother had previously had premature babies.  This baby was doing well, and then we had a significant increase in ambient air oxygen requirements up to 85%.  He is now on 60% FiO2.,PHYSICAL FINDINGS,GENERAL: He appeared to be pink, well perfused and slightly jaundiced.,VITAL SIGNS: pulse 156, respiratory rate 56, sat 92% and blood pressure 59/28 mmHg.,SKIN: He was pink.,He was on a high frequency ventilator with good agitation.,His echocardiogram showed normal structure. anatomy.  He presents with signs of significant pulmonary hypertension.  A large ductus arteriosus was observed with a bidirectional shunt.  Foramen ovale shunting was also noted with bidirectional shunting.  The shunt for the canal and foramen ovale was equal left to right and right to left.,IMPRESSION: My impression is that little boy, XYZ, has significant pulmonary hypertension.  The best therapy for this is to continue administering oxygen.  If the clinical condition worsens, he may need nitric oxide.  Certainly, Indocin should not be used at this time.  He must have lower pulmonary artery pressures for this to be taken into account. Thank you so much for allowing me to be involved in baby XYZ's care.

transcription: REASON FOR CONSULTATION:, New seizure., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:, The patient is a 2 1/2 year old female with a history of known febrile seizures, who was placed on Keppra oral solution 150 mg twice daily. to help prevent febrile seizures.  Although this treatment has been very effective in controlling her febrile seizures, she now has occasional brief periods of pauses and staring, during which she becomes unresponsive, but does not lose her postural tone.  According to Dad the typical spell lasts between 10-15 seconds, Mom says 3-4 minutes which probably means somewhere between 30-40 seconds.  Mom noted that an episode had recently occurred outside a store and was associated with perioral cyanosis, but no seizure activity was noted.  There have been no recent changes in her Keppra dosage and she is currently only on 20 mg/kg per day, which is overall a low dose for her.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Born at 36 weeks gestation via cesarean delivery at 8 pounds 3 ounces.  She has a history of febrile seizures and parents have reported an abdominal migraine, but upon further questioning it appears to be more of a food intolerance issue.,SURGICAL HISTORY: ,She has not had any surgery.,FAMILY MEDICAL HISTORY: ,There is a strong history of epilepsy on the maternal side of the family, including the mother with nonconvulsive seizures during childhood and additional seizures in maternal great-grandmother and a maternal great-aunt.  There is no other significant neurological history on his father's side of the family.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Currently lives with his mother, father, and two siblings.  She is at home full-time and does not attend daycare.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: ,A clear review of 10 systems was performed and revealed no additional findings other than those noted in the current illness history.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,Vital signs: Weight was 15.6 kg.  She was afebrile.  The remainder of her vital signs were stable and within normal limits for her age according to the medical record.,General: She was awake, alert and oriented.  She was not in acute distress, only slightly confused when trying to place the EEG leads. HEENT: Showed a normocephalic and atraumatic head.  His conjunctivas were nonicteric and his sclerae were clear.  His eye movements were conjugate in nature.  His tongue and mucous membranes were moist.,Neck: Trachea appeared to be in the midline.,Chest: Clear on auscultation bilaterally with no crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi.,Cardiovascular: Presented normal sinus rhythm without murmurs.,Abdomen: Showed soft, nontender, and nondistended, with good bowel sounds.  There was no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly, or other masses noted on examination. Extremities: Showed IV placement in the right upper extremity with appropriate IV restraints.  There was no evidence of blunting, cyanosis or edema.  She had no functional deformity in any of her peripheral limbs. Neurological: Neurologically, her cranial nerves were generally intact.  His strength was good in bilateral upper and lower extremities with no distal to proximal variation.  His overall resting tone was normal.  Sensory examination was generally intact, if not slightly affected, in the upper and lower limbs.  Reflexes were 1+ in the bilateral patella.  The toes descended bilaterally.  Coordination showed precise striking ability and good rapid alternating movements.  Gait examination was postponed at this time due to placement of an EEG probe.,ASSESSMENT:A 2 1/2 year old female with a history of febrile seizures, now concerned with episodes of unclear etiology, but somewhat concerning for complex partial seizures and, to a lesser extent, non-convulsive generalized seizures.,RECOMMENDATIONS,1.  For now, we'll try to capture the EEG as long as she tolerates it; however, if she needed sedation, I would defer the EEG until further adjustments were made.

transcription: INFORMANT: Dad on the phone.  Transferred from ABCD Memorial Hospital, rules out sepsis.,HISTORY: This is a 3 week old, Caucasian, NSVD boy transferred from ABCD Memorial Hospital to rule out sepsis and possible congenital heart disease.  The patient had a fever of 100.1 on 09/13/2006 taken rectally, and his mother, being a nurse, took the baby to the hospital and he was admitted to rule out sepsis.  All sepsis assessments were carried out, CBC, UA, LP and CMP, and as one murmur was rated 2/5, he also had an echo.  The patient was started on ampicillin and cefotaxime.  The ultrasound results came back and showed a patent foramen ovale/TSA with primary pulmonary stenosis, and then, given severe congenital heart disease, he was transferred here on vancomycin, ampicillin, and cefotaxime.  The patient was n.p.o. when he entered. He was taking 3/4 L of oxygen.  According to the note, there were subcostal retractions.  Arriving here, baby looks very healthy.  He has no subcostal retractions.  He does not require oxygen and his urine and stool tests are positive.  The stools are although green in color, and today in the morning he reached a fever of 100.1, but at the moment he does not have a fever.  ED classified this case as a direct admission.,SYSTEMS REVIEW:,Patient reportedly had fever, weight loss, and lack of appetite.  The day he had fever, no rash, no ear pain, no congestion, no rhinorrhea, no throat pain, no neck pain, no visual changes, no conjunctivitis, no cough, no dyspnea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, and no dysuria.  According to mom, baby felt limp on the day of fever and he also had stool every day from 4 to 6 hours, yellowish to green in color, but today the stool we noticed was green in color.  He usually urinates 4-5 times a day, but the day he had a fever his urine was also low.  Mom gave baby Pedialyte.,MEDICAL HISTORY:,None.,HOSPITALIZATIONS:,Recent transfer from ABCD to rule out sepsis and heart disease.,BIRTH HISTORY:,Born 08/23/2006 at Memorial Hospital, NSVD, no complications.  Hospital stay 24 hours.  Breastfed, no formula, no jaundice, 7 pounds 8 ounces.,FAMILY HISTORY:, None.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, None.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Lives with mom and dad.  Dad is a service manager at GMC; 4 year old son, healthy; and 2 cats, 2 dogs, 3 chickens, 1 frog.  They usually visit a ranch, but not recently.  No sick contact and no travel.,MEDICATIONS:, Took vancomycin, cefotaxime and ampicillin.,ALLERGIES:, No allergies.,FOOD:, Breastfeeds every 2 hours.,IMMUNIZATIONS:, No vaccinations.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,VITAL SIGNS: Temperature 99, pulse 158, respiratory rate 68, blood pressure 87/48, oxygen. 100% to room air.,MEASURES: Weight 3.725 kg.,GENERAL: Alert and comfortable and sleepy.,SKIN: No rash.,SMELLS: Extraocular movements intact.  PERRLA.  No runny nose.  No nasal cannula, but no oxygen is actively circulating and the anterior fontanelle is flat., NECK: soft, non-tender, supple., CHEST: CTAP., GI: bowel sounds present.  Non-tender, non-distended.,GU: bilaterally descended testicles.,BACK: straight.,NEUROLOGICAL: non-focal.,EXTREMITIES: no edema.  Bilateral pedal pulses are present and upper arm pulses are also present. LABORATORY DATA: As drawn on 09/13/2006 on ABCD, it showed WBC 4.2, hemoglobin 11.8, hematocrit 34.7, platelets 480,000. Sodium 140, potassium 4.9, chloride 105, bicarbonate 28, BUN 7, creatinine 0.4, glucose 80, CRP 0.5.  Neutrophils 90, bands 7, lymphocytes 27, monocytes 12, and eosinophils 4. Chest x-ray done on 09/13/2006 indicates a mild left upper lobe infiltrate, but as seen here and discussed with Dr.  UA and LP results are pending.  Blood, LP and urine cultures are also pending.,ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ,This is a 3 week old Caucasian baby boy admitted to rule out sepsis and congenital heart disease.,INFECTIOUS/PULMONARY DISEASE: ,Afebrile with 20 hour blood cultures so far, LP and urine cultures are negative.  W

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Arm and leg jerks.,HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient is a 10 day old Caucasian female, presented here with approximately 1 minute of bilateral arm and leg jerks, which started on the first day of her life and has occurred a total of 6 times since then.  Mom denies any apnea, perioral cyanosis or color change.  These movements are done without arching the back.  They mainly occur during sleep, so mom isn't aware of rolling her eyes.  Mom is able to wake the patient during these times and stop the movements of the patient's extremities. Otherwise, this patient has been active, breastfeeding well, although she falls asleep at the breast.  She currently drinks 15-20 minutes of breast milk every 2-3 hours.  She has increased diapers up to 8 wet stools and 6 to 7 dirty yellow stools per day.,EXAMINATION OF SYSTEMS: Negative fever, negative restlessness, eye tracking, some sneezing and hiccups.  This patient developed upper airway congestion over the past day.  She had no vomiting or diarrhea.  According to mom, she doesn't spit up and mom is also unable to notice a relationship between these movements and feedings.  This patient had no rash.  Mom was informed by the nurses at birth that her temperature might be low, around 97.5 degrees Fahrenheit.  Otherwise, the above history of current illness and other examination of systems is negative., BIRTH/PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:, Patient was an 8 pound 11 ounce, 41 week old infant, born via vaginal delivery without vacuum assistance or forceps.  There were no complications during pregnancy such as diabetes or hypertension.  Prenatal care began around 3 weeks and the mother maintained all visits.  She also denies any use of tobacco, alcohol or drugs during pregnancy.  Mom was positive for GBS, but denies any other infections such as urinary tract infections.  She did not have a fever during labor and received inadequate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis.  After delivery, this patient did not receive secondary antibiotics at the “borderline laboratories”.  She had jaundice after birth and received photographic treatments.  Her bilirubin level at discharge was approximately 11. Mother and child remained in the hospital for approximately 3 1/2 days. Mom denies any history of sexually transmitted diseases in her or her father.  She specifically denies any herpetic and vesicular genital lesions.  However, she has a history of human papillomavirus (vaginal) warts, removed 20 years ago.,SURGICAL HISTORY:, Negative.,ALLERGIES:, No known drug allergies.,DRUGS:, None.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, At home live mom, dad and brothers aged 18, 16, 14, 12 and a sister of 3 years.  All residents of the house are currently sick with colds, coughs and runny noses, except mom.  Also living at home are 2 dogs and 2 outside cats.  Mom denies any recent travel, especially recent vacations and no smoke exposure.,FAMILY HISTORY: Dad is with a stepdaughter who has seizures starting at age 14 and is currently on medication.  The patient's 16-year-old brother suffers from incessant, nonsustained ventricular tachycardia.  The maternal grandmother suffers from hypertension and diabetes.  There are no other children in the family seeing a specialist and no deaths of children under 1 year of age.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

transcription: HISTORY:, The patient is a 4 day old infant transferred here due to hyperbilirubinemia and some hypoxia.  The mother states that she took the child to the clinic this morning because the child had a yellow appearance and had a bilirubin level of 23 mg%.  The patient was then sent to the hospital where she had lab tests and was found to be hypoxic, but her oxygen had minimal supplemental oxygen.  She was also noted to be breathing periodically.  The patient is breast and bottle fed and is eating well.  There was no diarrhea or vomiting.  Empty well.  The bowels were regular.,According to the referral institution's report, because the patient had periodic breathing and was hypoxic, she was thought to be septic and she received a dose of IM ampicillin.,The patient was born at 37 weeks' gestation to a gravida 3, para 3 by repeat cesarean section.  Birth weight was 8 pounds 6 ounces and the mother's prenatal assessment was normal except for placenta previa.  The patient's mother apparently went into labor and then underwent a cesarean section.,FAMILY HISTORY: ,Positive for asthma and diabetes and there is no exposure to second-hand smoke.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ,VITAL SIGNS: The patient has a rectal temperature of 36.8, pulse rate of 148 per minute, respirations of 50 per minute, oxygen saturation of 96 on room air, but dropped to 90 and the patient received 1 liter by nasal cannula. GENERAL: Patient is jaundiced, well hydrated.  Has periodic breathing.  The color is pink and jaundice is also noted, scleral and cutaneous., HEENT: Normal., NECK: Soft., CHEST: Clear., HEART: Regular with a soft murmur 3/6.  The femorals are clearly palpable.  Filling of the hood is immediate.,ABDOMEN: A soft, small and reducible umbilical hernia is noted.,EXTERNAL GENITALS: Those of a female child.,SKIN: Icteric color.  Nonspecific rash on the body, which is sparse.  The patient has a cephalhematoma hematoma approximately 6 cm above the left occipitoparietal area.,EXTREMITIES: The patient moves all extremities well.  Has normal tone and good suction.,EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT COURSE:,Parents were told that I would repeat the labs and also catheterize the urine samples.  The parents were informed that the child had a murmur.  I spoke to Dr.  The case has been discussed with Dr. Y and he will admit this child for hyperbilirubinemia. CBC performed showed white count 15,700, hemoglobin 18g%, hematocrit 50.6%, platelets 245,000, 10 bands, 44 segs, 34 lymphs and 8 monos.  Chemical analyzes performed showed sodium 142 mEq/L, potassium 4.5 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, CO2 28 mmol/L, glucose 75 mg%, BUN 8 mg%, creatinine 0.7 mg% and calcium 8.0 mg%.  Total bilirubin was 25.4 mg, all unconjugated.  CRP was 0.3 mg%.  A blood culture was performed.  The catheterized urine sample was normal.  Parents were constantly kept informed of what was happening and the need for admission.  Phototherapy was instituted in the emergency room almost after the baby arrived in the emergency room.,PRINT:,Hyperbilirubinemia and heart murmur.,DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS:,Regarded as breast milk, jaundice, ABO incompatibility, galactosemia and ventricular septal defect.

transcription: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Chronic otitis media., HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: This is a 14 month old child with a history of chronic recurrent episodes of otitis media, totaling 6 episodes, requiring antibiotics since birth.  Chronic nasal congestion is also associated.  There had been no episodes of spontaneous perforation of the tympanic membrane, but there had been temperature elevations up to 102 during the acute infection.  He is currently admitted for myringotomy and catheter insertion under general facemask anesthesia.,ALLERGIES:, None.,MEDICATION:, None.,FAMILY HISTORY:, Non-contributory.,MEDICAL HISTORY:, Mild reflux.,PREVIOUS SURGERY:, None.,SOCIAL HISTORY:, Patient is not in daycare.  There are no animals in the house.  There is no exposure to passive smoking.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: Examination of the ears reveals poorly mobile retracted tympanic membranes on the right side with middle ear effusion.  The left ear is still a little black.  Nose, moderate hypertrophy of the inferior turbinates.  No polyps or purulence.  Oral cavity, oropharynx 2+ tonsils.  No exudate.  Neck, no knots, masses or thyromegaly.  Lungs are clear for A&P.  Cardiac examination, regular rate and rhythm.  No whispers.  The abdomen is soft and nontender.  Positive bowel sounds.,PRINT:,Chronic Eustachian tube dysfunction, chronic otitis media with effusion, recurrent acute otitis media and wax accumulation.,PLAN:,The patient will be admitted to the operating room for myringotomy and tube insertion under general face mask anesthesia.

transcription: PROCEDURE: Delayed primary thoracic closure.,INDICATIONS:,The patient is a neonate with a diagnosis of hypoplastic left heart syndrome who, 48 hours before the current procedure, underwent a modified stage 1 Norwood operation.  Given the scale of the procedure and the size of the patient (2.5 kg), we chose to leave the chest open to facilitate postoperative care.  He is now returned to the operating room for delayed primary chest closure.,PREOP DX:, Open chest status after a modified Norwood stage 1 operation.,POSTOP DX:, Open chest status after a modified Norwood stage 1 operation.,ANESTHESIA:, General endotracheal.,COMPLICATIONS:, None.,FINDINGS:, No evidence of intramediastinal purulence or hematoma.  He tolerated the procedure well.,PROCEDURE DETAILS:,The patient was brought to the operating room and placed on the operating table in the supine position.  After general endotracheal anesthesia, the chest was prepared and draped in the usual sterile manner.  The previously placed AlloDerm membrane was removed.  Mediastinal cultures were obtained, and the mediastinum was extensively irrigated and suctioned.  Both cavities were also irrigated and vacuumed.  The drains were flushed and repositioned.  About 30 cubic centimeters of blood was drawn slowly from the right ear line.  The sternum was then coated with a vancomycin paste.  The proximal aspect of the 5 mm RV-PA conduit was marked with a small titanium clip on its inferior aspect and with another on its lower right side.  The sternum was then closed with stainless steel wires, followed by closure of the subcutaneous tissues with interrupted monofilament sutures.  The skin was closed with interrupted nylon sutures and a sterile dressing was placed.  The peritoneal dialysis catheter as well as the atrial and ventricular pacing wires were removed.  The patient was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit shortly thereafter in very stable condition. I was the surgeon present in the operating room and responsible for the surgical procedure throughout the duration of the case.

transcription: PROCEDURE:  ,Transforaminal Epidural, lumbar.,INFORMED CONSENT: , The risks, benefits and alternatives of the procedure were discussed with the patient.  The patient was given opportunity to ask questions regarding the procedure, its indications and the associated risks.,The risk of the procedure discussed include infection, bleeding, allergic reaction, dural puncture, headache, nerve injuries, spinal cord injury, and cardiovascular and CNS side effects with possible of vascular entry of medications.  I also informed the patient of potential side effects or reactions to the medications potentially used during the procedure including sedatives, narcotics, nonionic contrast agents, anesthetics, and corticosteroids.,The patient was informed both verbally and in writing.  The patient understood the informed consent and desired to have the procedure performed.,PROCEDURE: , Oxygen saturation and vital signs were monitored continuously throughout the procedure.  The patient remained awake throughout the procedure in order to interact and give feedback.  The X-ray technician was supervised and instructed to operate the fluoroscopy machine.,The patient was placed in the prone position on the treatment table with a pillow under the lower abdomen to reduce the natural lumbar lordosis.  The skin over and surrounding the treatment area was cleaned with betadine.  The area was covered with sterile drapes, leaving a small window opening for needle placement.  Fluoroscopy was used to identify the boney landmarks of the facet joints and the planned needle approach.  The skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle within the planned approach were anesthetized with 1 % lidocaine.,With fluoroscopy, a *** spinal needle was gently guided into the superior-anterior neuroforamin lateral to the mid-pedicular line at ***.  Multiple fluoroscopic views were used to ensure proper needle placement.  Approximately *** of non-ionic contrast agent was injected into the joint under real time fluoroscopic observation.  Correct needle placement was confirmed by production of an appropriate epidurogram and radiculogram without concurrent vascular dye pattern.  Finally the treatment solution, consisting of *** was injected.,All injected medications were preservative free.  Sterile techniques were used throughout the procedure.,COMPLICATIONS:  ,None. transcription: PROCEDURE: , Right sacral alar notch and sacroiliac joint/posterior rami radiofrequency thermocoagulation.,ANESTHESIA:  ,Local sedation.,VITAL SIGNS: , See nurse's notes.,COMPLICATIONS: , None.,DETAILS OF PROCEDURE: , INT was placed.  The patient was in the operating room in the prone position.  The back prepped with Betadine.  The patient was given sedation and monitored.  Under fluoroscopy, the right sacral alar notch was identified.  After placement of a 20-gauge, 10 cm SMK needle into the notch, a positive sensory, negative motor stimulation was obtained.  Following negative aspiration, 5 cc of 0.5% of Marcaine and 20 mg of Depo-Medrol were injected.  Coagulation was then carried out at 90oC for 90 seconds.  The SMK needle was then moved to the mid-inferior third of the right sacroiliac joint.  Again the steps dictated above were repeated.,The above was repeated for the posterior primary ramus branch right at S2 and S3 by stimulating along the superior lateral wall of the foramen; then followed by steroid injected and coagulation as above.,There were no complications.  The patient was returned to outpatient recovery in stable condition.

transcription: DIAGNOSES:,1.  Cervical dystonia.,2.  Post cervical laminectomy pain syndrome.,Ms. XYZ states that the pain has now shifted to the left side.  She has noticed a marked improvement on the right side, which was subject to a botulinum toxin injection about two weeks ago.  She did not have any side effects on the Botox injection and she feels that her activities of daily living are increased, but she is still on the oxycodone and methadone.  The patient's husband confirms the fact that she is doing a lot better, that she is more active, but there are still issues yet regarding anxiety, depression, and frustration regarding the pain in her neck.,PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:,  The patient is appropriate.  She is well dressed and oriented x3.  She still smells of some cigarette smoke.  Examination of the neck shows excellent reduction in muscle spasm on the right paraspinals, trapezius and splenius capitis muscles.  There are no trigger points felt and her range of motion of the neck is still somewhat guarded, but much improved.  On the left side, however, there is significant muscle spasm with tight bands involving the multifidus muscle with trigger point activity and a lot of tenderness and guarding.  This extends down into the trapezius muscle, but the splenius capitis seems to be not involved.,TREATMENT PLAN:,  After a long discussion with the patient and the husband, we have decided to go ahead and do botulinum toxin injection into the left multifidus/trapezius muscles.  A total of 400 units of Botox is anticipated.  The procedure is being scheduled.  The patient's medications are refilled.  She will continue to see Dr. Berry and continue her therapy with Mary Hotchkinson in Victoria.

transcription: PROCEDURE: , Hip injection.,INFORMED CONSENT:  ,The risks, benefits and alternatives of the procedure were discussed with the patient. The skin over and surrounding the treatment area was cleaned with Betadine.  The area was covered with sterile drapes, leaving a small window opening for needle placement.  Fluoroscopy was used to identify the boney landmarks of the hip and the planned needle approach.  The femoral artery was located by palpation of the pulse.  The skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle within the planned needle approach were anesthetized with 1% Lidocaine.  All injected medications were preservative free.  With fluoroscopy, a *** spinal needle was gently guided into the ***.  Multiple fluoroscopic views were used to ensure proper needle placement.  Approximately *** nonionic contrast agent was injected under direct real-time fluoroscopic observation. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was sent to the recovery room in good condition.,DISCUSSION: , Post-procedure vital signs and oximetry were stable.  The patient was discharged with instructions to ice the injection site as needed for 15-20 minutes as frequently as twice per hour for the next day and to avoid aggressive activities for 1 day.  The patient was told to resume all medications.  The patient was told to be in relative rest for 1 day but then could resume all normal activities.,The patient was instructed to seek immediate medical attention for shortness of breath, chest pain, fever, chills, increased pain, weakness, sensory or motor changes, or changes in bowel or bladder function.,Follow up appointment was made in approximately 1 week.

transcription: PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,  Cervical epidural steroid injection, C5-6.,ASSISTANT:,  None.,ANESTHESIA:,  Local.,DETAILS OF PROCEDURE: , The patient was brought to the operating theater and placed prone onto the radiolucent table.  Subsequent monitored anesthesia care was administered.  The C-arm was brought into the operative field and an AP view of the lumbar spine was obtained with particular attention to the C5-6 level.  The neck area was then prepped with Betadine solution and draped sterile.  A metallic marker was placed over the C5-6 lamina and a skin wheal was raised in the skin.  A #20-gauge Tuohy needle was then advanced into the spinal canal using 1% Xylocaine anesthetic and the depth of penetration to the C5 lamina was determined.  The needle was redirected into the interlaminar space and advanced to the previously determined level.  A 10 cc syringe was then placed onto the end of the needle and, using an air-negative technique, the needle was advanced into the epidural space.  When a free flow of air was produced, a solution of 80 mg Depo-Medrol, 2 cc of 1% Xylocaine injectable, and 5 cc of normal saline was then injected into the epidural space.  The Tuohy needle was removed.  Betadine was cleansed from the skin.  A bandage was placed over the needle entrance point.  The patient was turned supine onto a regular hospital bed and subsequently allowed to be awakened from anesthesia.  The patient was taken to the recovery room in stable condition.

transcription: OPERATION PERFORMED: , Cervical epidural steroid injection C7-T1.,ANESTHESIA:,  Local and Versed 2 mg IV.,COMPLICATIONS:  ,None.,DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE:  ,The patient was placed in the seated position with the neck flexed the forehead was placed on a cervical rest.  The head and cervical spine were restrained.  The patient was monitored with a blood pressure cuff, EKG and pulse oximetry.  The skin was prepped and draped in sterile classical fashion.  Excess cleansing solution was removed from the skin.  Local anesthesia was injected at C7-T1.  An 18-gauge Tuohy needle was then placed in the epidural space with loss of resistance technique and a saline-filled syringe utilizing a midline intralaminar approach.,Once the epidural space was identified, a negative aspiration for heme or CSF was done.  This was followed by the injection of 6 cc of saline mixed with methyl prednisolone acetate 120 mg in aliquots of 2 cc.  Negative aspirations were done prior to each injection.  The needle was cleared with saline prior to its withdrawal.  The patient tolerated the procedure well without any apparent difficulties or complications.

transcription: PROCEDURE: , Left transforaminal epidural steroid injection L3-L4 (L3 nerve root) and left transforaminal epidural steroid injection L4-L5 (L4 nerve root) under fluoroscopic guidance., PATIENT PROFILE: , This is a 44-year-old female.  The patient reports significantly increasing pain over the past few weeks.  Additionally, the patient has associated radicular symptoms of pain, radiating to the L3 dermatome distribution and the L4 dermatome distribution.  She has been in post-posterior fusion and lumbar decompression status for the past few years.  Due to the nature of the patient's persistent pain, an epidural steroid injection is recommended.  Alternatives, benefits and risks were discussed with the patient.  The patient verbalized his understanding of the risks as well as the alternatives and wished to continue the procedure.  A signed and attested informed consent has been placed on file.,PRE-OPERATION DIAGNOSIS:, Left leg pain, Left leg weakness, Left radicular pain L3-4, Left radicular pain L4-5, Lumbar spondylosis.,POST-OPERATION DIAGNOSIS:, Left leg pain, Left leg weakness, Left radicular pain L3-4, Left, Radicular pain L4-5, Lumbar spondylosis.,ANESTHESIA:, Midazolam 2 mg IV Fentanyl 50 mcg IV.,FINDINGS:,PAIN MANAGEMENT:, Patient reports significantly increasing pain over the last few weeks.  The patient now rates the pain at 8/10.  The reported pain is at L3-4 and L4-5.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:,The patient was placed in the supine position on the radiolucent operating table.  The lumbar area was prepared and draped in an appropriate sterile manner.  The left L3-L4 level was identified for transforaminal epidural injection and the overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue were anesthetized.  A 22-gauge 3.5-inch B-bevel spinal needle was passed through the skin papule and advanced in a ventral direction until the tip of the needle was properly placed in the left superior posterior intervertebral foramen, as confirmed by AP and lateral fluoroscopic views.  No blood was drawn.  There was no CSF ​​flow.  Following a negative aspiration, 1 mL of Isovue-M200 was injected to perform the epidurogram.  There was appropriate needle placement and no intravascular or intrathecal flow.  1 mL of a 40 mg/mL solution of Kenalog and 1 mL of 1% lidocaine were injected. Attention was then turned to the next injection.  The lumbar area was prepared and draped in an appropriate sterile manner.  The left L4-L5 level was identified for transforaminal epidural injection and a skin wheal was made at the spinal needle entry site.  A 22-gauge 3.5-inch spinal needle was passed through the skin papule and advanced in a ventral direction until the tip of the needle was properly placed in the left superior posterior intervertebral foramen, as confirmed by AP and lateral fluoroscopic views.  No blood was drawn.  There was no CSF ​​flow.  Following a negative aspiration, 1 ml of Isovue-M200 was injected to produce the epidurogram.  There was appropriate needle placement and no intravascular or intrathecal flow.  1 mL of a 40 mg/mL solution of Kenalog and 1 mL of 1% lidocaine were injected.  The patient tolerated the procedure well.,DRAINAGES/DRESSING:, Sterile dressing applied, including bandage.,PATIENT TO RECOVERY ROOM:, The patient tolerated the procedure well and was brought to the recovery room in excellent condition.,COMPLICATIONS:, No immediate complications,DISCHARGE ORDERS:,DISPOSITION:, Discharge the patient home today.,ACTIVITY:, The patient can return to his normal activity level in 1 day.,FOLLOW-UP:, Appointment at the surgeon's office in 2 weeks,CPT4 CODE(S):,64483 LT, Injection, anesthetic agent and/or steroid, transforaminal epidural; lumbar or sacral, single level.,64484 LT, Injection, anesthetic agent and/or steroid, transforaminal epidural; lumbar or sacral, each additional level (list separately in addition to the primary procedure code).,76005,Fluoroscopic guidance and localization of the needle or catheter tip for diagnostic or therapeutic injection of the spinal or paraspinous

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Cervical spondylosis.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:, Cervical spondylosis.,OPERATION PERFORMED:, Radiofrequency thermocoagulation (RFTC), medial branch of the posterior sensory branches of the cervix at ***.,SURGEON:, Ralph Menard, M.D.,ANESTHESIA:, Local and IV.,COMPLICATIONS:,None.,PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:After obtaining appropriate consent, the patient was taken to the fluoroscopy room and placed on a fluoroscopy table in a supine position with a chest roll in place.  The neck was placed in a flexed position.  The patient was monitored with a blood pressure cuff, ECG, and pulse oximetry and received oxygen via a nasal cannula.  The patient was lightly sedated.  The skin was prepared and draped in a standard sterile manner. Under fluoroscopic control, the sizes of the articular pillars were identified and marked.  Local anesthesia is infiltrated subcutaneously and deep to these previously marked points.  Once anesthesia was established, a 10 cm 22 gauge insulated needle with a 5 mm non-insulated stimulating tip was placed in contact with the size of the articular pillars at the previously mentioned affected levels.  This was performed under direct fluoroscopic control using a gun barrel technique with PA views initially for orientation and then a lateral view to determine needle depth.  For RFTCs of the medial branches C3 to C6, needles are placed along the ventral aspect of a line that connects the anteroposterior largest diameter of the articular pillar but remains dorsal to the foramen as seen on lateral imaging.  For a C7 medial branch RFTC, the needle tip is positioned higher so as to cover the superior articular process.  For a C8 medial branch RFTC, the needle is placed at the junction of the superior articular facet and the base of the transverse process of T1. Sensory stimulation was performed at 50 Hz from 0 to 2.0 volts.  Stimulation was stopped once maximum voltage was delivered or the patient described a buzzing sensation indicating that it was a non-painful nerve, or this caused a replication of their concordant pain.  Stimulation was then changed to 2 Hz for motor stimulation and advanced to 2.0 volts or until motor stimulation was found at that level.  If motor stimulation occurred, the needle was repositioned to abolish it while still causing concordant pain, or the RFTC was discontinued at that level. If sensory stimulation caused concordant pain without motor stimulation, the area was then anesthetized with 1 cc of 0.5% Marcaine with 5 mg of methylprednisolone acetate.  Once anesthesia was established, radiofrequency lesioning was then performed at 65 degrees for 60 seconds.  The same procedure was carried out at all levels concerned.  The patient tolerated the procedure well without any difficulty or complications.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Low back pain.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: , Low back pain.,PROCEDURE: , Lumbar epidural steroid injection, L5-S1.,ANESTHESIA: , local.,SPECIAL EQUIPMENT: , Fluoroscopic unit.,PROCEDURE DETAILS: , The patient was taken to the radiology room and placed in a supine position where the entire back region was scrubbed, prepped and draped in a sterile manner with Betadine solution.  The lumbar area was then covered with sterile towels and sterile drapes.  The surgeon wore sterile gloves and mask to create a sterile environment for the epidural injection.  The X-ray fluoroscopy machine was then brought into the sterile field for PA radiographic visualization of the spine.  A Steinmann pin was then placed on the spine to locate the level of the intended injection.  Local infiltration using preservative-free 0.5% Xylocaine via a 25-gauge needle was then placed into the dermis and subcutaneous tissue.  A Tuohy needle was then oriented perpendicular to the skin and was then advanced through the dermis and subcutaneous tissues.  A continuous injection of preservative-free 0.5% Xylocaine was used when advancing the Tuohy needle into the deeper spiny tissues.  A solution of 80 mg of Depo-Medrol with 2 cc of 1% Xylocaine Injection and 5 cc of normal saline was then injected into the epidural space.

transcription: PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Low back pain.,POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: ,Low back pain.,PROCEDURE PERFORMED:,1.  L2-3 lumbar discogram.,2.  L3-4 lumbar discogram.,3.  L4-5 lumbar discogram.,4.  L5-S1 Lumbar Discogram.,ANESTHESIA: ,IV Sedation.,PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: ,The patient was brought to the radiology room and placed face down on a radiolucent table.  The C-arm was brought into the surgical field and AP, left right oblique, and lateral fluoroscopic images from the L1-2 to L5-S1 levels were obtained.  We then proceeded to prepare the lower back with a Betadine solution and drape it sterilely.  Using an oblique approach to the spine, the L5-S1 level was approached using an inclined C-arm with oblique projection to allow perpendicular penetration of the disc space.  A metal marker was then placed laterally and a needle entry point was determined.  A skin papule was lifted with 1% Xylocaine and a #18 gauge needle was advanced to the level of the disc space using AP, oblique, and lateral fluoroscopic projections.  A second needle, a 6-inch 22-gauge needle, was then introduced into the disc space and, with AP and lateral fluoroscopic projections, was placed centrally in the nucleus.  We then performed similar needle placement at the L4-5, L3-4, and L2-3 levels. A solution of Isovue 300 with 1 g of Ancef was then drawn into a 10 cc syringe and without informing the patient of our injection, we then proceeded to sequentially inject the disc spaces.

transcription: PROCEDURE NAME:, Hypogastric plexus block.,ANESTHESIA:, Local.,PROCEDURE:, The patient was in the operating room in a supine position with the back prepared and draped in a sterile manner.  Local anesthesia was used to create a skin papule 8 to 10 cm lateral to the L4 spinous process bilaterally from the midline.  Starting from the left side, a 6-inch 20-gauge needle was placed at the left facet L5-S1 under AP fluoroscopic view.  In lateral view, the tip of the needle was at the level of the lower third of the LS vertebral body, anterior aspect.  Next, 5 cc of Omnipaque dye was injected, showing linear spread along the anterior portion of L5 to the sacral promontory.  After negative aspiration, 18 cc of 0.25% marcaine plus 40 mg of Depo-Medrol was injected.  There were no complications.  The above sequence was repeated for the right side.  There were no complications.  The patient was discharged to outpatient recovery in stable condition.
